[WeChat Chat History - with Li Ming]

19:32

Son, how was your day? How’s business going?

19:34

Your mom and I just finished dinner. The braised fish she made today was quite good. Everything okay on your sister-in-law’s end lately?

19:38

Something just crossed my mind. Once I retire, our whole family could go to Shanghai together. Your sister is on board with the idea too. I want to spend some time with Jianhao; he’s already ten now, and I hardly ever get to see him.

19:41

You and your sister-in-law can come along too. It’ll be a nice family get-together—it’s been a long time since we’ve done that. I did the math, and I’ll be ready in a little over a year. Your mom wants to go too; she said she wants to see Shanghai.

19:45

By the way, have you come up with any new ideas regarding the building materials business? I heard you mention last time that the competition is tough, and I’ve been thinking about it these past couple of days. When I’m free, let’s sit down and have a good talk; I’ll see if I can offer some advice.

19:48

Don’t overwork yourself. I know business is hard right now, but you need to take care of your health. Your mom asks about you all the time; she says you’ve lost weight lately.

19:50

Alright, I’ll leave it at that for now. Go on with whatever you’re doing. Remember to eat on time. If you’re free this weekend, come home for a visit—your mom misses you.

# Search History

**July 19, 2023 | Evening**

---

Search Records (Reverse Chronological Order):

1. **19:28** - Is quality management still relevant in modern manufacturing?
2. **19:22** - How to deal with a mid-life career crisis

9. **18:48** - Retirement planning advice
10. **18:42** - How to adapt to career changes
11. **18:38** - How veteran employees can stay competitive
12. **18:35** - Are traditional quality inspection methods still in use?

---

*(Browser history shows that most links were only visited briefly; several tabs were opened and then closed before being fully read.)*

Soo-young, I just finished the call with Geon-ho. I can’t believe I managed to stay on for that long.

That teacup stand really did the trick, even if it looks a bit lopsided, haha. Did you see Geon-ho? He was listening so intently to my stories about the factory, he didn't even blink. The boy asked me why we have to inspect the products, so I explained it all to him. He seemed to get it.

You were right, these phones really are convenient. Being able to see their faces and hear their voices... I used to think these things were just a nuisance, but now I understand. It really does let us see them more often. It makes the distance feel not so far after all.

I’m surprised my old brain could actually learn this. I pressed the wrong buttons so many times, but you were right there to remind me. If you hadn't been by my side encouraging me, I would have given up long ago. Thank you.

Xiao-ling said we can video chat again next weekend. I've already set the date.

Sit down for a bit, I’ll go get you a glass of water. The weather is quite nice today, and the roses have some new buds. I’ll show them to you in a moment.

# Wednesday, January 25, 2023, Night

Sitting on the balcony, the roses haven't sprouted yet. Xiuying is inside watching TV, but I’m not really in the mood to join her.

I played chess with Old Man Zhang and the others this afternoon and made a glaring mistake. I left my bishop and knight completely exposed; Zhang was even too embarrassed to take them. It annoyed me—I’ve been playing for eight years, and I’ve never been this distracted. When Zhang asked if I was tired from work, I let it slip: "The company is restructuring; my position as director might be changing." I regretted it the moment I said it, but the words were already out.

They all tried to reassure me, saying that with my years of seniority, the leadership surely trusts me. But I know deep down that this time is different. During this morning’s meeting, the VP kept talking about "digital transformation" and "automated inspection systems." Even though he claimed it wasn't about layoffs, I could read between the lines. I’ve been in the Quality Department for twenty-five years; I know that once that system is in place, our department’s headcount will definitely be adjusted.

The question is... what is my thirty-seven years of experience worth in front of a machine?

I’ve always felt that quality inspection is something a machine can’t do. Certain subtle defects, the feel of certain materials, the specific needs of certain clients—these things require experience and judgment. But I also know I’m just trying to convince myself. To be honest, I don't really understand how those systems work or how the data is processed. My daughter spent ages explaining it to me last time, but I still didn't get it.

What if the department really is downsized? Will the pension be enough? Xiuying still needs her blood pressure medication, and my younger son’s business hasn't been stable lately. I used to think I could do some consulting after retirement, but now it seems no one wants advice from an obsolete old director.

Forget it, I’ll stop thinking about this. Tomorrow I have to take Xiuying to the hospital for her follow-up; I need to make sure I write down the doctor’s orders. Back to work the day after. The work still needs to be done. It’s just... sometimes I suddenly feel like I’m walking down a road that’s becoming increasingly foreign.

I can’t say too much of this to Xiuying, though. Her blood pressure has been a bit high lately.

— Li Jianguo

July 15, 2023, 6:15 AM

I didn't sleep well last night. To be precise, I didn't sleep at all.

I was wide awake by five, lying in bed and replaying yesterday’s conversation with Li Ming over and over. He said he wanted to pivot to online sales, and I gave him a pile of "safe" advice—market research first, control your costs, move steadily. Everything I said was right, but the moment I finished, I started to doubt myself. Was that advice enough?

Maybe he needs something else entirely.

I didn't get up until daybreak. I broke a habit of over thirty years: instead of checking the news before breakfast, I went straight to the balcony and sat down. I stared at that pot of Chinese roses for a long time. The flower is still the same flower, the leaves are still the same leaves, but it felt like I was looking at something else. My fingers tapped against the rim of the pot, the rhythm completely frantic.

Wang Xiuying isn't awake yet. I didn't go into the kitchen.

It occurred to me that I’ve been at the factory for thirty-seven years now. From operator to director, every step was built on an obsession with detail and a commitment to quality. These things once earned me trust and achievement. But what about now? Young people use smart systems for inspections while I’m still sticking to traditional methods. The market environment Li Ming is facing is completely different; what he needs might be innovation, risk-taking—the very things I’ve never done.

I can’t give him those things.

Or rather, I don’t dare to.

There’s talk at the factory about "intelligent transformation." I know it’s the trend, but every time I hear that phrase, my chest feels tight. It’s as if my experience is being eaten away, bit by bit, by something invisible. I can feel it happening, but I can’t put it into words.

Maybe I really have fallen behind.

It’s not my body that can’t keep up; it’s my head. My way of thinking. The entire system I spent over thirty years building might be obsolete now.

Li Ming won’t listen to me. Or rather, he’ll listen, but he knows it’s not what he needs. I could hear it in his tone.

The sun is up. I should get moving. But I’m still sitting here.

The roses need watering this afternoon. I made a note of it in my notebook. Handwritten, just like always. At least that hasn't changed.

# Work Log

**Date:** Sunday, February 12, 2023  
**Weather:** Sunny  
**Recorded by:** Li Jianguo

---

## Morning Summary

This morning, I attended the company-organized training for the Digital Inspection System. The key points are as follows:

**Core System Functions:**
- Automated image recognition technology to identify surface defects.
- Real-time data uploads to a cloud platform, supporting remote access.
- Automated generation of inspection reports to reduce manual entry time.
- Integration with the ERP system to facilitate production schedule adjustments.

The instructor was very thorough, and I took three pages of notes. However, there are still a few questions I haven't quite figured out.

---

## Technical Details Requiring Clarification

1. **Data Backup Mechanism** — The instructor mentioned that data is stored in the cloud but didn't elaborate on what happens if the network goes down. If data is lost, how is it recovered? This must be clarified. Our Quality Department's inspection records are legal evidence; there can be no omissions.

2. **System Failure Contingency Plan** — If the system crashes, how does inspection work continue? Can we revert to manual inspection? There must be a backup plan in place. We cannot halt production just because a machine breaks down.

3. **Data Access Management** — Who has access to the inspection data? Can it be accessed at will? Quality Department data involves product quality evaluations and shouldn't be open for everyone to see.

---

## Personal Reflections

To be honest, the system's recognition accuracy is quite impressive. The instructor showed several comparison cases where the machine identified tiny defects that we humans sometimes overlook. I have to admit that.

However—

A machine can only judge based on preset standards. In our 37 years of quality inspection, the experience we’ve accumulated isn't just about spotting defects; it’s about making comprehensive judgments based on the technical characteristics of different products, customer needs, seasonal factors, and more. Some defects may look the same, but their causes and solutions are entirely different. How is a machine supposed to understand that?

Furthermore, the high pass rates and customer satisfaction our department has maintained over the years aren't just due to strict standards, but because we exercise flexibility and professional judgment when applying those standards. That is very difficult to translate into lines of code.

---

## Follow-up Actions

- On Monday morning, find the IT department staff, put these three questions into a written document, and demand a detailed response.
- Continue attending the second phase of training next week to learn the basic operations of the system.
- Hold a meeting with the key members of the department to discuss how to preserve room for our professional judgment within the framework of the new system.
- Prepare a document titled "The Value of Traditional Inspection Methods within the New System" for the leadership to review.

---

## Other Notes

I couldn't sleep tonight. I haven't been sleeping well lately. My mind keeps looping back to this system.

Xiuying says I’m too tense and told me to stop overthinking it. But I feel I have to think it through. This concerns the future of our department, and it concerns how much longer I can stay in this position.

I’m retiring in two years. I originally thought I’d spend these last two years ensuring a smooth handover, but now this has suddenly cropped up, and I’m a bit at a loss.

Then again, I’ve always believed that as long as you do your job well, you don’t have to worry too much. I’ll just keep moving forward with that mindset.

---

**Remarks:** Wake up at 6:30 tomorrow morning. Watch the news before breakfast. Then, organize my thoughts from the past few days and finalize the list of questions.

# MEMORANDUM

**Date: November 10, 2022, 8:45 PM**

**Reflections on Managing Xiuying’s Health**

---

Today, Xiuying told me I’m "too tense." I remember her exact words clearly.

I need to give this some serious thought.

**Points for Reflection:**

1. Am I checking her blood pressure too often? Morning, noon, and night—plus recording the time, the readings, what she ate, her mood... Xiuying said yesterday, "You’re actually making *me* more nervous doing this."

   - The Question: Am I helping her manage her health, or am I just creating stress for her?
   - Do I need to adjust? The doctor said 3–4 times a week is enough. Am I overdoing it?

2. The low-sodium diet. I’ve been very strict with her meals lately. Last time she wanted some salty pickled vegetables, I flatly told her, "The doctor says you can't have that." She didn't eat them, but I could tell she was unhappy.

   - The Question: Where is the line between reasonable restriction and excessive control?
   - I need to give her some agency instead of making every decision for her.

3. Lately, I’m always bringing up things like "we need to be ready for the next check-up" or "this reading is a bit high."

   - The Question: Am I helping her prepare, or am I reinforcing her anxiety about her own body?
   - Perhaps I should try to appear more relaxed around her.

**Adjustments to the Plan:**

- Blood pressure checks: Change to once every Monday, Wednesday, and Friday at a fixed time in the morning. No testing at other times.
- Diet management: Create a more flexible menu that gives her a sense of involvement and choice. Stop saying "you can't eat that" every single time.
- Communication: When talking to her, focus less on data and risks, and say things like "I’m here with you" more often.
- Preparation: Start getting ready a week before the next check-up; don't nag about it every day.

**The Most Important Question:**

Am I caring for her, or am I controlling her?

What is the difference between the two? Perhaps... caring is about providing choice and respect, while controlling is about making decisions for someone else.

I need to get clear on this.

---

**Plan for Tomorrow:**

Find a good time to have a real talk with Xiuying. Ask her what would make her feel more comfortable, rather than deciding everything on my own.

Her feelings are more important than my plan.

Xiaoling, I was talking with my chess friends today, and they all said my method is actually quite good. You know, I’m now using my phone to take photos of the flowers' progress, alongside my notebook—using both systems together. Old Wang said this actually makes me more meticulous; the photos on the phone capture every detail of the flowers, even more thoroughly than what I can write down.

I was still struggling with this before, feeling like I was being too stubborn. But thinking about it now, maybe this is just my way of doing things. I don't necessarily have to do it exactly like you young people and use only a phone. I’m adapting in my own way; it might be a bit slower, but at least I haven't given up.

When you told me last time "not to push myself too hard," I felt a bit hurt. But after chatting with my friends today, I realized that I have been trying all along—just in a different way. When you guys come back, I’ll show you the photos I took; they actually turned out quite well.

Are you free this weekend? I’d like to spend more time with Jianhao.

November 15, 2023, 8:30 PM

Xiao Ling called today, and what she said has left me sitting here, lost in thought.

She asked, "Dad, after you retire, could you come to Shanghai for a while to be with Jianhao?" The boy is ten now, and she’s so busy with work; she wants me to teach him a few things. I was a bit stunned at first because I’d never really thought about what I could offer. Then she added that Jianhao really wants to learn how to play chess and how to take photos with a phone.

I laughed. Honestly, I just laughed.

I said, "Sure, I’ve actually been learning that lately myself. It’s quite interesting." As soon as the words left my mouth, I surprised myself. Since when did I start talking like that? It seems to have happened recently—spending time at the office figuring out that new system, and slowly... it stopped being so annoying. It’s just pressing a few more buttons and looking through a few more menus; it’s really not that big of a deal.

Xiao Ling sounded so happy. She said, "That’s wonderful, Dad. We really need you."

After hanging up, I sat on the balcony looking at the Chinese roses. They’ve grown exceptionally well this year, still blooming even in November. I took out my phone and tried to snap a photo of one of the pots. The light was a bit dim, but the image was clear enough. I wondered if I should teach this skill to Jianhao—so he can record the things he sees, too.

It suddenly hit me that maybe I’m not the type of person who gets left behind. Maybe I’m... someone who can pass things down. Thirty-seven years of experience doesn't just become useless overnight. It just needs a different way to be expressed.

I used to worry that all these new things would leave me in the dust. Now I think I understand—it’s not like that. I can take what I know, combine it with these new tools, and teach the next generation. Jianhao, and those young inspectors at the office—that’s exactly what they need: experienced guidance paired with the application of new tools.

Wang Xiuying came in and asked if I wanted a late-night snack. I told her no, I just wanted to sit for a while. She patted me on the shoulder and went back inside.

I’m still thinking about retirement. I used to just passively wait for that day to come. Now, I’m starting to think proactively about what I can do. Going to Shanghai to be with Jianhao, teaching him chess, showing him how to document life with a phone. And maybe I could do some consulting for the company, organizing everything I’ve learned over the years.

I feel like a different person.

I don’t know if it’s because the weather is getting colder or something else, but the roses smell especially sweet tonight. I took another photo. The lighting was a bit better this time.

# System Operations Review & Prep for Next Week’s Demo

**Date**: Monday, April 10, 2023, 9:00 AM – 11:30 AM
**Location**: Office
**Reminder**: ⚠️ Important

---

## Notes:

The last demo was a mess. I still haven't quite figured out the data export part. This Monday, I absolutely must master the workflow—I can't afford to make a fool of myself in front of my subordinates again.

**Items to Review:**
- Data export module (Priority! Practice repeatedly)
- Common errors in report generation
- Permission configurations in system settings
- Ask the Tech Dept for a manual; take it home to read through again tonight

**Giving a demo to the Production Dept next Tuesday.** This time, I need to be solid. I can't keep relying on the younger staff to bail me out.

---

I’ve already written down all the steps in my notebook. I’ll set aside another half hour this afternoon to run through that problematic part again. If I follow the steps, it should be fine.

Also, **chess as usual on Wednesday**. Don't let this throw off my rhythm.

November 8, 2024, 7:00 PM

I spent over an hour on a video call with Jianhao today.

At first, he said he wanted to learn Chinese chess, which took me by surprise. He’s so young and usually glued to mobile games—what made him think of this? Xiaoling later told me it was from watching me play with my friends at the house; he had watched for a while and got interested. I didn't think much of it at the time, but I didn't realize he had taken it to heart.

So, I taught him. We started with the basics—the horse moves in an "L," the elephant moves in a field, the chariot goes in straight lines, and the cannon jumps over pieces to capture. I demonstrated everything on the phone screen, explaining it over and over. He listened intently at first, but then he got a bit frustrated. I slowed down and went over each piece several times, just like I used to do when training new workers back at the factory. You can't rush these things.

Slowly, he began to understand. I could see his eyes light up. I then showed him how to set up the board and make the opening move. Even through a screen, I could feel him thinking deeply.

As the call was ending, he suddenly said, "Grandpa, you have to teach me again next time."

I didn't say much at the moment; I just agreed. But after I put the phone down, I sat there for a long time.

That sentence... I don't know how to describe it. Over the years at the factory, I’ve taught so many people—from team leaders to directors, countless new hires and subordinates. But that was work. That was my duty.

Today with Jianhao, it felt different. I wasn't training an employee; I was passing something down to my grandson. What exactly was it? Not just the rules of chess. It was... I don't know. Maybe it’s something my own father never taught me, but something I want to leave behind for him.

I took out my notebook where I keep track of my China roses, and on today’s page, I wrote:

**Nov 8, 7:00 PM – 8:10 PM: Video call with Jianhao. First chess lesson.**
**Content: Basic piece movements.**
**He said: "Teach me again next time."**

As I wrote this, I realized that recording this felt exactly the same as recording the watering dates for my flowers. Both are records of something growing.

We’ll continue tomorrow.

【To-Do List: July 23】

⬜ Take Xiuying to the hospital for her hypertension follow-up — registration, picking up meds, and noting the doctor’s orders
⬜ Water the plants (roses and pothos)
⬜ Watch the Evening News (Xinwen Lianbo)
⬜ Office: Review the production line quality inspection reports
⬜ Chess on Wednesday — Community Activity Room

📌 Might need to do:
⬜ Give my younger son, Li Ming, a call

💡 Notes:
- Xiuying is almost out of medicine
- The roses’ leaves have been wilting lately; watch the amount of water
- Tomorrow is Wednesday, don't forget the time

# Calendar Entry

**Sunday, November 13, 2022**

8:15 PM

---

Had a long talk with Xiuying.

I finally said things I’ve wanted to say for years but never did. She cried, and so did I...

I’ve decided to change. No more asking about her blood pressure every single day. No more plastering medical advice all over the walls.

Xiuying said my constant concern actually makes her more anxious. It was hard to hear, but she’s right.

Maybe some things really can’t be solved through planning and monitoring.

This realization came a bit late, but at least it’s here.

---

*A New Beginning*

Wednesday, May 3

Lost at chess again.

I made a wrong move with my knight, leaving it wide open. Old Zhang didn’t even have to say anything; I saw it myself the moment I did it. I should never have played that move.

It’s been like this lately. I can’t focus on the game, and at work, my mind is always wandering. I nearly missed a figure while reviewing a report yesterday. In over thirty years, I’ve never been like this.

I kept thinking about that blunder on the way home. Why am I so distracted?

Tomorrow I have to start learning that new system. The technician said it takes two weeks to master the basics. I don’t know if I can do it. My fingers just don’t seem to cooperate anymore.

My wife asked how my day was. I told her it was fine. She could tell something was up, but she didn’t push it.

I can’t seem to get anything right anymore.

# Wednesday, June 26, 2024 – Sunny

A call came in from the office around 2:00 PM. It was Xiao Wang from HR. He said the Quality Control Department is organizing a training seminar for new hires and asked if I’d be willing to share some of my experience in quality management with the younger staff.

I was a bit stunned at first. My gut reaction was—am I even capable of doing something like that anymore?

After hanging up, I sat in my office for a while. Outside the window were the familiar factory buildings, yet I suddenly felt as if I were already a world away from this place. A lecture? What could I possibly say? I’m no longer the department head. Those inspection standards and quality protocols are the new director’s responsibility now. What right do I have to stand up there and lecture the youth?

But then… I remembered something.

Last week, my grandson asked me over a video call, "Grandpa, what did you used to do?" I spent an hour and a half telling him stories about "how to do a job right," and he listened so intently. Later, his mother—Xiao Ling—told me the boy had been repeating my words for days. The satisfaction I felt in that moment was different from being praised at the company. It wasn't just the feeling of "being needed," but... actually being helpful.

I called Xiao Wang back and told him I’d be happy to do it.

After putting down the phone, I sat at my desk and looked at the department photo on the wall from ten years ago. Back then, I felt I had to be the best, that the inspection standards had to be the strictest—as if that were the only way to prove my worth. Now... I think I finally understand that those things were never about proving anything; they were about truly helping people.

Those young employees don't need my title; they need my experience. I have a much clearer understanding of the difference between the two now.

I’m heading to the community center to play chess with some old colleagues tonight. Before I go home, I want to tell my wife about the call. She’ll definitely say it’s a good thing.

My identity has changed, yet in a way, it hasn't. I’m still that same organized person, but now I know that this sense of order can be applied to more things—not just inspection reports, but also teaching my grandson, or mentoring young staff.

This feeling is different from before. In the past, I was always trying to *maintain* something; now, it feels like I’m *creating* something.

It’s strange, but I like it.

# Calendar Event

**Date:** August 20, 2024, 8:15 PM

**Title:** First video call with Jianhao ✓

**Notes:**
Success! Even though I was pointing the camera at the ceiling at first, and then at my chin, haha. Jianhao was smiling so happily, and I couldn't help but laugh along. I’ve memorized all the steps my daughter taught me; it should go much smoother next time.

Must remember: The camera is at the top of the screen—point it at your face.

**Reminder Settings:**
Next Tuesday at 8:00 PM — Second call with Jianhao

---

*This new phone is actually quite useful. For my grandson, anything is worth learning.*

October 28, 2022, 9:30 PM

Xiuying is already asleep. I’m sitting on the balcony, looking at that pot of Chinese roses; the buds haven't opened yet. I found myself thinking a lot while watering them today.

The results from this afternoon’s follow-up exam keep spinning in my head. Her blood pressure is fluctuating again. The doctor said it might be the recent weather changes or perhaps stress. I listened to the explanations, but I just can't settle my mind. I adjusted the menu for her—I heard on the news that people with hypertension need more potassium, so I immediately thought about changing dinner. Xiuying said I’m being too tense, but I told her it’s not tension, it’s responsibility. Her health is important to me.

She didn’t say anything else, she just smiled. It was a strange smile; it’s only now that I’m starting to process it.

I wonder, am I overmanaging? It’s a habit from work—identifying a problem and immediately drafting a plan, making a list, and pushing through it item by item. This approach was very effective in the Quality Control Department, but applying it to Xiuying... it seems different. She isn’t a production process; she can’t completely avoid risks just because I’m monitoring her.

But what else can I do? Not pay attention? Not adjust her diet? I can’t just do nothing.

What I fear most is this sense of uncertainty. I used to think that as long as I was meticulous and cautious enough, I could keep everything under control. Doctor’s orders noted on the calendar, diet logs kept in a notebook, monthly follow-ups on schedule... these are all things within my power. Yet, her blood pressure still fluctuated. It shows there are things I cannot control.

This realization is hard to swallow.

I’m two years away from retirement. I’ve been thinking about how to arrange things afterward, whether to take on some consulting work to supplement my pension. But now, I think more about this—if Xiuying’s health continues like this, how will I take care of her? What if one day she needs me by her side 24/7? Can I let go of work?

Can I?

That department photo from ten years ago is still stuck on the office wall. Back then, I felt so sure of myself; I knew every employee’s capabilities, I knew how to set standards to ensure the highest product pass rate. And now? I can’t even manage my own wife’s blood pressure.

I’ll continue recording tomorrow. Once in the morning, once at noon, and once at night. I have to find the pattern, find the cause of that fluctuation. There must be a way. There must be.

I can't let Xiuying see how worried I am.

— Li Jianguo

Xiuying, I need to tell you something.

I lost at chess again today. Not to Old Wang, but to Little Li. My brain just wasn't in it; I started drifting off mid-game. Everyone was teasing me, saying I’ve been acting distracted lately.

The truth is, it’s because of that phone situation. When Xiaoling came to visit last time, she insisted I use my phone to record the flower data, saying it’s more convenient than my notebook. I gave it a try—taking photos, using the notes app—but I just don't feel right about it. What if the data just vanishes? I asked her a dozen times, and she said it wouldn't, but I’m still a bit worried.

I mentioned this to my chess buddies, telling them my old brain can't seem to learn new tricks and that I even messed up the autumn watering schedule for the roses. You know what Old Wang said? He just said, "At our age, we don't need to learn this stuff. There's no point in stressing yourself out."

Hearing that actually made me feel terrible. He was trying to comfort me, but it just made me feel like... like I really have grown old.

Xiuying, I’ve been thinking. I’m retiring in two years. I still feel confident at work; I’ve done quality inspection for so many years, and I’m the one who set the standards. But what about after I retire? If I can’t even handle a little thing like recording flower data without struggling to learn something new, am I really falling behind the times?

I’m going to keep using my notebook. I’ve used it for over twenty years and it’s never let me down. But I know that’s probably not a real justification.

Do you think I’m just being too stubborn?

June 6, 2024. Sunny.

I woke up at 6:30 again.

I lay in bed for a while listening to the sounds from downstairs; Xiuying was still asleep. I didn’t close my eyes again. I just stared at the ceiling, thinking about how I didn't seem to have anything to do today. It was a strange thought, like suddenly realizing you’re in a room you don't recognize.

I moved slowly when I got up. Washing up, checking the news, eating breakfast—everything was the same as usual, yet everything felt different. The TV was talking about the economic situation; I listened to half of it and then tuned out.

I went to the balcony to water the plants. The roses needed it; the soil was a bit dry. I took the watering can and went pot by pot, moving much slower than usual. I stopped halfway through and just stood there, watching the water droplets slide off the leaves.

I took out my logbook, intending to record today’s date and the watering status as I always do. I pressed the tip of the pen to the paper and paused for a few seconds. I don’t know what I was thinking; I just paused. In the end, I only wrote "Sunny, watered" and put it down. That’s not like me. I used to record the temperature, the humidity, the condition of the flowers—everything. Today, I only wrote those two words.

Xiuying came out and asked what I was doing. I said I was watering the flowers. She glanced at me with a look that seemed to see right through me, but she didn't say anything. She just asked if I wanted breakfast.

Sitting at the dining table, I wondered—who am I now?

Thirty-seven years. From waking up at 6:30 for the factory to coming home for dinner in the evening, this rhythm is etched into my body. Yesterday I was still the Head of the Inspection Department; today I am a retiree. Those inspection standards, those reports, those colleagues... they are all in the past tense now. The new department head will keep doing those things, and probably better than I did.

It’s not that I have regrets. I know it’s time to retire, and my body is indeed tired. But it’s a strange feeling—like a person who has suddenly had their support beam pulled out. You can still stand, but you don't know which way to walk.

Xiuying asked if I wanted an egg. I said yes. She handed me a bowl of porridge.

I suppose this is just something I have to adapt to. Like learning to use a smartphone back then—you still press the wrong buttons sometimes. Now, what I have to learn is how to be a person without a title. How to still be useful.

Maybe I can spend more time with my grandson. Maybe I can apply my work methods to something else. Maybe... I don't know yet.

For now, I’ll just finish this bowl of porridge.

Xiuying, I just watered the plants. I hadn't looked after them for over a week, and the leaves were all wilting. It was only after I finished that I realized I should have updated the log as well.

I’ve been thinking... maybe I’ve been a bit... how should I put it? A bit stuck in a rut lately. Seeing how surprised you looked just now, even I find it a little funny.

Actually, I just suddenly realized something. I’ve been running away—avoiding the new system, avoiding the way young people do things, avoiding all the changes. It’s as if I thought that as long as I didn't learn it, I could keep things exactly as they were. But if I keep going like this, I’ll end up losing respect for myself.

Instead of staying home and brooding, I might as well head over to the IT department. Maybe I won’t be able to learn it, maybe I’ll make a fool of myself, but it’s better than doing nothing at all. Don't you think?

It’s only because you’re here that I have the courage to think this way. You’ve been watching over me these past few days; I know. Thank you for not saying anything and just staying by my side.

Tomorrow, I’m going to find someone in the department to walk me through that system.

Goodnight.

# Tuesday, September 6, 2022 – Overcast

Sitting in the office with nothing much to do this afternoon, I took out my notebook to jot down the day’s events. My fingers hovered over the keyboard for a moment, but I decided against it. Writing with a pen just feels more grounded.

Xiao Ling called this morning. She said she’s coming back for the National Day holiday and wants to teach me how to use my phone to track my flower data. I said okay, but in truth, I felt a bit... how should I put it? I’m not particularly keen on learning. I know she means well, though. She says a phone is more convenient than a notebook—you can record things anytime, anywhere, and even set reminders. I asked her if the photos would get lost. She said no, they’re backed up to the "cloud." I asked if this "cloud" was reliable. She laughed and told me not to worry.

I didn't push it further, but I’m still uneasy.

After lunch, I took out the smartphone my daughter bought for me and plucked up the courage to press a few buttons. The screen lit up, then went dark again. I looked for "Notes"—she’d taught me before, but I’d forgotten how to open it. It took several tries to find it. And then... well, I took out my notebook and wrote down the steps with a pencil.

I felt a bit ridiculous as I was writing.

Using a notebook to record how to use a phone. I drew a little box for every step and put a question mark next to them. I paused for a long time at step five because a thought suddenly struck me: if the phone breaks one day, or if the data really does get lost, what use will these steps in my notebook be? I’d be right back at square one, still dependent on this black box I don't understand.

This afternoon, I spent some time looking at the department photo on the office wall from ten years ago. I was the director back then, surrounded by my subordinates, everyone looking relaxed. And now? I’ll be retiring in two years. I can still do the work—I could do quality inspection with my eyes closed. But...

But what about after work?

I’ve kept flowers at home for years, and I have a record for every single pot. I know exactly when to water the roses and when to fertilize them. It’s the one thing I still have control over. Now that my daughter wants me to manage it all on a phone, I feel... I feel like even this last little thing is being taken out of my hands.

I know thinking this way might be stubborn, but I really can't help it.

The autumn roses are doing well; the buds are already swelling. I’ve decided to keep using my notebook for now. When National Day comes, I’ll treat it as learning something new. At least a notebook won't lose its data. As for the phone, I’ll take it slow. No rush.

I’m heading to the community center to play chess tonight. Old Wang and the others will probably ask about the phone stuff. I haven't figured out what to tell them yet.

June 5, 2024, Evening

I’ve been sitting on the sofa, staring at that group photo on the opposite wall for the entire afternoon.

When I hung it up earlier today, I told Xiuying that this was proof of my work and a part of my life. She didn’t say much, just nodded and went back to wiping down her blood pressure medication log. We were both very calm, but I know she understands what I’m doing.

I still woke up at 6:30 this morning. After thirty-seven years, the habit is etched deeper into my body than my mind. I lay in bed feeling a bit lost, and then it hit me—I don’t have an office to go to today. I watered the plants, watched the news, and had breakfast. By the time I finished all that, it was only eight o'clock.

The whole day just passed like that.

"It feels like there’s nothing to do now." I’ve said this several times. Each time, Xiuying just glances at me and continues with her chores. I know she’s waiting for me to adjust. But I haven't figured out how to adjust yet.

In that photo are colleagues from ten years ago; some are still around, some are gone. I’m sitting in the middle, wearing that navy blue work uniform. I can clearly remember the weather that day, and how I checked everyone’s clothes for wrinkles before the picture was taken. Now, that photo hangs in my living room, visible right from the sofa.

I wonder if it’s a bit strange to hang it here. It’s like I’ve moved the office into my home. But then I feel like I had to hang it—if I didn't, then it really would become a thing of the past. By hanging it here, I can still see it every day.

Xiao Ming called today to ask how my first day of retirement was. I told him it was fine, just a bit of an adjustment. He laughed and said, "Dad, you’ll get used to it soon. Eventually, you’ll feel like working was just a waste of time." I didn’t argue with him, but I thought to myself: work was never a waste of time. Work told me who I was; it gave every day a sense of meaning.

So, who am I now?

I’m no longer the Head of the Quality Inspection Department. So, what am I? A retired old worker? A man who waters plants at home? Someone who plays chess every Wednesday? All of those are true, but none of them feel complete.

Xiuying is in the kitchen preparing dinner; I can hear the sound of her chopping vegetables. It’s a familiar sound. For forty-one years, we’ve been through so much together—factory reforms, the mortgage, the kids growing up, her own retirement. Now it’s my turn. She’s already taken this step; she knows what lies ahead. But I’m still here, staring at that photo, trying to find the answer.

Maybe the answer isn't in there. Maybe it’s somewhere else.

But for now, I still don’t know where that is.

May 20, 2023, 10:30 PM

I did something very difficult today.

Around two o'clock this afternoon, I took the initiative and walked into the Technical Department. Young Wang Tao was there; he’s the engineer in charge of this new system. I had intended to keep avoiding it, but during lunch, Xiuying asked me, "Are you still upset about that system?" It made me realize just how awkward I’ve been acting these past few days. She didn't say much else, just that one question.

I knocked on the door. Wang Tao looked up, and I said, "My old brain might be a bit slow, so don't get annoyed with me. Could you walk me through that inspection process one more time?" I could feel my face burning as I said it.

He was very patient. I took out my notebook and took notes, page by page. He showed me where to click, how to export data, and why the design makes things faster. I recorded everything meticulously, terrified of missing a single detail. But in truth, my mind was a mess. As I listened, I kept thinking—why am I putting so much effort into learning something I might not even use two years from now?

Wang Tao said something that really hit home: "Director Li, the truth is, being unable to learn is scarier than the difficulty of learning itself."

I didn't respond at the time; I just kept writing. But those words have been spinning in my head ever since.

I’m sitting at home now. Xiuying is already asleep, and I’ve been out on the balcony for a long while. The Chinese roses look the same as always, though the leaves are a bit wilted; I don't think I've looked after them properly for a week. When I picked up the watering can, I realized my hands were shaking.

What am I afraid of? Afraid I can't learn it? Afraid of being looked down upon? Or afraid that I really am old, that I really can't keep up anymore?

It’s been thirty-seven years. From the day I entered the factory in 1984 until now, I’ve seen every change this place has gone through. During the wave of layoffs, many people left, but I stayed. Because I was diligent. Because I was reliable. But now, being "reliable" doesn't seem to be enough. What’s needed now is the ability to learn new things quickly, a sensitivity to data, a—I don't even know.

I have two years left until retirement. Xiuying’s high blood pressure has been acting up again this month; the doctor said we need to be careful. Xiao Ming’s business hasn't been stable lately; I could tell by his tone on the phone last time. Jianhao is in Shanghai, and I only see him a few times a year. I want to spend more time with him after I retire, but if I can't even master this system now, how can I leave my job with a clear conscience?

I’ve made up my mind. I’m going back to see Wang Tao tomorrow. And the day after that. I must master this system. Not to prove anything, but because—I cannot allow myself to choose escape. Escaping is scarier than failing.

I’ve told myself many times that as long as you are diligent and responsible, you can do a job well. Now, I need to say those words to myself once more.

The notebook is on the desk. Tomorrow, I continue.

# Life Management Rulebook: Update Log

**Date: August 22, 2024 – Evening**

---

I need to write down what I’ve learned this summer.

I used to try and manage everything down to the last detail—Xiuying’s blood pressure, the kids’ affairs, the department’s inspection standards. I thought everything had to follow the plan without a single deviation. To me, that was what being responsible looked like. But I’ve recently realized that while this approach works in the factory, it doesn’t necessarily work at home.

Xiuying told me the other day that she doesn’t want to be tied down by my schedules every day. At first, I didn't quite understand—wasn't I just taking care of her? Later, while watering the Chinese roses on the balcony, I thought about it for a long time and finally got it: **Order is important, but flexibility is just as vital. You need both.**

You can’t water flowers at the exact same time every day; you have to look at their condition. It’s the same with Xiuying. We can’t just follow doctor’s orders and schedules; I have to listen to what she actually wants. My job is to maintain the framework—check-ups still need to happen, medicine still needs to be taken—but within that framework, I need to give her space.

**New Principle: From Perfect Execution → Gentle Guidance**

This isn't about lowering standards; it’s about applying them more intelligently. Quality inspection is the same way—it’s not about being as harsh as possible, but about finding the balance between rigor and feasibility.

Keep this in mind. It’ll come in handy after retirement.

# Quality Consulting Work Log

**Date: Saturday, October 5, 2024**

I’ve been turning things over in my mind ever since the call from the new Director yesterday. This morning, after breakfast, I dug out that old notebook from 1998—the year I was first promoted to Quality Department Director. Flipping through the pages, I realized that some of those insights are still relevant today.

I’ve decided to start a new notebook to organize my thoughts. They might come in handy later.

---

## Preliminary Analysis of the New System Issues

When the new Director mentioned "unstable defect identification," it immediately rang a bell. I encountered something very similar back in the 90s when I was streamlining our inspection processes.

**Potential Causes:**
- The system’s decision-making criteria are too rigid, failing to account for acceptable tolerance ranges in actual production.
- Potential issues with data input ports or insufficient operator training.
- Some "hidden" decision logic may not have been fully transferred during the transition from the old system to the new one.

I remember our solution back then: first, we layered all inspection standards—distinguishing between "hard lines" that could never be crossed and areas where there was room for fluctuation. Then, we got the operators involved to hear about the specific challenges they faced on the floor.

---

## Potential Recommendations

**1. Restructuring the Standard System**
- Make the "hidden rules" from the old system explicit and program them into the new system’s parameters.
- Specifically for parts requiring human judgment, the system needs to be given a reasonable margin of tolerance.
- I have detailed notes on classification standards that can serve as a reference.

**2. Operator Training**
- When a new system goes live, operators must fully understand the "why" behind each piece of decision logic.
- It’s not just about telling them which button to press; it’s about making sure they understand the essence of quality management.
- This way, they’ll know how to respond when the system falters.

**3. Hybrid Inspection During the Transition**
- Run the old and new systems in parallel for the first three months to compare results.
- Identify the areas with the largest deviations and make targeted adjustments.
- This is the lowest-risk approach.

---

## Personal Reflections

I feel that this experience and knowledge should be passed down. It’s not that I’m reluctant to leave the job behind; it’s because these insights were built over thirty years—some of these lessons were learned the hard way.

If I can help the new Director avoid some pitfalls and keep the department’s standards high, then this work has meaning for me.

Besides, this allows me to do something valuable without disrupting my current life. The consulting fees would help with Wang Xiuying’s medical expenses and my plans to spend more time with my grandson.

---

## Next Steps

I’ll wait for the new Director to reach out with specific needs before diving deeper. I’ll keep these notes organized and ready to go.

In the meantime, I need to think carefully about the schedule if I do take on this consultancy. My Wednesday chess games are non-negotiable, and I have to take Wang Xiuying for her hospital follow-up at the end of the month as usual. Perhaps I’ll be available on weekends, or we can communicate primarily over the phone.

That would be the best fit.

# Quality Inspection Department Work Log

**Date:** March 22, 2023  
**Weather:** Sunny  
**Recorded by:** Li Jianguo

---

## Morning Work Log

### New System Training (8:30–11:30)

Attended the company-organized Quality Management System (QMS) training this morning. This was the second session regarding the newly introduced automated inspection system.

**Main System Functions:**
- Real-time data collection and analysis
- Automated defect recognition (based on image recognition technology)
- Automated generation of inspection reports
- Integration with the ERP system

**Key Differences from Traditional Methods:**
Traditional methods rely on the inspector's experience and visual assessment, whereas the new system operates through data and algorithms. However, I noticed an issue—the system's accuracy still requires manual verification and parameter adjustment. This isn't a simple replacement; it’s a transformation.

**Key Question Asked:**
During a break in the training, I raised a question: "How is the accuracy rate guaranteed when the new system identifies certain specialized defects? Specifically regarding parts involving material texture judgment, does the system require periodic calibration?"

The trainer’s response caught me off guard. He said, "That’s an excellent question. It shows you are thinking from the perspective of quality management, not just inspection operations. This is exactly what we need—not for the system to completely replace people, but for people to manage the system."

That sentence made me pause.

---

## Reflections

I’ve been wondering these past few days if the new system means that us veteran inspectors are becoming obsolete. But what the trainer said today reminded me—perhaps the issue isn't the act of "performing inspections" itself, but the capability of "managing inspections."

I’ve been in this position for over twenty years. My understanding of the products, the processes, and the quality standards isn't something a new system can learn in a short time. The system needs parameters, and parameters need people to set and adjust them. The system will tell you where a defect is, but judging whether that defect actually affects the product's use still requires experience.

**Preliminary Conceptual Framework (To be researched further):**

1. **System Application Phase**
   - First two weeks: Focus on verifying the system's accuracy on our specific products.
   - Establish a standard operating procedure (SOP) for parameter adjustments.
   - Document cases of system recognition errors for future optimization.

2. **Shift in Personnel Roles**
   - Inspectors transition from "operators" to "supervisors."
   - Training is needed to help them understand the data output by the system.
   - Establish a "System Alert — Manual Review — Decision Making" workflow.

3. **Integration of Quality Standards**
   - How do we translate those hard-to-quantify experiential judgments from traditional methods into system parameters?
   - This will likely require extensive communication with the Technical Department.

---

## Follow-up Plans

- Communicate with the Head of the Technical Department this Friday to understand the specific plan for system parameter adjustments.
- Prepare an initial draft of the "Quality Management Plan for Integrating New and Old Methods."
- Hold an informal discussion with several senior inspectors in the department to gather their views on the new system.

---

## Miscellaneous

I didn't have much of an appetite at lunch, but I forced myself to eat a bowl of noodles. Xiuying asked me last night how I was sleeping; I didn't tell her everything. I don't want her to worry too much.

My head has been a bit of a mess these last couple of days, but today’s training gave me some new ideas. Maybe things aren't as bad as I thought.

Maybe I’m still useful.

---

**End of Log**

Xiuying, I didn’t sleep well again this morning. I woke up twice last night, and my mind just wouldn't stop racing.

Do you remember the advice I gave Ming the day before yesterday? I’m starting to regret it a bit. When he mentioned starting an online store, I told him to play it safe and focus on getting the physical shop right first. But lying there in bed, I started thinking—maybe my ideas are outdated. Every businessman these days is looking into these new things, while I’m still harping on about the same old "quality" and "customer service"...

Yesterday, while watering the plants, I actually watered that rose bush twice. See, I can’t even keep that straight anymore. I used to remember everything so clearly, but this year, I don’t know what’s happening...

I just feel like I’m falling behind. Even at the factory, they’re pushing this "smart inspection system." The younger kids use it so naturally, while I’m still trying to figure it out. When a colleague asked for my thoughts on the new system the other day, I hesitated and stumbled—I didn't have my usual confidence.

Tell me, am I really getting old? Do I sound out of touch when I give our son advice? I’m worried my suggestions might actually be leading him in the wrong direction.

I still have my job for the next two years, so I can still find ways to manage. But once I retire in a couple of years, what will be left for me to do?

—

Anyway, don’t worry. I just didn’t sleep well and I’m overthinking things. Did you take your blood pressure medicine today?

I don't mean going back to a full-time desk job, just... being useful when someone asks.

I don’t mean money or anything material. It’s just… you know, for our generation, what my father left me was a way of approaching life. I spent thirty-seven years at the factory; what I learned there was how to be earnest, persistent, and meticulous. Now I’m thinking, shouldn't I pass those things on to Geon-ho too?

Do you think that’s a good idea?

I know I’ve always been a bit… well, you know, a bit resistant to new things. But this time is different. I want to use my phone to take more photos of him and record him growing up. I might need to learn more about how to use it properly—could you help me with that?

Actually, I don’t feel as anxious anymore. I’ve finally realized that retirement isn't the end; it’s just a chance for me to do meaningful things in a different way. What you said really changed my perspective.

Let’s brainstorm together tomorrow to see if there are other ways I can be there for him. You always have better ideas than I do.

December 18, 2024, 8:30 PM

Today, I recorded my tenth story.

This afternoon at home, while Xiuying was busy in the kitchen, I sat on the balcony with my phone and opened the recording app just as my daughter taught me. I wasn't nervous this time; my fingers were steady, and I didn't press any wrong buttons. I talked about the time I was promoted to Quality Department Director back in 1998, and why I always insisted on those "harsh" inspection standards.

The recording lasted twenty-three minutes.

Toward the end, my voice choked up a bit. I didn't expect that by speaking these things out loud, I would finally understand what I’ve been holding onto for the past thirty-odd years. It wasn't for any honors; it was just... I felt that if a job is worth doing, it’s worth doing right. Product quality is the lifeblood of an enterprise, and it’s my lifeblood, too. Looking back now, this conviction has stayed with me for so long—from being a frontline worker to a director, from my youth until now.

After I finished, I put down my phone and watched the Chinese roses on the balcony for a while. Two of them are about to bloom.

Xiuying asked how the recording went, and I told her it went smoothly. She smiled and said, "You're more skilled with that phone than I am now." I smiled, too.

Back in early November, I was still thinking about how I was getting old and running out of time; I felt a bit lost. But things are different now. These recordings, these stories—they are for Jianhao, and perhaps for my other grandchildren one day. I want them to know that their grandfather wasn't just some retired old man, but someone who took his work seriously for an entire lifetime. Perhaps that sense of earnestness will be of some use in their own lives.

So, I am not wasting my time. I am doing something very important.

I’ll continue tomorrow. There are still many stories left to tell.

Sunday, May 21, 2023

This afternoon, while watering the plants on the balcony, something suddenly came back to me.

During breakfast this morning, I took the initiative to bring up the new system with Xiuying. To be honest, I hadn’t dared to really talk to her about it these past few days, fearing she’d start worrying too. But for some reason today, it just came out—I told her the inspection department is switching to a new automated system, that I’m learning it, and though it’s a bit of a struggle, I should be able to get the hang of it.

After listening, Xiuying put down her chopsticks and patted me on the shoulder. She said, "You’ve always been capable. You went from an operator to a director; what hardship haven't you faced?" When she said that, I felt a sudden wave of relief.

Lately, my head has been too full. The new system, retirement, her blood pressure, Xiao Li’s business, my grandson in Shanghai... it feels like everything is spinning out of my control. Sitting alone in the office, frowning at those programs, I often feel like I’m becoming foolish. The young people figure it out with a few clicks, while I have to mull it over repeatedly and sometimes even ask them for help. It’s not a great feeling.

But Xiuying’s words gave me something. It’s not that the problem is solved, but... it’s the feeling of being seen. She didn’t offer empty platitudes like "don't worry"; she just patted me and said I’ve always been capable. It was as if she was reminding me that thirty-plus years of experience won't just vanish because of one new system.

While watering the flowers this afternoon, I found myself humming that old song, "The Story of Time." I haven't hummed it in a long while. As I watered the roses, I thought that maybe I don't have to carry everything on my own shoulders. Xiuying is by my side, and my son and daughter still look out for me. Maybe I don't need to keep proving myself in every single thing.

I still have to learn the system. Tomorrow, I’ll go back and keep asking that young lad how to operate it. But today feels a bit different—it’s not about forcing myself to do it anymore, but... well, it’s like someone believes I can learn it.

That feeling is actually quite grounding.

# Monday, September 16, 2024 – Sunny

6:35 AM, the usual spot. Xiuying’s blood pressure is 145/92 again.

As I noted the numbers down in my notebook, she was still half-awake. I didn't say anything right away, just like back at the factory when I’d see a test report with data exceeding the limit—record first, analyze second, and only then come up with a solution. It’s a habit I can’t change, and don't really want to.

She noticed my silence. She asked if it was high again. I said yes, but told her not to worry, that maybe it was just the salted fish from last night’s dinner. In reality, I was wondering if I should move our walks from the afternoon to the morning. That way, I could control one more variable. Or maybe have her drink less tea.

It’s a bit funny, actually—all those years at the factory, I learned the principles of quality management so thoroughly that they’re practically hardwired into me. Now that I’m retired, I have nowhere else to apply them, so I’ve ended up using them all on her. Every morning at this time, I measure her blood pressure, take notes, and think of countermeasures, as if I’m working on a project that will never be finished. But this time, I don't find it tedious.

Xiuying told me not to be so tense, that the doctor said these numbers are within a controllable range. She’s right. I know she’s right. But I still feel the need to do something. Maybe that’s just who I am—someone who can’t sit idle, someone who only feels at peace when everything is straightened out.

I poured her a glass of warm water, no sugar. As she drank it, she looked at me with something in her eyes I can’t quite put into words. Gratitude? Or maybe she feels bad seeing me fuss like this? I’m not sure.

I’m heading to the factory later; the new director has been asking me more questions about inspection standards. I’m thinking maybe I could continue doing some consulting work. It would supplement my pension and make me feel useful again. When Xiuying doesn't need me worrying over her too much, it’s a good arrangement.

But for now, I’m just sitting here by her side, waiting for her to finish breakfast.

Outside the window, the day has dawned.

Pruned the balcony roses this morning and noticed the new buds are already starting to peek out. They’ve grown faster this winter than in previous years, probably thanks to the decent weather we’ve been having lately.

Took a few photos. Seeing these little buds slowly grow makes me feel a sense of peace. Some things are just like this—there’s no need to rush. As long as you water and fertilize them on schedule, they’ll follow their own rhythm and grow.

Logged today’s watering date. I’ve been growing these roses for over a decade now, and every single pot has its own personality.

October 16, 2024  Sunny

It was another busy day, but the kind of busy that feels fulfilling.

The office called this morning—it was the new director. He said there were issues with the inspection standards again and asked if I could take a look. I walked him through my thought process over the phone, and he listened intently. When I hung up, I felt a bit surprised at myself—realizing that my work is still being used and that people still need me. It’s a good feeling; not the pressure of being needed, but more like... a sense that what I’ve done still has value.

Xiuying was in good spirits today. She was busy in the kitchen this morning, and seeing the color in her face, I figured her blood pressure must be stable. I accompanied her to the hospital for a check-up in the afternoon. The doctor said all her indicators look good as long as she keeps taking her medicine on time. I copied the doctor’s orders onto the calendar again, marking them in red ink. She laughed and said I’m more nervous than she is; I told her that’s just my job.

Had a video call with Jianhao tonight. The kid has been learning chess openings lately, so I taught him the "Knight's Jump" maneuver. He sat there on the other side of the screen with his brow furrowed in concentration, looking so serious. I took a photo with my phone—it’s a bit blurry, but I can see his focus. During the call, he asked me how to record a video on the phone. I didn't know how at first, but with Xiuying guiding me from the side, the three of us fumbled around for a while and finally got it to work. He was thrilled, saying he wanted to record the stories I tell him.

To be honest, I’m still quite clumsy with this phone. But for the sake of video calling Jianhao and seeing his face, the trouble is worth it. I’ve even started using it to take photos of the Chinese roses—though the pictures aren't as clear as what the eye sees, they sometimes capture details that I can cross-reference with my paper notebooks. I’ve found that some things can indeed be recorded more clearly this way.

I’m sitting on the balcony now. The moonlight is beautiful. The pot of red roses looks particularly soft under the moon, and new buds have sprouted. I was thinking about how all these years I seemed to be waiting for something—waiting to retire, waiting to have time, waiting for life to get back to "normal." But now I understand: life itself is right here. Accompanying Xiuying, teaching Jianhao chess, watching the flowers bloom and fade, taking a consultation call... this is life.

Retirement isn't so bad after all.

February 18, 2023 – Past 10:00 PM

Another night of poor sleep. I was still tossing and turning at 2:00 AM, my mind stuck on those same thoughts.

This afternoon, Xiao Wang and a few other young colleagues came to my office to discuss the new system. They talked about how automated inspections would boost efficiency and reduce human error. As I listened, I couldn't help but chime in—machines can't inspect experience. Only a seasoned inspector can spot those subtle, minute issues.

The moment the words left my mouth, I felt it—my voice sounded off. It was as if I were defending something. They just nodded politely and went right back to explaining their system.

I pointed to the quality achievement charts on the wall. Thirty-seven years. These numbers speak for themselves. Hearing myself say that, it felt like I was trying to prove something. But prove what, exactly?

After they left, I sat in my office alone and pulled out the old inspection records. Data from twenty years ago, ten years ago. Looking at those figures, I couldn't quite describe the feeling. This is what we built, bit by bit. But now?

I keep wondering: if the system really does take over, what are my thirty-seven years worth?

It’s a hard question to answer. Xiuying asked me what was wrong today; she said I was spacing out during dinner. I couldn't explain it to her. Her blood pressure has been a bit high lately, and I don't want to worry her.

I took out my notebook and started listing "what if" scenarios. What if the department is restructured? What if I’m forced into early retirement? Will the pension be enough? Both kids are busy with their own lives, and Li Ming’s business isn't exactly stable...

But I stopped halfway through. There’s no end to thinking like this.

Only two years left until retirement. I never gave it much thought before. Now, suddenly, it feels like time is accelerating.

The group photo from ten years ago is still hanging on the office wall. Everyone from the department was there then. Some have moved on since. I look at myself in that photo—I was younger, of course, but I didn't seem nearly as tired as I am now.

I don't know if it's physical exhaustion or just being weary at heart.

Tomorrow is another day. And the day after, I have to take Xiuying to the hospital for her follow-up.

Forget it. No more thinking. Time to sleep.

Xiuying, I wanted to talk to you about something.

I was playing chess with Master Wang and the others this afternoon, and he said something that’s been on my mind ever since. He said, "Your wife is a human being, not a machine." I didn't say anything back, but it left me feeling a heavy weight in my chest.

I’ve been thinking about all those spreadsheets, menus, and logs I’ve set up for you lately... Do you feel like I’m managing you like some kind of project? Like the way I used to handle quality inspections back at the factory?

That’s not what I intended. I just want you to be well; I want to get your blood pressure under control. But now I’m starting to worry—worry that I’m going about this the wrong way. I’m afraid you feel like I’m suffocating you rather than taking care of you.

Please, tell me the truth—what has been going through your mind these past few days? I need to hear how you really feel.

Sunday, September 29, 2024 – Sunny

I received a call from the new director today, which was a bit unexpected. He mentioned that the new system still has some loopholes in quality determination and asked if I could find some time to do some consulting for them. To be honest, I had mixed feelings when I first heard it. My retirement is already set in stone; do I really want to go back to all that?

But he got into the specifics—it was about the defect identification mechanism. As I listened, my mind just started racing automatically. I shared my thoughts with him: the new system is like a young employee—highly capable and quick to react, but lacking in experience. It needs an experienced mentor to guide it, to tell it when to be more cautious and what the logic behind the data really is. He listened very intently.

After hanging up, I told Xiuying about it. She asked me what I thought. I told her I was still considering it, as I don’t want to disrupt our current life too much—playing chess, gardening, and accompanying her to the hospital are all very important. She smiled after hearing this and said, "Of course you’re useful. We’ve always known that," then gently patted my hand.

In that moment, I couldn't quite describe how I felt. It wasn't the kind of satisfaction you get from work, but rather... her words carried so much weight. It was as if she was saying that my experience has value, and I have value as a person, regardless of whether I’m still in that position.

I’m thinking maybe I could give it a try. I wouldn't have to go back and sit in an office; I’d just offer some advice every now and then. As for the compensation... Xiuying’s follow-up exams, spending time with the grandkids and buying them gifts—all of these require money. Besides, I still have the energy, and the pressure wouldn't be too high.

I watered the Chinese roses this evening and noted down today’s weather and the state of the flowers. The buds have grown a bit larger.

I’m thinking of giving Li Ming a call to see how his business has been lately.

November 3, 2023, Evening

I’m sitting at home now, still thinking about what was said during today’s meeting.

In the conference room this morning, I used data to present the results of the new process—a 99.2% qualification rate and a 23% reduction in inspection time. As I spoke those numbers, I realized I wasn’t just reading a report; I was talking about something I helped create. It felt different.

The leadership said they want to roll this out to other departments and asked me to take the lead. In that moment, my eyes lit up, but it’s hard to describe exactly how I felt. It wasn't just happiness, nor was it entirely a sense of satisfaction. It was more like... a sudden moment of clarity.

I’ve been at this factory for 37 years. From operator to team leader, to section chief, and now to where I am today. All these years, I’ve been doing one thing—guaranteeing quality. That conviction has never changed. But lately, it feels like I’ve been doing something else: learning how to do that same thing in a new way. At first, I struggled to adapt; I was even a bit resistant. The systems, the data, the smart inspections... it all felt so foreign.

But now I understand. This isn't a rejection of what I did before. On the contrary, it’s a way to carry it forward in a new form. My 37 years of experience haven't been made obsolete by new technology; they’ve just been given new meaning. I can use that experience to guide how we use these tools more effectively.

While I was explaining the details to my subordinates this afternoon, I saw how intently they were listening. I realized my role has changed a bit—I’m not just the head of the inspection department anymore; I’m more like... how should I put it... a bridge. Connecting old experience with new technology.

Xiuying asked me how my day was, and I told her it was pretty good. What I actually wanted to say was that I feel like I’ve found a new reason to make these last two years before retirement about more than just waiting. I’m doing something truly meaningful.

I suppose this is what our generation ought to be doing. Not being phased out by a new era, but finding our place within it.

Back at it tomorrow. There are still a lot of details to refine.

# June 7, 2024, Evening

I’ve been sitting on the sofa for a while now. The TV is on, but I’m not really watching.

I called Xiao Ling this afternoon. She said work has been busy lately and Jianhao has a parent-teacher meeting coming up. As I listened, I found myself bringing up your mother’s high blood pressure. I told her I’ve been taking her to the hospital for her monthly check-ups and that the doctor said it’s under control. I’m not sure why I felt the need to mention that—maybe I just wanted her to know I’m taking good care of things at home. She said, "Dad, you’re retired now, you should just get some rest." I replied, "If you have time, come back for a visit. I’d like to see you all." When I said those words, even I felt a bit strange—my voice was steady, yet it sounded like I was pleading for something.

After hanging up, I’ve just been sitting here, looking at that group photo from the office. It was taken ten years ago; everyone from the department is in it. I’m sitting in the middle. My hair wasn't quite so white back then.

I still haven't decided which wall to hang this photo on. The living room? The bedroom? Or should I just leave it in the cardboard box? It’s the same with the quality performance charts—all those figures, those achievements... they’re all in the past tense now. I can recite the passing rate for every single year, but who do those numbers matter to anymore?

I still woke up at 6:30 this morning. No one can turn off the body’s internal alarm clock. It was only after I woke up that I remembered—there’s no office to go to. I watered the plants, watched the news for ten minutes, and ate breakfast. Everything felt the same as usual, yet entirely different. It felt like I was merely imitating my own life.

Your mother asked me, "Why are you sitting there all by yourself?" I said, "It’s nothing, just thinking." She didn't push further. She can probably tell, but she also knows I need to be alone for a while. After forty-one years, she knows me well.

Rationally, I know this is a good thing. I can spend time with her, see my grandson more often, and finally stop being so tense. But right now, it just feels... empty. For thirty-seven years, I was the Head of the Inspection Department. The one who set the standards, the one who solved the problems, the one people trusted. Now, that identity has simply ended. The gifts from colleagues, the applause at the farewell party—it was all telling me that this is over.

I try to tell myself that this is just a change of roles. Moving from the present continuous to the past tense. But I haven't figured out who I am supposed to be next.

Should I still wake up at 6:30 tomorrow?

# School App Instructions

**Date: October 18, 2024 (Sunny)**

Today, Xiuying showed me once again how to open that school app my daughter downloaded for me. I think I’ve finally got it this time, so I’m writing the steps down quickly before I forget and have to ask again.

**Steps:**

1. Tap the row of icons at the bottom of the phone screen and find "Apps" (the square with the nine dots).
2. Swipe up on the screen to find the "School App" icon (the blue one with a schoolbag on it).
3. Tap to open it.
4. Enter the username and password (use the account my daughter set up: Jianhao’s father’s name).
5. Tap on "Class Information."
6. From there, I can see Jianhao’s class announcements and homework.

**Key Points:**
- Remember the password; don't type it in wrong.
- If it won't let me in, close the app and try opening it again.
- I can check the class updates once a week to see how Jianhao is doing at school.

**Why this is useful:**
This way, I’ll know what’s going on with Jianhao at school, and we’ll have more to talk about during our video calls. Xiuying said I can also see his grades and teacher’s comments on the app, so I should pay more attention to those.

Next time I see Jianhao, I can ask him about things I saw on the app so he knows his grandpa is keeping up with him.
