October 16, 2024. Sunny.

Xiuying said she felt tired again today.

After lunch, she sat on the sofa, leaning back deeply into the cushions with her eyes half-closed. I asked if her blood pressure was high again; she shook her head and said she was just tired, that it was nothing. But I could tell—her complexion looked a bit paler than it did during last week’s check-up.

I didn’t say much. I just went to the kitchen to pour her some warm water. A couple of days ago, I made a point of asking the doctor, who said she needs to drink more warm water to help with her blood pressure. I tested the temperature three times before bringing it over to her. As she drank, I sat beside her, just watching her.

Later, I told her we needed to make some adjustments. I made a list: our daily meals need to be lighter, with less salt—I’ll learn how to make them taste good; we need to go for a walk every afternoon, and I’ll go downstairs with her; we need to go to bed earlier and stop watching TV until eleven. As she listened, her eyes began to moisten.

I didn't say anything more. I just reached out and gave the back of her hand a gentle squeeze. After forty-one years, sometimes words aren't necessary.

"You're really taking care of me, aren't you?" she said.

I just smiled. "That's how it's supposed to be," I replied.

In the afternoon, I went out to the balcony to check on the roses. They’ve bloomed exceptionally well this year, especially the red ones—bud after bud. I sat on my small stool, took out my notebook, and recorded today’s watering time and the condition of the flowers. When Xiuying walked over, I pointed them out to her, telling her how this plant grew bit by bit, from a tiny spring sprout to a bush full of buds.

She stood there and watched them for a long time.

I think that once I truly retire, I’ll have more time to take care of her, tend to these flowers, and maybe even teach our grandson how to appreciate them. While I’m still working, every day feels particularly precious.

Tomorrow, I need to go to the market and ask if there are any recipes that are light yet flavorful. I also need to go to the library to see if there are any books on diets for hypertension. These things—I have to do them right, one by one.

Just like inspecting products: details are everything.

July 13, 2023, Morning

I woke up twice again last night.

The first time was just after 2:00 AM. I seemed to be arguing with Ming in my dream about his supply chain business. I told him to play it safe and focus on the offline side first, but he said I didn't understand today’s market. I wanted to talk back in the dream, but the words wouldn't come out. Once I woke up, I couldn't get back to sleep.

The second time was around 4:00 AM. The same thoughts were still circling in my head. I heard the factory is planning a departmental restructuring; will my position be... forget it, there’s no use thinking about it.

I still got up on time at 6:30. I watched the news for ten minutes and had breakfast. Xiuying is still asleep.

Something went wrong while I was watering the plants today. I double-watered that pot of Chinese roses. Even though my notebook clearly said I watered them yesterday, I did it again anyway. My mind was completely elsewhere when I picked up the watering can; by the time I realized what I was doing, the water had already soaked in. This sort of thing has never happened before.

I opened my notebook to record it, but after writing the date, I stopped. Usually, I’d note down the amount of water and the condition of the flowers, but today... I just wasn't in the mood. I felt a flicker of frustration with myself as I closed the book.

Tomorrow I have to take Xiuying to the hospital for her blood pressure follow-up. The readings this month have been okay; the doctor said to keep taking the medication and come in for regular checks. I’ve copied all the doctor’s orders onto the calendar. I need to stay on top of this.

Xiaoling called yesterday and said Jianhao has started learning programming. A ten-year-old is already learning that, while I still have to have my daughter teach me how to use my phone. Sometimes I wonder—does all the knowledge I’ve gathered over thirty years at the factory mean anything to them?

Li Ming’s business has been under a lot of pressure lately, too. Last time we talked, he said he wanted to start an online store. I gave him some advice, but to be honest... I don't really understand that world. Everything I come up with is the "old way." After we finished talking, I wasn't even sure if my suggestions were actually helpful.

That group photo from ten years ago is still pinned to the office wall. Everyone in the department was there back then; we were all so young. And now? Some left long ago, and the new kids use smart systems for inspections—they’re much faster than us. I’m still using the old methods. The results are still correct, but...

Whatever. It’s light out; time for work.

Xiuying, listen to me. This morning at the breakfast shop, the lady running the place suddenly said to me, "Director Li, you seem much more energetic than before." I was stunned for a moment, not knowing how to respond. Then I told her, "Well, I finally have the time to pay attention to these things now."

When I said that, I was actually a bit surprised myself. It seems I really have been changing slowly over the past two years; I just hadn't quite realized it. All those years at the factory, I spent every day thinking about inspection standards and quality metrics. My head was so full that there was simply no room for anything else. It’s different now. While I’m having my porridge in the morning, I can actually look at the shop's decor, listen to the people chatting around me, and really feel these little things.

I think it’s a wonderful feeling. It’s not that work wasn't important, but... how should I put it? I finally have the chance to see what life itself looks like. It’s like the flowers I keep on the balcony. I used to grow them back then too, but it was always a hurried task to check off a list. Now, I can truly observe them—watching how the buds slowly grow bit by bit. The feeling is completely different.

Don't you think we should also give some thought to what we want to do after retirement? We don't necessarily have to keep ourselves busy all the time. Sometimes, just slowing down like this and keeping each other company is enough. It’s quite nice.

# Monday, November 15

**Health Management Adjustments**

10:00 AM - Accompany Xiuying to the hospital for her follow-up

---

**Notes:**

Switching to once a month should be enough. I used to keep track of things too closely—asking about her blood pressure every single day actually made her more anxious. The doctor said that managing hypertension isn't just about me hovering over her; she needs to be able to relax, too.

After this visit, we’ll just follow the doctor’s advice: take the medication as prescribed and watch the diet. I’ll stop recording all those numbers in the calendar every day.

Xiuying and I agreed that she’ll just let me know directly if she’s feeling unwell, rather than me asking her all the time.

Maybe this way will actually be better.

# Li Jianguo’s Private Records

**Date: November 3, 2023, Afternoon**
**Format: Phone Memos + Paper Notebook**

---

I sat in my office for a while after the meeting ended today.

The higher-ups mentioned they want to roll out that new inspection process and asked if I could take the lead on it. I didn’t agree right away; I asked for a few details first—the timeline, which departments would be involved, and how much support I’d have. He said it’s a major project and that he really wants me at the helm.

I thought it over and said yes.

To be honest, six months ago, I was resistant to this system. I thought it was too complicated, and I didn't see why I should change the methods I’ve used for over thirty years. But over the past half-year, I’ve slowly realized that this system actually makes sense. Just the other day, I took a photo of my Chinese roses with my phone and set a date reminder in my memos—it was much faster than writing it down by hand.

After the meeting, I walked my subordinates through a few technical details, and they listened very intently. It suddenly occurred to me that my thirty-seven years of experience aren't being rendered obsolete by this new system; rather, they’ve found a new purpose. I can help the younger generation understand *why* we do things this way, instead of just teaching them *how* to do them.

That is what our generation should really be doing.

When I got home this evening and was watering the plants on the balcony, I thought of Xiao Ming. His building materials store has been under a lot of pressure these last two years. Competition in the industry is fierce, and he sounds so exhausted on the phone sometimes. I’ve been trying to think of ways to help him, but my experience is in the factory, which is quite different.

But today it hit me—maybe I should tell him that change isn't a bad thing; the key is to find your new value within that change. It took me six months to wrap my head around this; perhaps he needs time too.

I think I’ll bring this up when I play chess with the guys next Wednesday. Some of them have gone through industry shifts as well; they might have some insights.

Tomorrow, I need to prepare the first draft of the rollout plan. I’ve decided to use a combination of my phone’s notepad and my computer to see how efficient it is. It feels a bit new, but I think I can manage.

Xiuying’s blood pressure was good today. The doctor said to just keep doing what we’re doing. I copied the medical advice out again and taped it to the fridge.

---

Must remember to give Xiao Ming a call—not right now, though. This weekend. I'll let him talk about the business; I have a few thoughts I’d like to share with him.

# Memo

**Date: July 28, 2024, Afternoon**

Xiuying, I’ve been thinking about this all morning.

I had a bad attitude yesterday. When you ate that piece of braised pork, my expression changed. Even though I didn't say anything, I know you noticed. I’ve been asking myself why I reacted that way.

I realized I’ve been treating your body the same way I used to handle things at the factory—like inspecting a product. Everything has to follow a standard, be recorded, and have zero deviation. But Xiuying, you aren’t a product. You are my wife; you are a living, breathing person. You have your own cravings, things you want to eat, and your own feelings.

Last night, I wrote a sentence in my notebook and then crossed it out. It took me a long time to understand: taking care of someone isn't the same as managing a product.

The doctor said you need to watch your diet, and that’s right. But how we do that should be something we figure out together. It shouldn't be me making the rules and watching you follow them. I’ve been a bit too strict. All these years we’ve spent together, you’ve never complained. Now that it’s my turn to take care of you, I’ve ended up...

I want us to look at some recipes together this weekend. Whatever you enjoy eating, we’ll find a way to make it a bit healthier. It’s not about me telling you what you can’t have; it’s about us figuring out how to eat in a way that makes you happy and is good for your health.

It also occurred to me that you used to say I was too much of a perfectionist. Back when I was at the factory, I insisted on everything being flawless. I see now that for some things, perfection isn't what matters most. What matters is that the two of us are okay, together.

When your follow-up appointment comes next month, we’ll go together. Not me "accompanying" you, but us going as a team. If there’s anything you want to ask the doctor, we’ll talk it over beforehand.

I love you, Xiuying. But I also need to learn that I can’t control everything. Sometimes, letting go is a form of love, too.

— Jianguo

*(He might never say these words out loud. But this weekend, he will take her hand and suggest they go to the market together. He will ask her what she wants to eat. He will listen to her. This is his way.)*

Friday Afternoon, July 21, 2023

I’ve been sitting here all afternoon. While Xiuying is busy in the kitchen, I’m just leaning back on the sofa. I’m not thinking about anything in particular, yet my mind won't stop racing.

Today is Wednesday, but I didn't go to play chess. I told Lao Zhang and the others that I wasn't feeling well lately, which wasn't entirely a lie. It’s just that… I didn't feel like moving. It’s been eight years, and I’ve never missed a Wednesday; this is the first time. Lao Zhang and the guys must be waiting for me in the activity room; they’ve probably already started by now. I feel a bit bad for letting them down, but I just didn't have the energy to go.

The Chinese roses on the balcony have wilted again. I should have watered them the day before yesterday. I saw them, and I knew they needed it, but I just didn't do it. The petals are drooping, and the leaves are losing their green. I used to care so much about this—I’d remember every watering date and watch the buds slowly grow. Now… I watch them wither, and I don’t feel much of anything. This isn't like me.

I’m wondering if I’ve truly grown old. It’s not about age, but rather… it feels like I’m slowly losing something. The things I’ve held onto for so many years don’t seem that important anymore.

When Li Ming called last time, he said there were new competitors in the building materials business and asked for my thoughts. I hemmed and hawed for a while but didn't offer any useful advice in the end. I could sense his disappointment over the phone, even though he didn't say it. I wonder if my advice is truly outdated. Does what I learned over thirty years in the factory even matter in today’s business? I’m not even sure myself.

Quality, detail, persistence—the things I’ve always believed in don’t seem to be enough anymore. Young people use software, data, and all this internet stuff that I barely know how to operate. Is my experience… like those old inspection methods, simply obsolete?

I’m a little afraid of retirement. It’s not that I’m afraid of having nothing to do, but rather that I’ll be even more useless then. At least now I still have a job, a department, and people who need my decisions. What about two years from now? If I can’t even stick to a chess game I’ve played for eight years, or care for the flowers I’ve tended for so long, how will I get through my retirement? What will I use to fill all that time?

I flipped through last year’s diary. Back then, I was still recording the growth of the flowers and planning ways to help Li Ming with his business. And now? Now, I can’t seem to wrap my head around anything.

Xiuying is calling me for dinner. I need to get up.

May 8, 2023, 2:30 AM

I can't sleep.

That system interface is spinning in my head again. And a certain clip from yesterday’s evening news—I just can't shake it. Xiuying was snoring, so I got up; I didn't want to wake her.

When I walked out to the balcony, I saw the roses had withered.

That pot of red roses—I only just recorded the watering date the day before yesterday. Now the leaves are all drooping. I touched the soil; it was bone dry. I actually forgot. How could I even forget something like this?

I stood there for a long time, just staring at the flowers.

I thought about the system operation steps I’d jotted down in my notebook. I took it out and read through them again, but some parts have already become a blur. It’s only been two days—how can I not remember? Those young people get it on the first try, but I have to look at it over and over, and I still can't get it to stick.

I asked myself—is there anything I can still do right?

I can’t learn the system. I can’t take care of the flowers. I can’t help with my son’s business; I just call once a week to check in, but it doesn’t really change anything. My grandson is in Shanghai, and I can’t be there for him. If I can’t even look after these flowers, what use am I?

There’s a group photo in my office from ten years ago, a gift from my subordinates. Back then, I still looked the part. And now? What have I become? Just an old man who can’t learn a new system. I’m two years away from retirement—is the company just waiting for me to clear out on my own?

Xiuying said she’s been feeling a bit dizzy again lately. I take her to the hospital every month, and the doctor says her blood pressure is under control, but I’m still worried. How am I supposed to protect her? I can’t even protect myself.

The roses are still withered. I didn’t water them right away. I just stood there, looking at them, as if I were looking at myself.

I have to go to the office tomorrow. That system will be waiting for me. I’ll have to pretend everything is fine and maintain my image as the Director in front of my staff. But I know the way they look at me has changed.

I’m tired.

It’s not a physical tiredness. It’s something else.

# Diary

**Wednesday, July 10, 2024  Sunny**

Xiuying laughed at me again.

I spent the entire afternoon yesterday drafting this week’s menu using engineering drawing methods. I used the same set of rulers and pencils I used back at the factory for technical blueprints, marking the sodium and potassium content for every dish. I even used a red pen to box the key data points that need strict monitoring. Finally, I attached a sticky note: "Updated July 10, 2024," with a note below: "Further optimization next week."

When she saw it, her eyes widened, and then she started laughing. "Are you setting national food standards for me?" she asked, sitting on the sofa, shaking her head as she looked at the menu.

I answered her quite earnestly at the time: "Standards are there to ensure quality. Your health is the most important quality indicator in this house."

The moment the words left my mouth, I stunned myself for a second.

But it’s the truth. Back in my department, I was ruthless about inspection standards; now, I am just as serious about her blood pressure logs. It’s not that I have nothing better to do now that I’m retired... it’s just that I’ve suddenly realized this is where my seriousness belongs most.

Xiuying didn't say anything else. She just looked at me with that gentle gaze—a mix of teasing and acceptance. She’s grown used to me being like this. Over the years, that’s how she’s always looked at me; even when my over-seriousness bordered on the ridiculous, she never truly pushed back.

Sitting beside her and looking at that menu, I suddenly felt... no regrets.

It’s not that my work wasn't important. I was serious about those thirty-seven years, and I am proud of them. But this feeling now—the feeling of being needed and understood by my wife—seems deeper. Those standards, those records, that "continuous improvement"—they haven't vanished; they’ve just found a new place to be useful.

Next week’s menu is already forming in my head. I also want to head to the market to ask if there are better ingredient pairings for someone with hypertension.

Tomorrow is Wednesday, time for chess with my old colleagues. But today, I want to sit with her and check her blood pressure first.

October 15, 2022 | Sunny | Saturday

I accompanied Xiuying to the hospital for her follow-up appointment today.

I got up at 6:30 AM, watched the news for ten minutes, and headed out right after breakfast. Registration, queuing, blood pressure checks... everything was the same as usual. But the doctor’s words left me feeling a bit uneasy.

Her blood pressure fluctuations have increased. Her systolic pressure hit 150.

When I heard that, my pen stopped mid-sentence. I asked the doctor if there was something wrong with the equipment—I know how that sounds, but I just wanted to be sure. The doctor said the equipment was fine and that we needed to be more vigilant. Xiuying chimed in, saying it was no big deal and likely just due to the recent change in weather, promising she’d be more careful. She sounded casual, but I could tell she was actually a bit worried too.

The doctor adjusted her dosage and ordered some new tests. I noted down every instruction in my notebook—medication times, dietary restrictions, the date of the next check-up. My handwriting was neater than usual because I know full well that these details have become vital now.

I didn't say much on the drive home. Xiuying asked if I was tired. I just said, "We need to be more careful," and then lapsed into silence. My fingers kept tapping on the steering wheel without me even realizing it.

Truth be told, I was thinking... I used to believe her hypertension was manageable; as long as she took her meds on time and watched her diet, everything would be fine. It was just like managing the Quality Inspection Department—set the standards, enforce them strictly, and the results are predictable. But now... what do increased fluctuations mean? They mean things are starting to slip out of my control.

I’m only two years away from retirement. If Xiuying’s health fails before then, what will happen? What can I do? Is our financial situation enough? What preparations do I need to make?

These questions circled in my head all afternoon.

At dinner tonight, I made a decision—starting tomorrow, I’m going to check her blood pressure every single day. Not just during monthly check-ups, but daily. I’ll log it and see if there’s a pattern. I’ll be stricter about her salt intake. I will... be prepared.

Xiuying asked what was on my mind. I told her it was nothing, just thinking about how to keep her blood pressure more stable. She gave a little smile and said I always overcomplicate things.

Maybe so. But I’d rather overthink things than be caught off guard by a sudden turn of events.

Starting tomorrow, I’m buying a blood pressure monitor to keep at home.

Xiuying, I’m out on the balcony watering the flowers. The roses are looking quite good today; I’ve given them all a thorough soaking.

You know, as I was sitting there, a thought suddenly struck me. These flowers—they have to adapt to the changing seasons, from winter to spring, from the dry spells to the rainy season. They don’t have a choice, yet they survive. I’ve never really thought about it that way before.

I haven't been sleeping well these past few days, and I know you’ve noticed. That meeting... I’ve been dwelling on it, overthinking everything. I didn’t even go to play chess this afternoon—the first time I’ve ever missed a game. I know you’re worried about me. To be honest, I’ve been worried about myself, too.

But just now, while I was watering the plants, I wrote something down in my notebook: "The times are changing, but I can still learn." I underlined it with a pencil, just like I used to do when confirming inspection standards back at the factory. Xiuying, I suddenly realized that I’m not just passively waiting for something bad to happen. I can still do something.

In these thirty-seven years, I’ve seen the factory transition from a planned economy to a joint-stock system. I’ve seen so many people leave, but I stayed—because I knew how to learn. It’s the same way now. Maybe I’ll be slow to pick up the new system, maybe there are things I won’t understand at first, but I can learn.

I feel a sense of peace that I haven't felt in a long time. Not the kind of peace that comes from running away, but... the kind that comes from accepting reality.

I’m going to work tomorrow; I’m going to attend that meeting. Whatever the outcome, I’m ready. Don’t worry, I’m okay.

We’ve weathered so much together; this time will be no different.

I’ll serve you dinner in a bit. I’m actually starting to feel a little hungry.

I just watched the video Jianhao sent me again. "Grandpa, how are your flowers doing?" he asked, his voice coming through crystal clear. Sitting alone on the balcony, I replayed it several times, unable to stop myself from smiling every single time.

It got me thinking for a long while.

Back at the factory, my value was tied to inspection reports, numbers, and standards. I did everything with rigor and perfection. But what about now? Now, my value might just be a story I told Jianhao, or teaching him how to play Chinese chess, or... the flowers I grow.

"Are you only realizing this now?" Xiuying asked me in that gentle tone of hers. I told her yes, I’m only just realizing it. Retirement isn't the end of life; it’s more like... a new beginning. It’s just not the kind of beginning I had imagined.

I thought retirement meant slowing down and that I might feel empty. But that’s not it at all. My days are full now because I know what I need to do—I want to be there for my grandson, record our stories, and pass on what I’ve learned over the years. There are no clear-cut KPIs like there were at the factory, yet it feels somehow more important.

Jianhao said he wants to learn chess. When I heard that, I felt a deep sense of peace. It wasn't just that he wanted to learn the game; it was that he wanted to learn something from *me*. This feeling... it’s different from the way it felt to teach my subordinates inspection standards back in the day. This time, it feels like I’m truly passing something down.

I made another note in my journal of the dates I watered the plants. The Chinese roses are doing exceptionally well this year; they should bloom even more brilliantly next spring. I want to show them to Jianhao when he comes back.

Maybe this is exactly what I’m meant to be doing now.

November 13, 2022, 8:15 PM

Xiuying cried tonight.

It wasn’t because she was in physical pain; it was because of me.

We were sitting in the living room, and I had pulled out the calendar as usual, getting ready to note down her medications and follow-up appointments for tomorrow. Suddenly, she said, "Jianguo, please stop doing this." I didn't catch her meaning at first and asked what she meant. She said, "You’re so tense every single day. It hurts me just to watch you."

It finally hit me. All these years, the things I’ve been doing—accompanying her to the hospital every month, scribbling doctor’s orders on the calendar, asking about her blood pressure every day, recording every bite she eats—I thought I was helping her. In reality, I might have just been adding to her stress.

I sat there for a long time without saying a word.

Later, I told her that I’ve decided to change how I do things. I told her, "I trust the doctors, and I trust you." Even as the words left my mouth, I struggled to believe them myself. For years, I’ve relied on planning and monitoring to give myself peace of mind. In the factory, that system worked—strict inspection standards guaranteed product quality. But Xiuying’s blood pressure... some things just can’t be managed into submission.

Her eyes welled up. We just sat there, neither of us saying anything more.

I feel exhausted right now. It’s not the kind of tired you get from work; it’s a sense of... powerlessness, I suppose. I’ve realized that after thirty-seven years as a Quality Control Manager, I’ve developed a specific way of solving problems, but that way might not apply to real life. Maybe I’ve been loving her the wrong way all along.

The department photo from ten years ago is still hanging on my office wall. Back then, I felt like I had everything under control. Now, I’m not so sure.

I have work tomorrow. But tonight, I’ve decided to put the calendar down. I’ll let Xiuying decide for herself when to go for her check-ups and let her listen to her own body. I need to learn to... let go.

It’s much harder than I imagined.

I understand now that this kind of care has no finish line. It has simply found a new recipient, that’s all.

Xiuying, I’ve been thinking, and there were some things I felt a bit awkward saying out loud today.

You’re right—I still have you, and I still have this home. I know that. It’s just that my head has been a bit of a mess these past couple of days, like I’ve suddenly lost something. But none of this is your fault. I saw everything you did today—looking through the photo albums with me and listening to me repeat those same old stories.

I’m still adjusting, and it might take me a little while. But don't worry, I’ll be okay. After all, there are still so many flowers to water, and I’ve got that chess game with Old Wang and the guys next Wednesday.

Thank you for not rushing me today and for not overdoing the comfort. Just having you there makes me feel at ease.

Xiuying, I’m home.

While we were playing chess today, Old Wang asked me if my position was secure; he said he’d heard the company might be restructuring. I told him I’ve thought it through. Whether the position is "secure" doesn't really matter that much anymore—what matters is what I’m capable of doing. Everything I’ve learned in quality inspection over all these years won't just vanish because of a job title.

I also told them I’m considering staying on as a consultant for the company after I retire. I could pass down the experience I’ve built up over thirty-seven years, help the new director streamline the processes, and mentor the younger inspectors. This way, I can supplement my pension and keep myself busy. Don't you think?

I felt incredibly focused during the game today; I won three rounds in a row, which hasn't happened in a long time. My old colleagues all said I’ve got my groove back. I feel it too—that suffocating feeling from the reforms is finally gone.

Actually, I’m starting to feel more and more that retirement isn't an end, but a new beginning. We can really start planning things out. Your recovery is going well—what did the doctor say about next month’s follow-up? I was thinking that once I officially retire, we can spend more time with our grandsons, especially Jianhao in Shanghai. And for our younger son’s business, I’ll have more time to help him look over things and run some analyses.

Also, I’ve been wondering if I should learn how to use my phone to take better photos. Old Zhang showed me some pictures of flowers he took today; they were crystal clear. I’m thinking I could photograph the progress of the Chinese roses on the balcony, and I could use the reminder function on the phone's memo app too. Even though I still prefer my paper notebooks, combining the two methods seems like a good idea.

Don't worry, I feel much more at peace now. Our days ahead are going to be very full.

My supervisor said something during the meeting that left a deep impression on me: "Director Li is adopting a data-driven management mindset." To be honest, the comment caught me a bit by surprise. Over all these years in quality inspection, I’ve always believed my value lay in having a sharp eye and maintaining strict standards. The new system was a bit uncomfortable at first, but looking back, it has given me a fresh perspective on quality management. I’ve been with this company for 37 years. If I can pass this mindset on to the next generation before I retire—helping them approach quality management more scientifically—perhaps that will be my final contribution.

Xiuying, I wanted to tell you something.

I just finished playing chess with Master Wang and the others, and I’ve been thinking about it on my way home. Master Wang said something that I can’t stop turning over in my mind—he said, "Companionship is the most valuable thing there is."

You know, back when I was in the department, I used to treat everything like a project. The inspection standards had to be set just so, the processes had to be overhauled this way or that—everything had to be orderly. Back then, I thought that was where my value lay.

But now... I suddenly understand. All these years, going with you to the hospital, keeping track of your doctor’s orders, watering the flowers together on the balcony, you leaning on my shoulder while we watch the news at night—this is the real "project." It’s just that this project has no inspection standards or pass rates, yet it’s worth more than anything I ever did at the factory for over thirty years.

Our grandson sent me a video this afternoon. He was watching cartoons on his phone. Even though I still don’t quite know how to operate those gadgets, seeing him smile like that... in that moment, I thought: this is what I should be doing now.

I’m not trying to preach or anything. I just want you to know that I see it clearly now—these days we spend together, this is my most important work.

I’d like to sit with you a while longer tonight.

# Notebook Entry

---

While playing chess with the regulars today, Master Li noticed my flower journal and asked why I keep such meticulous records. I told him that I am "keeping these flowers company," not "managing" them.

He smiled and said that was a beautiful way to put it.

Later, Jianhao called and asked why Grandpa needs to record exactly when the flowers are watered. I told him it’s just like how I am there for him—I need to know what you need and when you need it, and I have to stay attentive to your changes so I can take good care of you. He seemed to understand.

**Core Thoughts:**
- Caring for flowers and caring for people follow the same principle.
- It requires patience, attentiveness, and consistent presence.
- It’s not about "management" for the sake of quick results; it’s about "companionship" from the heart.
- This shift is important—the things I learned in my professional life now carry even greater meaning when applied to my personal life.

**Reminder to Self:**
Hold onto this feeling. Use this same logic when spending time with Xiuying, the children, and my friends. Don't try to "manage" anyone; just be there for them.

Living this way is what brings true peace of mind.

# Diary

June 13, 2024, Evening

I had a phone call with Xiao Ming today. He said business at the building materials store hasn't been great lately—the competition is fierce, and he’s feeling a bit stuck and discouraged. I helped him analyze the market situation and offered a few ideas, like how to differentiate his business, how to retain old customers, and how to develop new channels. We talked for about twenty minutes.

While I was speaking, I felt like I had come back to life. My voice was strong, my mind was sharp; it felt just like being back in the department. Xiao Ming listened intently and asked me several questions. I gave him some advice, and he said it was helpful.

When I hung up, I felt a flicker of joy. I felt like I was still useful, that I could still be of help to my child.

But after sitting for a while, that happiness began to fade.

I started thinking: this is just one time. What about next time? Once his business stabilizes, will he still need me? What help can I really offer him? I’m no longer in the Quality Department, and I’m no expert in the building materials industry. The things I just said—he might forget them in a couple of days.

Now I’m sitting in the living room looking out the window. The sun is about to set, casting an orange glow on the building opposite mine. I find myself wondering—what about tomorrow? And the day after? How does a person keep feeling like they are truly alive?

Xiuying is in the kitchen cooking, and the news is playing on the TV. Everything is the same as usual. And yet, it feels different.

I used to know exactly what I was going to do tomorrow. Now, I don't.

That group photo of the department is still at the office; I never brought it back. I just remembered. I should go pick it up tomorrow.

November 8, 2023, Evening

Xiuying asked me when I learned how to use a smartphone. I just smiled and said, "It really is quite convenient." She seemed a bit surprised, since I’m usually the one complaining about what a hassle phones are.

But this afternoon, I realized it’s not just about learning which buttons to press.

Her blood pressure has been a bit high again, and the doctor said we need to track it more regularly. I used to record it in a calendar notebook, but sometimes I’d miss a day. While I was at the office this afternoon, it occurred to me—why not try that health management app on the phone? I asked Xiao Li how to use it, and he set it all up for me. Now I can set reminders to prompt Xiuying to check her pressure at the same time every day; the data saves automatically, and I can even see the trend charts.

I told Xiuying, "We can't afford any mistakes with your blood pressure management. I want to try a more scientific approach." After hearing that, she agreed.

To be honest, I even surprised myself.

For years, I’ve always felt that new things were too complicated, that the old ways were more reliable. But this time is different. It’s not about following a trend; it’s about actually solving a problem. I’ve used new systems in the Quality Inspection Department for so many years, and now I can use a phone to help Xiuying. It feels like my experience hasn't become obsolete—it’s just taken on a new form.

While watering the flowers tonight, I remembered that I’d taken a few photos of the Chinese roses and saved them on my phone. I used to only record things in my notebook, but now I can include pictures. Sometimes looking at a photo is clearer than reading words.

I haven't completely given up on my notebook. But I’ve found that I’m no longer so resistant to these things.

Perhaps this is what "adapting" really means. It’s not being forced to change, but slowly discovering that some changes actually make sense.

I’ll be retiring in two years. I was thinking that maybe there’s still more I can do. Not just gardening or playing chess, but perhaps using these new things I’ve learned to keep contributing ideas to the company.

That thought makes me feel a sense of ease.

September 26, 2024, Morning

I spent a long time thinking again last night.

My wife said, "Even in retirement, you still can't shake that habit." She said it with a smile, but I could hear it—it wasn't a criticism; it was understanding. As I was organizing my rose notebook, she watched me compare records from previous weeks, analyze the color changes in the leaves, and write down the adjustment plan for this week. She’s right; I’m taking care of these flowers using the exact same methods I used for product inspection.

To be honest, I never thought there was anything wrong with that.

When I left the factory, I thought I would leave all of that behind for good. But I realize now that it wasn't just a way of working; it’s *my* way. The details, the records, the comparisons, the adjustments—these things are in my bones. Taking care of roses and inspecting products are, at their core, the same thing. It’s all about identifying the problem, finding the cause, and figuring out how to improve.

That phone call from the new director actually panicked me a bit at the time. I felt like I had already let go, and being suddenly pulled back felt unsettling. But thinking about it now, maybe there is no such thing as "letting go." I’ve just changed the setting and the medium, but I’m doing the same thing.

What my wife said made me feel much more at peace. She wasn't saying I couldn't break a bad habit; she was saying—this is who I am. I am this kind of person, and retirement won't change that. And the person she accepts is exactly this version of me.

Looking at it this way, there’s nothing to agonize over regarding that consulting gig. It’s not about going back to the past; it’s just continuing to use the tools I’m already using now. I’ll keep it measured, keep my own pace, and not let it disrupt my current life.

The roses are blooming exceptionally well this year. I noted in my book that after that rain in mid-September, the new shoots grew incredibly fast. I should see buds by next week.

When I got up at 6:30 this morning, it was still dark. I watched the news for ten minutes and had a sip of tea. My wife was still asleep, so I just sat on the small balcony, looking at the flower pots.

I feel a bit of anticipation, and a sense of calm.

# November 6, 2022, Early Morning

I just took Xiuying’s blood pressure. 148/92.

I stood there for a long time, holding the monitor, the numbers just staring back at me from the screen. I didn't write it down in the logbook immediately like I usually do. I just... watched it.

Last week’s average was 140/88. Why did it go up again this week?

She didn't sleep well last night. Xiuying said she woke up twice in the middle of the night, and I heard her too. It might be the cooling weather; she always tends to sleep fitfully during the change of seasons. Could that be it? Lack of sleep causing blood pressure fluctuations—it’s possible. I remember hearing that at a health lecture once...

But we’ve controlled her diet so well this week. I prepared yesterday’s meals exactly as the doctor ordered—clear broth, low salt. She was very cooperative with her eating, too.

Maybe it’s because I’ve had too much going on at work lately, and she can sense my stress? Sometimes I’m at home thinking about company matters—the reforms, the restructuring. Even though I don't say anything to her face, she might be able to tell. Could she be getting tense because of me?

I’m wondering if I should go to the hospital and ask the doctor ahead of time, instead of waiting for next month’s follow-up. 148/92... it’s not extremely high, but it’s a noticeable increase from last week. This number is a bit unsettling.

Or... is there something wrong with my recording method? I always measure it right after she gets up, before breakfast. This timing should be correct, right? The doctor didn't specify a particular time to measure.

Never mind, I’ll just keep recording according to plan for now. It might just be a one-time fluctuation; it doesn't necessarily mean anything. I can't start overthinking just because of one data point. I need to stay calm.

But I’ve decided: I’m going to ask Xiuying about her sleep last night—ask her more specifically. Maybe I can find some clues from her.

It’s 6:30 now. I should go prepare breakfast.

*2024.12.3*

Thursday, June 27, 2024, 14:00

Sitting on the balcony, the sunlight filters through the leaves of the Chinese roses, casting dappled shadows that dance across my notebook. Xiuying is inside watching TV; I can hear her light snoring. At this time of day, she always naps for a while.

I’m looking at that red rose. My last entry was three days ago when the bud was just starting to show color. Today, it has fully bloomed, but I can see the outermost petals are already starting to curl slightly. In another two or three days, it will begin to wither.

I was thinking—this is just like the stages of life.

This time last year, I was still agonizing over my retirement. Back then, I felt like that unblossomed bud—tightly wrapped up, not knowing what I would look like once I opened. I was terrified of losing my identity as the "Director of Quality Inspection." I feared that without the job, I wouldn't know who I was anymore.

Now I understand. A flower doesn't lose its value by blooming; in fact, that is the moment it reaches its peak beauty. And withering isn't the end; it’s just preparation for the next cycle.

I wrote this sentence in my notebook: "I am no longer that tightly wrapped bud. I have bloomed."

It sounds a bit silly, but my hand trembled slightly as I wrote it.

I had a video call with Jianhao recently. He used my phone to show me the crafts he made at school. I still pressed the wrong buttons on the screen at first, but this time I didn't get frustrated. I asked him with a smile, "Is Grandpa a clumsy old fossil?" He said, "Grandpa isn't clumsy, Grandpa is learning." That one sentence left me speechless.

Li Ming called again last week to ask about the building materials store. Instead of rushing to give him advice like I used to, I asked him, "What do you think?" He said he was considering pivoting to high-end custom designs. We talked for a long time—not me lecturing him, but the two of us thinking things through together. It felt different.

Xiuying says I’ve become more talkative lately. She’s right. I used to listen in silence, but now I’ve started speaking up—not giving work instructions, but sharing my real thoughts.

I think my new identity is slowly becoming clear. I’m not the "Director of Quality Inspection" anymore; I’m a grandfather, a husband, a friend, someone willing to help others, and someone who observes flowers. All these identities added together make me feel more fulfilled than that job title ever did.

I’m playing chess with my friends this afternoon. I’ve been looking forward to it all week.

The scent of the roses drifts over—faint, but distinct.

I continue my log: watering dates, fertilization status... then I write a sentence at the bottom: "Life needs to be recorded, too. That’s the only way to see clearly which season you are in."

Xiuying is awake and calling me in for lunch. I close my notebook and walk quietly into the house.

September 15, 2024  Sunday  Sunny

I was on the balcony watering the roses when the call from the office came this morning. The new director’s voice sounded a bit frantic over the receiver. He said the new automated inspection system was having trouble identifying a specific type of micro-crack defect, and two batches of products had almost slipped through. My hand froze on the watering can.

In that instant, it felt as if I had been transported straight back to the office. My brain immediately kicked into gear—parameter adjustments, optical recognition angles, the completeness of the sample library... I didn't even realize I had started using professional jargon, my speech quickening. It wasn't until the new director said, "Director Li, could you come and take a look?" that I remembered I’d been retired for three months.

After hanging up, I sat on the balcony for a long time. The watering can was still in my hand; water dripped from the rose leaves. My gaze drifted between several things: the ten-year-old department photo in my office, the calendar entry for my wife’s follow-up appointment next Wednesday, and the date of the last watering I’d noted on the flowerpot.

To be honest, hearing about that problem sparked something I hadn't felt in a while. It wasn't an urge to go back to work, but... how should I put it? The feeling of being needed. Not in a passive way, but the feeling of someone reaching out to me, believing I’m the one who can solve it. Over the past three months, I’ve grown used to this new rhythm—taking Xiuying to the hospital, gardening, playing chess with friends every Wednesday, looking at photos of my grandson. It’s a full life, but sometimes I wonder if those thirty-seven years of experience are just sitting there, gathering dust.

I told him, "Let me hear the details first." I didn't refuse immediately, nor did I agree. This decision makes me a little uneasy.

If I actually take on a consulting role, could that fee supplement my pension? Maybe it would help me take better care of Xiuying; her high blood pressure is under control for now, but I’m always worried. And then there’s my grandson—I want to spend more time with him before I "fully" retire. But the problem is, if I start consulting, will I get tied down by work again? Will I start working overtime again, or have less time to take Xiuying to her appointments?

I don't want to go back to that state. But I also don't want my knowledge and experience to just sit idle.

Perhaps... this could be a new point of balance. Not as the "Director of Quality Inspection," but as a "Consultant." Going back occasionally to solve specific problems without being there every day. Maintaining my current pace of life while still feeling that I have value.

I’ll call the new director back tomorrow. I’ll get the full picture of the situation first, then decide.

Just as I’ve always done—calmly list all the options and analyze them one by one.

November 12, 2022, Evening

Xiuying brought up wanting salted duck eggs again today. I was sitting on the sofa, blood pressure log still in hand, and my gut reaction was to say no—the doctor said low salt. But this time, I didn't refuse right away.

I watched her sitting there, looking a bit disappointed. Suddenly, I remembered what my son said offhandedly on the phone the other day: "Dad, Mom says you've been way too strict lately." And that look my daughter gave me the last time she visited. I realized then that I might have turned "taking care of her" into... what?

I said to Xiuying, "Well, let's have a little bit then, in moderation."

She looked up at me, a glimmer in her eyes. Then she said something that caught me off guard: "Now, *that* is the Li Jianguo I know."

I didn't respond immediately. I just sat there, watching her go to the kitchen and take out a salted duck egg. It was a small one, and she only ate a quarter of it.

Later, while watering the plants on the balcony, I kept thinking about it. I held the watering can, watching the water settle on the rose leaves, and noted down today's date and the amount of water in my notebook—a long-standing habit of mine. But it suddenly occurred to me: are these records a form of care, or a form of control?

Lately, I’ve been obsessed with how to better manage Xiuying’s blood pressure. Lists, plans, data analysis... it felt as if I were meticulous enough, I could eliminate every possible risk. But maybe... maybe some things aren't meant to be fully controlled.

Xiuying looked so content eating that quarter of a salted egg. She looked much more at ease than when I serve her a bowl of plain noodle soup.

I was thinking that I’ll be retiring in two years. I’ll have more time to spend with her then. I can teach my grandson how to play chess, just like I do with my friends. I won't have to spend every day in the office staring at lab reports. Maybe by then, I can learn to... loosen up a bit. To allow for some imperfection.

But I’m still not quite used to it. I’ll keep recording the blood pressure. It’s just that... perhaps the purpose of the record should be companionship, not control.

It’s a new thought. I’ll need some time to get used to it.

# Saturday, September 24, 2022 – Overcast

That issue with the phone came up again today, and I still feel a knot in my chest.

Yesterday afternoon, I discovered a folder in the photo gallery called "Recently Deleted," and inside were several photos I’d taken of my Chinese roses. It gave me quite a scare—how did perfectly good photos end up deleted? I called Xiaoling immediately, my voice trembling with urgency, asking her if they were about to be gone forever. She laughed at me over the phone, saying it’s just like a "recycle bin" and that the photos are still there, so I shouldn't worry.

But one thing she said stuck with me: "They will be permanently deleted after thirty days."

Thirty days. Just hearing that makes me feel uneasy.

Xiaoling spent a long time explaining it, and I understood what she meant, but it still feels like a thorn in my side. When I record things in my notebook, I can see them whenever I flip the page; not a single word ever goes missing. But this phone? It’s like a black box. I have no idea when it’s going to make a decision on its own. How can I trust it with things that matter to me?

Last night, I took a red pen and wrote in my notebook: "Phone Gallery: 30-day deletion limit"—and circled it several times with force. I have to remember this deadline, just in case everything vanishes one day without me even knowing.

I checked the gallery again this morning. The "Recently Deleted" folder is still there. The photos are still there. But I’m still restless.

I know my daughter means well. She says using a phone to track the floral data is more convenient and that I won't have to worry about losing my notebook. But I’ve used notebooks for so many years and never had a single problem. Now, asking me to trust something I don’t understand and can’t grasp... to be honest, it’s hard.

Maybe I’m just getting too old. Times have changed; everyone else is using these new gadgets while I’m here overthinking it. But I just can’t wrap my head around it—why must I put my data in a place where I have absolutely no control?

Anyway, let it be for now. I’ll keep using the notebook and give the phone a try too. A backup plan for both—that’s the only way I’ll feel at peace.

Besides, autumn is here, and the watering cycle for the roses needs adjusting. That, at least, is something I’m sure of.

Xiuying, something interesting happened today while I was playing chess.

I took out my phone in front of my chess buddies because I wanted to show them the video call my daughter taught me how to use. To be honest, I was a bit self-conscious at first, worried I’d mess up the steps. But I followed the instructions she wrote down for me, step by step, and it actually worked.

Old Wang and the others all crowded around to watch and asked how I learned to do it. I told them my daughter taught me—she even set up a shortcut on my phone. Now I can call Jianhao every week; I can see him and hear his voice. You know, as I was saying that, I even surprised myself. Someone like me, who used a Nokia for twenty years, can now use a smartphone to video chat with my grandson far away.

The guys were all happy for me. Some said I was a quick learner, and others said it’s great that I’ll get to see my grandson so often. We even watched Jianhao in Shanghai together. Even though it was just through a phone screen, hearing his laughter made everything feel different.

I was in such a good mood afterward that I won two games in a row, which hasn't happened much lately. I’ve been thinking, maybe retirement isn’t so bad after all. I’m no longer the quality control director at the factory, but I can be a dedicated grandfather—applying that same dedication to learning new things and being there for my grandson.

Don't you think that’s actually quite nice?

Master Wang, thank you for coming by today.

I’ve been home alone thinking for a long time, and I couldn't get what you said out of my head—"We all need you." To be honest, these past few days I’ve been feeling... how should I put it? Just feeling useless. With the situation at work, the issues with my son, and my wife’s health, it felt like nothing I did was right.

I was actually a bit embarrassed when you showed up, but hearing you say that everyone noticed I hadn't been around for chess made me realize... I wasn't just stuck in my own head alone. You guys were still thinking of me.

November 15, 2024, Afternoon

I just stepped out of the office. Walking down the stairwell, I suddenly realized my pace felt much lighter than usual. It’s a strange sensation.

The new director asked me a question today about the quality management system. It wasn't one of those technical "how do we set this parameter" questions, but something deeper—how to find the balance between data systems and human experience. Without overthinking it, I just shared the thoughts I’ve gathered over all these years. I wasn't instructing him; it felt more like I was thinking out loud, organizing the ideas in my head as I spoke.

He listened intently and even took notes.

Back when I was the director, subordinates would come to me with questions, I’d give them the answers, and they’d go execute. Back then, I was the one who "needed to be heard." It’s different now. Now, I’m sharing a philosophy rather than giving orders. I’m only just now truly beginning to feel the difference.

It’s hard to put into words. It’s not exactly the satisfaction of being needed—though there’s a bit of that. It’s more of a... validation? It’s the feeling that what I’ve done for over thirty years has value not because of the title I held, but because the work itself was right. Now I can pass it on, not as a director, but as someone who simply understands how these things work.

My wife asked me the day before yesterday if I’d feel restless once I fully retired. I didn't know how to answer her then. Now, I think I’m starting to understand.

It’s not about going back to work. It’s about discovering that the value of the work has been there all along; it’s just continuing in a different way.

The sunlight was pleasant as I walked out of the building. I remembered the potted rose at home—I should probably cut back on the watering this time of year. I need to make a note of that in my journal when I get home tonight.

Tomorrow, I have to take Xiuying to the hospital for her follow-up appointment.

Life goes on. But right now, it feels like there’s something new in that "going on."

# December 20, 2024 | Friday | Sunny

Sitting on the balcony, I see the roses have entered dormancy. The leaves are falling day by day, leaving the stems bare. I’m flipping through my gardening log—from the first bud in spring to now, a whole year’s record is right here.

Suddenly, I feel a wave of emotion.

A flower’s life is just like this: sprouting in spring, blooming in summer, bearing fruit in autumn, and resting in winter. Every season looks different, but each is necessary. Winter isn't the end; it’s just preparation. It will return next spring.

I wonder, is my life like this too?

Thirty-seven years of work—that was my blooming season. I poured all my energy into it: setting standards, inspecting products, leading teams. On the office wall hangs a group photo from ten years ago; we were all in our prime then. That was my summer.

Then came retirement. At first, I panicked. I felt suddenly hollowed out. Without the job, who was I? During that time, I often sat here staring at the flowers, but I was actually looking at myself—someone left behind by time.

But things are different now.

This consulting work isn't about returning to the past, nor is it just keeping busy for the sake of it. It’s like winter dormancy—I’m not depleting myself; I’m accumulating. I pass on thirty-seven years of experience to the new director and help the company solve problems, but no longer in that "all-out" state. I have a rhythm now: twice a week, two hours each time. I can be a consultant, a husband, and a grandfather.

These identities don't conflict. Like the four seasons, they are all me.

I called Xiaoling yesterday and took the initiative to say I’d teach Jianhao how to play Chinese chess. I used to always think, "I'll spend more time with my grandson once I retire," but that "once" never seemed to arrive. Now, I’m not waiting anymore; I’m just doing it. Jianhao was so happy on the phone, saying he wanted to learn. I suddenly felt that this is exactly what I should be doing right now.

Xiuying is in the kitchen making dinner. Her blood pressure has been well-controlled this month. I’ve noted down the doctor’s orders again; the calendar is already covered in scribbles. This is also my job now—taking care of her, growing old with her.

I don't know if this counts as the autumn or winter of my life. But I’m not afraid anymore.

When winter passes, spring comes. But even if spring were never to come, winter itself has meaning.

The flowers know this truth. And now, so do I.

Good morning, Xiaoling. I tried the method you taught me and took a photo of the Chinese rose.

[Image: A pot of pink Chinese roses with buds just beginning to bloom and lush green leaves, set against the corner of an old balcony]

Is this the right way to take it? Then I save it on my phone. I followed the steps you told me, but I’m still a bit worried—could the photo just disappear all of a sudden? You mentioned something about a "cloud" last time that could help save them, but I didn't quite understand it.

My current method is to still write down the date and the condition of the flowers in my notebook after taking the photo. That way, I have a record in both places. Do you think that’s okay? Anyway, I’ve used my notebook for so many years that I’m not afraid of anything going wrong. I’ll just treat the phone as a little helper.

How are things on your end? Are you free this weekend? What has Jianhao been up to lately?

Thank you for being so patient in teaching me; I know I’m a slow learner. But I really am trying, even if I still forget things sometimes. Please bear with me.

Xiuying, are you awake?

I was out on the balcony watering the plants just now, and halfway through... I just sort of froze.

The flowers are the same, the soil is the same, but I was just standing there and suddenly didn't know what I was doing.

Do you think it’s going to be like this every day from now on?

I know it sounds a bit silly. When you retire, you’re supposed to take it easy—water the plants, play some chess, right? But...

I still woke up at six-thirty. I watched the news for ten minutes and had breakfast. Everything was exactly the same as before. Yet, something just feels off.

Maybe I’m overthinking it. Don’t worry.

I just wanted to ask—have you noticed anything... different about me lately?

At dinner that evening, Li Jianguo set down his chopsticks and turned toward Wang Xiuying.

"Xiuying, Xiao Ling called me today."

He paused for a moment, as if gathering his thoughts.

"She said the way I take photos with my phone and then write things down in my notebook... she said it’s clever. She said it’s not some kind of 'second-best' option, it’s... it’s a practical way of doing things."

Wang Xiuying looked up at him.

There was a sense of relief in Li Jianguo’s eyes, though it was still tinged with a hint of uncertainty.

"I was worried she thought I was just being stubborn. But when she said that today, I realized that maybe it’s not that I can’t learn. It’s just... I’ve found a way that works for me."

He picked up his chopsticks, then set them down again.

"I’ve been thinking... I’ll keep trying. I don’t have to do everything on the phone. I’ll just... take it slow."

He turned to his wife, his voice softening with a bit more warmth.

"Don’t you think so?"

## Key Points from Morning Discussion with Wang Tao

I took the initiative to visit the Tech Department yesterday afternoon. Wang Tao was very patient in walking me through the new system. I took diligent notes in my notebook, terrified of missing a single detail.

### Core Functional Modules

**1. Data Collection Interface**
- Inspection data from every production line can be uploaded in real-time.
- No more manual form-filling, tallying, and re-entering data into the computer like we used to do.
- Wang Tao calls this "single entry, multi-terminal synchronization."
- *My Understanding: It’s essentially combining the old "Daily Inspection Report" and "Weekly Summary Sheet" into one.*

**2. Standards Library Management**
- All the inspection standards I developed can be entered into the system.
- Upper/lower limits and judgment grades can be set for every standard.
- Inspectors scan the product barcode on-site, and the system automatically pulls up the corresponding standard.
- *My Understanding: This is like the "Quality Standards Manual" I keep in my hand, but digital—inspectors won't have to flip through pages anymore.*

**3. Abnormalities & Alerts**
- If any data point falls outside the standard range, the system automatically flags it in red and issues an alert.
- We can immediately trace it back to the specific production line, shift, and operator.
- *My Understanding: Before, I had to review every single report to find a problem; now, the system proactively tells me where the problem is.*

### My Questions

- ? Can the inspection standards be customized? Or are they permanent once set? **→ Ask Wang Tao this weekend.**
- ? If an inspector makes a mistake and enters wrong data, how do we trace and correct it?
- ? How are data backup and security guaranteed? (This is vital; quality data is a matter of customer trust.)
- ? Can I check real-time data on my phone from home?

---

## Personal Reflections

As Wang Tao was explaining, it slowly dawned on me—the logic of this system is actually identical to the quality inspection workflow I’ve followed for 37 years:

**The Old Workflow:**
Inspection → Recording → Summarizing → Analysis → Decision-making

**The New System Workflow:**
Inspection → Auto-recording → Auto-summarizing → Auto-analysis → Alert-based Decision-making

In other words, the system is just automating what I used to do by hand. **The logic hasn't changed; it’s just faster and more accurate.**

Thinking about it this way, my 37 years of experience aren't being discarded—they are being **coded into this system.** My understanding of quality standards and my grasp of inspection processes are still relevant. What I need to learn now is simply how to use a new tool to execute those standards.

It’s a bit like... I used to use a hand file, and now I have a power tool. The craftsmanship remains the same; only the tool has been upgraded.

---

## Action Plan

- **This Weekend:** Re-read the operation manual Wang Tao gave me and clarify the main functional modules.
- **Next Monday:** Do a trial run in the Tech Department’s demo environment—don't be afraid of making mistakes.
- **Next Wednesday:** Mention this to my chess buddies and see what they think (some of them have gone through system updates at their factories too).
- **Goal:** Master basic operations within two weeks; be able to handle daily work independently within a month.

---

## Remarks

Wang Tao mentioned that this system has already been implemented in several other companies with good feedback. He also said that once I’m familiar with it, I can help train the new inspectors.

...That thought makes me feel much better.

It’s not that I’m falling behind the times; I just need a little time to wrap my head around new things. I can do this.

# Proposed Adjustments to Xiuying’s Blood Pressure Management

**Date: November 14, 2022 (Evening)**

**Recorded by: Li Jianguo**

---

## Identifying the Problem

I’ve been reflecting on this a lot lately and realized that my approach to managing Xiuying’s blood pressure may have gone off track.

Checking the logs every single day and getting anxious over every minor fluctuation has become a habit. The doctor mentioned that this might actually be increasing her psychological burden. Ming also told me today that Mom’s spikes are sometimes "scared out of her" by my constant worrying.

Hearing that hurt, but I know he’s right.

## Areas for Change

**1. Stop Daily Blood Pressure Monitoring**
- ~~Switch to weekly scheduled measurements.~~
- Record data only as frequently as the doctor requires; do not add extra sessions on my own.
- Trust Xiuying’s ability to sense her own physical condition.

**2. Communication with the Doctor**
- During her next follow-up, ask the doctor directly for the recommended monitoring frequency.
- Stop trying to be "clever" by adding extra "management measures."
- Follow medical advice strictly without overstepping.

**3. Give Xiuying More Autonomy**
- Let her decide what to eat and what to do.
- My role is to **support**, not to **monitor**.
- If she says she’s fine, she’s fine. Stop prying.

**4. Adjust My Own Mindset**
- Accept that some things are beyond my total control.
- Not every problem can be solved with standards, like a quality inspection.
- Do what needs to be done, then leave the rest to the doctor and to her.

## Action Items

- [ ] Have a heart-to-heart with Xiuying this weekend and share my thoughts.
- [ ] Put the blood pressure logbook away; keep it out of sight.
- [ ] Listen to the doctor at the next appointment; stop overthinking things on my own.
- [ ] Go to bed earlier; stop losing sleep over this.

---

**Notes:**

In my professional life, I’m used to systematizing everything—setting standards and enforcing them strictly. That approach works for quality management, but when applied at home to show care for a person, it can be overbearing.

Xiuying is not a product. You cannot ensure "compliance" through more rigorous "inspections."

I need to learn when to let go. That is a lesson for me as well.

Xiuying, I’ve been out here on the balcony thinking for a long time.

Just now, while I was watering the Chinese roses and watching the buds slowly open, it suddenly hit me—we’re just like these flowers. Youth is the budding, middle age is the full bloom, and now we’ve reached a different stage. It’s not about withering; it’s... how should I put it? It’s a new kind of growth.

I wrote some thoughts down in my notebook. I used to feel that leaving work meant losing something, like I was being hollowed out. But these past few days, video chatting with Jianhao and seeing him show me his drawings through the phone, I suddenly understood—I haven’t lost my identity; I’ve gained more of them. I’m not just the Director of Quality Inspection; I’m your husband, Jianhao’s grandfather, and a friend to my chess buddies. These roles are every bit as meaningful as my job ever was.

The year you retired from the textile mill, I was still worried about whether I’d be able to adapt to a new life. Looking back now, you figured it out long ago. In the ten years you’ve been home, you’ve kept everything so organized and tended to the flowers so well; our life has actually become fuller than it was before. I want to learn from you.

I’ve been thinking about what we could do together once I truly retire. You said you wanted to see the Bund in Shanghai—let’s go. As for Jianhao, I want to spend more time with him, teach him how to play Xiangqi, and tell him stories about when we were young. And these flowers—we can look after the balcony together and make it even more beautiful.

I’d still like to do some consulting for the new director, but not for the sake of a title this time. I just want to pass on what I’ve learned over the years. Just to be helpful, that’s all.

I’m feeling a bit sentimental, I suppose. All these years, you’ve been right there, watching me get caught up in the whirl of work, never once complaining. Now, I want to really be there for you and the family—to watch the flowers bloom and fade, play chess, and eat the meals you cook. A life like that is actually very fulfilling.

We still have plenty of time.

(Transcribed from a WeChat voice message from Li Jianguo to his wife Wang Xiuying, evening of June 27, 2024)

# Work Log

**Date:** Wednesday, July 5, 2023
**Weather:** Sunny, 32°C
**Recorded by:** Li Jianguo

---

## Morning Summary

8:30–11:00: Processed inspection reports in the office. Used the new system to import data for three production lines. There was a discrepancy in the dimensional data recognition for the second line. The system flagged 8 parts as out-of-tolerance, but after re-checking with traditional methods, there were actually only 3. This issue is worth noting.

I didn't rush to the tech department; instead, I checked the system logs myself first. I found that the problem might lie in the OCR recognition threshold settings—data at the third decimal place is prone to misreading. I documented the specific error cases and took photos.

In the afternoon, I sent an email to the technicians, describing the problem clearly:
- The specific models of the three parts involved.
- A comparison of the data before and after recognition.
- My hypothesis (likely an issue with recognition precision).
- A suggestion for them to try adjusting the parameters.

Doing it this way is much more useful than simply saying "the system doesn't work well."

---

## Afternoon Work

14:00: Department meeting. Discussed the application of the new system in the inspection workflow.

During the meeting, I made a suggestion: could we add a "manual review" step before the system exports the report, especially for precision parts? This way, we can leverage the system's efficiency while ensuring our inspection standards don't slip. The Director nodded and said it was a good idea.

After the meeting, a few subordinates asked for my thoughts on the system. I told them, "Actually, this system is quite interesting; the key is to understand its logic. It’s not here to replace us; it’s here to help us handle repetitive tasks. Our job is to use our experience to make up for its shortcomings."

As I said those words, I felt a shift in my own perspective. For the past two weeks, I had been resisting the system, feeling it was complex and clunky. But today, when I actually sat down to analyze that data discrepancy, I realized I was thinking about it and trying to understand it, rather than just being defeated by it.

---

## Personal Reflection

I’ve been in this industry for 37 years, moving from an operator to a director, and I’m familiar with every link in quality management. When I was learning this new system recently, I did feel a sense of frustration. As someone in their 60s, facing so many complex interfaces and parameters, it’s hard not to wonder if I’ve truly fallen behind the times.

But today’s incident made me realize that perhaps the problem wasn't that I couldn't learn it, but that I was approaching it the wrong way from the start. I had been passively memorizing operating steps instead of actively trying to understand the logic behind the system. When I changed my mindset—treating it as something "to be understood" rather than "to be memorized"—things started to click.

I still have the opportunity to keep doing this work for another couple of years, so I might as well do it right. It’s not about proving anything; I just feel that if I’m going to do a job, I should do it well. That has always been my principle.

---

## To-Do List

- [ ] Follow up on the tech department's reply tomorrow.
- [ ] Organize notes on common issues with the new system.
- [ ] Prepare a specific proposal for the "manual review step" before Friday's departmental meeting.

---

**End-of-day Status:** Feeling alright; a bit more relaxed than in recent weeks.

June 29, 2024  Sunny

I ran into Mr. Wang at the community center this afternoon.

It’s a bit strange, actually. We’ve seen each other a few times while playing chess, but we never really talked. Today, he sat down next to me, and we just started chatting. He asked how I’d been lately, and for some reason, I ended up telling him everything that’s been on my mind—about retiring, that initial sense of loss, and the feeling of slowly finding my way out of it.

He listened very intently, and then he said something that made me feel a lot lighter. He said, "Retirement isn't the end of life; it’s just a new way of living." Just that one sentence.

I’ve been wondering if I’m just taking too long to adjust. After thirty-seven years at the factory, suddenly letting go left me feeling like something was missing. Xiuying says I’ve been spending a lot of time lately just staring blankly at the flowers on the balcony, and she asks what I’m thinking about. To be honest, I couldn't really put it into words. But hearing Mr. Wang say that made me realize—I’m not lost; I’m just looking for a new direction.

Over the past few months, I’ve been teaching my grandson how to play Chinese chess during our video calls. He’s not very good yet, but he’s so serious about it. I explain the moves to him, and he just blinks and listens. Xiuying laughs in the background, saying we look like two little old men. In those moments, I feel like there’s a kind of value in this. It’s different, but it’s real.

And then there are the flowers. I’ve started recording the growth of the Chinese roses every day. From the first bud to the full bloom, I write down every detail. Doing this makes me feel like I’m still doing something meaningful, even if it doesn't show up on any inspection report.

Mr. Wang said he went through the same thing. After he retired from teaching, he felt lost for a while too. Then he started practicing calligraphy, and now he comes to play chess every week; his life is actually more fulfilling than before. He said, "Look, now we finally have the time to do the things we actually want to do."

I thought about that photo on my office wall from ten years ago, and the old photo albums Xiuying has been organizing at home. I’m thinking that maybe I should record these stories and tell them to my grandson.

Mr. Wang suggested we go for a walk together tomorrow, down by the river. I said yes.

It’s a good feeling.

Soo-young, I just got off the phone with Geon-ho.

You’ll never guess what—the boy has been telling his classmates stories about me at school. Stories about how I used to inspect products at the factory. Geon-ho’s mother said even the teacher was listening intently. Hearing that... it just melted my heart.

I’ve made up my mind: tomorrow, I’m going to try that video call thing my daughter taught me. I used to always say I was afraid I’d mess it up, but thinking about it now, how hard can it be? I worked at the factory for thirty-seven years; am I really going to let this beat me? I’ll just take it one step at a time, just like my inspection work. There’s no reason I can’t get it right.

I went over the steps my daughter wrote down for me again and copied them into my notebook. I’ll give it a try tomorrow evening and see if I can get a video chat going with Geon-ho. The boy wants to see me, and I want to see him too. We can’t just keep relying on his mother to pass messages back and forth.

You probably think I’m being a bit silly, taking notes for something so small. But I just really want to do this right. We’re getting older, and we have to cherish every chance we get to be there for our grandson.

Go ahead and eat, don’t wait for me. I’m going to go over the steps one more time until I have them by heart.
