November 16, 2022    Sunny    Afternoon

Xiaoling called just now while I was still in the office. She brought up the issue I’ve been having with my phone.

I originally thought she would try to comfort me like last time, using that "Dad, don't worry too much about it" tone. That tone actually hurts the most—it sounds like she’s saying, "Forget it, you’re too old to learn." I can hear it.

But she didn't.

She said, "Dad, your method is actually very wise."

I didn't catch her meaning at first. She explained it again—taking photos with the phone as a backup while keeping formal records in a notebook. She said it ensures no data is lost while maintaining reliability. She called it a "method that blends the old with the new."

As I listened to her, I was sitting at my desk piled high with today’s inspection reports, the factory chimneys visible outside the window. I suddenly felt like crying, but the tears didn't come.

For over a month now, I’ve been stuck in a cycle of self-doubt. I felt like I couldn't learn, that I really was getting old, and that even my own daughter had given up on the expectation that I could keep up with the times. It’s a terrible feeling. In my work, I know exactly what to do, but with this, I felt completely useless.

But what Xiaoling said... she wasn't just comforting me; she was validating me.

I said, "Then... I’ll keep trying."

My voice was a bit thick, but I meant it. It wasn't that forced, reluctant kind of "trying." I truly want to continue.

Xiuying asked me how my day was tonight. I didn't go into detail; I just told her Xiaoling had called. She gave me a look and didn't ask further. She knows.

Sitting here at home now, looking at that smartphone, I’m not as afraid of it anymore. Maybe I don't need to master it completely. Maybe this is just who I am—using it in my own way. There’s nothing wrong with that.

Tomorrow, I’ll keep at it.

Xiuying, I wanted to talk to you.

Just now, while I was watering the plants on the balcony, I noticed that the rose... the leaves have all withered. I remember it was blooming so well before I retired, but now... I can’t tell if I’ve been watering it too much or too little. I looked through the notebook, and the last entry was June 28th. I haven't written anything since then.

I sat there thinking for a long time, feeling like I can’t do anything right anymore. Back at the factory, I could set inspection standards with perfect order; I had the trust of the entire department. Now, I can’t even take care of a single potted plant.

...I’m not complaining. It’s just that sometimes I wonder, what am I still capable of doing?

Looking at me now, do you think I’ve... become a bit useless, too?

(Pause)

Do you think I should start recording the watering schedule again? Or... should I just forget it?

September 20, 2022, 6:30 AM

I checked my phone again. The screen is still lit; it hasn’t suddenly gone black. The photos are still there, and the words in my notes are still there too.

This is the fourth time this week already. Every day before breakfast, I have to open my phone and take a look, just like checking to see if my flowers are still alive. Wang Xiuying teased me yesterday, saying I’m more nervous about this than I am about my Chinese roses. I didn’t argue, because it’s true.

I still mostly remember the steps my daughter taught me during the National Day holiday. Open the camera, take a photo, open the notes, type… but I always feel like something isn’t quite right. The things inside the phone are like they’re stored in an invisible box; I don’t know if they truly exist or if they’re just an illusion. What if one day it won’t open? What if the data just vanishes?

So, I still stick to my notebook. Yesterday, when I watered the roses, I took a photo with my phone first, then opened my notebook and recorded the date, the amount of water, and the condition of the flowers, just like always. But this time, I added an extra line: "Recorded with phone photo."

As I wrote that sentence, even I felt it was a bit ridiculous. What does it even mean? Am I recording that I’m using a new tool? Or am I reminding myself that the data in this phone has actually been noted down by me, so there’s no need to be afraid?

 I can’t really say.

But it was only after doing that that I felt a bit more grounded. Two systems: one is the paper I can touch, and the other is the phone my daughter says is reliable. This way, even if something goes wrong with the phone, I still have my notebook. This way, even if one day I really…

Never mind, I won’t think about that.

Sometimes I just wonder, why go to all this trouble? The methods I’ve used for over thirty years have never failed me. Now I’m forced to learn new things, and it’s so exhausting. My daughter means well, I know. My grandson watches cartoons on the phone, and I know times have changed. But still…

It’s 6:30 in the morning, and it’s still dark outside. I’m sitting on the sofa with the phone in my hand and the notebook on my lap. I can’t bring myself to put either of them down.

I have to go to work in a bit. I’ll have to check those inspection reports again. Those, I can understand; those, I can control. At least at the factory, I still know what I’m doing.

Two more years until retirement.

I’ve been thinking about that sentence a lot lately.

November 28, 2024, 7:30 PM

Sitting on the balcony. The roses are still in bloom; I didn't water them today, I'll do it tomorrow. My phone is resting on my lap; I just finished a video call with Jianhao.

Over the past month, I feel like I’ve accomplished something quite significant, though it sounds a bit silly to say out loud—I’ve learned how to use a smartphone. Not just "knowing how to answer a call," but truly learning it.

The day before yesterday, while organizing my photo album, I realized that all the records of my calls with Jianhao from this past month were right there. The videos, the photos, the drawings he showed me, the chess stories I told him... I sat there, scrolling through them one by one, and suddenly found myself at a loss for words. In the past, I could never have kept these things. Back then, seeing someone was just a visit, and leaving was just a departure; you had to get photos developed just to see them. Now, it’s all right here in this little screen, clear as day.

I wrote out a plan in my notebook. Just like setting inspection standards back at the factory, I listed everything I want to do—video calls three times a week, stories to tell Jianhao, chess lessons to teach him, photos to take. When Xiuying saw me writing, she laughed and said, "You’re stricter with your grandson than you were with the products." I told her no, it’s different... but actually, she’s right. I just want to do this well.

At the end, I wrote a sentence: "What I leave for my grandson isn't just stories, but his grandfather’s companionship." After writing it, I took a photo and sent it to Xiaoling.

To be honest, I’m not afraid of the phone anymore. Sometimes I still press the wrong button, but I don't get nervous. I know how to fix it. More importantly, I’m starting to want to learn other things—like how to edit videos on the phone, to turn these stories and photos into a documentary. A thought like that would have been impossible a month ago.

Retirement doesn't seem so scary anymore. I have things to do, people to keep me company, and goals to reach. Every day has its own rhythm. That’s enough.

Tomorrow, we continue.

Son, your mom just asked me what’s for dinner, and it reminded me that I needed to tell you something.

A few days ago, Director Zhang reached out and asked if I could help him out with some consulting work. It’s mostly about quality management—they’ve got some new processes they’re not quite sure about. I didn’t give him an answer right away; I wanted to talk it over with your mom first.

Your mom thinks it’s a good idea, since I’m just idling around at home anyway. So, we’ve decided to go ahead with it—just two or three days a month, nothing major. This way, I can stay connected to the field without disrupting our current pace of life. Your mom also said it’s better for my health to have something to do, rather than just gardening and playing chess all day.

I think it’s a good balance. It’s not going back full-time, but I’m not completely cut off either. You see, this is how I think about things now—everything has to be discussed and decided together with your mom. For all those years at the factory, I was the one making all the calls. Now, I actually find this way much more grounding.

How’s the building materials business going on your end? The weather’s been nice lately, so it should be a good time for it, right?

# Health Management Plan for Wang Xiuying
**Start Date: July 1, 2024**

---

## I. Daily Blood Pressure Monitoring

**Measurement Times:**
- Morning: 6:45 AM (30 minutes after waking up, before eating)
- Evening: 8:30 PM (2 hours after dinner)

**Recording Format:**
| Date | Morning | Evening | Remarks |
|------|---------|---------|---------|
| 7/1  | 138/88  | 135/85  | Baseline data |

**Recording Requirements:**
- Use a medical-grade monitor; take two readings each time and record the average.
- Record in the dedicated notebook using black ink; no crossings-out or alterations.
- If abnormal fluctuations occur (exceeding 150/95), immediately note down that day's diet and emotional state.
- Summarize every Sunday to analyze trends.

---

## II. Dietary Control Scheme

**Knowledge to Acquire:**
- Low-sodium diet standards (limit daily salt intake to under 6g).
- List of forbidden foods for hypertensive patients.
- Beneficial foods: Wood ear mushrooms, kelp, winter melon, mung beans, hawthorn.

**Implementation:**
- Breakfast: Plain congee with pickled mustard tubers and an egg (rinse the pickles in warm water first).
- Lunch: Steamed fish or lean meat + stir-fried greens + white rice.
- Dinner: Noodle soup or plain congee + greens.
- Snacks: Wood ear mushroom soup, mung bean soup (no sugar added).

**Shopping List:**
- To buy at the market this week: Winter melon, wood ear mushrooms, kelp, hawthorn.
- Buy a low-sodium salt substitute to try out.

---

## III. Exercise Schedule

**Goal:** 30 minutes a day, 5 days a week.

**Specific Schedule:**
- Mon/Wed/Fri: Go out at 6:50 AM for a 40-minute walk (two laps around the neighborhood).
- Tue/Thu: 15 minutes of simple stretching at home + 20 minutes of "Zhan Zhuang" (standing meditation).
- Weekends: Depending on the weather, accompany Xiuying for a stroll in the park.

**Precautions:**
- Avoid strenuous exercise; keep a slow pace while walking.
- Stop immediately if dizziness occurs after exercise and record the incident.

---

## IV. Hospital Follow-up Schedule

- **Next Follow-up:** August 1, 2024 (Fixed follow-up on the 1st of every month).
- **Advance Booking:** Call to make an appointment 3 days before the check-up.
- **Check-up Items:** Blood pressure, urinalysis, renal function (as per doctor's orders).
- **Items to Bring:** Blood pressure logbook, medical insurance card, ID card, previous test reports.

---

## V. Areas for Potential Improvement

- [ ] If blood pressure remains consistently above 140/90, consult the doctor regarding medication adjustment.
- [ ] Monitor Xiuying for symptoms like dizziness or chest tightness; seek medical attention immediately if they occur.
- [ ] Evaluate the effectiveness of the diet plan after one month; adjust if necessary.
- [ ] If exercise is difficult to maintain, consider alternatives (e.g., Tai Chi).
- [ ] Learn more about hypertension; watch health programs on CCTV.

---

**Remarks:**
This plan is not set in stone. We will evaluate its effectiveness monthly and adjust based on blood pressure data. Xiuying says I’m stricter than the doctor—but this is my way. These are habits built over thirty-seven years at the factory, now applied to taking care of her. The principles of quality management are the same: standards, records, and continuous improvement.

Starting today.

## Benchmarking Against Emergency Plan

I wrote in my notebook before that if we only covered the bare essentials—medical, food, utilities—monthly expenses should be around 1,600-1,800 RMB.

Current spending is nearly 1,000 RMB higher than that figure.

Most of those "variable" costs are actually things I want to give to the family. The grandkids' pocket money, Xiuying’s clothes, keeping Li Ming company... these could all be "saved."

But if I really had to save, what else could I cut?

The 300 RMB for the breakfast shop? It’s been 20 years. If we ate somewhere else, Xiuying would say I’m "making a fuss" again. Besides, the lady who runs the shop always asks me how Xiuying’s health is doing.

The 500 RMB for the grandkids? During New Year, Li Jianhao asked me if he could get a new backpack. I agreed right then and there.

---

## Pension Estimate

Based on the company’s pension calculation, I should receive an average of 3,500-4,000 RMB per month.

Subtract the 1,600-1,800 in essential expenses, and that leaves 1,700-2,400 RMB.

The numbers look okay. But Xiuying’s medical costs might increase. The doctor said that as she gets older, her hypertension medication might need adjusting.

If the consulting work continues, adding another 1,000-1,500 a month, things would be even more secure.

But that depends on whether the company is willing to keep me on as a consultant. That’s still uncertain.

---

## Other Thoughts

Looking at these numbers makes me feel a bit tired.

Xiuying mentioned recently that she wants to go to Shanghai to see Li Xiaoling and Jianhao, but plane tickets, accommodation... that’s another big expense. I didn't say no, but I didn't say yes either.

The competition in Li Ming’s building materials business is indeed tough lately. He said on the phone last time that some months he barely closes any deals. I want to help him, but what can I do? I don't know the first thing about building materials.

Maybe after I retire, I’ll have more time to sit in his shop and offer some advice. But even that won't change much.

Forget it, I should sleep. I'll deal with it when it's light out.

I have to go to the hospital with Xiuying for her follow-up tomorrow.

*No matter how complex this system is, I have to master it. I’ve been in quality inspection for over thirty years; I can’t let things slip just because the tools have changed.*

Monday, August 12, 2024 – Sunny

I got a call from the office this morning. They said the new Quality Control Director was running into some trouble and asked if I could offer some advice. I hesitated for a moment—I’m already retired; should I really be getting involved in this stuff again?

But as I listened to him describe the situation, my brain just started ticking automatically. It was the same old problem: balancing inspection standards with production efficiency. I walked him through it: first, identify the non-negotiables; second, check for flaws in the process design; and only then consider adjusting the standards. We talked for about twenty minutes.

After I hung up, Xiuying asked me how it went. It was then that I realized—for the first time in these past two weeks, I felt like I was still useful.

Since I’ve been home these two weeks, I still wake up at 6:30, watch the news, and eat breakfast. But honestly, I’ve felt empty inside. I’ve done the gardening, played chess, looked through old photo albums—I’ve done it all, yet something always felt missing. I even started to doubt myself: once a person retires, what are they worth? That ten-year-old group photo is still on the office wall, but that’s all in the past now.

But today was different. When that young director was listening to my explanation, I could tell he was taking notes. I could feel that what I was saying actually mattered to him. That feeling... it was a bit like being back in the department.

I told Xiuying, "It feels good that someone still needs my experience." She smiled and said I finally looked like my old self again.

I didn't tell her how much I’ve actually been struggling these past two weeks. I thought retirement meant letting go of everything and just resting. But it turns out it’s not like that. It’s not that I hate resting; it’s just that... I need to be needed.

Maybe I don't have to walk away from work entirely; maybe I just need to change how I do it. Consulting, passing on experience—that seems like it could work. I haven’t fully figured it out yet, but today’s call made me feel, for the first time, that maybe retirement isn't the end, but just a change of roles.

Back to the gardening tomorrow. The Chinese roses need watering.

Xiuying, have lunch early today, don't wait for me.

Did you remember to take your blood pressure medicine? The doctor said you must take it on time and not miss any doses.

Are you feeling dizzy or unwell at all today? If you are, go lie down for a bit and don't overwork yourself.

When you're cooking, try to use less salt. I looked at the list the doctor gave us last time; we both need to start eating lighter from now on. You know it’ll help with your blood pressure.

The weather is nice this afternoon, but don't stay out on your feet for too long. Get some good rest at home, and I'll be back this evening.

October 10, 2024, Evening

Xiuying went back to the hospital for a check-up today. Her blood pressure was a bit higher than last month. The doctor said she needs to watch her diet, eat less salt, and walk more. I took a photo of the doctor’s orders while listening—my daughter taught me how to do this; you just press the camera icon. After that, I marked the date for the next check-up on the calendar: November 8th.

I told Xiuying that we need to take good care of our health so we can spend more time with the grandkids. Her eyes welled up a bit, and she patted my hand without saying a word.

To be honest, I’ve been thinking lately that in the two years since I retired, it feels like I’ve been waiting for something, though I never knew what. There was less and less to do at the office, and sometimes an entire day would pass with nothing to accomplish. But things are different now.

I made a plan for Xiuying:

**Blood Pressure Management**
- Measure once every morning and evening; record it in the phone’s "Notes" app.
- Diet: Plain porridge with pickled vegetables for breakfast; fish and green vegetables for lunch; light meals for dinner.
- Walk with her for 30 minutes in the neighborhood every evening.

**Medication**
- Take at 6:30 AM; I’ve marked it on the calendar.
- The doctor said not to miss a dose.

Back at the factory, this was exactly what I did—sorting out complex things and pushing them forward step by step. Applying it to Xiuying feels a bit different. It’s not for production targets anymore; it’s so she can live well. It’s so we can be there for the grandkids.

Using the phone is still a bit of a struggle. I always have trouble finding the Notes app interface, so I ended up asking Li Ming for help. But I’m learning, one feature at a time. Taking photos, marking dates, setting reminders... these things aren't actually that complicated; it’s just that no one taught me at first.

Sitting on the balcony looking at the roses, it suddenly occurred to me that maybe this is what I’m supposed to be doing now. Not setting standards in a factory, but taking care of the person beside me. Keeping Xiuying’s blood pressure stable so the grandkids have a healthy grandmother.

It feels like I’ve found something. It’s not the same kind of achievement I felt at the factory, but it feels more real.

Tomorrow, we continue.

# Memo

**Date: September 3, 2024, 7:00 PM**

**Subject: Communication Approach with Li Ming**

I spoke with Li Ming on the phone today. He brought up the recent loss of customers at his building materials store. I was just about to jump in and list out solutions like I used to, when it hit me—he’s 35 now. This is his business, not mine.

So, I started by asking: "What’s your take on the situation?"

He shared his thoughts. Only after listening did I say: "My view is that you could try partnering with design firms, but you know your business best. Do you think that’s a viable path?"

He seemed to breathe a sigh of relief. Later, he even took the initiative to ask for my input.

**Points to remember:**
- Don’t rush to provide answers.
- Listen to his thoughts first.
- When offering suggestions, use phrases like "My view is..." followed by "but you know best."
- Let him make the decisions; don't make them for him.

This shift isn't easy, but it feels right. My wife has said similar things to me before.

**Keep doing this next time.**

*Note: I’ve looked at that ten-year-old department photo in the office so many times. Some of those employees have been promoted; others have moved on. I can’t keep trying to manage other people’s lives my way.*

Xiaoling, something interesting happened at the chess club today that I just have to tell you about.

Before we started playing, I took out my phone and showed everyone the video call logs and photos of your son. You wouldn't believe how surprised those old chess buddies of mine were; they kept asking how I learned to use all that. So, I told them all about how you taught me step-by-step, and how we now have our fixed video calls with Jianhao every Wednesday.

Old Wang even joked, "Director Li, you’ve really mastered the technology!" I told him I have my daughter to thank for that—if it weren't for her patient teaching, I’d probably still be fumbling around trying to figure out how to answer a call.

What makes me happiest is that I can now actually take the initiative to chat with Jianhao. A couple of days ago, he told me all about his school’s sports meet, and I even took some photos of him and his classmates—the angles might not have been great, but he was so happy when he saw them. It’s a different feeling, you know? It’s not like before when I could only hear updates through you; now I can see him directly, hear his voice, and we can even laugh together.

When you said back then, "Dad, this way we can see each other more often," I truly understand what you meant now. This phone has changed the way we are together.

Thank you for not giving up on teaching me, and thank you for your patience. I see all the effort you’ve put in.

**Wednesday, September 25**

Spent the afternoon playing chess with the guys in the activity room, and I brought my phone along. Old Wang asked why I was bothering with that gadget, so I showed them the photos I took a few days ago—the roses on the balcony are in bloom, and the pictures actually turned out quite well. I also gave them a little demonstration of how I video call my grandson. I felt a bit self-conscious, but they all said the picture was crystal clear and asked if I’d bought a brand-new phone.

In truth, it’s just those few steps my daughter taught me; I can pretty much do them without any mistakes now. Old Zhang even said this "old dog" is a quick learner and that I’m paying more attention than he did when his son tried to teach him. Ha, I suppose it’s because I want to chat with my grandson more often.

In the evening, I successfully video-called Jianhao again. He showed me his new backpack from over in Shanghai. I feel like I’m getting the hang of this—not as nervous as I was at the start.

Writing this down so I don't forget how I got here.

October 16, 2024 | Sunny | 6:30 AM

Woke up at the usual time again. Xiuying was still asleep, so I got out of bed quietly without making a sound. Outside, the sky was just beginning to brighten, and the birds downstairs were chirping clearly.

After washing up, I sat on the small balcony to check the news, sipping tea and looking at my Chinese roses. The bud that appeared yesterday looked a bit fuller today; I picked up my phone to take a photo and record it.

Taking photos is fine, but what happened next was frustrating. I tried to make a note of it in the memo app, but somehow I messed up and deleted the entry. I tried several times but couldn't get it back, so I finally gave up. This damn phone...

I didn't get too upset, though. I still have my notebook, after all. I took it out and did things the old-fashioned way—date, bud size, watering status, weather—recording everything one by one. The feeling of the pen gliding across the paper is still the best; it feels much more solid and grounding than poking away at a screen.

I spent over seventy minutes on the balcony, pruning a few branches, watering every pot, and moving two of them so they could get more even sunlight. My mind felt exceptionally clear while doing these things, just like when I used to inspect products at the factory—you have to be meticulous, follow a system, and never be careless.

Xiuying is up now, making breakfast in the kitchen. Listening to her movements and smelling the aroma of the congee, it suddenly occurred to me—this is how I’ve lived all these years. Every day has things to do, and every thing has its place.

The phone incident wasn't actually that bad. I’m learning; even if I’m slow and make mistakes sometimes, I haven't given up. I can still use the camera, and even if the memo was deleted, I have my pen and paper. Using both methods together is actually more reliable. Thinking about it that way, it’s not so bad after all.

Later, I’ll accompany Xiuying to the hospital for her blood pressure check-up. I might get a consulting call from the company this afternoon. Tonight, I’ll be playing chess with my friends.

Life goes on like this, day by day, in good order.

And that is enough.

# August 3, 2024  Sunny

Got back from the hospital around 2:00 PM. Xiuying’s follow-up results were pretty good; her blood pressure is more stable than last month. The doctor said to stick with the current medication plan. I asked again if the new blood pressure medicine would cause any issues, and the doctor patiently explained everything to me. I wrote it all down in my notebook—the timing, the dosage, the date of the next check-up, and things to watch out for.

At the hospital, I noticed how focused I was. Listening intently to the doctor, taking notes one by one, even proactively asking a few questions. This feeling... how long has it been?

In the consulting room, Xiuying told the doctor, "With him here, I’m at ease." Just that one sentence. My eyes stung a bit, and I felt a slight tug at the corners of my mouth. She looked at me, but I turned away, pretending to tidy up our things.

For the past two weeks, I’ve felt like a piece of furniture. I still wake up at 6:30 AM, but I get up without a purpose. I water the plants on the balcony, record the date, and then what? I go to the community center in the afternoon to play chess—win a few, lose a few—then come home to eat. The days pass, but it feels like nothing is happening. Sometimes I flip through that ten-year-old group photo from the office, looking at those familiar faces and remembering the days when I was needed.

But today at the hospital was different. I was doing something meaningful, and it mattered to Xiuying. She trusts me; she relies on me. I’m not inspecting product quality anymore, but I am monitoring her health and ensuring her safety. This feeling... it’s different from before, yet somehow the same.

Maybe I still have value. I just need to find new ways to prove it.

But it’s too early to overthink this. For now, I’ll just make sure I have the doctor’s orders down clearly and take her back for her follow-up on time next month.

Xiuying was still asleep. I didn't make a sound, just got up and watered the plants. The rosebuds have grown a bit larger; I made a note of it in my notebook. I stared at it for a long time before putting it in the cardboard box. My fingers lingered on the paper for a moment, as if to confirm—all of this was real; it all happened.

When I closed the office door for the last time, I stood at the entrance for a long while. I looked back at that wall; the group photo from ten years ago was still pinned there. I walked over and gently touched everyone’s face with my finger. Director Wang, Xiao Li, Old Zhang… they’re all still there. It’s just me who’s leaving. My eyes felt a bit stinging, so I turned and left quickly before anyone could see.

Now I’m sitting at home, looking at the boxes filled with my belongings. Which wall should I hang that group photo on? The living room? Or the bedroom? What about the quality achievement charts? I’m pondering these strange questions as if using them to fill the time.

Xiuying asked if I wanted breakfast. I said yes, but I don't really have an appetite.

It’s not that I didn't want to retire. I know it’s a good thing. But right now… I don’t quite know who I am anymore. For thirty-seven years, I was the Head of the Inspection Department. And now? I’m a retiree. It sounds so empty.

Should I go to the community center this afternoon? Or is that on Wednesdays? I’ll have to check the calendar.

Maybe I should call my son and ask how his business is going. Or find time to visit my grandson. These are things I’ve wanted to do for a long time, and now I finally have the time. But why does it feel like time is even tighter now?

Never mind. I’ll just finish organizing these things first.

Tuesday, September 17, 2024
Sunny

Yesterday, I got a call from the company. They said the new system is having trouble identifying a specific type of defect and asked if I could take a look. Xiuying was in the kitchen cooking, so I took the call on the balcony. Listening to their description, it felt like the gears in my head started turning again.

After hanging up, I stood on the balcony for a while. The Chinese roses are still blooming; they’ve grown well this year. But my mind wasn't on the flowers.

This morning, I dug out my notebooks from ten years ago—the ones where I recorded inspection standards and defect classifications. As I flipped through them, a strange feeling came over me. It wasn't that I was reminiscing about "glory days," but rather a sudden realization that these things are still here, as if they never went out of style. The problems the new system can't solve might just have answers in my notes. It makes me feel... I don't know how to put it. It’s not pride, and I certainly don't want my old job back. It’s just a confirmation of one thing: those thirty-seven years weren't for nothing.

Sitting in the study, I picked up a pencil and wrote the title "Analysis of the New System's Defect Identification Mechanism" in a fresh notebook. I stopped halfway through and just stared at the title. I didn't keep writing; I just let it sit there. It was a strange gesture—it felt like both a beginning and a confirmation.

Xiuying asked what I was busy with. I told her it was nothing, just thinking about some work stuff. She said, "Then think it through, just don't overwork yourself." She’s always like that—simple words, but they make me feel grounded.

The question now is whether I should take the job. If I do, how much time will it take? Will it mess up my current rhythm? Can I still make it to the chess game next Wednesday? Is Xiuying’s follow-up appointment next month still at the same time? And then there’s the consulting fee... if it pads the pension a bit, maybe I can spend a few extra days with my grandson when he’s on break.

I have no desire to go back to the factory. That stage of my life is truly over. But this feeling—of being needed, of being needed with respect—I have to admit, it makes me feel something. It’s more than just the opposite of a sense of loss.

The pen is still in my hand. I haven't decided whether to keep writing.

Xiuying, I had a video call with Jianhao today and taught him how to play Chinese chess.

The kid was a bit confused at first, so I took it slow, explaining how the horse moves and how the elephant jumps. He was listening so intently, even taking notes on a piece of paper. Since he’s in Shanghai, we don't get to see him much, so I’m glad I finally got to teach him something.

Right before we hung up, he suddenly said, "Grandpa, you have to teach me again next time." Xiuying, when I heard that... how do I put it? I just felt this deep sense of peace.

All those years at the factory, I was always focused on quality control and teaching the workers how to do things the right way. Since retiring, I’ve sometimes wondered what I have left to offer. But after talking to Jianhao today, I realized—the things I can pass down to my grandson actually mean something to him. It’s not just about chess; it’s... well, it's a grandfather’s experience.

Don't you think? This brings me more joy than any of those commendations back at the factory ever did.

I’m going to have a think tomorrow about what else I can teach him.

# Memo

**November 12, 2022 | Afternoon**

Xiuying’s blood pressure was a bit high again today. 140/88.

I’ve been thinking—maybe it’s not the medication. It might be because I’ve been acting so tense around her lately, and she can sense it. I’m recording and analyzing everything every day, as if I’m working on some high-stakes project. But that’s not what she needs from me.

She told me, "Don't be so nervous, I'm fine." I didn't have an answer at the time.

Tonight, while watering the plants on the balcony, it suddenly hit me—perhaps "perfect management" is a form of pressure in itself. I’m trying to control everything through data and schedules, but life isn't an assembly line. And Xiuying’s health isn't just a lab report.

There are some things I simply cannot control.

I’ll still keep the records tomorrow, and I’ll still take her to the hospital. But maybe I need to learn how to... loosen up a bit? Not to give up, but just to stop being so tight.

Where is the balance?

I need to think about this some more.

October 15, 2022, 9:45 PM

Xiuying is already asleep. I’m sitting in the study, still unable to settle down.

Ever since we got back from her follow-up appointment today, the doctor’s words have been looping in my head: "The blood pressure fluctuations are a bit significant." A systolic reading of 150. I’ve looked at that report over and over; the numbers don’t change, but I just can't put my mind at ease.

Aren't we told that taking medication on time keeps it under control? I give her exactly what the doctor prescribed, and her diet has been consistently bland—I barely use any salt. I even marked every dose on the calendar. How can there still be fluctuations?

I spent some time on my phone looking up complications related to hypertension. When Xiuying pushed the door open and walked in, I quickly turned off the screen. She glanced at me but didn't say much, only mentioned that the doctor said this was normal. I nodded, but my mind was still stuck on the words I’d just seen: stroke, kidney failure, myocardial infarction.

I shouldn't think this way. But I can't help it.

Starting tomorrow, I’ve decided to measure her blood pressure twice a day—once in the morning and once in the evening. I’ve already laid it out in my notebook: when to measure, which column to record it in, what dietary precautions to take, and what preparations are needed before the next check-up. It’s just like setting inspection standards back at the factory; I need to standardize and systematize everything. That’s the only way to gain control.

Only then will I be able to relax.

But... why do I still feel so uneasy?

I’m two years away from retirement. In these two years, nothing can go wrong with me, and nothing can go wrong with Xiuying. Are our savings enough? If she needs to be hospitalized, will that money cover it? Is there anything I can do after I retire to supplement our income?

Thinking about these things makes me feel useless. Xiuying is sleeping soundly in the next room, completely unaware that I’m sitting here overthinking everything. Maybe it’s better this way—at least she doesn't have to worry along with me.

But I have to think. I have to be prepared.

That group photo from ten years ago is still on the office wall. My subordinates were all smiling so happily. Back then, I felt like everything was under control. And now? Now I realize there are so many things I simply cannot control.

Tomorrow, I’ll stick to the plan. Record daily, organize the data weekly, and prepare well in advance for the next follow-up. This is all I can do.

I hope it’s enough.

October 18, 2022, Evening

I spoke with Xiaoling on the phone. She said the kids want to video chat this weekend and asked if I’d be free. I told her I was, so she started teaching me how to use that "video call" feature on the phone.

I listened intently and took notes, but I still couldn't get the hang of it. She patiently explained it once; I asked again, and she explained it again. Finally, I asked her, "Do you think Dad is just too old to learn new things?"

There was a pause on the other end. She said, "Dad, you’ve already learned how to take photos, and that’s already quite an achievement."

I could hear it. She had given up.

Not on me, but on the idea of me "actually learning" it. She was just comforting an old man who couldn't keep up. I could hear that "settling for less" tone in her voice—the way you’d tell a child, "You’re already so smart."

I said, "Don't worry about me, I'm fine." But the truth is, I felt a heavy lump in my chest.

After hanging up, I sat there for a long time. Looking at that phone, I thought about how many times I’ve tapped and asked over these past few days just to learn these features. Every time, I felt so foolish.

Later, I opened my notebook to the page where I track the watering of my Chinese roses. The leaves on that pink one are turning yellow now, all because I’ve been so distracted lately and forgot to water it on time. I’ve even messed up the one thing I’m good at because of this phone.

I thought, forget it. I’ll just use my notebook to record things about Jianhao. Just like I do with the roses. I’ve used it for years and it’s never failed me.

But… I wonder, if I can’t even learn how to use a phone, what will I be able to do after I retire? I can’t just grow flowers and play chess for the rest of my life. What about two years from now?

I’m so afraid.

Xiuying, when we were playing chess today, Old Wang and the others said I look much better than last week. They said I finally have some "life" in me again.

I didn't think much of it at the time and just laughed it off. But later, walking back alone, it hit me—they were right.

I really have been different these past couple of days. Yesterday, during the video call with Jianhao, I took the initiative to teach him how to grow roses, and he listened so intently. The day before, I gave Ming some advice regarding the building materials shop. I’ve done these things before, but the feeling is different now. Before, I did them because I felt I "should"; now, I do them because I "want" to.

You know, I’ve been brooding over this retirement. These past two months have been hard; I kept feeling like I was no longer useful. I looked at that office group photo countless times, as if asking myself—where did the "useful" Li Jianguo go?

But what my chess friends said today made me realize—maybe I had it all wrong.

Retirement isn't the end of life. That’s how I’ve been looking at it. But actually, it might just be a different kind of beginning.

I’m no longer the Director of Quality Control, but I can be Jianhao’s grandfather and be there for him more. I don’t have to stare at inspection reports every day, but I can take good care of you, go to the hospital with you, and have the time to talk just like we did when we were young. I can help Ming run his business better, play more rounds of chess with the guys, and properly tend to my roses.

These things have meaning, too. Maybe even more meaning.

I want to experience this new beginning with you. Not just me trying to adjust on my own, but us, together.

Also, I was thinking—maybe I could take on that consulting work for the new Director. Not for the money, and not to prove I’m still useful. Just... to still contribute something, to pass on what I’ve learned over these thirty-seven years. That way, I won’t be leaving that place entirely, but I won’t be trapped in it either. What do you think?

The weather is lovely today. How about we go for a walk together after dinner? I’d really like to talk with you.

# Saturday, June 8, 2024

Woke up at 6:30 AM. Eyes wide open, staring at the ceiling.

There’s no reason to get up this early anymore. The thought circled in my head, feeling a bit strange. For thirty-seven years, waking up at 6:30 has been a physical habit, as natural as breathing. And now? Now there’s no factory, no inspection reports to review, no employees to meet. I lay in bed for another forty minutes, trying to catch a bit more sleep, but it wouldn’t come. Xiuying was still snoring.

Got up. Watered the plants. The rose leaves looked a bit wilted, so I gave them some water. I wrote the date in my notebook—June 8th, sunny. Then what? I just sat on the sofa.

I still watched the evening news. But I realized I wasn’t really listening. My eyes were on the screen, but my mind was elsewhere. That group photo appeared before my eyes again—the one of the whole department from ten years ago. I picked it up and looked at it for a long time. A long time. Where are those people now? Some must have retired already. I counted them—twenty-three people. I knew every single one.

During breakfast, Xiuying said, "Now you can finally get some proper rest." I nodded without saying much. She was trying to comfort me. I know that. But I didn't know how to respond. I wanted to say something warm, like I usually do, but the words vanished before they reached my lips. I just took a sip of my porridge.

Who is that in the mirror? I took a look. It’s still me, but not quite. No work uniform, no ID badge, no sense of being needed. I stood in front of the mirror for a while.

It’s 10:00 AM now. I’m sitting here with a pen, wanting to record something. But I don’t know what to write. I should feel happy, shouldn't I? But what I feel is a strange emptiness. Like a room that’s been filled with things for thirty-seven years and was suddenly emptied. The room is still there, but it’s hollow.

I’m still holding that photo. I want to hang it somewhere in the house. But where? The living room? The bedroom? Or should I just keep holding it and not move?

Li Ming is supposed to call this week. I need to think about what to say to him. How is his business doing lately? I should ask.

Xiuying needs a follow-up checkup for her high blood pressure next month. I need to mark that on the calendar.

There’s no reason to wake up at 6:30 anymore. But I’ll still wake up tomorrow. I know I will.

# Diary

November 2, 2022 | 9:30 PM

I’m obsessing over that pot of pink roses again.

When I was watering it this afternoon, I took a photo with my phone to see if the leaves were actually recovering. After taking it, I started checking—did the photo save? Is it clear? Could it be accidentally deleted? I checked it three times, terrified the data might just vanish. Even then, I wasn't at ease, so I wrote down today’s date, the amount of water, and the color of the leaves in my notebook—just like I’ve been doing all these years.

But this time was different.

I drew two question marks next to the entry in my notebook.

Wang Xiuying saw it and asked, "Still agonizing over that?" I didn't give her a straight answer. I just said, "I don't know which way is right." She sighed and didn't say anything else.

To be honest, I can’t even explain what I’m so conflicted about. Am I worried the photo on the phone will get lost? Or do I feel the notebook records aren't accurate enough? No, it’s neither. I genuinely don't know which way is right anymore. I don't know if my judgment is sound. I don't know if the plant is actually recovering. I don't know if I used the right amount of water. I don't know...

This "not knowing" is like a hole, getting bigger and bigger.

It started with not being able to learn how to use a smartphone, and now, even the thing I’m best at—taking care of flowers—is making me doubt myself. I’ve used this notebook for so many years and never slipped up. But now I’m asking myself: am I really sure? Or am I just kidding myself?

I’m retiring in two years. If I can’t even learn how to use a phone, and if I’m starting to mess up small things like gardening, what happens after I retire? What will I be able to do then?

I’m sitting here now, staring at those two question marks. It’s as if they’re asking me: Li Jianguo, have you still got it?

I have no answer.

February 24, 2023, around 7:00 PM

My chess game was terrible today.

By the middle of the game, I had placed my knight in a completely illogical position. My chess partner pointed it out immediately, and I saw it too, but I just hadn't registered it in the moment. They were polite and didn't say much, only asking in a "you seem tired" kind of tone. I casually mentioned that work has been exhausting lately, but I knew deep down that it was a weak excuse.

Eight years. We’ve kept this chess circle going for eight years, and I’ve never made such a glaring mistake. It wasn't a calculation error; it was as if my basic intuition for the game was off. In the end, I just resigned. That’s not like me.

I couldn't stop thinking about it on the way home.

It’s not about losing a game. It’s the sudden realization that while I was playing, my heart wasn't in it. What was I thinking about? The same old things—the company’s system overhaul, what will happen to the department, and whether everything I’ve built over these thirty-seven years still holds any value. I got so lost in those thoughts that I couldn't even play a decent game of chess.

I spent a long time writing in my notebook. I started by analyzing that wrong move; the knight’s position was indeed illogical, likely a lapse in judgment caused by a lack of focus. That analysis is correct. But as I kept writing, I found myself asking a different question—am I really getting old? Am I really failing to keep up?

It’s a terrifying question. Because if the answer is yes, then everything that follows becomes uncertain.

I have always trusted my experience and judgment. Over thirty-seven years, I used them to build a department, set standards, and earn recognition. But lately, all I hear are words like "automation," "systems," and "data." The younger staff operate things I don't quite understand, and they do it faster than me. I wonder if, one day, I will truly become replaceable.

It’s not a fear of losing my job. I’m only two years away from retirement. What I truly fear is that the things I spent most of my life building have suddenly become unimportant. Those details, that experience, that obsession with quality I took such pride in—do they still have any meaning in this new era?

Xiuying is in the kitchen cooking, and the news is playing on the TV. I’m sitting here, flipping through my notebook, looking at a photo of the department from ten years ago. We were all a bit younger then.

I need to figure this out. Or rather, I need to accept that some things might really be changing.

But my head is still a bit of a mess right now.

I'll pick this up again tomorrow.

Xiuying, I’m here.

I didn't go to play chess today. I told Old Wang and the others I wasn't feeling well. Actually... that’s not entirely it. I was just sitting in the office and suddenly didn't feel like moving. I came back this afternoon and just sat by myself for a while.

Don’t worry, I’m fine. I’ve just been thinking about some things.

You see, I’ve worked at this factory for over thirty years. I’ve always played by the rules, and I take pride in my work when it comes to quality. But now... I’m starting to feel like all this experience might not be worth much anymore. A couple of days ago, Xiao Ming asked me how to move the building materials side of things online. I spent ages giving him advice, only to realize later that they were already doing it, and everything I said was outdated. I’ve been wondering, did my suggestions actually help him? Or was I just holding him back?

And then there’s the company—I heard there’s a reorganization coming. The young people are using all these new systems, while I’m still stuck with the old ways. It’s not that I’m afraid of the hard work, it’s just... I feel like I might really be falling behind.

(Pause)

I haven't watered the plants these past two days, either. I meant to do it today, but I just ended up sitting there.

It’s nothing, really. I’m just a bit stuck in my own head. Don’t you go worrying about me. Your blood pressure...

I’ll be fine tomorrow.

# Retirement Life Plan

**Date: November 28, 2022, 8:00 PM**

I’ve been overthinking things lately, and I can’t help but feel a bit unsettled. I had a chat with Xiuying today, and she said that retirement doesn't necessarily mean everything comes to an end. Thinking about it, she’s right. Instead of worrying blindly all day, I should plan out my post-retirement days properly. This is how I’ve lived my whole life—having a plan is the only way to feel at peace.

## I. Daily Life (Must Maintain)

- **Gardening Records**: Continue tending to the roses and greenery on the balcony. I have over a dozen pots now; I need to organize the logbook more thoroughly.
  - Check growth status once a week.
  - Dates for watering, fertilizing, and pruning must be recorded clearly.
  - Consider buying a few new varieties; I’ll finally have the time to study them properly.

- **Wednesday Chess Games**: The Xiangqi (Chinese Chess) sessions with old colleagues at the community center must not stop.
  - I’ve kept this up for eight years; after retirement, I’ll actually have more time for it.
  - Maybe I can even teach my grandson how to play.

- **Monthly Check-ups with my Wife**: Must continue monitoring Wang Xiuying’s high blood pressure.
  - Note all doctor’s orders on the calendar; not a single one can be missed.
  - This is the most important priority.

## II. Family Time (To be Increased)

- **Grandson, Li Jianhao**: He’s ten now and lives in Shanghai. We don’t spend enough time together.
  - I can visit Shanghai more often after I retire.
  - Teach him chess, tell him stories about the factory.
  - Maybe help him with his phone... (Actually, let’s set that aside for now; I haven't mastered it myself yet).

- **Li Ming’s Two Children**: They live here in town, so we see them during holidays.
  - I'll have more time to spend with them.
  - I can help Li Ming analyze his business; his building materials store is under a lot of pressure, and I might be able to offer some advice.

- **Daughter, Li Xiaoling**: Even though she’s far away in Shanghai, our weekly calls must continue.
  - Aim to visit her more times a year.

## III. Work Transition (Finding New Value)

- **Consulting Work**: The new director taking over my position might need guidance.
  - My inspection standards and experience shouldn't just go to waste.
  - I could continue as a consultant; it would supplement my pension and make me feel useful.
  - No need to rush this; I’ll see how things go after I officially retire.

## IV. Personal Learning (Difficult, but worth a try)

- **Smartphone Usage**: I’m still a bit conflicted about this.
  - Right now, I use the phone to take photos of my flowers, but I still keep a paper log.
  - Doing both in parallel feels more secure.
  - I can learn slowly after retirement; there’s no rush.
  - My daughter says there's no harm in having a "fallback plan," so I’ll just treat the phone as a backup tool.
  - Maybe I can learn how to video call my grandson?

## V. Health and Routine

- Maintain the habit of waking up at 6:30 AM.
- Keep going to the same old breakfast spot; that shop has been there for twenty years.
- Organize the family photo albums; I have over a dozen of them that need proper categorizing.
- Occasionally browse the news and watch history documentaries; I shouldn't lose this habit.

---

**In Summary**: Retirement isn't the end; it’s just continuing in a different way. I was in control of my work, and I should be in control of my life too. Listing all these things out makes me feel much more grounded. Xiuying is right—I still have plenty of things to do.

August 10, 2024  8:30 PM

When Xiao Ling called today, an idea flashed through my mind.

She mentioned that Jianhao has started learning chess recently and asked if I’d have time to teach him. Hearing that, I felt a sudden pang of bitterness. In the past, I definitely would have said, "I'm too busy with work, wait until I retire." Now that I am retired, I actually didn't know how to answer.

But this time was different. I said I could teach him via video call. Even though I’m not great with smartphones, a video call should be manageable.

Xiao Ling paused on the other end of the line, then said, "Dad, this is the first time you’ve ever offered to use video call." I realized then that she was right.

After hanging up, I took out my phone and tried to find the video call function. I used to ignore those little icons on the screen entirely. I barely touched the apps my daughter installed for me. But today was different; I searched for it very earnestly.

After looking for a while, I asked Wang Xiuying, and she pointed it out to me. It wasn't that complicated, actually. I tried opening it, and seeing my own face on the screen made me feel a bit self-conscious. But then I thought about Jianhao sitting in Shanghai while I sit here, and how we could look at the chessboard together while I tell him, "Here’s how you make this move," and I felt...

Never mind, I can't quite put it into words.

I used to think there was no point in learning these new things. I was almost retired, so why bother with the hassle? But now, for the sake of teaching my grandson chess, I think I’m willing to try.

I’ll practice again tomorrow. Maybe I can ask my chess buddies how to use it when we play this Wednesday.

I’m a bit tired, but my heart doesn't feel quite so empty anymore.

# Diary

**Monday, July 1, 2024, Morning**

Xiuying is still asleep. When I got up at 6:30, she was still curled up under the covers. Instead of turning on the news immediately as I usually do, I tiptoed into the living room and took the new blood pressure monitor out of its box.

I remembered everything the doctor said. During the last follow-up, he mentioned that while her hypertension is under decent control, it needs more systematic monitoring. I thought to myself at the time: isn't this just like quality management at the factory? You can't just rely on occasional checks; you need daily, continuous records.

I bought a dedicated notebook at the stationery store—white and very clean. Last night, I used my fountain pen to neatly write "Wang Xiuying’s Health Management Plan – Started July 2024" on the title page. My hand trembled a little as I wrote, but I kept the handwriting tidy. Just like when I used to set inspection standards for my department, this time, it’s for the person who matters most.

At seven o'clock, Xiuying got up. I was ready. As she sat on the sofa, I wrapped the blood pressure cuff around her arm. She smiled and asked, "What are you up to?" I told her, "Per the doctor's orders, we’re measuring once every morning and evening." She didn't object; she just held out her arm.

I was very focused during the measurement. The numbers came up—systolic 138, diastolic 88. I wrote them down in the notebook, noting the time and her state at the moment (just woke up, well-rested). Seeing how serious I was, Xiuying suddenly laughed and said, "You're even stricter than the doctor."

Hearing that gave me a feeling of satisfaction I can't quite describe. It wasn't pride, but... being seen, I suppose. She saw that I was putting my heart into it. All those habits I developed over the years at the factory—being meticulous, earnest, never letting a detail slip—I always thought they were only useful for work. But now I understand that I can use those same traits to take care of her. It’s not a step down; rather, it’s giving the best of myself to the most important person in my life.

I poured her a glass of warm water and reminded her to keep her meals light today. She laughed again and said, "Alright, my Quality Inspector."

I didn't say anything, just continued organizing my notebook. I have to measure again tonight. I’ve already planned it out in my head—twice a day, a weekly summary, and noting any circumstances if there are abnormal fluctuations...

A life like this is actually quite good.

October 5, 2024, Evening

I learned something new again today.

This morning, Xiuying mentioned that the forecast said it would rain tomorrow. It suddenly occurred to me—I can check that myself. I still remembered the steps my daughter taught me last time, so I followed her instructions, one step at a time. I opened the weather app, typed in our city, and there it was: tomorrow's forecast. It really was that simple.

Looking at the screen displaying "Rain tomorrow, 18-24°C," the first person I thought of was Li Jianhao. We’re having a video call this Saturday, and I can ask him how the weather is over in Shanghai. I can remind him to wear a jacket or ask if he needs to bring an umbrella to the park. Before, I had no way of confirming these things when they crossed my mind, but now I can know in advance.

During lunch, I gave Xiuying a demonstration. As she watched me navigate the phone, she suddenly said, "You really are a good grandfather."

I didn't say anything at the time, but the feeling in my heart... it was strange. It wasn't the kind of recognition you get at work, nor was it like being praised. It felt more like... being seen. Being seen doing something right.

Xiuying went to the kitchen after saying that, and I stayed there with the phone, checking the weather one more time. This time wasn't about learning how to do it; I genuinely wanted to know if it would rain. The smartphone has slowly changed from something I was afraid to touch into a tool that helps me get things done. No, I should say it’s become something that helps me take better care of my family.

I remember when I first got this smartphone; I was even afraid to press the buttons too hard. Now I can open apps on my own, check the weather, and video call my grandson. My daughter said I was a fast learner, and I didn't quite believe her then. But now... it seems she was right.

It’s just like when I was doing inspections at the factory. You start off knowing nothing, but as long as you’re willing to take it step by step, remember the procedures, and practice repeatedly, you’ll eventually master it. I used to think I was too old to learn new things. It turns out that's not true. You just need a reason.

When I talk to Jianhao on Saturday, I’m going to tell him, "Grandpa knows how to check the weather now." I wonder if he’ll be happy to hear that.

Xiuying is still in the living room watching TV. I’m sitting here with the phone on my lap. If it rains tomorrow, I must remind her to wear an extra layer.

2024-10-05

Xiuying, take a look at this calendar.

I’ve marked everything for the month. The red ink is for your follow-up appointment—it’s still on the 15th, and I’ve already checked in with the department; it's set for 2:00 PM. The blue ink is for my consulting work; I need to give them feedback on the new system's parameter adjustments by the 8th. The green ink is for my weekly chess games on Wednesdays.

This way, nothing will get mixed up.

You asked if I used to keep track of things like this before? It’s actually different. Back then, work filled up the entire calendar. Now... now there’s a bit of everything in there, but nothing feels particularly overwhelming. The consulting work is just something I pick up occasionally; it’s not like being at the factory where I had to worry every single day.

By the way, regarding that parameter issue the new director asked me about last time—I gave it some more thought yesterday, and there might be another way to approach it. But there's no rush; I'll let them try the current plan first and take another look if they run into problems.

How are you feeling? Any discomfort lately? When we go to the hospital this week, remember to ask the doctor about your last blood pressure readings.

I need to organize those files in a bit, but it won’t take long. Let’s watch the news together tonight.

Xiuying, I just checked and noted down that recipe for steamed fish. I’ll make sure to ask the fishmonger to pick out a fresh one when I go buy it tomorrow. How has your blood pressure been lately? Did you feel any dizziness today?

I was thinking we could go for a stroll downstairs together this weekend; the weather looks quite nice. We don't have to go far—just a slow lap around the neighborhood. The doctor said that moderate activity is good for your blood pressure.

By the way, is that warm water I poured for you still there? Remember to drink it before it gets cold.

I’ll be back early tonight so we can have dinner together.

# Video Call Operating Steps

**Date Recorded: August 15, 2024, Evening**  
**Source: Taught by my daughter, Li Xiaoling**

---

## Process for the First Video Call

**Step 1** Turn on the phone and find the "WeChat" icon at the bottom of the screen (the green one; my daughter marked it for me).

**Step 2** Tap into it and look for "Contacts"—it should be in that bottom row.

**Step 3** Find "Li Jianhao" in the contacts (my eldest grandson; my daughter has already added him).

**Step 4** Tap his name. A screen will appear with a "Video Call" button—it looks like a camera.

**Step 5** Press it and wait a moment. Once Jianhao answers, you will be able to see him.

**Step 6** Just start talking. Remember to look at the camera at the top of the screen.

---

## What Might Go Wrong

- If I can't find WeChat: Ask Wang Xiuying or look at the photo my daughter sent again.
- If the call doesn't go through: Don't worry, try again. He might be busy.
- If I can't see his face: Check if the camera at the top is covered.
- If the sound is too low: Press the volume button.

---

## Points to Note

✓ Check the battery before calling (should be above 50%).  
✓ Find a well-lit spot so Jianhao can see me clearly.  
✓ Don't tap the screen randomly; it's easy to hang up by mistake.  
✓ If something goes wrong, take a deep breath and try again.

---

## Next Practice Plan

**First real call with Jianhao: This weekend (August 17)**  
Practice one more time with my daughter first to make sure everything is okay.

**Goal:** Successfully see my grandson's face and chat for more than 5 minutes.

---

*These steps are just like the quality inspections I used to do at the factory—follow them one by one, don't skip any, and nothing will go wrong.*

November 18, 2022   Sunny

I woke up at 6:30 this morning, watched the news for ten minutes, and had breakfast. Xiuying was still asleep, so I didn't disturb her.

Yesterday’s events were still on my mind. First thing this morning, I thought about that phone again.

The Chinese roses on the balcony have sprouted new buds, and I wanted to take a photo to document it. In the past, I would just use my notebook—recording the date, the amount of water, and the condition of the leaves in clear detail. But this time, I didn't reach for the notebook first. Instead... I picked up the phone.

I felt a bit nervous. I pressed it twice, but it wouldn't focus. I pressed the wrong button. I paused for a moment. I didn't get frustrated like I did a few days ago; I just calmly adjusted the angle and tried again. This time, it worked.

After taking the photo, I opened the "Notes" app—the feature my daughter taught me. I typed it out, character by character: "November 18, 2022. New rose buds. Watered 200ml." I typed slowly, but I didn't make any mistakes. Then, I took out my notebook and wrote the same thing down with my pen.

When I finished all this, I felt... how should I put it? It wasn't frustration. On the contrary, I felt a sense of satisfaction.

I’ve been thinking that maybe I was being too hard on myself before. I don't have to use a phone exactly like young people do, but I don't have to give up on it entirely either. Using both methods together isn't a bad thing. The digital note is a backup; the notebook is the formal record. This way, I feel at peace.

Xiuying got up and saw me on the balcony with my phone. She asked what I was doing. I showed her the photo of the roses. She smiled and said, "See, you’re getting the hang of it." I didn't say much, but I smiled too.

This smile felt different from how I felt yesterday.

Maybe I’m not as incompetent as I thought. Maybe I just... need to find my own rhythm. It’s not a black-and-white choice; it’s about adapting slowly, one step at a time.

I’m playing chess with my friends this afternoon. I remember last time they were talking about which apps their grandkids use. Now I can actually join the conversation a little. Not much, but... at least I’m not completely lost anymore.

It’s a good feeling.

July 5, 2024, 8:45 PM

I just got off the phone with Xiao Ming. He mentioned that business has been a bit stressful lately—the competition in the building materials market is indeed fierce. I walked him through a few strategies, and he listened very intently. At the end, he said, "Dad, you’re really meticulous." I didn't say much at the time, just gave a brief "mm-hmm," but inside... I had a feeling I can't quite put into words.

That sentence stayed with me for a long time.

Back at the factory, my superiors used to say I was "diligent," and my subordinates would say I was "exacting." Back then, I was the Director of Quality Inspection; I had a title, authority, and a whole system of standards. And now? I’m retired. I no longer have that identity. But when Xiao Ming said, "Dad, you’re really meticulous," I suddenly realized—I’m still doing the same thing.

Only the subject has changed.

I used to set standards for factory products; now, I’m setting standards for Xiuying’s blood pressure management. The day before yesterday, I bought a more precise monitor and created a log for her, recording everything by date, time, and value. When my son heard me talk about this over the phone, I heard something in his voice... was it relief? Or respect? I can’t quite tell.

I told him, "Don't worry, your mother and I are doing great. Just focus on your business." After saying that, I suddenly felt a new weight on my shoulders. It wasn't the pressure of work, but... the weight of family.

It’s a bit strange. It took me thirty-seven years to grow into the identity of "Director of Quality Inspection," yet it’s only taken a few months to find my footing as a "caregiver." I’ve even started using professional jargon without realizing it—telling Xiuying we need to "systematically manage her health." She teased me, saying, "You’re stricter than the doctor." But it didn't feel wrong; instead, it felt... exactly like what I should be doing.

Maybe this isn't about losing something. Maybe it’s a transformation.

I’ll check Xiuying’s blood pressure again tomorrow. Her readings have stabilized a bit this week.

— Li Jianguo

# Rose Care Log - September 26

**Date:** September 26, 2024  
**Weather:** Overcast, approx. 22°C  
**Observation Time:** Around 6:50 AM  

---

## Pot #3 (Pink, near the window)

**Current Status:** Yellowing leaves observed, primarily concentrated on the bottom 3–4 leaves. I didn't notice this during yesterday's watering; just spotted it this morning.

**Diagnostic Analysis:**  
Comparing this with the records from the past two weeks, there might be an issue with the watering frequency lately. The weather has been cooling down, so transpiration rates should be dropping, yet I’ve been sticking to my summer watering routine. It looks like overwatering has led to waterlogging at the roots. Given the yellowing leaves and no signs of pests, I’m fairly certain it’s overwatering.

**Adjustment Plan:**  
- Suspend watering for 3–4 days to let the soil dry out completely.
- Check if the drainage holes at the bottom of the pot are clear.
- Switch to a weekly watering schedule; decide on future watering based on how dry the soil surface feels.
- Maintain good ventilation to speed up moisture evaporation.

**Follow-up:** Re-examine leaf condition in 5 days.

---

**Notes:** Xiuying says I’m more meticulous with my flowers than I am with her (lol). Honestly, I just believe in doing things right, no matter what it is. Since I’ve brought these flowers into our home, I ought to take them seriously.

It was 9:30 PM, and the only sound in the living room was the hum of the television. Li Jianguo sat on the sofa, clutching his phone, the glow of the screen illuminating his tired face. Wang Xiuying walked out of the kitchen and handed him a glass of warm water.

He looked up at his wife, hesitated for a moment, and finally spoke.

"Xiuying, sit down for a bit. I want to tell you something."

Wang Xiuying sat down beside him and turned off the TV.

"Xiao Ming called me today. He said he wants to transition the business online and change the supply chain model." Li Jianguo paused, his voice dropping lower. "After I heard him out, I just told him, 'You decide for yourself; I trust you.'"

He paused for a long time, his gaze fixed on the teacup.

"But the truth is, I just didn't know what to say. I wanted to give him some advice, but... I realized I don't understand any of it. 'Online transition,' 'supply chain'—I’ve heard these terms, but I don't really get them."

Wang Xiuying didn't answer immediately; she just listened quietly.

"I worked in the factory for over thirty years, from an operator to a manager. My control over quality, my obsession with detail—those are things I’m sure of. But now..." There was an indescribable weariness in his voice. "Is my advice even useful to Xiao Ming? Can I even help him?"

He turned to his wife, a sense of uncertain pleading in his eyes.

"I’m thinking that maybe my experience really doesn't matter to them anymore. Maybe I really have fallen behind the times."

After a few seconds, he added one more thing, his voice even softer:

"Xiuying, tell me... am I really getting old?"

# Phone Memo

**Date: August 8, 2023, Afternoon**

---

## Adjustment Plan for Batch A3 Defects

Just finished discussing the system alerts with Xiao Wang. Regarding the batch inspected this morning, the system flagged them as "surface scratches," but I’ve examined the physical units—those aren't scratches; they are textures left by mold wear. We’ve seen this issue before.

**Adjustments Needed:**
- The system’s surface roughness parameters might be set too sensitively.
- Suggest adjusting the threshold from 0.8 to 1.2 to see how it performs.
- At the same time, we need to add a "texture directionality" logic; not all textures are defects.

**To-Do List:**
1. Have Xiao Wang adjust the parameters tomorrow; I will verify the results.
2. Document this case—similar issues occurred in 1987 and 2003, which shows this is a recurring pattern of mold aging.
3. We need to build a "Historical Defect Library" so the new system can truly learn from experience rather than just crunching numbers.

**Thoughts:**
It’s not that the system is bad; it just needs our experience to help it get smarter. If this system can fully digest the logic I’ve used over the years, the department will be able to maintain its standards even after I retire.

I need to have a good talk with Xiao Wang about this approach next week.

---

*Check the A3 samples one last time before heading out.*

Xiuying, I’m back. I went to the community center to play some chess for a while tonight.

I lost the first game; my mind was a bit elsewhere. But I found my rhythm in the second and managed to pull off a win. Old Wang and the others were all there. They asked how work’s been lately, and I just told them it’s alright—the company is going through some reforms, so I’m just taking my time to adapt.

They all joked that since I hadn't shown up for two weeks, they thought something had happened to me. I just said I’d been busy with work but finally managed to carve out some time this week. Actually… it’s nothing, really; I’ve just had a lot on my mind lately.

But playing chess today made me feel a bit better. It’s still the same group of people, the same chessboard, and the same old rules after all these years. It felt a bit like getting back to a normal life.

Go to sleep early. I’ll head to bed after I wash up. I have to get up early tomorrow.

Wednesday, June 19, 2024
Sunny

3:30 PM. Just got back from the community activity room.

Old Wang asked me why I was back so late today, and only then did I realize the afternoon had already slipped away. I spent the whole morning playing chess with a few friends—lost two games, won one. Uncle Zhang said I looked "much better than last week, more full of life." I laughed then—not a forced smile, but a genuine laugh.

They asked what I’d been up to lately, so I told them about how well the Chinese roses are blooming. I also mentioned my video call with Jianhao last week—the kid learned how to play chess in Shanghai. He’s not very good yet, but the way he sits there scowling, trying to figure out his next move, really is the spitting image of me. I even told them about my younger son’s building materials business; things have picked up lately, and he’s thinking about expanding the storefront.

As I was talking, I realized my tone had completely changed. It wasn’t that nostalgic "this is what I used to do" feeling, but rather a sense of "this is what’s happening now."

Old Wang said something that I’ve been thinking about ever since. He said, "Retirement isn't the end of life; it’s just the beginning of another kind of life." I didn't say anything at the time, just nodded. But sitting here at home now, I suddenly understand.

For all these years, I tied myself to the identity of "Director of the Quality Inspection Department." I thought that without that title, I was nothing. But in reality... I’m still a father, a grandfather, and a husband. I’m still tending to my flowers, playing chess, and giving my son advice. These things were always there; I just hadn’t taken a proper look at them before.

I’m thinking that maybe I had it wrong from the start. It wasn't the work that gave me value; I have value simply as a person. It’s just that before, this value was so filled up by work that I didn't notice anything else. Now that space has opened up, I can see clearly—there was so much more all along.

Xiuying poured me a cup of tea and asked why I was sitting there dazed. I told her it was nothing, just that I felt... a little different. She gave me a look but didn't push further; she just sat down beside me.

I thought about that department photo from ten years ago hanging on the office wall. I used to look at it all the time, as if I were trying to confirm something. Now, I don't feel the need to look at it so often.

Tomorrow, I have to take Xiuying to the hospital for her follow-up. The day after, I might go with my younger son to see his new storefront. Jianhao might video call again this weekend. Next Wednesday, more chess.

Life goes on like this. Only now, I think I finally get it—this is what life is.

- **Daily Potassium Intake:** 3,000–4,000mg

## Cooking Techniques & Tips

1.  **Prioritize High-Potassium Ingredients:** Bananas, potatoes, red beans, black beans, spinach, winter melon.
2.  **Flavoring Alternatives:** Use lemon juice, vinegar, ginger, garlic, and scallions to replace salt.
3.  **Meat Preparation:**
    *   **Strictly Prohibited:** Luncheon meat, salted fish, preserved sausages.
    *   Soak fresh meat in water for 30 minutes before cooking.
    *   Prioritize fish and lean cuts of meat.
4.  **Soup Preparation:** Do not use bouillon cubes. Use fresh ingredients for broth and add salt only at the very end.
