# Diary

September 25, 2024 | Sunny

This afternoon, I was playing chess with a few old friends in the activity room. Master Li noticed me taking out my flower care log and asked about it. I flipped through the pages for him, talking about how the roses have been growing lately and the right way to water pothos so the roots don't rot.

He watched for a while, then suddenly said, "Old Li, you’ve changed."

I didn't catch his meaning at first. He continued, "Back in the day, you used to talk to us about quality inspection—it was all about standards, processes, and management. Now, when you talk about flowers, even your vocabulary is different."

It was only then that I realized I had indeed been using the word "companionship." I wasn't "managing" the flowers, nor was I "maintaining" them; I was keeping them company. It’s just like accompanying Xiuying to her hospital check-ups or sitting with my grandson while he learns to play chess.

"It’s a good change," Master Li said, patting me on the shoulder.

When I got home tonight, Xiuying asked me what was on my mind. I told her it was nothing, just a thought I had.

In truth, I was thinking about those thirty-seven years at the factory. I spent them chasing perfection, standards, and keeping everything under control. Back then, I thought that was what it meant to be "valuable." And now? Now, I record the growth of my flowers not to meet some standard, but simply to watch them bloom bit by bit. It’s not that I’ve lowered my standards; it’s just that my definition of "doing a job well" has changed.

Last time, my grandson asked me why I record the watering dates so meticulously. I thought about it for a moment and told him, "Because that’s the only way to truly understand them and keep them company." He nodded, as if he understood.

Perhaps this is the true meaning of retirement. It’s not about losing anything, but finally having the time to provide companionship—to Xiuying, to my grandson, and to those roses.

Writing these thoughts down makes me feel more grounded.

Master Li was right; I have changed. But this time, I’m not afraid of the change.

March 15, 2023, 7:00 PM

When Xiuying held my hand, my eyes welled up a bit. I didn't let her see.

I called my chess partner this afternoon and told him work was too busy this week, so I wouldn't be coming for our match. As the words left my mouth, even I could hear how forced my voice sounded. He tried to convince me, saying there’s always more work to do but chess shouldn't be missed. I declined anyway. For eight years, I haven't missed a single session. And just like that, I broke the streak.

After hanging up, I sat in my office staring at a department photo on the wall from ten years ago. I looked so much more energetic back then. And now? What am I even doing?

Lately, my head has been filled with nothing but that automated inspection system. I’ve taken the initiative to learn its specs, read the technical manuals, and ask the younger staff questions. But the more I learn, the more uneasy I feel. I don't quite understand that stuff; it feels hollow, lacking any real substance. I trust things I can see and touch—my eyes, my experience, the judgment I’ve built up over thirty-odd years. But it seems like no one needs that anymore.

The company says they’re bringing in the new system. I’ve heard there might be a "structural reorganization." Maybe I really can't keep up. That thought has been circling in my head over and over.

Xiuying asked me what was wrong. I told her it was nothing, just that work has been complicated lately. She didn't push; she just held my hand. In that moment, I felt a profound sense of powerlessness. It wasn't because of work—it was because I realized I’ve truly grown old. What am I afraid of? Being replaced by a machine? Having no value after retirement? Or the fact that I’m genuinely falling behind the times?

I’m only two years away from retirement. I used to have it all figured out—gardening, playing chess, spending time with my grandson. Those plans seem a bit flimsy now. I wonder if I should re-evaluate. Maybe I could do some consulting after I retire, use my experience to help the new director. But as soon as I think that, I ask myself: Is my experience even worth anything anymore?

I had no appetite for dinner. Xiuying poured me a bowl of soup, and I managed a few sips. She’s watching TV, and I’m sitting here wondering: if the day comes when I’m truly obsolete, what will I do? I’ve never thought this way before.

I need a good night’s sleep. I have to take Xiuying to the hospital for her follow-up tomorrow.

But I know I probably won't sleep well.

Thursday, September 5, 2024  Sunny

I got a call this morning from my old company. It was the new Quality Control Director. He said they’ve run into an issue with a specific batch and wanted to get my take on it.

To be honest, I hesitated. I’ve been retired for so long now that being consulted like this felt a bit flattering, if I’m being truthful. But when he said, "We really need your input right now," I agreed.

I spent an hour walking him through the problem. I broke it down step by step—from raw material inspection and processing workflows to the final product standards. He was taking notes the whole time on the other end, constantly asking for details. It felt just like being back in the office; I made sure to cover every angle for him.

After hanging up, I sat on the balcony for a long while. Xiuying was in the kitchen cooking, and people were strolling downstairs. It was a very ordinary afternoon.

But I noticed my mood had shifted. It wasn't that smug "look, I'm still useful" feeling, nor was I trying to prove anything. It was... how should I put it... a sense of groundedness. Knowing that the expertise I’ve gathered over all these years can still help someone. It wasn't for myself; I just genuinely wanted to see the problem solved.

It’s a strange feeling. Back when I was in the office, I dealt with these things every day, but I don't think I ever felt it quite this clearly. Back then, it was about responsibility, standards, and getting things right. Now... it just feels like wanting to help.

I looked at the roses on the balcony; the new buds have grown a bit larger. I noted down today’s date and the amount of water I used in my logbook. Just like I’m recording this afternoon.

Perhaps I’ve been constantly asking myself, "What can I still do after retirement?" and the answer doesn't lie in the work itself. It’s... the feeling of being needed, I suppose. But this kind of "being needed" isn't about proving I’ve still "got it"—it’s because someone truly needs a hand.

Thinking about it this way, the emptiness in my heart doesn't feel quite so vast.

# Tuesday, October 18, 2022 – Sunny

I spaced out in the office again this morning. Director Zhang came in with the lab reports to ask for my instructions, and I actually didn't catch a word he said. I even had to ask him to repeat himself. He looked a bit awkward, and realizing my attitude was off, I quickly apologized. This isn't like me.

To be honest, I’ve been distracted ever since we came back from the hospital follow-up last week. Xiuying’s blood pressure fluctuated quite a bit this time; her systolic hit 150. The doctor said it might be the recent change in weather, or perhaps she’s had too much on her mind lately. They adjusted her dosage and told us to be even more careful with her diet. I’ve noted all the doctor’s orders on the calendar, but I still feel a weight in my chest.

I used to think this was something within my control—take the medicine on time, eat less salt, go for regular check-ups, and everything would be fine. But this fluctuation has left me feeling a bit panicked. I’ve started wondering: what if it keeps getting worse? What if something happens to her before I retire? I’m only two years away from retirement; I thought these last two years would just be about handing over my work and waiting to collect my pension. Now, it seems it won’t be that simple.

At noon, I sat in my office looking at a department photo from ten years ago hanging on the wall. Everyone was so young back then, and these things never even crossed my mind. Looking at it now, I realize how fast time flies. In two years, I’ll be leaving this place where I’ve worked for thirty-seven years. And with Xiuying’s health... I don’t know how to describe this feeling. It’s not exactly fear, but just a sense that things are slipping out of my control.

I’ve decided that starting tomorrow, I’ll take Xiuying’s blood pressure every day and record it in a notebook, just like I track when I water the plants. That way, at least I’ll have a handle on her condition instead of just passively waiting for a doctor’s diagnosis. I also need to run the numbers to see if our savings will be enough if she needs more medical care. Once I retire, there won't be a monthly salary anymore; I have to think ahead.

Maybe I should call Li Ming tomorrow and ask how his business is going. And Xiaoling in Shanghai—I need to find time to visit her. These things seem more important than work now. Still, I have to do my job well; I can’t let my personal life affect the department’s work.

Tomorrow, I need to have a real talk with Xiuying and see if something has been bothering her lately. She’s sometimes reluctant to speak up, but I have to find a way to help her relax.

Xiuying, let me tell you...

The IT department gave us training on the new inspection system today. I was a bit scared at first, worried I wouldn't be able to pick it up. But... I listened carefully anyway, followed their steps, and even asked a few questions.

The young guy from IT was actually quite nice; he explained the logic behind the system to me. It got me thinking about whether we could combine our old quality control methods with this new system. So, I shared my ideas with him, and they seemed to think it was a solid suggestion.

I told them it might take me a while to fully master it, but I’m confident I can learn.

Xiuying, I feel like... maybe there’s still something I can contribute.

It’s not just about being useful; it’s just that... I don't feel so hopeless anymore.

What do you think?

But I’ve realized that I don’t need to be in a rush to define it—it is simply whatever it is in the moment. When the flowers are in full bloom, it’s a rose in bloom; when the petals fall, it’s still that same rose.

I feel like I’ve been constantly asking myself who I am now that I’m retired. But maybe... an identity isn't something you have to hurry to find.

Sunday, October 23, 2022. Sunny.

I accompanied Xiuying to the hospital for her follow-up yesterday. The doctor said her blood pressure is fluctuating again—150 systolic and 95 diastolic. I was a bit stunned in that moment because it was clearly in the 140s last month. The doctor added a new medication and said we need to observe the results.

I didn't say much on the drive home. Xiuying asked if I was angry; I said no, I was just thinking. In truth, I didn't know what to think.

After getting home, I did something I’ve never done before. I opened my computer and created an Excel spreadsheet for Xiuying to monitor her blood pressure. Date, time, systolic, diastolic, medication, diet... I labeled every column clearly. It was just like doing quality management at the company; not a single data point could be missed.

Then I put up sticky notes in the kitchen, bedroom, and living room. Reminders for medication times, reminders to avoid salty food. I also ordered a more precise blood pressure monitor online, one that claims to be accurate down to the single digits.

Seeing me like this, Xiuying smiled and said, "You're bringing your work home with you." Her tone was gentle, but I could detect a hint of helplessness.

I didn't answer. Because I realized it too—I am treating her body the same way I treat a production process. Just as I do at the factory, I am trying to foresee, record, and control every possible problem. But this time, I know deep down that this method might not work.

There are some things I simply cannot control.

This realization feels very foreign to me. For thirty-seven years, I’ve been used to mitigating risks through meticulous management. In my professional life, this has always been effective. But Xiuying’s blood pressure doesn't follow my plans. The medicine works, but not completely. Her diet is controlled, yet it still fluctuates.

Sitting at my desk, looking at that Excel sheet and those sticky notes, I suddenly felt a sense of powerlessness. It’s as if I’m trying to use a ruler to measure something irregular.

I’m retiring next year. I’ve been thinking about what to do afterward—maybe taking on some consulting work or spending more time with my grandson. But now, I think more about how to take good care of Xiuying. It’s not that her condition is severe right now; the doctor said it’s actually under decent control. But this fluctuation made me realize that the future may hold many more things I cannot predict.

I need to prepare for this "unpredictable." But I don't know how.

There are still many blank pages in this notebook. I want to keep writing, but my mind is a bit of a mess right now. Maybe things will be clearer tomorrow.

Or maybe, they will never be clear.

# Plan for Learning the New System

**Date: March 26, 2023, Evening**

It’s decided—I’m going to master this new system. I can’t keep going on like this.

## Specific Arrangements

**Phase 1 (This Week)**
- Monday to Friday: Stay for half an hour every day after work.
- Have Xiao Wang (the new young guy) walk me through the basic logic of the system.
- Focus on: How to input data, how to generate reports, and how to look up history.
- Write down the steps in my notebook and take it one step at a time.

**Phase 2 (Starting Next Week)**
- Get some hands-on practice, starting with simple inspection reports.
- Note down any questions immediately and ask them the next day.
- Aim to be able to handle basic data entry independently within a week.

**Phase 3 (In Two Weeks)**
- Try using the new system for daily departmental tasks.
- Compare the traditional methods with the new system to see where efficiency can be improved.
- If problems arise, find ways to fix them.

## Potential Difficulties

- I’m getting older and I learn new things slowly—but I can’t give up; I need to be more patient with myself.
- I might make mistakes—that’s okay, just keep practicing; I still have time for now.
- I might get frustrated sometimes—when that happens, I’ll stop for a bit, have some tea, and continue once I’ve cooled down.

## Help Needed

- I’ll have to rely on Xiao Wang quite a bit. He’s young and quick; I must be humble and learn from him.
- Xiu-ying mentioned I could ask her daughter for help (she’s in finance, so she should understand these systems).
- If all else fails, I’ll check the instruction manual on my phone.

## Others

- Stick with it; no giving up halfway.
- Treat this as a new challenge, just like when I learned Quality Management back in the day.
- Once I’ve mastered it, I might even be able to help the new Director with some questions.

**Keep going. Two years to go—I’m not giving in.**

# Diary

August 25, 2024  Sunny

This afternoon, while playing chess at the community activity room, I took the initiative to pull out my phone and show it to Old Wang and the others. To be honest, I felt a bit self-conscious at the time, but more than that... how should I put it? I felt a sense of pride.

I showed them how to start a video call and then called Jianhao. My grandson’s face appeared on the screen; he was having dinner over in Shanghai, and he grinned the moment he saw me. I could hear him clearly saying, "Hi, Grandpa," and I could see every movement. Old Wang and the guys all crowded around to watch, teasing me, "Old Li, look at you, teaching an old dog new tricks!"

I just laughed and told them it’s exactly like doing inspections back at the factory—as long as you take it step by step, there’s no problem. I remember very clearly when my daughter taught me: step one, click here; step two, click there. It’s just like following an inspection protocol. As long as you don't rush and stay meticulous, you can figure out even the most complicated things.

I won two games of chess today, which hasn't happened much lately. Sitting on the bus home, I found myself thinking: maybe... retirement isn't such a bad thing after all?

When I first retired a while back, I felt a constant sense of loss. Thirty-seven years of work suddenly vanished, and all those forms and reports in the office became someone else's business. Sometimes I’d pull out that ten-year-old department photo from my office, and looking at it would make me feel a bit miserable. I felt like I had suddenly become useless.

But things are different now. Now, I can talk to Jianhao every week; I can see him and hear his voice. This feeling... it’s more fulfilling than completing a difficult inspection task back at the factory. Xiuying says my spirits have been much better lately, and it’s true. I have things to do now—learning how to use my phone, chatting with my grandson, and tending to my Chinese roses.

I called my daughter tonight to thank her for being so patient while teaching me. She said, "Dad, you're learning much faster than I expected." I smiled when I heard that. It’s not really that I’m a fast learner; it’s just that... I know how important this is to me, so I take it seriously. It’s as simple as that.

Tomorrow, I’ll keep at it. Next Wednesday, when I play chess with the guys again, I want to show them one more time. I probably won't feel as shy about it then.

Maybe I should start logging the times I talk to Jianhao on my phone, just like I record the dates I water the flowers. That way, my retired life will have a sense of order again.

Xiao Ming, I’ve been thinking over what you said the other day.

You’re right. I shouldn't be taking everything upon myself. I’ve been overthinking things lately and making the situation with your mother way too complicated. From now on, I’ll just follow the doctor's orders and support whatever your mother does. I don’t need to be obsessing over those numbers every single day.

Yesterday, I let your mother have a salted duck egg. She was so happy—she said that’s the Li Jianguo she knows. Hearing that made me relax a lot, too. Sometimes, being too tense just isn't the answer.

This whole thing has gotten me thinking about how I want to live after I retire. I can’t keep trying to micromanage every little thing. There’s more to life than this, isn't there? How’s the business going over there? Are you free to grab a meal together this week?

Xiuying, I went over to the factory this morning.

You know, walking through those familiar gates, I could still smell that scent of machine oil. Thirty-seven years... it’s like that smell is etched into my very bones. The production workshop is as noisy as ever, with the roar of the machines coming at you in waves. When I was young, I could stand there all day and not feel a bit of fatigue, but today, after listening to it for just a while, I found it a bit much to take.

The new director gave me a call saying there was an issue with the quality inspection system, so I thought I’d go take a look. When I walked into the office, guess what? That inspection standard sheet I developed all those years ago was still posted on the wall. After all this time, they’re still using it. Seeing that sheet... I had a feeling I can’t quite put into words. It wasn’t regret about leaving, but more like... a sense that something I created is still there, still serving a purpose.

I showed them where the problem was. It wasn’t actually that complicated; the new system’s detection mechanism just wasn't sensitive enough to certain subtle deviations. I proposed three solutions: first, adjusting the parameter sensitivity; second, adding a manual review step into the workflow; and third, performing periodic spot checks using the old methods for comparison. They listened very intently.

When the new director thanked me at the end, I just replied, "It’s the least I could do." As I said it, I suddenly realized—I wasn't speaking from my old position as director anymore; I truly meant it. That sense of distance was gone.

Xiuying, they asked if I could continue doing this kind of consulting. Not going back to work full-time, just offering advice when problems come up. They’d pay a consulting fee, too. I’ve been thinking—this wouldn't disrupt our current life, and it would help supplement our pension. You know, with your blood pressure check-ups and the grandkids' expenses and all...

But I want to hear what you think. Do you think this is a good idea? Or do you think I’ll end up getting tied down by something again? I really like the pace we have now, and I don’t want to ruin that. But if it’s just helping out once in a while, it seems like it might be okay.

What do you think?

September 28, 2024, around 7:00 PM

I was out on the balcony watering the roses when Xiaoling called today. She told me Jianhao won an award at school—third place in a math competition. My eyes welled up the moment I heard it; I couldn’t help myself. I managed to tell her, "Jianhao is amazing, Grandpa is so proud of you," but my voice was a bit choked up.

Xiaoling said Jianhao wanted to video call me. I got a little nervous because I’m still not great with this phone. Xiuying helped me turn on the camera, and there he was. The boy was in his school uniform, smiling so bright, holding up his certificate for me to see. I could see it perfectly clearly, even though the screen is a bit small.

He asked me when I’m coming to Shanghai to see him. I told him I’d go as soon as I have time. What I was really thinking, though, was that maybe I can go more often once I retire.

During the call, Jianhao taught me how to take photos with the phone. I kept pressing the shutter button, but all I got were pictures of the ceiling. Xiuying was laughing at me from the side. Eventually, I finally managed to snap a photo of him with his certificate. Instead of complaining that it was "too much trouble" like I usually do, I just smiled and told him, "Grandpa is learning, don’t you worry."

After we hung up, I used that "Notes" app on the phone to write down: "Jianhao won an award at school; remember to get him a gift." Xiuying looked a bit surprised to see me fiddling with the phone. To be honest, I’m a little surprised at myself, too.

Sitting on the balcony later, I found myself thinking—maybe all these years I’ve been asking myself "what else can I do," and the answer is finally starting to surface. It’s not in the office; it’s right here. Being there for Jianhao, teaching him things, remembering every moment of his life. These little things seem to matter much more than I thought.

I’m still a bit clumsy, and I still press the wrong buttons on the phone. But Jianhao doesn’t mind. And Xiuying doesn’t mind either.

Maybe I should keep learning these new things. Not to keep up with the times, but so I can be there for them in a better way.

I need to fertilize those roses tomorrow. They’ve been growing quite well lately.

October 16, 2024  Sunny

I just hung up the phone with the new Director a moment ago, and I’m still a bit dazed.

Sitting here in the office, I found myself staring at that department group photo on the wall from ten years ago. All those young faces, those bright smiles... some left long ago, others are still here. I pointed it out to Xiuying, and when she asked what was on my mind, I told her, "The company still needs my input." The moment the words left my mouth, I even surprised myself.

It’s not about proving anything. It really isn't.

All these years, I’ve been wondering what I’d do after retirement. How I’d spend my days. Taking care of Xiuying, playing with my grandson, chess, watering the plants... those are all wonderful, but it always felt like something was missing. That feeling of being needed. Not the "positional" kind, but... the kind where someone asks for your opinion, listens to you, and feels that your experience still carries weight.

The new Director said, "The standards you established are still the strictest in the industry, and we want to uphold them." I’ve replayed that sentence in my head several times now. They haven't forgotten. The rules I set, the things I stood for—they’re still there, still working. They’re still ensuring the quality of our products.

I was hesitant at first. Going back to the office after retirement, worrying about these things again... would Xiuying mind? What about the video calls with my grandson?

But after thinking it over, I was the one who suggested meeting at the office face-to-face. I told them I could make the time. As soon as I said it, I knew I truly wanted to go. It’s not just about being needed passively; it’s about wanting to contribute actively. It feels different.

Thirty-seven years. From an operator to a Director, every step was built on an obsession with detail and a commitment to quality. Those things don’t just vanish because I’ve retired. Now, I can pass them on in a different way—not through a title, but through consulting, through advice, through the passing down of knowledge.

When I teach my grandson chess, I’m teaching him rules and tactics. When I take Xiuying to the hospital, I’m learning how to care for someone’s health. And now, I can teach the new Director what I’ve learned at the factory.

This isn't retirement. This is just continuing in a different way.

I still need to call Jianhao tonight. That kid was asking me about chess again. I need to figure out how to explain the "knight's move" in a way he’ll actually understand.

There’s still so much to be done in life.

# MEMORANDUM

**Date: Wednesday, July 24, 2024**

**Subject: Reflections and Adjustments to Xiuying’s Health Management Plan**

---

I was chatting with my chess buddies today about Xiuying’s blood pressure management. After I finished explaining the whole routine—the walks, the diet, the measurements—Old Zhang said something that stuck with me all afternoon. He said, "You’re managing a product, not taking care of a person."

I didn't say much at the time, but I kept thinking about it on the way home. He’s right.

**Current Practices (To be maintained):**
- Increase walking frequency to 4-5 times a week, roughly 30 minutes each time.
- Dietary adjustments: Low salt, light meals, and regular eating times.
- Continue keeping the blood pressure logs.

These are all correct. However, the problem lies elsewhere:

**Areas for Improvement:**

1. **Frequency of Blood Pressure Checks** — My original plan was three times a day. Thinking back, this might be putting too much pressure on Xiuying. I’ll change it to once in the morning and once in the evening, with extra checks only if she feels unwell. I need to give her more autonomy rather than me dictating exactly when she must be tested.

2. **Listening to Her Thoughts** — This is the most important part. I’ve been so focused on executing the plan that I haven't truly asked Xiuying what she wants. Does she really want to walk that many times a week? Are the dietary changes making her uncomfortable? I need to talk to her about these things regularly—not as a notification, but as a discussion.

3. **Daily Rituals** — It shouldn't just be "management"; it needs warmth. For example, we should talk more during our walks instead of me timing them in my head. We should look at the roses in the garden together, rather than just walking for the sake of exercise.

**Next Steps:**
- Sit down with Xiuying this weekend to go over the plan again and hear her thoughts.
- Change the blood pressure log from "Mandatory" to "Recommended."
- Keep playing chess on Wednesdays, but stop talking about health management like it’s a work report.

Old Zhang is right. The habits I picked up from thirty years at the factory are good, but not everything requires such rigid standards. Xiuying isn't a product; she’s my wife.

---

**Note:** Continue recording the watering schedule for the roses as usual; that part is fine.

March 22, 2023 – Past 11:00 PM

Can't sleep. Got up and flipped through my flower-pressing journal, only to realize I didn't record last Wednesday's chess club meeting. That was my first time missing it in eight years. Looking back at the previous pages, they’re filled with dates, weather notes, and little scribbles like "Old Zhang won three games" or "Played an endgame." Looking at those entries now, they feel a bit foreign.

Today’s training... I’m still processing it.

The young people next to me could grasp it all with a few keystrokes, while I just sat there thinking—this is it, I’m truly old, I really can't keep up anymore.

But...

When the trainer got to "System Inspection Accuracy Verification," I don't know where the nerve came from, but I asked: "Can this system adjust inspection standards based on different product characteristics, just like manual inspection does?"

I thought it was a stupid question. But the trainer paused and said, "That’s a great question. It shows you’re thinking from a quality management perspective, not just a technical one."

That moment... I don't know how to describe it. It was like someone suddenly told me I wasn't obsolete.

Later, during the break, another idea came to me—how to combine traditional inspection experience with the new system. I just mentioned it in passing, but a young employee and the trainer both seemed interested. They asked if I could elaborate.

So I did. I stumbled over my words a bit, but they were listening. They were actually listening.

Xiuying asked me how today went, and I told her it was okay. But honestly, I’m still thinking about that feeling from this afternoon—the feeling of being needed. It wasn't just "I can still do inspections," but rather "my experience still has value." It feels different.

Training continues tomorrow. I want to hear more of what they have to say.

Maybe... I’m not completely over the hill yet.

Maybe there’s still a chance.

Li Ming mentioned yesterday that he might come back this weekend; he has some business matters he wants to discuss with me. The building materials sector is indeed a tough market right now.

As for my grandson... during the last video call, he was busy with a mobile game, and I couldn't quite catch what he was saying. I was thinking, now that I’m retired, maybe I should learn more about these things so it’s not such a struggle to talk to the kids.

Sitting here now with nothing to do.

Xiuying is tidying up the kitchen. I should go help her.

When Xiaoling called, I was on the balcony watering the Chinese roses. She mentioned how busy work has been lately and that Jianhao is still settling into his new class at school. As I listened, it suddenly hit me—this time next year, I’ll be retiring.

After hanging up, I sat on a small stool on the balcony, just watching the flowers. Some of the buds were already starting to show color. I took out my phone and, following the steps my daughter taught me, snapped a photo of them. The flowers looked quite clear on the screen.

I thought to myself: instead of waiting until retirement to worry about how to spend my days, I might as well plan it out now. Jianhao is ten this year; in two or three years, he’ll hit puberty, and he might not be as easy to bond with then. I asked Xiaoling if it would be possible for me to go to Shanghai for a few weeks next summer. I want to teach Jianhao how to play Chinese chess, and I also want to show him how to use a phone to capture interesting things through photography.

Xiaoling laughed on the other end of the line and said, "Dad, you’ve really thought this through."

I didn't say much, but I felt a sense of relief. All these years, I’ve always felt short on time, constantly rushing between work and home. Now that I’ve cleared my head, I realize retirement isn't an end; it’s finally having the time to do the things I’ve always wanted to do but never could.

Wang Xiuying called me from the kitchen for dinner. I put my phone down and took one last look at that photo of the flowers. Not bad.

I’ll keep recording tomorrow.

I used to record the status of my flowers with pen and paper, but now Xiuying is teaching me how to take photos and save them on my phone. She says it’s faster than writing. **[Notes]**

There are over 100 photos in my phone album now. Most are of flowers, the weather, and things around the house. Xiuying teases me, saying, "You’re using your phone as a diary."

I told her, "Not exactly. I still need the paper albums; those important photos still need to be printed and pasted in. But this way, I can look at them anytime without worrying about losing them."

To be honest, I’m still learning how to use the phone. Sometimes I press the wrong button, and sometimes the photos come out blurry. But Xiuying and Jianhao are very patient in teaching me.

Now I take a few photos every day. The roses, the weather, the path we walk, my wife’s smiling face, screenshots of my grandson... these little things have become quite important to me now.

Xiuying, are you asleep yet?

I was out on the balcony watering the plants just now and happened to find that photo album from over ten years ago. Do you remember? Back then, Xiaoling was still on a business trip in Shanghai, and Jianhao was only a few months old. That photo of our whole family in the park...

As I looked at it, my eyes started to sting a little.

It’s not that I’m sad, it’s just... time really flies. We’ve gotten old. I remember thinking back then about how I’d spend my time with our grandson once I retired. Now Jianhao is already ten, and I’m still this busy.

How have you been feeling lately? Has your high blood pressure flared up again? I’ve noticed you’ve been standing in the kitchen for a long time lately; are you getting tired? Remember to ask the doctor about it during your check-up next month—don’t just push through it.

I was thinking, I’ll be retiring in another two years. Then it’ll just be the two of us in this house every day... watering the plants, playing chess, spending time with our grandson? I don’t know if that’s enough. I mean, is there... anything else we still want to do?

Don’t laugh at me, I’m just feeling a bit... worn out. There’s so much going on at work; sometimes I can’t tell if I’m physically exhausted or just weary at heart.

But seeing you doing well at home makes me feel a bit more at ease. Let’s grow old together.

Goodnight.

# MEMORANDUM

**Date: November 2, 2022, Evening**

---

Xiaoling called today and had a lot to say. I’m writing it down because I’m afraid I won’t be able to think it through clearly otherwise.

She said my care for your mother is "a bit over the top." That was the phrase she used. She mentioned how I insist on accompanying her to the hospital every month and how I’ve memorized every medication schedule and dietary restriction to the letter. She asked if I’m being too tense.

I didn’t give her a direct answer at the time. But sitting here now, I’m wondering—

**Is she right?**

Am I really overdoing it?

Ever since her blood pressure fluctuated during the last check-up, I have indeed become more cautious. I watch every grain of salt in her food. Yesterday, she wanted some salted fish, and I refused her outright. She didn’t say anything, but I could tell.

Xiaoling said, "Dad, Mom’s hypertension is well under control. The doctor said as long as she takes her medicine on time and comes in for regular check-ups, she’ll be fine. You don’t need to keep yourself so wound up."

She also said, "You’re about to retire. You should be thinking about how to relax, not pouring all your energy into worrying."

**The problem is—**

If I’m not this careful, what if something happens to her?

I know there are things I can’t control. But for the things I can, I want to do my absolute best. That’s how I’ve always done things.

But… Xiaoling has a point. I can see that your mother gets frustrated being watched so closely sometimes. She’ll say, "I’m not a child."

**I need to figure this out:**

What does "moderate" care actually look like?

Am I protecting her, or am I trapping her?

Maybe… I need to trust her a little more. Trust that she can take care of herself. Trust what the doctor says.

But that is much easier said than done.

I’ll think about it more tomorrow. For now, I’ll just leave Xiaoling’s words here. Once I have an answer, I’ll look back at this.

---

*Also, I could ask my chess partners when we play next Wednesday. They’re all around the same age; maybe they have some experience with this.*

November 8, 2022, 10:30 PM

I went over the last two weeks of blood pressure logs again. The data is right there in front of me; there’s no room for interpretation.

Xiuying’s blood pressure hasn't improved.

I’ve listed out the figures for every day:
- October 25 to November 8, 14 days in total.
- Morning systolic pressure mostly fluctuates between 140 and 148.
- The afternoons are slightly better, but never below 135.
- According to the doctor, it should be under 130 to be considered well-controlled.

I’ve followed every part of the plan. The low-sodium diet—I’ve even learned how to read the sodium content on food labels. The daily walks—I’ve accompanied her around the neighborhood for forty minutes every evening after dinner, without fail. Taking the medication on time—I place the pills right next to her teacup to make sure she doesn't forget. Recording the blood pressure—every morning at 7:00 and every evening at 7:00, using the same monitor, in the same position.

Everything has been done by the book. But the numbers just won't trend downward.

My temples have started to ache. A dull throbbing that began yesterday afternoon. It didn't let up at the office today either. I drank two cups of hot water; it didn't help.

Is there something wrong with my plan?

Or rather... is this something I simply cannot control?

The thought makes me feel a bit lightheaded. I sat in my office for a while, leaning back in my chair with my eyes closed. At two o'clock this afternoon, the sunlight streaming through the window was blinding. I remembered Xiuying saying last Friday that she hadn't been sleeping well lately, and I didn't press her for details at the time. Thinking back now, could it be because she’s also worried about her health?

I wrote in my notebook: "Need to talk to the doctor again. Maybe it’s not an execution problem; maybe the regimen itself needs adjusting."

But I don't have the courage to call the doctor tomorrow. I want to watch the data for a few more days. Maybe it’s just a period of fluctuation; maybe there will be an improvement next week.

I don't want to admit that there might be nothing I can do.

This feeling is unfamiliar. At the factory, I could always solve problems through stricter standards and more meticulous inspections. But this time, no matter how much I record or plan, nothing seems to change.

Xiuying is asleep in the bedroom now. I can hear her steady breathing.

And here I am, facing my notebook, facing these cold, hard numbers.

Tomorrow, I will continue to record. I will continue the walks and the low-sodium diet. Because stopping is more terrifying than keeping going.

# Evening Conversation with Wang Xiuying

**February 18, 2023, 21:15**

Xiuying, something happened today that I wanted to talk to you about.

A few of the younger employees came to see me this afternoon. They mentioned that the company might be introducing some kind of automated inspection system. They said that in the future, inspections could be handled by machines and that efficiency would be much higher. At the time, I just... well, I talked to them about the importance of traditional inspection. I told them there are some things machines simply can’t detect—things that require the eyes and the intuition of someone with years of experience.

But after I said it, I felt a heavy weight in my chest.

It’s not that I’m against them, and I know that technological progress is inevitable. I just can’t help wondering: if we really start using this new system on a large scale, what will happen to the Quality Department? Will there be layoffs? I’ve been hearing a lot about that kind of thing lately.

I didn't ask the leadership, nor did I get into the specifics. But you know, before leaving work, I went through the quality data from the past twenty years myself, just to look at what we’ve achieved. The numbers are all there—the passing rates, the customer complaint rates... they all look great. But as I kept flipping through them, I started to feel a bit sad.

It’s not that I regret the work I’ve done. I’ve never regretted it. It’s just... I wonder, if the department really does get restructured one day, will our lives be affected? Will the pension be enough? Your medical expenses...

Never mind, I’m probably overthinking it.

By the way, how has your blood pressure been lately? Have you been taking your medicine on time? You have that follow-up at the hospital next week, right? I believe it’s on Thursday. Don’t forget—I’ll be going with you.

Actually, I just wanted to talk to you. Don't worry. We’ve made it through so many years together; this is nothing.

It’s just that sometimes it feels like everything is changing. Everything except you.

# March 24, 2023, 8:00 PM

Sitting on the balcony, the roses look the same as ever. I checked my notebook; the last time I watered them was March 20th. They’re due today.

I have a cup of tea in my hand. It’s gone a bit cold.

It’s been a strange week. Last Wednesday, I didn’t go to play chess. That was the first time in eight years. I didn't even call anyone to explain; I just didn't show up. My mind was a mess during that time, and my sleep was ruined—I’d wake up after four in the morning and couldn't get back to sleep. Wang Xiuying tried to talk to me a few times, but I didn't give her much of a response. Thinking back now, I feel a bit of regret.

I went to the community activity room this afternoon.

Old Zhang and the others were all there. They looked at me a bit strangely when they saw me, but they didn't ask too many questions. We started playing. I was definitely distracted during the first game, made several terrible moves, and ended up losing. At that moment, I was still thinking about the company, that meeting, and... forget it, I won't think about it anymore.

But the second game was different. I don't know why, but by the second game, my focus slowly returned. It was the same familiar layouts, the same routines, the same opponents. I won.

Old Wang asked me, "How’s work lately?"

I paused for a moment. Then I said, "Not bad. There are some reforms at the company, but I’m slowly adapting."

As I said those words, I was a bit surprised myself. Two weeks ago, I couldn't have said something like that. Back then, my head was filled with despair, feeling like everything was coming to an end.

When I left the activity room, I lingered at the top of the stairs for a while. No one saw me. I just stood there and quietly said to myself—I’m still here.

It wasn't some grand declaration. It was just... a confirmation. Confirming that I’m still alive, that I can still play chess, and that I can still step out of the house. That I can still win it back in the second round.

The rosebuds are a bit larger than last time. They might bloom in a few days. I need to make a note of that.

Tomorrow morning, I’ll get up at 6:30, watch the news, and have breakfast. Business as usual.

March 6, 2023, Morning

I lay in bed for a long while before finally getting up. I didn't get up immediately when the alarm went off, which isn't like me. Xiuying asked me what was wrong; I told her I just had a lot of work. She didn't push further, but I could tell she was worried.

Ever since I received the meeting notice last night, I haven't been able to relax. "Institutional restructuring"—those two words have been spinning in my head all night. I didn't sleep well. I woke up at three in the morning and couldn't get back to sleep.

I lingered in front of the mirror for a long time this morning. The dark circles under my eyes are deep, and my face is etched with exhaustion. There was a look of unease in the eyes of the man in the mirror that I’ve never seen before. In over thirty years, I have never seen that look on my own face.

I couldn't eat breakfast. The pickles and congee sat in the bowl, untouched. Xiuying asked me again from the kitchen, and I told her I really wasn't hungry. She sighed and didn't say anything else.

What exactly does "restructuring" mean? Will my position change? Or... will I be sidelined? I’ve been in this department for twenty-five years, working my way up from shift leader to director. The achievements of all those years are right there on the office walls, along with that group photo from ten years ago. Is it possible that all of this is about to...

No. I need to stay calm. Wait for the specific details of the meeting first. I can't let my mind wander.

But... am I really falling behind? The young people use those automated systems while I’m still using the old methods. I don't know how to operate those new things. Even when my grandson watches cartoons on his phone, I have to ask him how to work it. Maybe the leadership saw it long ago—that this old-timer is ready for retirement.

No. I still have two years. Two years.

I need to organize the quality data from over the years once more. I need the data to speak for itself. Experience and judgment are things a machine cannot replace. I need to make everyone see that.

Wait for the meeting notice. Don't overthink it for now.

But I have to do something about this lack of sleep, or how am I going to handle what’s coming...

Xiuying’s high blood pressure needs a follow-up checkup this month, too.

I spent this afternoon teaching Jianhao how to play Chinese chess in the activity room. He was much more focused this time than last, asking several questions about the "why" behind each move. I explained everything to him step-by-step, just like I used to train new employees back at the factory—the horse moves in an "L," the elephant moves in a square; every move has its logic.

But after saying that, I felt something different stir inside me. It was like a sudden moment of clarity.

All those years at the factory, I felt useful because I could teach people and pass things down. Now, teaching my grandson chess at home feels like the same thing. It’s just a different person and a different place.

Jianhao ended up winning a game against me. When I asked if he let me win on purpose, he said with total sincerity, "Grandpa, I’m just better than you."

I couldn't help but laugh.

October 20, 2022, Morning

I woke up fifteen minutes early again today. No particular reason, I just couldn't sleep soundly. I was wide awake by 5:45. I lay in bed for a while, listening to Xiuying’s breathing. She was sleeping deeply, her breaths steady and even; only then could I feel at ease. Once I confirmed that she was "all right," I got up to wash.

Ever since we got back from the hospital yesterday, I haven't been able to stop thinking about what the doctor said—her blood pressure fluctuations have increased. Her systolic reached 150. Xiuying pretended not to hear and just kept taking her medicine, but I could tell she was a bit nervous too. She didn't say anything, and I didn't push, but those words have been stuck in my heart ever since.

Last night, I made a decision. I dug out a new notebook and decided to record her medication times, blood pressure readings, and diet every single day... just like how I used to manage quality control metrics back at the factory. When I told Xiuying about the idea, she was a bit dismissive, saying, "There's no need for all that trouble." But I insisted. I told her that the doctor would have clearer data to look at during the next follow-up, and that’s the only way to truly help her.

In truth, I know it’s not just for the doctor.

I keep wondering what will happen if Xiuying’s health continues to fluctuate like this. I’m only two years away from retirement; I had planned to finally rest, spend time with my grandson, and do some gardening. But now... I don’t know. If something happens to her, what will I do? Will we be okay financially? Will I be able to take proper care of her?

There are no answers to these questions, but I have to do something. At the very least, I can keep every data point clear. I can keep a grip on every detail. That way, I won’t feel so afraid.

The notebook is ready. I’ll start recording tonight.

Work matters don't seem very important anymore.

Xiuying, I’m home.

I met a new friend today while playing chess at the community center. His name is Mr. Wang. We talked for quite a while; he’s been retired for a few years now. He said something that I’ve been thinking about ever since—he said that retirement isn't the end of life, but a new way of living.

You know, I used to feel like leaving work was like losing a part of myself. I’ve slowly been coming out of that funk lately, but hearing him say that made me truly understand. We aren't losing anything; we’re starting something new.

I’ve been thinking—we’ve both been so busy all these years. You were managing everything at home, and I was at the factory. But now, we finally have time. Mr. Wang mentioned that he often goes for walks in the park with his wife, just to look at the flowers and chat. They even join some of the community activities together.

I was wondering, maybe we could give that a try? How about we go for a walk in the park this weekend? I can tell you the names of the flowers and how to look after them—you know how much I’ve gotten into that lately. Or maybe we could join Mr. Wang and his wife sometime and make some new friends.

I’m finally starting to realize that retirement can actually be very fulfilling. It’s not just about me going off to play chess; the things we do together will be much more meaningful.

What do you think?

# Technical Risk Analysis Regarding the Implementation of the New Inspection System

**Date:** Monday, January 16, 2023
**Recorded by:** Li Jianguo

---

## I. System Stability Issues

I’ve been reflecting on this since yesterday’s meeting. The new system claims to improve efficiency, but having worked in quality inspection for 37 years, I know one thing for certain: **when the machines fail, the production line grinds to a halt.**

Points that require confirmation:
- Is the system backup mechanism robust? What is the recovery timeframe after data loss?
- Can the system operate independently during a network outage?
- What is the contingency plan for hardware failure?

The ERP system in the neighboring workshop crashed the year before last, paralyzing the entire line for four hours. We cannot allow this to happen at the quality inspection stage.

---

## II. Data Accuracy and Traceability

A fatal weakness of automated inspection is that **it can only measure programmed parameters.** However, quality issues often hide in the details—the nuances that require human experience to judge.

For example:
- Last year, we had a batch where all data points were "qualified," but during a visual inspection, I spotted microscopic cracks on the material surface. It turned out the customer did encounter issues later. The machine's sensors couldn't capture that at all.
- Then there is the issue of welding porosity; sometimes it’s invisible on the surface but poses an internal quality risk. This requires experience and intuition.

**Question List:**
- Can the new system’s detection precision truly match our current standards?
- If a defect is missed, how is liability defined? Is it a system failure or a parameter setting issue?
- How will the legal validity and traceability of inspection reports be guaranteed?

---

## III. Risks During the Transition Period

This is the most practical concern. We cannot switch from fully manual inspection to full automation overnight.

**Critical factors to consider:**
- How do we ensure data consistency while the old and new systems run in parallel?
- How long is the employee training cycle, and who is responsible for it?
- If the system results conflict with manual inspection results, which one takes precedence?
- Will there be fluctuations in product quality during this transition?

My department currently has eight inspectors, all of whom have undergone years of training. A sudden system swap will undoubtedly cause chaos in the first three months.

---

## IV. Cases Where Human Experience is Irreplaceable

This is vital; I need to organize these cases properly.

**Case 1:** In 2019, a customer reported anomalies during product use. All data showed the batch was qualified. However, by comparing historical records, I found that while a certain parameter was within range, it was higher than the same period in previous years. Investigation revealed the raw material supplier had been changed. Machines cannot make this kind of "trend judgment."

**Case 2:** Regarding a quality complaint handled last year, the customer reported noise issues. The test data was perfectly normal. I insisted they disassemble the unit and found a minute deviation in the internal component positioning—a deviation within the "acceptable" range, but one that caused resonance in actual use. This requires a deep understanding of product structure.

**Case 3:** In 2018, I judged based on experience that a specific production line needed maintenance, even though no problems had surfaced yet. A week later, a key component on that line showed signs of early wear. This was based on years of recognizing the patterns of equipment operation.

---

## V. Proposed Contingency Plans

If the new system is to be implemented, I need to have backup plans ready:

1. **Retain Manual Spot-Checks** — We cannot rely solely on automation; at least 10-15% of products should undergo manual re-inspection.
2. **Establish a Data Comparison System** — Run the old and new systems in parallel for three months, comparing results daily.
3. **Develop a Rapid Rollback Plan** — If the automated system fails, we must be able to switch back to manual inspection within two hours.
4. **Enhance Employee Training** — Don't just teach them how to operate it; make sure they understand the system's principles and limitations.
5. **Retain Key Positions** — The QC Supervisor and 2-3 senior inspectors must be retained; they are our final line of defense for quality.

---

## VI. Questions for Senior Management

- Does the system vendor have successful case studies in similar industries?
- What is the technical support response time when the system fails?
- If the system leads to missed defects, who bears the legal liability?
- Does the implementation of the new system imply a restructuring of departmental personnel?

---

**Personal Thoughts:**

I am not against progress. In 37 years, I’ve seen many changes—from the earliest hand-written logs to today’s spreadsheets—and I’ve learned them all. The new system certainly has its advantages. However, **quality is the lifeblood of the enterprise.** This isn't just a slogan; it has been my professional creed for decades.

If we are to adopt this system, we must ensure it does not weaken our inspection capabilities. A machine can do many things, but where it falls short, humans must step in. This isn't about being stuck in the past; it’s about being responsible for quality.

I will continue to learn this new system. But I will also ensure that my 37 years of experience and judgment do not go to waste.

---

*To be continued/updated*

July 18, 2023, 10:30 PM

Xiuying is already asleep. I’m sitting on the sofa with the photo album still spread open across my lap.

When I spoke to Xiao Ming on the phone this afternoon, he mentioned wanting to pivot his business strategy and break into online sales. I listened for a while and offered some advice—things like securing a solid offline foundation first and expanding gradually, rather than chasing quick profits. He didn't say much, just a few "uh-huhs." Since hanging up, I haven't been able to stop thinking about it.

Was my advice right? Or do I simply have no grasp of the challenges he’s facing now?

After dinner, I dug out the old photo albums. There’s the award certificate from 1998, right when I was promoted to Quality Department Manager. And the department group photo from ten years ago—the same one that’s still taped up in the office. I traced the faces in the photo with my finger—Master Wang, Old Zhang, Xiao Li... some have moved on, others have retired. I whispered their names aloud, as if to confirm they truly existed.

Back then, I was so certain. Quality was life; customer satisfaction was everything. I managed the department by those principles. Our pass rates were high, and our clients trusted us. I knew exactly what I was doing and why I was doing it.

And now? The factory is undergoing "intelligent transformation." The young people use systems for inspections that are ten times faster than we ever were. I’m still using the old methods, still clinging to those "exacting standards." At the last meeting, when they were discussing using AI to optimize workflows, I didn't dare say a word.

The advice I gave Xiao Ming was the same. All I could come up with was "steady" and "don't rush," because that’s the wisdom I’ve gathered over sixty-odd years of living. But is that wisdom... still worth anything today?

Xiuying came out of the bedroom and, seeing me still sitting there, sat down beside me. She didn't say anything, she just stayed with me. Her hand was on the back of mine, warm and steady. I didn't close the album; I just let it stay open there.

I’m thinking that maybe I’ve fallen behind the times. The things I once stood for now look like nothing more than an old man’s stubbornness. Xiao Ming doesn't need my "steadiness." What he needs is... I don't know. I really don't know.

I have to take Xiuying back to the hospital for her follow-up tomorrow. I need to write the doctor’s orders on the calendar. At least that is something I can still do well.

# Pre-retirement Knowledge Transfer Plan

**Date: November 25, 2022**  
**Recorded by: Li Jianguo**

---

## I. Five Key Work Modules for Knowledge Transfer

### 1. Establishment and Maintenance of the Inspection Standard System
- The rigorous standards accumulated over thirty-plus years must not be lost.
- Experience must be systematically documented; we cannot rely solely on oral tradition.
- Scope: Raw material inspection, in-process inspection, and all stages of finished product inspection.
- **Key Point**: The strictness of our standards directly impacts customer satisfaction—this is the lifeline of our department.

### 2. Quality Issue Handling Procedures
- How to quickly pinpoint the root cause of an issue.
- Coordination methods with the production department.
- Response and traceability mechanisms for customer complaints.
- These are essential skills the new Director needs to master as soon as possible.

### 3. Personnel Management and Team Building
- Performance evaluation criteria for subordinates.
- How to maintain strict standards without dampening team morale.
- Where departmental cohesion comes from (that group photo from ten years ago wasn't just for show).

### 4. Communication and Coordination with Superiors and Other Departments
- Finding the balance between upholding standards and maintaining flexibility.
- How to secure understanding and support from leadership.
- How to handle pushback or grievances from the production department.

### 5. Daily Management Details
- Key points for reviewing inspection reports.
- Standards for archive management and data recording.
- Cost control (balancing inspection costs with quality costs).

---

## II. Communication Plan with the New Director

| Timeline | Content | Method |
|----------|---------|--------|
| Early Dec | First formal handover; explaining the inspection standard system | One-on-one in the office |
| Mid-Dec | Walk through a complete inspection cycle together | On-site practical training |
| Jan | Handle several real-life quality issue cases | Case study discussions |
| Feb | Attend coordination meetings with the production department | On-site observation |
| Mar | Gradual transition to independent handling under my supervision | Phased transition |

---

## III. Milestones

- **Now to late December**: Intensive transfer period; at least two in-depth sessions per week.
- **January to March**: Transition period; the new Director gradually takes the lead while I serve as a consultant.
- **April to May**: The final stretch before my official retirement; ensuring he has truly mastered the role.
- **Post-retirement**: Stay in touch and provide consulting support as needed.

---

## IV. Success Criteria

1. **The new Director can independently develop and adjust inspection standards.** ✓
2. **The department's product qualification rate does not decline.** ✓
3. **Customer complaint handling processes run smoothly.** ✓
4. **Acceptance and recognition from subordinates.** ✓
5. **He knows to reach out to me for advice when facing challenges.** ✓

---

## V. Personal Reflections

This is more than just a simple "handover." Thirty-seven years of work cannot just be cut off. What I have learned in this position isn't just technical skill—it’s an attitude toward work, quality, and people. If I can pass these values on, my worth won't disappear just because I retire.

The new Director is a conscientious person; I can tell. The questions he asks are very practical. I believe he will do a good job.

This plan must be written clearly and recorded in detail. We cannot rely on memory; we must rely on the written word. This is the only way to ensure nothing is missed.

---

**Next Steps**:  
Schedule a meeting with the new Director on Monday to show him this plan and get his feedback.

August 22, 2024, past 8:00 PM

Xiuying brought up the fertilizer issue again today. I’d been feeding the roses strictly according to the ratio chart, but she said it seemed a bit strong and asked if I could dial it back a little. At the time, I was all set to explain why that specific ratio was correct, and after a while, she just stopped talking.

After dinner, I pulled out my logbook to check on how the roses have been growing this year. As I was flipping through the pages, something suddenly felt off.

I started wondering—have I been doing this for the past thirty years? Setting standards at the factory, and then setting standards at home. When to eat, when to go to the hospital, even how Xiuying should rest. I’ve laid everything out in spreadsheets, managing it all like a production line.

Xiuying asked me what was on my mind. I said, "Maybe the two of us... maybe we need a bit more flexibility." She asked, "Are you talking about us?" I nodded and added, "Not just you, but me too."

She smiled and said, "I knew you’d eventually realize that."

I’m sitting on the balcony now, looking at the roses. They don’t grow according to my schedule; some buds are full and bursting, while others are taking their time. I’ve been so busy recording their status that I don’t think I’ve ever really "listened" to what they need. It’s just like Xiuying said—she knows what she needs, yet I’ve been making all the decisions for her.

What have I learned this summer? I suppose it’s this: order is important, but so is flexibility. Life isn’t an engineering project; you can’t live it strictly by the blueprints.

Tomorrow, I’ll make the fertilizer a little weaker. Maybe Xiuying is right.

Honestly, I should have realized this a long time ago.

Ming, I’ve been thinking, and I felt I should talk to you.

I had a conversation with your mother a couple of days ago. She told me I’ve been too high-strung lately, and that it’s starting to get to her. I didn’t say anything at the time, but after sitting by myself for a long while, I realized she was right.

Do you remember our last phone call? You mentioned how I’m always asking about your business or whether you’re eating well. Your mom also said that my daily habit of recording her blood pressure feels like I’m performing a quality inspection. At the time, I was a bit defensive—I felt I was just showing I cared. But it’s slowly sinking in now: I think I’ve been bringing my work habits home with me.

At the factory, I have to control every detail to ensure quality. But home is different. Your mother’s health, your business, the kids growing up... no matter how meticulously I try to manage these things, there are parts that are simply beyond my control. I didn’t want to admit that before; I always felt that if I just tried hard enough, I could keep everything on track. I see now that thinking this way only puts pressure on me and all of you.

I’ve decided to change how I do things. It’s not that I’ve stopped caring, but I need to learn to trust the doctors, trust your mother, and trust that you can handle your own affairs. I’m still keeping track of her blood pressure, but I won’t nag her about it every single day. And when you talk about your business, I’ll listen, but I won’t be so quick to jump in with advice.

To be honest, this isn't easy for me. This is just how I’ve lived my life all these years. But what your mother said really woke me up—maybe with some things, letting go is actually a better way of showing I care.

Do you think I’m looking at this the right way?

Xiuying, are you there?

I just got back from the office. Something went wrong again today—the leaves on that pink rose bush are turning even yellower. I looked through my notebook and saw that the last time I watered it was ten days ago... What was I busy with ten days ago? Oh, right, those were the days my daughter was pestering me to learn how to use this smartphone.

I remember now. I had intended to record the watering date that day, but I got so flustered and overwhelmed by this phone that I ended up forgetting. Now the flowers have all wilted.

Tell me, am I really getting too old? I can't even take proper care of the flowers I've looked after for all these years.

I was thinking... I’m retiring in two years. I'll be home every day then, but look at me—I can't even handle these little things now. What will I do then? What am I even capable of doing?

(A brief pause)

What do you think? Am I overthinking things?

July 17, 2023, Night

Sitting on the balcony, the roses look the same as ever, though the leaves are wilting a bit. I forgot to water them yesterday. I pulled out my notebook to write something down, but my hand just stopped there.

I made a wrong move with my knight during chess today.

It sounds like no big deal, but I know what was going through my head. It’s been eight years, every Wednesday in that activity room, and I rarely make mistakes. Today, I didn't just mess up; I messed up at a critical moment. Old Wang glanced at me and didn't say anything, but I could feel him going easy on me.

Later, the others started doing it too. Their play became cautious, as if they were tip-toeing around someone in a bad mood. I could tell.

For the first time, I took the initiative to leave early. I said I was a bit tired, but that wasn't it. I just didn't want to see those looks anymore—the kind of look you only give to someone who needs to be looked after.

On the way back, I thought about my last conversation with Li Ming. He asked me if the building materials store should pivot to online sales, and the advice I gave him was the same old stuff—stay steady, be conservative, don't rush. He didn't argue, but I could hear the disappointment in his voice. I’m starting to think that maybe he didn't need advice like mine. Maybe my experience just can't keep up anymore.

The factory has been talking about "smart upgrades" again lately. The reports the young inspectors generate with the system are faster and more accurate than the ones I do using traditional methods. Watching them navigate interfaces I don't quite understand, I couldn't help but wonder—is this how it ends for me?

I used to be so sure I knew what was right. Quality is life; customer satisfaction is everything. These principles worked for me; they took me from a frontline worker to a supervisor. But what about now? Maybe Li Ming doesn't need quality as much as he needs speed. Maybe the factory doesn't need my experience as much as it needs the technical skills of those young kids.

I’m not wallowing in self-pity. I’m just thinking that maybe I’ve always believed I was stable and reliable, when in reality, I’ve just been using old methods to deal with a world that has already changed. And now, even my friends are starting to look at me with that kind of pity.

The rosebuds haven't bloomed yet. I must remember to water them tomorrow. At least that is something I can still do right.

I wasn't good with smartphones at first, but now it helps me connect with my grandson far away. This feeling is better than anything.

September 15, 2024, Evening

I was on the balcony watering the China roses when the call from the office came. Xiuying was in the kitchen clearing the dishes, and the TV was playing the closing theme of the evening news.

It was the new director. He said the automated inspection system was having trouble identifying a specific type of micro-crack. They had adjusted the parameters several times to no avail, and the client had already filed a complaint. He asked if I had time to come take a look.

My first instinct was to decline. I’ve been retired for three months; they should figure these things out on their own. I made sure the handover was thorough.

But after hanging up, I sat there with the watering can still in my hand, suddenly feeling a bit lost. That was the work I did for thirty-seven years. When it comes to micro-crack identification, I can pinpoint the likely error with my eyes closed—it’s probably an incorrect angle parameter on the optical sensor, or the threshold was set too high.

Xiuying came out of the kitchen and asked what was wrong. I told her what happened and mentioned I was worried about disrupting our routine. Life has been good lately—accompanying her to the hospital for check-ups every month, playing chess on Wednesdays, staying home on weekends. I didn’t want to start running back to the office every day because of this.

Xiuying sat down beside me and said something I’m still thinking about: "Your skills don't just vanish because you retired. If you can help, why refuse? It would make me happy to see you help out."

I didn’t answer right away. Looking at her face, something suddenly clicked.

Over these past three months, I’ve been wondering if I’ve truly "settled down." Gardening, chess, and the news—these are all fulfilling, but sometimes I wake up in the middle of the night thinking about the quality achievement charts on the office wall and the standards I established. I thought retirement meant letting go of all that completely; otherwise, it meant I hadn't truly accepted that I was getting old.

But that wasn't what Xiuying meant. She was saying that I can take care of her and still use my skills occasionally. The two aren't mutually exclusive.

I’ve decided to go. Not because I want my old job back—that identity belongs to the past. It’s because if I can continue to be useful in a different way, why say no?

I’ll go check on the system tomorrow. I feel a bit excited, yet very calm. It’s a strange feeling—as if I’m neither returning to the past nor denying it, but rather finding a new place for myself.

Xiuying is watching TV now. I’m sitting on the balcony, the roses swaying gently in the night breeze. I’ve noted today’s watering time in my notebook.

Some things, retirement cannot change.

Xiuying, are you up?

I sat there for a while just now. It wasn't until I was putting on my loungewear that I remembered... starting today, I don't have a uniform to put on anymore.

(Pause)

Thirty-seven years. Every day, I’d wake up at this exact time, watch the news for ten minutes, have breakfast, and then change into my uniform. This morning, as I held the clothes in my hands, I suddenly... I didn't quite know what to do with myself.

It’s not that I have any regrets. I know it’s time to retire, and my body is definitely tired. But... (Pause) ...You know, I sat on the edge of the bed for a long time before I realized that I used to have things to do every single day. Checking reports, coordinating with the production department, handling complaints... at least I knew what I was doing.

And now? (Pause) Now I’m sitting here, the sun is just coming up, and I have the whole day ahead of me, but I have no idea how to spend it.

Water the flowers? Play chess? Or... just pace around the house like this?

(A long pause)

Do you think I’m overthinking things?

I know I have you, the kids, and the grandkids. None of that has changed. It’s just... it feels like a piece of me is missing. Like all of a sudden, I’ve become useless.

(Pause, voice softening)

Never mind, don't listen to my rambling. Go ahead and have your breakfast. I’ll go... I’ll go water the flowers.

August 7, 2024, Evening

I was playing chess in the activity room this afternoon and made a real mess of a move. When my opponent asked me what happened, all I could say was, "Oops, I guess I drifted off." To be honest, this has been happening a lot over the past two months. I’ll be sitting at the chessboard, eyes fixed on the game, but my mind is miles away. It’s getting embarrassing.

Halfway through the game, Xiao Ming called. He said business isn't great again—the competition in the building materials market has been brutal lately. As I listened, I started to focus, and my head suddenly cleared. I suggested that he try partnering with home renovation companies; they have the clients, we have the supplies, and they could help each other out. Xiao Ming sounded quite pleased and said, "Dad, you’re still the one with the best ideas." After hanging up, I felt a bit better.

When I got back to the game, I found my concentration was much better. Later, a new player showed up, and I took the initiative to explain some chess strategies to him. It was only while I was talking that I realized I can still think things through clearly and that I can still be of help.

It’s a strange feeling. When Xiao Ming said he needed my advice, I felt like I came back to life. It was as if I’d suddenly found something. But now, sitting here, that empty feeling is starting to creep back in.

Xiuying says I’ve been a bit distracted lately and told me to stop overthinking things. She’s right, but I don’t even know what I’m thinking about. It’s just that life feels a bit unmoored.

Tomorrow, I’ll go to the hospital to accompany her for her check-up as usual.

November 2, 2024, 10:15 PM

Xiuying is asleep. I’m sitting on the small balcony, watching the rose leaves sway gently in the night breeze.

I just watched the video Jianhao sent. He was singing at school—a bit out of tune, but with a huge smile on his face. I watched it three times. Then I just sat here, lost in thought.

Suddenly, a question hit me: how much time do I have left to be with him?

I wrote that sentence down in my notebook. My pen trembled slightly on the paper.

I’m sixty-three this year. In two years, I’ll be leaving the factory for good. Jianhao is ten now; in another ten years, he’ll be twenty. Will he still need me to tell him stories then? Will he still want to see the photos I take? How much longer can Xiuying and I stay by his side?

The moment the thought surfaced, a dull ache set in. It wasn’t a sharp pain, but a slow, sinking feeling. It’s like spotting a hairline crack in a product at the factory—it works for now, but you know it’s bound to fail eventually.

Xiuying walked out of the room. She didn’t ask what I was doing or why I was sitting here. She just looked at me and said, "That’s why we have to make the most of our time with him now."

Just that one sentence.

I didn't answer right away. But her words seemed to pull me out of that "how much time" loop. It’s not that the question vanished, but rather—I think I realized that instead of asking how much time is left, I should be asking how I should spend the time we have.

What can I leave behind for Jianhao? Not money, nor anything expensive. Maybe some stories? Some photos? Or perhaps just this—sitting here with him, truly listening to him sing off-key?

I opened my notebook and, beneath the line "How much time do I have left to be with my grandson?", I wrote another: "Start leaving something behind, starting now."

The pen didn't tremble this time.

The roses are still swaying. I need to water them tomorrow.
