# Wednesday, September 18, 2024 – Sunny

3:30 PM. Just hung up the phone with Li Ming.

When the new director called this morning, I didn’t think much of it. He mentioned that the automated inspection system was failing to identify certain tiny surface scratches, and a batch of goods had been returned by the client. He asked if I could drop by to take a look and offer some advice. I agreed without hesitation and we set a time for Friday afternoon.

After I put down the phone, Xiuying asked me what was wrong. It was only then I realized I was sitting bolt upright, my teacup untouched in my hand. It was a strange sensation—having been retired for three months, suddenly being needed again felt like... like someone knocking on your door just to check if you’re still home.

I brought it up with Li Ming during lunch. He asked if I’d be charging a consulting fee. I said of course, otherwise it would be awkward for them to keep bothering me. He chuckled and said, "Dad, looks like you’re going to keep on shining." I didn't give him a direct answer, but the first thought that flashed through my mind was that this could add a bit more to the budget for my wife’s hospital follow-ups.

When that thought surfaced, I paused for a moment.

Because I suddenly understood why I had agreed to this consultation. It wasn't because I couldn't sit still, nor was it because I missed being the director. It was... this money could help Xiuying get a more thorough check-up for her hypertension. Last time, the doctor suggested adding a cardiac ultrasound; I saw the price and didn't bring it up again. Now, with this consulting fee, we won't have to wait.

And then there’s my grandson. Jianhao is ten now. I want to spend more time with him before he gets much older. This money might support a ten-day or two-week trip to Shanghai for Xiuying and me.

I didn't tell Li Ming any of this. But I think this is the real reason behind my decision.

It’s not about some continuation of professional value, nor is it about being unable to let go of work. It’s very practical—I still have people to take care of and things I want to do, and this consulting job just happens to help me do them a little better.

I’ll head to the company on Friday to look at the issue. It shouldn't be hard to solve; I’ve seen that kind of defect under a microscope hundreds of times. The new system’s parameters probably just need adjusting, or perhaps it just needs a human eye to double-check at a certain stage.

It won't disrupt my current life. It’s just a supplement in the gaps. I’ll still play chess with my friends every Wednesday, the flowers still need tending, and Xiuying’s hospital check-ups cannot be missed.

Thinking this way, I feel at peace.

Xiuying is out on the balcony watering the plants; I can hear the sound of the hose. The roses are about to bloom again.

I’ll continue to log the watering schedule tomorrow.

September 6, 2024, 7:00 PM

I’m still thinking about something Xiao Ling said during our phone call tonight.

She told me that Jianhao has been telling stories at school, and they’re all about me. He talks about how I get up at 6:30 every morning and how I water the flowers on the balcony. She said Jianhao even asked her, "Why doesn't Grandpa video chat with me?"

When I heard that, I was holding the calendar in my hand, and suddenly, I was at a loss for words.

While Xiao Ling was teaching me how to use the video call feature on my phone, I kept thinking to myself—it’s not that I don’t want to learn, I’m just truly afraid of messing it up. But hearing my grandson say that made me think: what is there to be afraid of? I worked in the factory for thirty-seven years; I’ve seen all sorts of complicated things. How could a simple video call defeat me?

I took out a pen and wrote down every step Xiao Ling told me. Open WeChat, find "Video Call," tap it—just a few steps. She said I could try it tomorrow and there was no rush. I told her there’s no need to wait until tomorrow; I can try it right now.

Xiuying saw me like this and laughed. She said, "Old Li, are you finally seeing the light?" I didn't answer her, but I thought to myself: this is just like my work in the inspection department. No matter how complex a problem is, you take it step by step, piece by piece. Nothing is perfect right from the start.

I gave it a practice run. I was still a bit clumsy, but I didn't break anything. Tomorrow is the weekend, so I’ll give Jianhao a video call. I want to see him, and I want him to see me.

All these years, I’ve always thought that I’m getting old and falling behind the times. But now I feel that maybe it’s not that I can’t keep up, but that I haven't truly tried. For my grandson, I can learn.

I’m writing this down to remind myself—some things are worth doing.

Xiuying, I’m back.

I didn't play well again today. Old Wang and the others could tell; they even asked me what was wrong. I just told them I’m still adjusting... (pauses) To be honest, I don't even know how to put it into words myself.

I made a terrible move, in a game I should have won. I didn't even realize it until Old Wang pointed it out—it was a bit embarrassing. I’ve been playing for over thirty years, and I’ve never been this distracted. My head is just full of clutter.

I keep thinking about how, back when I was still working, Wednesday chess was just chess. Everything else was set aside. But now, the board is right in front of me, yet my mind is drifting somewhere else.

(long pause)

What do you think I should be doing now? I still wake up at 6:30 every morning, watch the news, have breakfast, and then what? The plants are watered, the house is tidied up. The rest of the time is just... empty.

I know it’s been ten years since you retired from the factory. How did you do it? I watched you manage the house, keeping everything so organized; you never seemed to feel this emptiness. Is there something wrong with me?

When I was working, no matter how tired or frustrated I got, I knew what I was doing and why I was doing it. Now... (pauses) Now, I’ve lost that feeling.

Oh, Li Ming called this afternoon. His business is hitting some snags again. I wanted to give him some advice, but I realized I didn't have any good suggestions. I used to be able to think things through clearly, but now my brain just isn't working right.

Don't worry, I’m just a little tired. I’ll rest for a bit after dinner.

# How to Make a Video Call on Your Phone

**Date: August 17, 2024**

**Step-by-Step Instructions (Verified):**

1. Open the "WeChat" app on your home screen
   - It’s the green icon with the speech bubbles
   - Xiaoling helped me set up a shortcut

2. Tap the "Contacts" tab at the bottom
   - Not "Chats," but "Contacts"
   - It’s the third one over

3. Find the name "Li Jianhao"
   - Xiaoling already saved it for me
   - That’s my grandson’s name

4. Tap his name to open his profile page
   - You will see several buttons
   - Look for the "Video Call" button (not "Voice Call")
   - The icon looks like a small camera

5. Tap the Video Call button
   - The screen will turn black while waiting to connect
   - Don’t rush to press anything else
   - You should see your grandson’s face in about 5–10 seconds

**If something goes wrong:**
- Screen stays black → Check if the internet is connected → Start over
- Can’t find the "Video Call" button → Check if you tapped the wrong name → Go back and look again
- Can’t hear clearly → Check the volume buttons on the side → You can ask Xiaoling for help

**Daily Practice Time:**
- 6:50 AM – 7:10 AM, after reading the morning news
- Do a "dry run" first (don't actually call him)
- Only call him once you feel comfortable with the steps

**Notes:**
- These steps are already written in my notebook; this is a backup
- My daughter says I’m a fast learner
- Goal: Successfully make the first video call by this weekend

Sooyoung, look at how serious Jianhao is. I was explaining to him how to care for the roses, and he was asking questions even more detailed than I expected. He asked why some buds need to be pinched off and how I can tell when it’s time to water them.

I told him, "Look at these leaves—if they still feel a bit soft to the touch, it means there’s still moisture in the soil, so there’s no rush to water." And he actually crouched down right there and felt the soil himself.

This kid has a mind of his own. He’s not like we were back then, just doing whatever the adults told us.

When he gets a bit older, I’ll have to teach him how to keep records. I’ve been keeping this notebook for nearly ten years now, writing down every single date I water or fertilize. That’s the only way to really see the patterns.

What do you think? Does that sound like a good plan?

November 22, 2023   Evening

I won three games of chess today.

It feels a bit strange saying that, as if I’m showing off. But I want to record it because I was truly focused during those three games. For the past few months, whenever I sat at the chessboard, my mind would wander—thinking about the company reforms, wondering if my department would be restructured, and dwelling on the fact that I’m only two years away from retirement. My chess partner, Old A, asked me several times if I was feeling unwell; that’s when I realized I was indeed out of sorts.

Today was different. While the games were underway, my head was clear.

At noon, Old A asked me, "Lao Li, I heard the company is restructuring. Is your position secure?" I blurted out without thinking, "My value isn't in my title, but in what I can do." I surprised even myself with that answer, but it’s something I’ve truly come to realize lately.

Last week, the leadership approved the new inspection workflow I proposed. It wasn’t anything groundbreaking—just a method that combines system data with manual experience. But it made me realize that these thirty-seven years haven't been for nothing. The young newcomers are fast with the systems, but when the system fails to catch a problem, they still have to rely on experience. What I can do is organize that knowledge, pass it on to them, or continue working as a consultant after I retire.

Retirement isn't the end; it’s a new beginning—I finally understand what that means now.

Xiuying’s blood pressure was good today; I made another note of it on the calendar. She asked me why I’ve suddenly become so talkative. I just smiled and didn't say much. It’s nothing, really—just that the weight on my chest has finally been lifted.

Tomorrow, I keep going. Next Wednesday, more chess.

Saturday, June 15, 2024. Sunny.

I watered the roses again today. My movements were much slower than usual; even I noticed it.

My hand holding the watering can stopped several times. It wasn't fatigue, it was just... I didn't know if I should keep going. In the end, I finished. I recorded the soil moisture in my notebook: June 15, 10:45 AM, soil moisture moderate, new leaves unfolding well. My handwriting was a bit shaky.

Looking at those words, it suddenly occurred to me that in all those years of signing inspection reports, my hand never shook. Back then, I knew exactly what I was doing; every decision carried weight. And now? I’m watering flowers and recording data, like I’m filing an inspection report that no one will ever read.

While sitting on the balcony this morning, that thought popped into my head again—should I keep doing some consulting work for the company? The new director might still need my advice. I even rehearsed how I’d say it to him... but then I thought, isn't that just self-deception? I’ve already left; I shouldn't keep looking back, trying to grasp at that feeling of being needed.

Thinking that way made my heart ache a little.

At lunch, Xiuying asked me what was on my mind. I told her nothing, just wondering when the flowers would bloom. She smiled and said they’d bloom when they’re ready, and that I’m just being impatient. I didn't argue with her.

Later, I flipped through the notebook and wrote a sentence on a blank page: "Flowers bloom and wither; it’s all nature. People are the same." After writing it, I just stared at the words, not knowing if I was comforting myself or mocking myself.

Probably a bit of both.

Thirty-seven years. From an operator to where I ended up. Every decision, every report, every moment of being trusted over those years... it’s all in the past tense now. I understand that this is normal—everyone has to retire eventually—but understanding and accepting are two different things.

Li Ming called this afternoon. He said the building materials business has been a bit tough lately and wanted my opinion. I analyzed a few things for him and shared some thoughts. It wasn't until after I hung up that I realized—at least in this moment, I can still be of help. Someone still needs me to figure things out.

That thought made me feel a little better.

But only a little.

Xiuying needs to have her blood pressure checked tonight. I’ve already flipped the calendar to June and noted down all the doctor’s orders. These small things, I can still do.

The roses are still outside the window, buds growing one by one. I’ll continue watering them tomorrow, and I’ll continue recording. As if nothing has changed.

But everything has changed.

Wednesday, July 24, 2024 – Sunny

I got back from the community center around 3:00 PM. I still feel a bit stifled.

I was playing chess with Old Wang and Old Chen today, and I told them about the plan I made for Xiuying. More daily walks, blood pressure checks three times a day, a low-sodium diet—I even made a spreadsheet. What to eat and when, exactly how much salt... I had it all calculated. Old Wang listened for a while, then suddenly blurted out, "Old Li, your wife is a person, not a machine."

I didn't say anything at the time. I just kept setting up the chess pieces.

But that sentence has been spinning in my head ever since.

It’s not that I haven't thought about it. Sometimes Xiuying teases me, saying I’m managing her like a factory production line. I thought she was just joking. But hearing Master Wang say it so bluntly today made me realize... maybe it wasn't entirely a joke.

I just want her to be well. You can't be careless with high blood pressure; I’ve read the materials, and I know how serious the consequences can be if it’s not controlled. So, I wanted to use the most scientific, systematic way to do things right. That’s not wrong, is it?

But...

I’m wondering if something is off. The plans I made for her—when to walk, what to eat, how to measure her blood pressure—are they a bit too... rigid? Sometimes she says she wants something salty, and I just tell her "no." She wants to sleep in, and I remind her it’s time for her walk.

Is this care, or is it... control?

I never thought of it that way. For over thirty years at the factory, that was how I worked—set the standards, follow them strictly, allow no deviations. That was the only way to guarantee the quality of the product. But Xiuying isn't a product. She’s my wife.

I’m sitting on the balcony now, looking at the roses. They need regular watering and fertilizing, but I never force them to bloom according to my schedule. Sometimes they bloom a bit later or earlier than I expect, and I just accept it. Why can’t I be like that with Xiuying?

At lunch today, she asked me if she could skip some of the blood pressure checks this weekend and just do it once. At the time, I told her that’s not what the doctor said. But thinking back, maybe I should have asked her why she was asking. Maybe she’s tired. Maybe what she needs isn't more data, but just... for me to be with her, without so many rules.

I haven't figured out how to talk to her about this yet.

Maybe tomorrow. I’ll just leave this thought here for now.

# MEMORANDUM

**Date: Evening of October 19, 2022**

---

How much longer can I keep doing this?

The question popped into my head at the office this afternoon, and it’s still spinning around. Looking at that group photo on the wall from ten years ago—everyone looked so young. Now? Some have already retired. I’m almost there, too.

**Things I need to sort out:**

1. **The Timeline**
   - 2 years until retirement (end of 2024).
   - What handovers need to be completed in these 2 years?
   - Departmental reforms are still up in the air; heard there might be adjustments.
   - What if I take early retirement? Can I afford it financially?

2. **Pension and Living Expenses**
   - How much will my pension actually be? (Need to check with HR).
   - The mortgage is paid off—that’s a plus.
   - My wife’s monthly medical bills; she can’t miss her blood pressure meds.
   - My younger son’s building materials business isn't stable yet; I can’t rely on him.
   - Consider taking on consulting work? The new director will likely still have questions for me.
   - But how much does consulting actually pay?

3. **My Wife’s Health**
   - Her levels were up a bit again at this month’s check-up; the doctor said she needs to watch her diet.
   - I need to be more attentive—it’s not enough to just mark it on the calendar.
   - If something happens to her one day, I need to be prepared.

4. **The Children**
   - Xiaoling is in Shanghai; she’s busy with work, and we see each other too rarely.
   - My grandson is 10 this year; I haven't spent enough time with him.
   - How is Xiaoming’s business doing this quarter? I need to find a chance to really ask him.
   - They have their own lives; I can’t depend on them.

5. **My Greatest Fear**
   - At work, I’m still useful; I still have value.
   - But once I retire?
   - I can barely figure out a smartphone—what else am I capable of?
   - It’s not about just having free time, it’s... I don’t know what I’ll be for.
   - Gardening, chess, watching the news—is that enough?

6. **Immediate Actions**
   - Organize my work files and prepare for the handover.
   - Get clarity on the consulting work; see if I can take on a few projects.
   - Spend more time with my wife, and not just at the hospital.
   - Find ways to see my grandson more, while I still can.
   - Save that pot of pink roses; prove that I haven't completely lost my touch.

---

Things are still a bit messy. But at least I’ve written it down; it’s better than letting it loop in my head.

I’ll keep thinking tomorrow.

It was a little after ten at night. Li Jianguo sat on the edge of the bed while Wang Xiuying, having finished getting ready for sleep, was watching TV. He remained silent for a while before finally speaking up.

"Xiuying, I went to see Wang Tao today."

Wang Xiuying turned her head, her eyes filled with concern.

"Oh? What happened?"

"I... I want to learn that new system. I’ve been avoiding it, but today I felt like I couldn't keep doing that."

He paused, his voice softening.

"But Xiuying, this old brain of mine... it’s just so slow. Wang Tao explained it all to me once, and I took notes, but there are still so many parts I don't understand. I kept asking questions for ages, terrified he’d get fed up with me."

Wang Xiuying put down the remote and moved a little closer to him.

Li Jianguo sighed, his gaze fixed on the floor.

"I was thinking... I’ve only got two years left until retirement. What can I even contribute during these two years? If I can't learn anything new, then..."

He didn't finish the sentence, only rubbed his face with his hands.

"I used to have some confidence in myself. Working my way up from an operator to where I am now, I thought I was doing alright. But now..."

He turned to look at his wife.

"I’m just glad you’re here. Otherwise, I really wouldn't know what to do."

Wang Xiuying reached out and gently patted his shoulder. She didn't say anything to comfort him; she just stayed there by his side in the silence.

Li Jianguo leaned against her, feeling the day's exhaustion finally find a place to rest.

That’s just how I am—sometimes I overthink things. Work, family... it all just keeps spinning in my head. But these are my burdens to carry, and you don't need to worry for me. Your health is what matters most, so please get some good rest.

We’re still going to the hospital for your follow-up next Wednesday, and I’ll be there with you. Don't overthink things.

I was sitting in my office when my phone screen lit up. It was a message from Li Ming, saying that business at the construction materials store had hit another snag and he wanted to hear my thoughts.

I stared at the message, my finger hovering over the screen for a long time.

In the past, I would have opened my notes app immediately and listed out points one, two, three, four, and five—laying out every possible solution with the airtight precision of an inspection report. But now… I put the phone down.

Outside the window, the November sunlight looked a bit cold. I thought about what Wang Xiuying said yesterday, and those few lines I’d written in my own notebook over the past few days.

I picked up the phone again and started typing. But this time, I didn't make a list.

---

**Message to Li Ming:**

Son, I hear you. The construction materials business is tough; I know that.

But to be honest, I’ve been thinking about something lately—I used to always try to solve every problem for you, as if I had all the answers. The truth is… I’m not that certain myself.

You’re the one on the ground there; you know the market better than I do. Any advice I give might not even be right. Instead of me throwing out random ideas from over here, why don’t you think it through yourself and see which path feels viable to you?

I trust you. You’ve brought the business this far, which proves you’ve got a good head on your shoulders.

I’m playing chess with some friends on Wednesday; let’s sit down and have a real talk then. No need to rush an answer to me now. Take your time.

Some things can’t be rushed.

---

I looked at the message and edited it one more time. I deleted the sentence "Watch your cash flow"—that sounded too much like the old Li Jianguo.

Finally, I hit send.

I set the phone on the desk and looked at the group photo from ten years ago hanging on the office wall. My subordinates all had big smiles on their faces. Was I too strict back then?

Then again… it was probably because I was strict that they trusted me.

I sighed and opened today’s inspection report.

Two more years. Two more years until retirement.

I started wondering what kind of person I’ll become by then.

# chat_private

Xiuying, I had a phone call with Li Ming tonight.

He brought up how business has been lately, and I offered him a few suggestions. Then, out of the blue, he said, "Dad, you seem to have changed since you retired. You’ve become... warmer."

I didn't say anything at the time; I just gave a little smile.

But later, while I was out on the balcony watering the flowers by myself, that sentence kept playing over in my head. I started thinking back to those years when he was still small—us going to the park together, me telling him stories about the factory while he sat on my shoulders. Back then, I was always in such a rush, my head constantly filled with work.

Looking back now, those moments of being there for him were more important than anything else.

Maybe I really do have more time to think about these things now that I'm retired. Before, it always felt like there wasn't enough time; now, it feels like there's almost too much.

Do you think I’ve been a bit different lately?

I’m not really asking for an answer, I just... wanted to talk to you.

Back to watering the flowers tomorrow. The roses should be blooming soon.

Xiuying, I’m back.

I was a bit off my game while playing chess today—made quite a few clumsy moves. Old Wang and the others noticed; they didn't say anything, but I could feel it myself. Halfway through, I knew I was going to lose. My mind was just somewhere else entirely.

Old Wang told me later that it’s normal. He said I should give myself some time and that I’ll adjust eventually. My eyes got a bit misty hearing that, but I managed to keep it together.

What do you think? Have I not been acting like myself lately? I’m starting to lose track of it myself. I still get up, water the flowers, and watch the news like always, but it all feels like a dream. There’s just no spark in any of it.

Old Wang is right, I suppose—this does take time. And I know this is all part of the process. It’s just…

Never mind, let’s eat first. What’s for dinner today?

July 15, 2024, Morning

Sitting on the balcony, looking at that pot of Chinese roses. The leaves have started to wither.

I’m trying to remember when I stopped watering them. I flipped through my logbook; the last entry was June 28th. That was two days before I retired. Everything after that is a total blank.

This would have been impossible before. For over twenty years, I never missed a beat. The watering intervals in spring, the frequency in summer, cutting back in winter… I had it all down to a science. No matter how busy things got at the office, I always remembered these flowers. And now? I’m home all day, yet I’ve forgotten.

Xiuying reminded me yesterday. I nodded and said I knew. But this morning, I still didn’t water them.

I picked up my pen to continue the log, but my hand froze mid-air. What is there to record? I used to keep track because—well, why? Because I wanted the flowers to thrive, because there was a routine, because it was the only way to keep life orderly. Now, I have all the time in the world, yet I can’t even manage this.

It’s a bit ironic.

For thirty-seven years, I woke up at 6:30 every morning, watched the news, and went to the office. Checking workflows, reviewing reports, handling complaints. Those numbers, those standards, those problems that required my decisions—they let me know what I was doing and why I was doing it. Now, that’s gone. I still wake up at 6:30, but then what?

Water the flowers? Play chess? Watch TV?

These are all the "right" things to do. I know they are. But when I’m actually doing them, it just feels like… I’m doing things without knowing why.

Xiuying is in the living room watching TV; she asked if I wanted to join her. I said yes. But after sitting there for a while, I came back out to the balcony.

That rose bush is really wilting now. I should go water it. But I’ve been sitting here, thinking for a long time, and I still haven’t moved.

Maybe tomorrow.

Or… I don’t know.

June 17, 2024. Evening.

Today felt a little different.

I got a call from Xiao Ling this afternoon saying Jianhao wanted to video chat with me. I was a bit nervous—that phone is still a real nuisance to me. Xiuying was right there trying to teach me; I poked at the screen clumsily several times, nearly hitting some strange button. But this time, I didn't give up, and I didn't just hand the phone over to Xiuying. I wanted to try it myself.

When Jianhao appeared on the screen, I froze for a second. A ten-year-old boy, a whole Shanghai away, and suddenly his face was right there, so clear. He asked how I’d been doing, asking so earnestly, like a little grown-up. I didn't know how to answer, so I started talking about the China roses on the balcony.

I took the phone over to the balcony and clumsily turned it toward the flowers. I showed him the pink rose that was about to bloom and told him a story I’d made up—about how the flower was like a little girl, stretching her petals every day, waiting for the sun. Jianhao listened intently and even asked if I could teach him how to grow flowers.

I said, "Of course. When you come back, Grandpa will teach you."

After I hung up, I sat on the small stool on the balcony and looked at those flowers for a long time. Xiuying called out from inside, asking how it went, but I didn't answer right away. I was thinking that this was probably the first time I’d actually used that phone for something meaningful. It wasn't forced, and it wasn't reluctant.

It’s a strange feeling. It’s not like the feeling of being needed at work—that was a responsibility. This was different. Jianhao needed me to tell him stories, needed me to teach him things, needed me simply as his grandfather. Not because I’m the Head of Quality Control, but just because I’m his grandpa.

I pulled out my notebook and wrote a few words next to today’s date: "Video call with Jianhao. He wants to learn how to grow flowers."

As I wrote that, I realized what I’ve been looking for all these days. I wasn't looking for the feeling of being back at work, or even just the feeling of being needed. I was looking for... something that belongs to me. Something that doesn't belong to the factory or a job title, but belongs to Li Jianguo, the man.

Maybe retirement isn't as scary as I thought. Maybe I just need a little time to figure out who else I can be.

Xiuying is calling me for dinner from the kitchen. I put the phone aside and head in.

June 3, 2024  Sunny

Today was my first full day of retirement.

I still woke up at 6:30 AM. My internal clock didn't stop just because I didn't have to go to work. I lay in bed listening to the news for ten minutes while Xiuying was already busy in the kitchen. It hit me then—usually at this time, I’d already be washing up and getting ready to head out. Now... what am I supposed to do?

Yesterday’s farewell party is still spinning in my head. I didn't expect to have so much to say. Thirty-seven years—from entering the factory in 1984 until now—every memory came flooding back. When I mentioned the comrades I’d worked with, my eyes welled up and my voice choked. I’ve never been one to cry in front of people, but yesterday I just couldn't help it. Maybe it was because that was the moment I truly realized—it’s really over.

My hands were shaking when I accepted the photo album. I felt it myself. My colleagues said it was a group photo from ten years ago. I spent so long in the Quality Department, inspecting countless products and setting those rigorous standards with these people... Now they are still there, but I am leaving. I gripped their hands so tightly when we shook, as if I were trying to confirm something.

Now all those things are here at home. The quality achievement chart from my office, the department photo, the inspection logs I used for years... I haven't decided where to put them yet. Xiuying asked if I wanted to hang them in the living room, but I told her I’d think about it. Maybe the study is better? But then I feel like looking at them might be a bit painful.

Before leaving the office yesterday, I stood at the door for a long time. Finally, I turned off the lights and locked the door. Thirty-seven years, and that was the last time I’d use that key.

The new director took over smoothly; I handed everything over clearly. He asked me some questions, and I explained everything to him patiently. But as I spoke, I thought—these things I know by heart are now being handed to someone else. Those inspection standards, those principles I’ve upheld for so many years... I always said that quality is the lifeblood of an enterprise, and I never wavered in that belief.

But what about now? I’m no longer the Director of the Inspection Department. Am I still the man who holds that belief? Or has that belief become a thing of the past, just like the items packed away in those cardboard boxes?

During lunch, Xiuying asked what I wanted to do this afternoon. I said I didn't know. She smiled and said, "That’s what retirement is—all the time belongs to you now." I smiled back, but it felt a bit forced.

All the time belongs to me... That sentence should sound like something to be happy about. I do want to spend more time with her and see my grandson more often. But right now, I mostly just feel empty. It’s like something is missing from the room, but I haven't figured out what it is yet.

Maybe I should call Jianhao and ask how he’s doing. Or help Li Ming think through things for his building materials store. I can still do those things.

But it’s not quite the same as that feeling of being needed in the office.

Tonight, I have to take Xiuying for her blood pressure check-up. I must make sure I do that well. At least that’s one thing I still know exactly how to do.

— Li Jianguo

October 23, 2024 – Around 7:00 PM

While we were playing chess today, Old Wang asked if I’d gone back to work at the factory lately. I just smiled and said, "No, I’m just helping out and taking a look at things once in a while." He pressed a bit further, and it was only then that I realized how much my choice of words had changed.

In the past, if someone had asked, I probably would have said, "I’m the Director of Quality Inspection." That was an identity, a position. Now, saying I’m "helping out" feels different. It’s not about returning to that seat; it’s about... using what I’ve built up over the years to solve some problems. It feels much lighter.

I was particularly absorbed in the game today. Old Li’s knight was threatening my rook, and I suddenly saw the layout three moves ahead. My mind was racing while playing, and it felt exactly the same as when I was analyzing the new system parameters at the factory last week—it’s all about looking at the whole picture and finding that one critical point. It only occurred to me after the game that these two things aren't actually separate. This is just how my brain works, whether it’s on a chessboard or in quality control data.

Xiuying asked why I seemed so happy today, but I couldn't quite put it into words. Maybe it’s because I suddenly understood something—I don’t have to go back to being the Director, but I don’t have to leave that field entirely either. It can be like this: helping out occasionally, and spending the rest of my time gardening, playing chess, or accompanying her to the hospital. These things don’t conflict.

I’m taking Xiuying for her follow-up appointment tomorrow. The doctor said last time that her blood pressure is under control, but I still want to hear more of his advice. I need to keep up the habit of noting down new medical instructions on the calendar.

I’m a bit tired, but it’s that good, fulfilling kind of tired.

Xiuying, I wanted to tell you something.

Xiaoling taught me how to use my phone to record flower data over the National Day holiday, and I gave it a try. But... I kept asking her, "Will the photos get lost?" and "Is the data still there?" I asked so many times that she was about to lose her patience with me. Ha.

You know what? I even used a pencil to write down the steps for using the phone in my notebook, recording it step by step, even drawing question marks next to them. I find it a bit funny myself—using a notebook to record how to use a phone. It’s like... I just can’t let go of this notebook.

I’ve been thinking, maybe it’s not the phone’s fault. For thirty-seven years, I’ve used notebooks to record flowers and test data; everything was in my hands, clear as day. Now, being asked to use something I don't understand makes me panic. My daughter means well, but I...

Xiuying, when you retired from the factory, did you feel this way too? Like you couldn't keep up anymore? I’m still working, and I’m already starting to feel afraid. In another two years, when I actually retire, if I have to learn new things even for these small chores at home, what will I be able to do then?

I know there’s no way around it. That’s just how the times are. But I still feel a bit... unsettled.

Do you think I’m overthinking things?

November 14, 2022, Evening

I spent over an hour on the phone with Ming.

I originally just wanted to ask how business was at the building materials store this week, but we ended up talking about Xiuying’s blood pressure. I told him I’ve decided to stop recording her readings every single day. He was silent for a moment, and then he said, "Dad, letting go is also a form of love."

I didn't answer at the time. After hanging up, I sat on the sofa and couldn't stop thinking about those words.

What have I been doing all these years? I’ve used notebooks to log every time she takes her medicine, every instruction from the doctor, every single digit on the blood pressure monitor. I thought this was what it meant to care. But lately, Xiuying has told me a few times that she feels a bit tired—tired of being "cared for" by me. I didn't listen then; I thought she was just being polite.

Now I finally understand.

In my professional life, I’m used to controlling everything. Setting standards, supervising execution, recording data, identifying problems, and fixing them. For over thirty years, this methodology made my department the strictest in the company. I was proud of that. But... life is not a system that can be fully managed. Xiuying’s blood pressure, Ming’s business, my grandson’s upbringing... these aren't things I can fully master through planning and monitoring.

I have been trying to stay in control. Trying to gain a sense of security through that control. But now I realize that this approach doesn't work.

I’ve been so exhausted lately. Insomnia, anxiety. Files are still piled up in the office, but sometimes I just stare at them blankly. I wonder, have I been doing it wrong all along? Or is it just that... I need to learn how to let go?

Ming is right. Letting go is also a form of love. Perhaps what is needed more than my logs and monitoring is... trust. Trusting that Xiuying can take care of herself, trusting the doctors' expertise, and trusting the natural rhythm of life itself.

Tomorrow, I’m going to put those blood pressure logs away. It’s not that I don’t care; it’s just a different way of caring.

I’ve been sitting here thinking for a long time. It’s dark outside. Xiuying is in the bedroom watching TV. I can hear her voice; she sounds relaxed, without that feeling of being watched.

Maybe... this is the beginning.

# Key Points for Phone Usage - Must Remember

**October 26, 2022, Evening**

---

## How to Mark Up Photos
- Open Photos → Find the photo → Tap "Edit" in the top right → Look for the pen icon to write.
- Always double-check before saving; don't just close it.
- ⚠️ You must tap "Done" before closing, otherwise you will lose your work.

## Backup Steps (Important!)
1. Open Settings - Look for "Cloud Services" or "Backup" options.
2. Connect to Wi-Fi before starting; do not use cellular data.
3. A "✓" will appear once the backup is finished; it’s only successful when you see this.
4. **Check the backup status once a week** ← Very critical.

## What NOT to Do
- ❌ Don't tap the delete button randomly, especially the red one.
- ❌ Don't tap "Confirm" without reading carefully first.
- ❌ Don't force-close active apps right before turning off the phone.
- ❌ Don't force a shutdown even if the battery is low.

## Daily Checklist
- [ ] Check if photos are saved properly after turning on the phone in the morning.
- [ ] Check for new backup notifications in the afternoon.
- [ ] Confirm the phone is acting normally before bed.
- [ ] If "Storage Space Low" appears, delete some old photos.

---

## Keep Using the Notebook
Keep records in both the phone and the notebook; that’s the only way to be safe.
The notebook has been used for so many years and has never failed.
The phone is just an aid; don't rely on it completely.

---

April 10, 2023, 8:15 PM

Another night of poor sleep. My phone call with Ming yesterday keeps playing over and over in my head.

He talked about the new orders at the building materials store, going into quite a bit of detail. Usually, I would have asked about the pricing, checked on the competition, and helped him analyze how to maintain quality control. But I didn't. I just absentmindedly muttered, "Make sure you keep the quality up," and then thought about hanging up as soon as possible.

He noticed. He asked if I was too tired from work.

I said I was a little tired. Then I didn't say anything else.

After hanging up, I sat in my office for a long time without moving. I was thinking: what am I doing? Ming was opening up to me about his business, and I wasn't really listening. This isn't like me. I used to care about every decision he made; I’d take the initiative to offer advice. And now?

I admit I’ve been exhausted lately. That new automation system has me a bit overwhelmed; I’m struggling to learn it. The company’s restructuring is also making me uneasy. Then there’s my wife’s high blood pressure, and the fact that I’m not in Shanghai enough to be there for my grandson... all these things are piling up, and I feel like I don't have the strength to change any of it.

But that’s no excuse. I shouldn't project this sense of helplessness onto Ming. He needs a father who can listen and offer advice, not one who is mentally checked out.

I wonder, am I really getting old? Not in years, but in energy. I used to be able to handle work, family, and taking care of my wife all at once... now, a single system update can leave me in shambles.

Ming didn't say anything more, but I could feel his disappointment.

I’ll call him again tomorrow. I need to listen to him properly. No matter how tired I am, I can't just go through the motions when it comes to my son. That is my most basic responsibility as a father.

I have to find a way to adjust. I can't keep going on like this.

I had intended to demonstrate how to quickly generate a weekly report, but the interface froze, and the exported data came out as complete gibberish. I felt a bit flustered. Young Xiao Wang stepped in to help and sorted it out in just a few clicks. I just muttered, "The system might be a bit unstable," and then headed back to my office.

To be honest, it wasn't a system issue. It was that I hadn't memorized the operating procedure.

In the afternoon, I sat alone in my office and went over the error messages three times, re-recording every step of the process. I was pressing so hard with my pen that I nearly tore through the paper. It’s an uncomfortable feeling—making such a blunder in front of my subordinates. What must they think of me?

I’ve been in this department for over twenty years. I set the inspection standards; I streamlined the workflows. In the past, when I spoke about quality management in meetings, no one dared to interrupt. Now, a single new system is enough to make a fool of me in the conference room.

**Areas for Improvement:**
- I haven't fully mastered the steps for the data export module, especially the logic behind parameter filtering and format conversion. I need to spend more time mapping it out.
- I should test things multiple times before a demo; I can't just glance at the manual and expect to perform.
- Next time a problem arises, I shouldn't be vague. I should honestly admit it was my own operational error. This would also set an example for the younger staff—that there’s no shame in admitting a mistake.

**Next Steps:**
- Set aside half an hour every day this week to go through all five major modules of the system.
- Seek out Xiao Wang privately on Wednesday afternoon to clarify the parts I don't understand.
- Organize another small-scale demonstration next Friday; it needs to be handled more steadily this time.

Actually, I know this system has to be used sooner or later. This is the direction the company is heading, and I can't change that. Rather than constantly running into trouble, it’s better to take the initiative and learn it well. That way, I can give a proper account to the department and to myself.

It’s just that sometimes I wonder why these things always have to come so fast.

---

*End of Log*

June 30, 2024, 8:30 PM, Overcast

Sitting on the balcony, the flowers have all been watered. Another rose bloomed today—pink, and even fuller than the one from last week. I noted down the date and the flower's condition in my notebook. It’s a habit I can’t break, and I don’t want to.

Xiuying is in the inner room watching TV; I can hear the sound of the news. Her high blood pressure has been well-controlled this month, and the doctor said we can stick with the current plan. I marked the date for her next check-up on the calendar again, even though it’s still three weeks away.

I pulled out that summary I wrote last week and read it over. To be honest, I felt a bit embarrassed when I was writing those words. But looking at it now, there doesn't seem to be anything wrong with it. I’m truly no longer afraid of losing my identity as the "Director of Quality Control." Over the past month, I’ve realized that I am actually many things—I am Xiuying’s husband, the children’s father, my grandson’s grandfather, a friend to my fellow chess players, and now, a consultant to the new director. All these roles added together make me feel more useful than that job title ever did.

I took a video call from Jianhao this morning. The little guy made a great move using the strategy I taught him. Seeing that proud look on his face through the screen, I suddenly thought: this is the retirement life I wanted. Not big events, just small moments like this.

It’s a bit strange to say, but I’ve been used to plans and order my whole life. Now, my daily routine seems like nothing special—watering flowers, watching the news, playing chess, keeping Xiuying company, recording a few things—but this kind of order actually makes me feel more grounded. More grounded than I ever felt facing those inspection reports and complaints in the office.

Tomorrow I’m going to the community center to play Xiangqi. Old Wang said he wants to play three rounds with me. I’m looking forward to it.

Still, I find myself thinking about that group photo in the office, the one the department staff gave me ten years ago. Back then, I felt like that was my everything. Looking at it now, it was just a part of my life. A very important part, but not the whole thing.

Alright, time for bed. Xiuying is calling for me.

Xiuying,

I was out on the balcony watering the roses just now, and I found myself thinking about what you said yesterday.

You said I’m stricter than the doctor—that made me laugh. Honestly, you’re right. But I wanted to tell you, it’s not that I want to control you; it’s just that... I need to do this to have peace of mind.

For thirty-seven years at the factory, that’s just how I was. Standards had to be strict, and inspections had to be meticulous—that was the only way to ensure the product was flawless. Now that I’m retired, I’ve realized I can’t seem to break the habit. But I want to apply that habit to the person who matters most to me. Your blood pressure is just like the product quality I used to manage—there’s no room for carelessness.

I’ve come up with a plan for us. Every morning at 6:30, I’ll take your blood pressure and record it on the calendar. As for our diet, I’ve been doing some research; we need to cut back on salt and oil. You also need to take your blood pressure medication on time—I’ve marked the schedule on the pillbox for you. Also, when the weather is nice, let’s go downstairs for a stroll together. No need to rush, just a slow walk.

Don’t laugh at me. I just want you to stay healthy so you can be by my side for many more years to come.

What should we have for dinner? I’m thinking of making something light for you.

— Jianguo

# Parameter Adjustments for Quality Inspection System - Troubleshooting Strategy

**Date**: October 21, 2024  
**Time**: 6:45 AM  
**Subject**: Analysis of Data Fluctuations in the New System

---

## Issue Description
The new supervisor reported abnormal fluctuations in inspection data and inconsistent pass rate determinations. We need to identify the root cause.

## Preliminary Hypotheses (To be verified)

**Potential Cause 1 - Parameter Range Settings**
- Check if the tolerance bands match the actual production process.
- Were any product line standards missed during the last upgrade?
- Are the parameter thresholds too strict or too loose?

**Potential Cause 2 - Sensor Accuracy Issues**
- What is the calibration cycle for the sensors in the new system?
- Has any benchmarking been done against the old system?
- Could ambient temperature changes be affecting the readings? (We’ve encountered this before.)

**Potential Cause 3 - Algorithmic Logic**
- Data sampling interval settings.
- Rules for outlier exclusion.
- Is the sample size sufficient?

## Recommended Troubleshooting Steps

1. **Step 1 - Historical Data Comparison**  
   Pull the inspection records from the past month to look for patterns in the fluctuations. Determine if they are random or correlated with specific times/batches.

2. **Step 2 - On-site Verification**  
   Select several disputed products and re-test them using traditional methods to compare against the new system's data.

3. **Step 3 - System Audit**  
   Have the technicians check the sensor calibration status and the parameter configuration files.

4. **Step 4 - Incremental Adjustments**  
   Start with the most likely cause and adjust one item at a time, performing small-batch tests after each change.

---

## Information Required
- The exact timestamp when the fluctuations began.
- Which specific product models are affected?
- How long has it been since the new system went live?

---

*Mapping out the strategy first; we’ll dive deeper once he provides more details. This shouldn't be a major issue—the system itself is likely fine; it's probably just a matter of parameters or calibration.*

August 10, 2024  Sunny

I’ve been thinking about something Xiuying said last night.

"My Director Li has finally learned how to reflect," she said. There was a hint of teasing in her voice, but I could tell she wasn't mocking me.

As I sat on the balcony watering the plants, it suddenly hit me what I’ve been doing these past few days. I had filled my notebook with a dense health management plan—walking schedules, meal pairings, medication reminders, even calculating exactly how much salt she should consume. It was like drafting inspection standards back at the factory: line by line, with no room for negotiation.

Xiuying didn't resist; she never does. But yesterday, when I asked her, "Do you think an hour-long walk is too much?" something flickered in her eyes. In that moment, I realized—I was treating her like a product.

So, I made a decision. I took out my notebook and crossed out the title on the cover, "Health Management Plan for Wang Xiuying." I replaced it with "Our Health Plan." My handwriting was a bit shaky; my hand was trembling.

Then I asked her how she wanted to arrange the walks. She said, "You decide," but I told her, "No, this time we decide together." She looked at me and smiled. There was something in that smile I can’t quite put into words—maybe the feeling of being seen, or perhaps being trusted.

For over thirty years, I’ve been used to being in control. In the factory, control meant quality; it meant my worth. But home is different. Xiuying isn't a product; she’s my partner. I’ve always known this in principle, but knowing it and doing it are two different things.

This morning, she suggested an idea of her own—could we sit in the park for a while during our walk and look at the flowers? Instead of immediately pulling out my notebook to calculate the time, I just said, "Sure, let's go together."

She smiled again.

I realize I still have a lot to learn. But this time, I’m willing to take it slow.

Thursday, September 22, 2022 | Sunny

I spent the entire afternoon yesterday messing around with this, and I’m still feeling a bit frustrated.

My daughter was right—taking photos of the roses with my phone is definitely more convenient than using a camera. But the problem is, what happens after the photo is taken? I stare at the picture on my phone and have no idea how to link it with the date and my watering logs. Use the "Notes" app? I tried, but it’s just typing a few words; how is that any different from my notebook? I still have to pull out my physical journal and write down today’s date, the condition of the flowers, and the amount of water used. The end result is that I have a photo on my phone and a record in my notebook—doing both is actually more of a hassle than before.

I asked my daughter if the data might suddenly vanish. She said no, mentioning something about "cloud backups." I don’t really understand it. My notebook sits right there on the balcony; I can see it, I can touch it, and in thirty years, I haven’t lost a single word. This phone is a black box. I don’t know how it works, so how can I trust it?

I was still thinking about this when I got up at 6:30 this morning. The watering cycle for the autumn roses needs to be adjusted, but I have to figure out this recording system first. Otherwise, if I can’t even manage my own data, how am I supposed to give my son advice on his business?

Deep down, I know my daughter means well. She’s so busy in Shanghai, yet she still worries about these little things for me. I also know that times are changing and all the young people use phones. But this old brain of mine is slow to learn. I’ve mastered the camera part, but as for how to organize everything on the phone and ensure it’s safe... I’m still a bit uneasy.

Whatever, I’ll just try both. I’ll keep using the notebook and try to use the phone at the same time. Keeping both records means nothing can go wrong. I don’t have that many years left anyway; I’ll just treat it as a way to keep myself busy.

It’s just that sometimes I wonder: if I can’t even learn a small thing like this, will I fall even further behind in my retirement?

August 23, 2023, Evening

I’ve been thinking about this afternoon’s meeting.

My supervisor patted me on the shoulder in the conference room and said I had good insights into the new system. He asked if I could help explain to the new Director how to integrate the system with our actual inspection workflows. I was stunned for a moment—I’ll admit, when I was staring at those interfaces and data tables a while back, I kept thinking, "This is too complicated; I might really be falling behind."

But hearing him say that made me realize: I haven’t been phased out.

What was I thinking? I was thinking that in these thirty-seven years, I’ve learned more than just how to spot a defect in a product. I’ve learned how to think—how to find patterns in a pile of data, and how to deduce potential loopholes in an entire process from a single detail. These are things a system cannot learn. A system is just a tool.

I spent the afternoon organizing several key cases I’ve worked on over the years, thinking about how to explain them using the "language" of the new system. It suddenly clicked—I’m not being replaced by the new system; I’m just doing the same things in a new way. That thought brought me a huge sense of relief.

Xiuying asked me what was wrong, seeing me standing on the balcony for so long. I didn't explain much, just said I’d figured something out regarding work. She poured me a cup of tea and didn't press further. That was enough.

I’ve been thinking about retirement. Before, the thought of it felt empty—as if life were just going to come to a halt. But it’s different now. If I can pass this experience on to the new Director, helping him master the system faster so our inspection standards stay ahead in this new era... if I can do that, I’ll have no regrets when I retire.

I also thought of Jianhao. That kid is ten now, and he’s much quicker with a smartphone than I am. I’ve always felt a bit embarrassed about being out of touch in that department. But today I thought, maybe I don’t need to compete with him over who’s better at using a phone. I can teach him how to think through problems and how to look at complex situations. On that front, his grandfather still has plenty to teach.

Xiaoming’s business has been under pressure again lately; I mentioned it to my chess buddies on Wednesday. I think I have a deeper understanding now of "how to find a breakthrough in difficult times" than I did before. When he calls next time, I can have a good talk with him.

A new page in my notebook. Tomorrow, we continue.

Thursday, September 8, 2022 | Sunny

Yesterday afternoon, I tried taking some photos of the Chinese roses with my phone. I’d written down all the steps my daughter taught me during the National Day holiday, but actually doing it felt clumsy. I spent a good while frowning at the screen, pressed the wrong button twice, and it took three tries just to get one clear shot. After I finished, I still turned around and took out my notebook to record today’s watering, the bud count, and the color of the leaves. Only then did I feel a sense of peace.

When Xiuying called me for dinner this afternoon, I remembered to check if the photo had actually saved. I scrolled around for ages but couldn't find it. My heart immediately sank—is this thing even reliable? Could the data just vanish into thin air? Xiuying came over to help; she tapped a few times and found it in something called the "Gallery." Looking at that photo, I kept repeating, "Thank goodness it’s still there, thank goodness." Xiuying laughed at me, saying I was worrying over nothing.

But I just can’t shake the feeling. For over twenty years, my notebooks have never let me down. Every single entry is right there; you just flip the page and see it. But this phone? I have no idea where the photos are actually stored. How can I be sure they won't just disappear one day? If they ever did, I wouldn't even have a backup. So, I’ve decided to stick with my notebook and just use the phone as a backup. Using both systems is the only way I can feel at ease.

I know my daughter means well. She says using a phone is more convenient and allows for instant recording. But at my age, learning new things is just slow. I can give it a try, but I can’t rely on it completely. Besides, I’m retiring in two years anyway; there’s no need to force a change over something so small.

Tomorrow, I’ll continue with the same routine: notebook plus phone. The autumn roses need their watering cycle adjusted. The flowers are looking good this month; I’ll keep observing.

Xiuying, I’ve been thinking—we really need to step up our exercise. Taking medicine alone isn't enough; we need to get moving.

I did some research, and it says walking is especially effective for people with high blood pressure. We used to go twice a week, but I think we should increase it to three times. How about we go to the park together for a stroll at 3:00 PM every Monday, Wednesday, and Friday?

I’ve already marked it on the calendar. Since it’s so hot lately, we’ll just walk slowly in the shade; there’s no rush. I also looked into it and found that we should avoid salty foods when we're focusing on walking, so I’ll be more mindful when I cook from now on.

Don’t worry, this is just a normal part of managing things. A lot of people go through this. If we stick with it, I’m sure the numbers will look much better when we go back to the hospital for a check-up next month.

I’ll wait for you downstairs at 3:00 PM tomorrow. Remember to put on some comfortable shoes.

Back home that evening, Li Jianguo changed into his slippers and sat on the sofa. The sound of Wang Xiuying tidying up the dishes drifted in from the kitchen. He glanced at the date circled in red on the calendar, then quickly looked away.

When his wife emerged from the kitchen, he turned on the TV with the remote, keeping the volume low.

"Xiuying, there was a meeting at the company today," he said, his tone as casual as if he were talking about the weather.

Wang Xiuying sat down beside him and handed him a cup of tea. "Another meeting?"

"Yeah. They said that within three months, the entire inspection system will be replaced with a new automated one." He paused, blowing on his tea. "Three months to switch over every single process."

Wang Xiuying nodded, not speaking immediately.

"The system is a bit complicated," Li Jianguo continued, his voice dropping slightly. "I looked through the manual, and I have to admit, it’s a bit of a struggle for this old brain to wrap around." He gave a self-deprecating laugh, but it didn't reach his eyes.

"You’ve made it through all these years; you can learn this too," Wang Xiuying said, patting his hand.

Li Jianguo didn't respond right away. He stared at the TV screen, though he clearly wasn't watching. After a moment, he spoke again:

"Only two years left until retirement. Learning this system now..." He didn't finish the sentence, just shook his head. "It feels like experience doesn't count for much anymore. We used to judge product quality based on experience; now, it seems like the machines have the final say."

He picked up his teacup and took a sip.

"I don't know what more I can contribute to the company for these last two years," he said softly. "Maybe they’ve been preparing for the newcomers for a long time already."

Wang Xiuying didn't interrupt him. She knew that when her husband was like this, he didn't need many words. He just needed her there.

"Tomorrow I’ll go find the people in the tech department and see if they can explain things to me in more detail," Li Jianguo added. "I have to learn it, one way or another." There was a sense of helpless persistence in his voice.

He turned to look at his wife, his eyes weary but filled with a silent longing—hoping she would say, *It’s okay, you can definitely do it.*

The evening news was playing on the TV, the only sound in the living room. Li Jianguo’s fingers tapped lightly on the armrest of the sofa, the rhythm slow and steady.

# Diary

Friday, November 25, 2022

This morning, Xiao Li (the new director) came by again to ask me about the inspection procedures. To be honest, this wasn't the first time. I used to find it a bit annoying, wondering why he had so many questions, but today I felt differently.

Sitting in my office, I looked at that group photo from ten years ago on the wall and the quality achievement charts from over the years. It suddenly hit me that maybe I shouldn't just be answering his questions; I should be systematically teaching him everything. While I’m still here, I should pass down what I’ve accumulated over thirty-plus years.

I shared this idea with him. He listened intently, his eyes lighting up. We looked over those achievement charts together, and I told him the stories behind every number—how we made certain improvements, why we did things that way, and the challenges we faced. He took meticulous notes in his notebook.

I realized that as I was talking, I no longer felt like I was "leaving" something behind. Instead, it felt like I was doing something very important. It’s like passing an object from one hand to another; it’s still there, just with a new person to guard it.

This afternoon, I drafted a plan in my notebook—a list of things I want to systematically teach him before I retire. As I was writing, I came to understand that the work I’ve done all these years won't just vanish because I retire. It will stay here in this department, carried on by these people.

It’s a bit strange to say. Just a few days ago, I was still agonizing over that smartphone issue, feeling old and out of touch. But now, I feel a sense of relief. Maybe I don’t need to learn every new thing. I have my own expertise, and that is also very valuable.

Before Xiao Li left, he said he hoped I could still drop by occasionally to give some guidance after I retire. I nodded, feeling a warmth in my heart.

I’ll continue organizing the materials tomorrow. Some things must be written down clearly.

# Diary

June 16, 2024 | Sunny

Woke up at 6:30 AM, same as usual. Xiuying was still asleep, so I didn’t turn on the lights and felt my way out to the balcony in the dark.

The Chinese roses have bloomed. It’s the red one. I remember my last entry was on May 28th; the buds were still tightly closed then. This morning, just as the light hit them, I realized they had fully opened.

I stood there and watched them for a long time. I didn't grab my notebook; I just watched.

Suddenly, a thought popped into my head: *The rose blooms slowly, just as life unfolds slowly.*

I was a bit surprised at where that came from. Usually, my notes are things like "watering date," "pests on leaves," or "needs pruning." Why did I think of that today?

Over the past few weeks, I feel like I’ve been in a rush to find something. Rushing to find a new identity, rushing to prove I’m still useful, rushing to fill that void. Every day I’m thinking—what else can I do? Who else can I do something for? When I flip through old photo albums and see that group photo from the office, my chest feels tight.

But standing on the balcony looking at those roses, it suddenly occurred to me—I don’t need to rush to find a new identity. Perhaps identity itself is formed gradually through this process. Like this flower, it doesn't bloom all at once; it opens bit by bit. I am the same right now, opening up bit by bit.

When Xiuying came out, I was still standing there. She didn't say anything, just rested her hand on my shoulder from behind. She knows what’s been on my mind lately, even though we haven't really talked about it.

I turned around to look at her, and she gave me a smile. It was faint, but very warm.

Maybe I should start recording different things. Not just data, but... feelings.

Need to fertilize the roses today.

Xiao Ming, I just had a video call with Xiao Hao. They’re off for Christmas over there, and the little guy was absolutely thrilled.

I told him a story—one from back when I was working in the workshop. There was this tolerance standard for a part; everyone else said I was being too strict and that it would slow down production. But I stood my ground because I knew it was the only way to guarantee quality. Later, when the feedback from the client came back glowing, everyone finally understood why I did it.

Xiao Hao listened so intently. He asked me, "Grandpa, do you regret it?" I told him, "No, I don't. It’s always worth it to stand by what’s right." The little guy nodded and said he wants to stand by what’s right when he grows up, too.

Heh, sometimes I think the things our generation went through are actually useful for the kids. It’s not about preaching big philosophies; it’s these little stories, one by one, that show them how to be a person of character.

How are things at the building materials store lately? Is the competition still as tough as before?

# Wednesday, June 12, 2024 - Sunny

I went to the community activity room to play chess this afternoon, and my mind drifted again.

Old Wang was sitting across from me. Right from the opening, I just couldn't find my focus. I’ve been playing Xiangqi for over twenty years, but for the first time, the board felt like a foreign land. The Central Cannon, the Screen Horse—routines I could play with my eyes closed—I kept messing them up today. Old Wang didn't say anything, but I could tell he was letting me win. That felt even worse.

Halfway through the game, I suddenly realized what I was thinking about—I was wondering where I should hang that department group photo from the office. The living room is too conspicuous, but the bedroom feels too private. With that distraction, I moved my horse to the wrong spot. Old Wang let out a sigh. He didn't say anything harsh, but that sigh spoke louder than words.

He said something that I’m still turning over in my mind: "It’s normal. Give yourself some time; you’ll adapt slowly."

I nodded and pretended to keep playing, but my eyes felt a bit hot. I didn't let him see. People like us don't really show that kind of thing in public.

On the way home, it hit me—I’m not the Director of the Quality Inspection Department anymore. That identity is just... gone. Thirty-seven years. I started as a front-line operator and worked my way up to Director, and now it’s suddenly over. Rationally, I know this is right; this is what retirement is. But emotionally... I haven't figured out who I am yet.

I still woke up at 6:30 this morning. I watched the news for ten minutes, had breakfast, and watered the Chinese roses on the balcony. I wrote another entry in my logbook—the date of watering and the condition of the flowers. These daily records all look the same, but they aren't. Before, I’d have to rush to the office after watering the flowers; now, after I’m done, there’s nothing left to do.

Xiuying asked me how the chess game went. I said it was fine. She didn't push further. She can probably tell.

I wanted to call Jianhao tonight to ask how he’s doing, but I didn't know what to say. He’s in Shanghai and I’m here, so far apart. I used to think about spending more time with him after I retired, but now... now that I have all the time in the world, I don't know how to use it.

I’m still thinking about what Old Wang said. "Give yourself some time." Maybe he’s right. Maybe I just need time to get used to this new version of myself—a self without an office, without inspection reports, and without subordinates.

I’m still me. It’s just... I haven't quite figured it all out yet.

# Work Log

**Date:** Friday, November 3, 2023  
**Weather:** Sunny  
**Recorded by:** Li Jianguo

---

## Morning Summary

This morning, we held a summary meeting for the Quality Department's process optimization. I presented the proposal for integrating the new system with traditional inspection methods to senior management and the entire department.

**Key Data:**
- Pass rate increased to 99.2% (up 0.8 percentage points from before the reform)
- Inspection time reduced by 23%
- Customer complaint rate dropped by 15%

These numbers are tangible. Since we began piloting the new process in September, I have kept a close eye on every single link. My subordinates’ execution has also been spot-on—especially the younger inspectors; they grasp the system much faster than I do.

## Meeting Reflections

After hearing the report, management proposed rolling out this process company-wide. I was a bit surprised at first, but on second thought, it proves that this methodology truly has value.

During the meeting, I said something that even caught me off guard: "This is exactly what our generation should be doing."

Thinking about it now, I’ve spent all these years in inspection being meticulous and maintaining strict standards. But that was mostly about holding the line. Now, it’s different—I am using 37 years of experience to guide how the new system can work better, and how the younger generation can make smarter judgments based on that system. This isn't just about adapting to new things; it’s genuine innovation.

I used to be a bit resistant to those new systems, thinking they were overly complex. But over the past few months, I’ve gradually come to see it clearly: the system has its advantages, and human experience has its value. Combining the two is where real progress lies.

## Afternoon Work

After the meeting, several subordinates came to ask about specific details. I answered them one by one. Xiao Wang, in particular, wanted to know how to make secondary judgments based on system data. I shared some of my old empirical methods with him, and he listened very intently.

During this process, it suddenly occurred to me that it would be quite meaningful to continue participating in this kind of work as a consultant after I retire. I wouldn't necessarily need to be in the office every day.

## Personal Reflections

I felt quite energized today. This meeting gave me a renewed sense of professional mission. It’s not that my previous work lacked meaning, but rather that I feel my value has been redefined—I’m not just an inspector anymore, but someone who can guide innovation.

I’ll talk to Xiuying about this idea when I get home tonight. Also, I need to organize today’s meeting notes into the archives; this process proposal might become the company standard in the future.

---

**Remarks:** Tomorrow, continue following up on the pilot implementation of the new system in other sections. Prepare a detailed rollout plan for next Monday.
