**Created**: March 26, 2023, Evening  
**Status**: Confirmed

August 30, 2023, past 8:00 PM

Master Wang said something during our chess game today that has been on my mind ever since.

He said, "Looks like you’re keeping up with the times." I just smiled and didn't think much of it then. But now, sitting alone on the balcony after watering the roses, the weight of those words is finally sinking in.

To be honest, I was beyond frustrated when I first started messing around with that new system at the office. I felt like my thirty-plus years of craftsmanship were being backed into a corner by a machine. I wasn't sleeping well for a while, and Xiu-ying noticed. I didn't say anything to her, but she knew.

But today, while playing chess, I found myself bringing up the system out of the blue. I said it was "actually quite good" and that it "helps us spot things we might otherwise overlook." Master Wang and the others listened intently and even asked me how to use it. As I explained it to them, I surprised myself—I realized I’ve actually started to understand the thing.

Finally, I said, "We can't just cling to the old ways forever. New things have their own merits; we have to learn how to use them."

The moment I said that, something felt different. It wasn't that forced "I have to learn" feeling anymore; it was genuine acceptance. It was as if... I was no longer an old man being left behind by the times, but an experienced professional learning something new. There is a world of difference between those two feelings.

Master Wang told me, "You've got a good mindset." I didn't give much of a response at the time, but I felt a sense of peace in my heart.

Sitting here now, I’m thinking that maybe there’s still something I can contribute before I retire. It’s not about mastering every single new technology, but... I can use my thirty-seven years of experience to help the younger generation use these new tools more effectively. That way, when I leave, the department won't fall into chaos just because I'm gone. The thought makes me feel like I still have value.

Xiu-ying just asked me what I was thinking about out here on the balcony. I told her "nothing much." But really, I was thinking that maybe getting older doesn't have to mean becoming obsolete. Maybe it just means needing a different perspective on yourself.

I’ll get back to learning that system tomorrow. No rush. One step at a time.

# Notebook Entry

**Wednesday, September 28, 2022, 8:30 AM**

A large patch of leaves on that rose bush turned yellow yesterday. I flipped through my notebook—the last time I watered it was September 24th. Based on the autumn cycle, that should have been fine. I checked the soil carefully; it’s still damp.

I’m wondering if it’s this phone that’s been messing with my head lately, constantly switching between the notebook and fumbling with the phone to take pictures. Xiaoling insisted I use the phone to keep track, saying "that way the data won't get lost and you can check it anytime." I gave it a try and took a few photos, but I have no idea how those words ended up in the memo app—it’s just a total mess.

The result? I kept switching back and forth between the phone and the notebook, and ended up getting confused about the plant's condition. I should have spotted the problem yesterday, but it got dragged out until today.

I’ve recorded the situation in my notebook anyway—**September 28: Rose leaves turning yellow, possibly due to overwatering or poor ventilation. Moved to a new spot, reduced watering frequency.**

This feels more grounded.

I’ve used notebooks for over twenty years and never had a mishap like this. The data is crystal clear—when to water, when to fertilize, when to prune—it’s all there at a glance. As for this phone, I’m still not convinced. If the data just vanished one day, I wouldn’t even know how to get it back.

Xiaoling will probably call this weekend. I need to think about how to tell her. I can’t just say the phone is hard to use—she’ll definitely say, "That’s exactly why you should use it, so you don't miss anything." My old brain is slow to learn new things, but it’s not like I’m completely hopeless.

Better to just keep the notebook records up to date for now. This, I can handle.

Xiuying, I wanted to tell you something. The new director called today. He said there are some issues with the products coming off the line under the new inspection system, and he’s hoping I can go in and take a look.

I was a bit hesitant at first. You know I’m so close to retirement; I really just wanted to settle down and stay home with you. But he was so sincere—he said, "Your input is truly vital to us." Hearing that... it felt different. It’s not that I’m looking to go back to work full-time, it’s not that. It’s just the feeling that the experience I’ve built up over thirty-odd years still has some value.

So, I agreed to head over there tomorrow.

Don’t worry, I’ve thought this through. I’m not going back to being a director; I have no interest in that. I’ll just be there as a consultant to observe and offer suggestions. If they have a problem, I’ll help analyze it, and that’s it. I won’t be going every day, and I won’t let it mess up our life.

To be honest, I was thinking the consulting fees could help supplement our pensions. Your blood pressure needs regular check-ups, and those costs add up. And then there’s Jianhao—I want to be able to visit him in Shanghai more often, and this money could help with that. He’ll be eleven next year; they really do grow up fast.

I just feel like this is the right decision. It won't disrupt our current routine, and I get to do something meaningful. What do you think?

We can talk more about it over dinner. I’ll walk you through the details of those quality issues then.

Xiuying, I wanted to talk to you about something...

Lately, while I’ve been out on the balcony watering the plants, I’ve been thinking. You know I’ve been at the factory for thirty-seven years now; I know quality inspection inside out. But I heard recently that the company might be bringing in some kind of automated system, and it’s got me wondering...

At my age, do you think I can still keep up with all that new stuff?

It’s not that I’m afraid of hard work—you know how I am. It’s just that I don’t feel steady about it. With the way things used to be, I knew where I stood. You’d hand me a report, and I could spot a problem at a glance. But these systems, all this data... I feel like I can’t see through them. Look at our son—he handles his phone like it’s nothing, while I can’t even press the right buttons.

And another thing... I’ve been wondering if we’re prepared enough. For after I retire, I mean. The house is ours, so that’s fine. But you’re always on medication for your high blood pressure—what if we need better medical care down the road? I go with you for your check-ups every month, and the doctor says we need to keep it under control, but I’m still a bit worried.

Maybe we should find some time to go over the household accounts properly? I just want to know exactly where we stand.

Sigh, maybe I’m just overthinking things lately. This morning when you asked if I wanted an extra egg, I didn’t even hear you clearly and had to ask you to repeat yourself. That’s not like me.

Don’t worry, I just wanted to hear what you think. What do you say?

# Health Management Plan for Wang Xiuying

**Date Created:** July 5, 2024  
**Duration:** Long-term  

---

## I. Blood Pressure Monitoring Standards

| Item | Standard | Notes |
|------|----------|-------|
| Measurement Time | Daily at 6:50 AM and 8:30 PM | Fixed times; margin of error within 5 minutes |
| Measurement Site | Left arm | Seated, back supported, feet flat on the floor |
| Target Values | Systolic < 140, Diastolic < 90 | Per doctor's orders |
| Recording Method | Calendar + Phone Memo | Dual records; bring the calendar to the hospital for monthly check-ups |

---

## II. Dietary Control Plan

**Principles:** Low salt, low oil, light flavors  

**Prohibited List:**
- Pickled/Preserved foods (pickled vegetables, salted fish, cured meats)
- Processed meats (luncheon meat, ham)
- Deep-fried foods
- High-sodium seasonings  

**Weekly Menu Framework:**
- Mon/Wed/Fri: Noodle soup/Plain porridge + Green vegetables
- Tue/Thu/Sat: Steamed fish/Poached chicken + Winter melon soup
- Sun: Regular home-cooked meals (strictly controlled salt intake)  

**To-Learn List:**
- [ ] Research recipes for hypertension patients (consult the community health center next week)
- [ ] Identify high-potassium foods (beneficial for blood pressure)
- [ ] Verify the salt content in soy products  

---

## III. Exercise Schedule

| Time | Activity | Frequency | Duration |
|------|----------|-----------|----------|
| Tue / Thu | Walking (around the community) | 2x per week | 30 minutes |
| Daily | Household chores | Daily | As needed |

**Walking Route:** Home → Community North Gate → Along the river → Return (approx. 1.5 km)  

---

## IV. Hospital Follow-up Plan

- **Frequency:** Once a month
- **Next Date:** August 5
- **Items to Bring:** Blood pressure logbook, Medical Insurance Card, previous test reports
- **Key Questions:** Is the blood pressure stable? Is medication adjustment needed? Are there any other dietary restrictions?  

---

## V. Daily Reminders

✓ Ask "How are you feeling today?" every morning while taking her blood pressure.  
✓ Check the shopping list when buying groceries; do not buy high-sodium foods.  
✓ Prepare comfortable walking shoes in advance on Tuesdays and Thursdays.  
✓ Review blood pressure data at the end of the month to check for abnormal fluctuations.  

---

**Note:** This plan is just like the inspection standards I set at the factory—clear criteria, orderly execution, and regular improvements. Xiuying's health is the top priority.

   - One per week, not "never again"
   - Reduce salt intake from other sources on those days
   - This way, she won't feel deprived

3. **Cooking Methods — Learn to replace salt with other flavors**
   - Vinegar, lemon, spices
   - Xiuying said she doesn't necessarily need things salty; it’s just a habit
   - I need to experiment more with new recipes

4. **Tracking Method — A change in approach**
   - No more lists of "violations"
   - Change it to "What we ate today and blood pressure levels"
   - Look for patterns, not mistakes

---

**Reflections:**

In the factory, strict standards guarantee quality. But at home, flexibility is sometimes more important than perfection. Xiuying has stayed by my side for over thirty years, not because I’m a good manager, but because we built a life together. Taking care of her should be about companionship, not management.

When we go for her follow-up next week, I’ll ask the doctor: will one salted duck egg a week really affect her blood pressure? That way, I can give her a reasonable answer based on facts, not just my own rules.

September 22, 2024  Sunday  Sunny

Last night, I pulled out that notebook again—the one I bought right after I retired. Flipping to the last few pages, I saw the things I wrote three months ago: "Project List," "Health Management Plan," "Companionship Schedule." Back then, I was still using my old quality control methods to map out my life, as if I were designing a production process. Looking at them now, I can’t help but chuckle.

But it’s not just a laugh.

Yesterday, Jianhao called and said he wanted me to teach him how to play Xiangqi. I was thrilled and agreed immediately. After I hung up, Xiuying asked me what I was smiling about, and only then did I realize the grin hadn't left my face. I told her, "Our grandson wants to learn chess." Xiuying said, "Look at you—retired for three months and you still get excited like it’s a work assignment." I told her it wasn’t work; it was... I paused for a moment before I figured out how to say it—it was life.

That’s the difference.

Back at the factory, I used a "project management" mindset for everything, including taking Xiuying to the hospital or calling Li Ming. At the time, I thought that was being "responsible." Now I realize it was actually a form of escape—I was using work methods to handle life so that I wouldn't have to truly feel life itself.

Recently, I started a new list in the same notebook. But this time, it’s different. Instead of a "Health Management Plan," I wrote "Days with Xiuying." Instead of a "Companionship Schedule," I wrote "Stories with my Grandson." They look similar on paper, but they feel completely different. One is a task; the other is living.

While watering the flowers on the balcony this morning, it suddenly hit me—I’ve used a logbook to manage those Chinese roses for years, but I never once thought of them as a "project." I simply love watching them grow, noting the dates, and seeing the buds slowly bloom. Why did I feel the need to "manage" the most important parts of my life instead?

Maybe that is what I’ve truly learned over these past three months. It’s not about how to plan retirement; it’s about letting go of the habit of needing to "control" and "optimize" everything.

Xiuying’s blood pressure has been stable again this month; the doctor said she’s doing great. I still mark it on the calendar, and I still go with her for check-ups. But now I know that this isn't a "project"—it’s just our way of being alive together.

The quality of life isn't determined by one's job title. I truly understand that now.

Xiuying, come sit with me for a moment.

Xiaoling called just now. She said Jianhao has been sharing stories about his grandfather at school. The teacher even asked him if they were true. Hearing that... how should I put it? It gave me a strange feeling.

Do you remember? A while back, I mentioned wanting to record some of my memories. At the time, I wasn't really sure what the point was. But Xiaoling said something today. She said, "Dad, you’ve changed lately. It’s like you’ve found something." I thought about it, and I think she’s right.

She also said, "Something like this would be a lifelong treasure for your grandson."

I never really thought that the things I talk about could mean so much to them. All those years at the factory, my only focus was doing the job right and keeping the quality up. But now... things feel different. I have this new rhythm to my life—video chatting with Jianhao three times a week, playing chess on Wednesdays, watering the flowers every day, and taking you to the hospital. These things seem ordinary, but they all feel like they have a new meaning now.

Knowing that Jianhao is sharing my stories at school made me realize that maybe I really should record these things properly. Not just for him, but... well, I just feel like these things shouldn't just disappear.

What do you think? Am I overthinking this?

Still, I feel like maybe I should keep going with it.

September 12, 2024, Evening

I was playing chess at the community center with Old Wang and the others this afternoon—the same old game, same old faces. But today felt a little different.

Old Wang asked me how retirement was treating me, and I just blurted out, "I used to think retirement would be boring, but now I realize that the best parts of life aren't found in work." The moment the words left my mouth, I stunned myself. Since when did I start talking like that?

I never would have thought this way before. Thirty-seven years at the factory—every standard, every report in the Quality Inspection Department was where my value lay. Even now, having left that position, I still occasionally think of that ten-year-old group photo on the office wall and the respect my subordinates had for me. But now… it doesn't seem that important anymore.

What surprised me most was Xiuying. A while back, I drew up a health plan for her: when to take her blood pressure, when to go for walks, what to eat… At the time, I thought that was the best way to take care of her. But recently, she took the initiative and said to me, "Let’s go for a walk together." That one sentence meant so much more than the blood pressure readings themselves. I realized I’d been using my quality-inspector mindset to "manage" her health instead of actually living life with her.

While playing chess today, I mentioned this to Old Wang and the guys—how Xiuying has been asking to go for walks lately, and how I felt that mattered more than the numbers. As I spoke, I noticed my tone had changed. It didn't feel like I was "reporting on work" anymore; it felt… warm, like I was sharing something that truly made me happy. Old Wang and the others laughed, teasing me that I was "finding my stride in my old age."

I didn't argue. But I was thinking: it’s not about "finding my stride." It’s that I finally understand—life isn't a project, and it’s not about executing a plan perfectly. Walking with Xiuying, listening to her talk about the new plants she’s managed to keep alive, seeing the expression on her face—that is what life is.

I used to think my value came from doing things flawlessly. Now I know that perhaps real value lies in living life, however imperfectly, with the person closest to you.

It’s a strange feeling. I’ve lost that sense of "control," but it feels like I’ve gained something far more important.

Another walk with Xiuying tomorrow. No need to check the watch, no need to keep a log. Just walking.

Monday, June 10, 2024. Sunny.

When I woke up at 6:30 this morning, it finally hit me—I don’t have to go to work today.

The thought flashed through my mind in the bathroom, and I just stood there, toothbrush halfway to my mouth, not knowing what to do next. I finished washing up anyway, simply because it’s what I’ve done every single morning for thirty-seven years. Xiuying was still asleep, so I went out to the balcony to water the plants. The leaves on the Chinese roses didn’t look quite right. I pulled out my notebook to jot it down, but my pen stalled in mid-air—watering dates, plant conditions, fertilization schedules… what’s the point of these records anymore? I used to water the flowers while thinking about things at the office. And now?

I put the notebook down.

I watched the news for ten minutes and had breakfast. Xiuying asked what my plans were for today; I told her I didn't have any. She said that was good, that I could finally get some rest. I nodded, but the truth is, I don’t know how to "rest."

I spent the morning pacing around. First, I thought about playing a game of Xiangqi, but today isn't Wednesday. I thought about calling Li Ming to ask how things were going at his building materials store, but then I realized the timing wasn't right—he’d definitely be busy. I thought about flipping through last year’s gardening logs to see if I’d missed anything, but I put them down after two pages. That’s all in the past now.

I sat on the sofa, staring at the department photo from the office. It was taken ten years ago. Taped below it were the annual quality performance charts I’d compiled myself. Now, all these things are packed in a cardboard box sitting on top of the bedroom wardrobe. I thought about hanging the photo somewhere in the house, but I couldn't figure out where it would fit.

I sat there like that for three hours.

At lunch, Xiuying asked if I was feeling unwell. I told her no, just a bit tired. She poured me a bowl of soup. As I drank it, I wondered: Is this what retirement is? Just watering plants, watching the news, eating, and sitting around?

Rationally, I know this is normal and that it takes time to adjust. But right now, I just feel… out of place. It’s as if the office door has closed behind me, and I haven’t figured out which way to walk yet.

Try again tomorrow.

# Diary

Sunday, September 8, 2024  Sunny

3:30 PM. Just stepped out of the study. I was on a video call with Jianhao for a full forty minutes.

I had intended to teach him the basic moves of Chinese chess, but I ran into trouble with the phone screen—I couldn't get it to rotate no matter what I did. I was getting a bit flustered, trying to figure out how to show him the board. Jianhao, on the other hand, was very patient. "Grandpa, try swiping like this," he said. I followed his lead, and the screen flipped.

In that moment, I was a bit stunned.

"I guess I have things to learn from you, too," I said. The moment the words left my mouth, I surprised myself. Back at the factory, I never would have said something like that. I was the director then; I set all the processes and standards. I was used to that feeling—knowing how to do things, doing them, and doing them perfectly.

But this time, I found I wasn't uncomfortable at all. In fact, I felt... relieved?

Jianhao told me about playing chess at school recently. He mentioned losing to a classmate and asked me why the "Horse" moves in such a peculiar way. Instead of giving him the answer directly, I asked, "Why do you think that is?" He thought for a moment and said maybe it’s because the Horse needs to protect itself, so it moves differently.

I said, "That’s a good question. Let’s think about it together."

Xiuying heard me say this from the kitchen and gave me a smiling glance from the doorway. She didn't say anything, but I knew what she was thinking.

After the call ended, I took out my notebook, intending to record something as I usually do. But instead of writing down watering dates or the condition of the flowers, I wrote a single sentence:

"My conversation with Jianhao reminded me that sometimes, being taught is also a form of learning."

I stared at the sentence, my pen resting there.

For thirty-seven years, I have defined my value through "perfect execution." Every standard in the Quality Department, every report—it all had to meet my requirements. It made me feel secure; it made me feel useful.

But now... I don't know if it's because retirement is approaching, but I’m starting to feel that perhaps being "useful" isn't just about "managing things well." Maybe imagining why the Horse moves the way it does with Jianhao, or deciding what to eat for the weekend with Xiuying—maybe those things are useful, too.

Perhaps even more so.

Xiuying just asked if I wanted to go for a walk downstairs. In the past, I would have checked my schedule first and considered the time. This time, I just said yes. We walked together, her arm in mine, chatting about trivial things—which vendor's vegetables had gone up in price, which school the neighbor’s granddaughter got into.

I didn't try to "manage" this time, nor did I think about what I was getting out of it. We were just... walking.

On the way back, it suddenly occurred to me that maybe this is what I’ve been looking for all along. Not a perfect plan, but the feeling of just being with her.

The sun is still bright. I think I’ll finish writing this and then watch the news for a bit.

Tomorrow, I have to take Xiuying to the hospital for her follow-up checkup.

That’s all for today’s entry.

Xiuying, I’ve been thinking it over and wanted to reach out.

I’ve been replaying what you said the other day in my head. You didn't try to lecture me; you just sat there quietly by my side and told me I didn't need to be so afraid. My mind was a bit of a mess at the time, but your words have slowly started to sink in.

I’ve made up my mind—I’m going to learn this new system properly. Instead of sitting around overthinking things, I’d rather put my energy into what I can actually do. You were right; the work I’ve done all these years doesn't just lose its value because of a new system. I’ve made peace with it now.

Also, thank you for the braised pork you made the other day. I know you were worried too, yet you were still thinking about what I like to eat. When you held my hand during dinner, you didn't say anything, but I felt it.

Once I retire, we could think about spending more time in Shanghai with our grandson. I remembered Xiao Hao saying he wanted to learn Xiangqi—I can teach him. Thinking about it that way makes me feel much more grounded.

Go to sleep early, don't wait up for me. I feel much better now.

— Jianguo

# Saturday, November 12

**Allowing Xiuying a bit of salted duck egg — A shift**

Had a long talk with Xiuying this afternoon. She mentioned she wanted some salted duck egg. In the past, I would have refused her outright. Today, I didn't. Instead, I told her she could have a small amount. She was a bit surprised, and honestly, so was I.

Thinking back, maybe I’ve been too black-and-white about things lately. The doctor said to control her salt intake, not to cut it out entirely. The look in her eyes while she was eating was different. I recorded her blood pressure afterward, and it was still within the normal range this evening.

Maybe some things don't need to be controlled so strictly. It’s okay to let go where it counts.

# Things to Teach Jianhao Before Retirement

**Date: November 15, 2023, Evening**

---

## I. Playing Chinese Chess (Xiangqi)

- [ ] Start with the basic rules—how the Chariot, Horse, Cannon, Soldier, and General move.
- [ ] Begin with simple openings; don't make it complicated right from the start.
- [ ] Be patient. Kids take time to understand; it’s okay to repeat things a few times.
- [ ] We can play a few simplified games first using only a handful of pieces.
- [ ] Teach him the habit of thinking one step ahead and looking two steps further; this is crucial.
- [ ] After each game, explain the "why" behind the moves so he understands the strategy.

## II. Mobile Photography and Journaling

- [ ] Teach him how to open the camera, focus, and press the shutter.
- [ ] Pay attention to the light when photographing flowers—they look clearer in the sun.
- [ ] Try different angles—experiment with both close-ups and wide shots.
- [ ] Teach him to use the phone’s memo app to record the date and weather—for example: "Nov 15, Sunny, watered the Chinese roses."
- [ ] This way, he can see how the flowers change over time; it’s very interesting.
- [ ] He can also use this method to record his own life and build a good habit.

## III. Organizing Photos and Notes

- [ ] Jianhao might take a lot of random photos, so I need to teach him how to categorize them.
- [ ] Create different albums based on dates or themes.
- [ ] The same goes for notes—keep them clear so they make sense later on.
- [ ] In my experience, building the habit of staying organized from the start saves a lot of trouble later.

## IV. Potential Activity Plans

- [ ] Try to spend more time with him in Shanghai after I retire.
- [ ] Take photos together on his balcony or in the garden downstairs.
- [ ] Find a quiet place to teach him chess—at home is best.
- [ ] Take him to the park to see flowers; we can walk, take photos, and I can share some knowledge about plants.
- [ ] If there’s a chance, teach him how to garden—start with something simple.

## V. Preparations Needed

- [ ] Buy a chess set for him—preferably a wooden one with good quality.
- [ ] Prepare a small notebook for him to record the dates and details of his photos.
- [ ] Might need to install a photo-organizing app on his phone—but ask his mother first.
- [ ] Brush up on the phone's memo features myself to make sure I can teach him properly.
- [ ] Think about other things he might be interested in; don’t just stick to chess and photography.

---

**Notes:**
There’s no rush with this plan; take it slow. Jianhao is ten now, and some things might still be a bit difficult for him to grasp, but that’s okay. The most important thing is to spend time with him and let him know his grandfather cares. Once I actually retire, I’ll have plenty of time.

Besides, I’m still learning some of these things myself. Learning and teaching at the same time—that sounds like a good way to do it.

# Thursday, August 22, 2024

**[Flag] A Conversation with Xiuying — A Turning Point**

After dinner, Xiuying mentioned she wanted to put a chair on the balcony; she didn’t want to stand every time she watched me water the flowers. My first instinct was to draw up a plan—when to buy the chair, where to place it to minimize blocking the light, and how to waterproof it. But the way she looked at me made me stop.

She wasn't waiting for my arrangements; she was simply sharing her thoughts.

We sat there together, watching the roses for a while. She said they were blooming better than last year. It hit me then that for all these years, I’ve been managing these flowers with a logbook—watering dates, fertilization cycles, pruning rules—as if I were managing quality control in a factory. But when Xiuying looks at the flowers, she sees the flowers themselves.

Later, we decided to go buy the chair together this weekend. She wants a blue one. I didn't offer any counter-suggestions.

This summer, I think I’ve learned something. It’s not about abandoning order, but about leaving room within that order—for her ideas, and for life itself.

To be continued tomorrow.

September 10, 2022, Evening

I had a phone call with Xiaoling. She asked me again how I was getting on with the new phone.

I told her I’d tried it out and that the camera works. It’s the truth; I did take a few photos of the flowers, following the steps she taught me during the National Day holiday. She sounded very pleased, saying that now I’ll be able to record the growth of the Chinese roses.

But I immediately asked her—could the words in the "Notes" app just disappear?

She laughed and told me I was overthinking it. She said modern phones have "cloud backup," so nothing gets lost. I’m still a bit uneasy. She’s so busy in Shanghai, and I don’t want her to think I’m a nuisance, so I didn’t push it. But I can’t help wondering: is this "cloud backup" actually reliable? What if the phone breaks one day? What if there’s a system glitch?

My old brain is slow at picking up new things. I asked Xiaoling if she ever forgets how to use these features. She said no, that it’s all very simple. I didn't say anything after that.

My method for now is to write everything down in my paper notebook first—the watering dates, the amount of fertilizer, where I pruned—all clearly documented. Then, I try to type it all into the phone’s Notes app. That way, even if something goes wrong with the phone, I still have my notebook. I’ve used notebooks for so many years, and they’ve never let me down.

I know Xiaoling means well. She says that once I retire, using my phone to keep records will be more convenient, and I’ll be able to look at the photos whenever I want. I understand that. It’s just...

I wonder why I have to learn new things for even the smallest tasks. I’ve worked at the factory for thirty-seven years; I’ve handled quality inspection with a steady hand and never had a single issue. But now, even for managing the flowers at home, I have to rely on something I don't truly understand.

Two years until retirement. Two years.

Sometimes I wonder, what will be left for me to do then? My job will be gone, my identity will be gone. The flowers, playing chess, spending time with family... will that be enough?

Anyway, I shouldn't overthink it. Tomorrow, I’ll keep watering the roses according to the plan in my notebook. That, at least, is something I’m sure of.

Xiaoling, I tried taking photos again. There weren't as many failed attempts this time, haha.

Just now, while I was watering the plants on the balcony, I took several shots of the roses. The first few were all blurry, so I adjusted the angle and the sunlight, and I finally managed to get a clear one. The flowers are blooming beautifully—take a look:

(Photo)

I want to show this to Jianhao when we video call on Tuesday and ask him if he likes it too. Your mom says I’m "showing off," haha, but I just want the boy to see the flowers his grandpa grew.

I’ve memorized all the steps you taught me. Even though I still pressed the wrong buttons at first, I’m getting the hang of it now. You were right—it’s all about taking it step by step, just like the inspection work we used to do.

What time on Tuesday would be best to call Jianhao? I want to prepare a bit in advance and put the flowers somewhere with good lighting.

Let me know how you’ve been lately when you have a moment.

Dad

Tuesday, July 11, 2023
Sunny

I returned to the office from the workshop a little after 3:00 PM.

The new inspection system was acting up again, so I walked over to take a look. Xiao Chen was operating it. I stood there for a while and suddenly blurted out, "So, in that case, are old inspectors like us even needed anymore?" The moment the words left my mouth, I felt foolish. Xiao Chen’s face turned bright red, and he started stammering, unable to find an answer. Seeing him like that, I quickly added, "It’s okay. Just learn it well—the future depends on you guys."

Back in the office, I sat for a long time.

The photo on the wall is still there—a group shot of the whole department from ten years ago. I leaned in to look closer, counting the faces one by one. Back then, my hair wasn't so white, and there was a certain energy in my face. I tapped my fingertip against the image of my younger self. Back then, the inspection standards I set were strict, but no one dared to breathe a word of protest. Our product pass rate was the highest in the factory, and the customers were satisfied. Back then, I was useful.

And now? Everything I’ve built up over thirty years can be replaced by an automated system. I’m not saying the system is bad; it’s definitely a good thing. But I wonder: all that judgment I developed through my hands, my eyes, and my experience—what is it worth now?

I spoke with Li Ming on the phone the day before yesterday. He said he wants to open an online store and overhaul the supply chain. I gave him some advice, all "safe" ideas—focus on running the current shop well before thinking about expansion. He didn't really push back, but I could hear something in his voice. I’m starting to think that maybe he doesn't need my conservative suggestions. Maybe the things I’ve learned over these thirty years are of no use to him at all.

I heard the company might be restructuring the departments. I don’t know what that means for me.

Xiuying’s blood pressure was a bit high today; I noted the numbers on the calendar. She asked me what was wrong, saying I looked pale. I told her it was nothing, just that I was a bit tired. The truth is, I don’t know how to talk to her about any of this.

Work continues tomorrow. The inspection reports are still piling up.

But right now, I’m having a hard time figuring out what exactly I am doing.

Li Jianguo: Sometimes you feel like you’ve lost something—like when I retired and left the office. But I didn't actually lose anything; I just transitioned. I’m still working in quality control now, just in a different way.

September 20, 2024, Evening

Director Zhang called today. He wanted to know if I’d be interested in staying on as a consultant after I retire—just two or three days a month. I didn’t say yes right away. Instead, I found myself saying, "I need to discuss it with my wife first."

After I hung up, I caught myself feeling a bit surprised. In the past, I would have jumped at an offer like this without a second thought. Back then, my mind was entirely consumed by work—how to run the Quality Department, how to climb the ladder. Xiuying used to tell me I was too obsessed with my job, but I never really listened.

But things are different now.

I brought it up with Xiuying over dinner. She looked a bit surprised at first and asked what I thought. We sat there at the dining table and just talked it through. I told her that two or three days a month was acceptable, but only on the condition that it doesn't interfere with taking her to her hospital follow-ups or take away too much time from our grandson. Hearing this, Xiuying smiled and said, "It sounds like you’ve already made up your mind."

And she’s right. I have.

Over the past two years, I’ve slowly come to understand what a "meaningful life" actually looks like. I used to think my value was tied to my job, to those inspection standards, and to the respect my employees had for me. Now I know that value is also found in being by Xiuying’s side during her monthly hospital visits, in the sense of pride I feel watching my grandson learn how to play chess, and in the relaxation of my Wednesday chess games with old colleagues.

I haven't lost anything; I’ve just redirected my focus. That obsession with detail, that pursuit of order, that sense of responsibility—those things haven't disappeared. They are just being applied to more important things now.

I can take the consulting job, but it won’t be the center of my life anymore. The center of my life is this home, it’s Xiuying, it’s my grandson, and it’s those small, everyday, yet genuine moments of companionship.

Thinking about it this way makes me feel truly at peace.

# Diary

**Sunday, October 2, 2022 | Sunny**

I called Xiaoling again this morning.

It was about the same old thing—how to add date stamps to photos. She taught me yesterday, the day before, and the day before that. I could hear the exhaustion in her voice when she said, "Dad, I’ve already explained this to you so many times." I quickly told her, "I’ve got it, I’ve got it," and hung up.

But the truth is, I haven't got it.

I sat on the balcony, looking at that pot of pink roses. The buds haven't fully opened yet, and the leaves look a bit wilted. I flipped through my notebook; there was an entry from September 28th: "Overwatered; possible waterlogging at the roots." Below that, I had noted "Under observation" in red ink. This record is clear; I can see every detail, know exactly where the problem lies, and how to fix it.

My phone was sitting right next to me. Inside that screen are all the photos I took of my flowers, but I have no idea when they might just vanish. My notebook is in my hands; I can turn to any page, and every single record is right there. But the phone? It’s like a black box. I press a button, information goes in, but I don't know where it lives or when it might disappear.

I admit, I’m a little afraid of the thing.

Xiaoling says I’ll never learn this way. She’s right. This is the fourth time I’ve called to ask the same question. I can feel her patience wearing thin. It makes me even more anxious—not because the phone is so complicated, but because I’m being a burden to my daughter and still failing to learn.

As I sat there, it suddenly hit me that I’ll be retiring in two years. I won’t be the Director of Quality Control anymore; I won’t have the inspection standards I know so well, or the subordinates who listen to me. What will I become? Just an old man sitting at home with a phone, having to ask his daughter how to use it?

I looked over the past month’s entries in my notebook. Every line is clear; every decision is backed by logic. These are things I have complete control over. But it feels like the world isn't waiting for me anymore. Even my grandson knows how to watch cartoons on a phone, yet here I am, struggling just to figure out a photo.

 I’m really falling behind.

Maybe I should keep trying. But I’m going to keep my notebook. At least with this, I can feel at peace.

I’m 63 this year, only two years away from retirement. Lately, I’ve been thinking about it more and more. I’ve worked for thirty-seven years; I know quality inspection inside out. I set the standards, I know the processes, and my subordinates trust me. But what happens after I retire? What will I have left to do? Tending to flowers, playing chess, spending time with family—will that be enough to fill the rest of my days?

It’s the one thing I can still control. If I hand even this over to a phone I don't understand and can't grasp, then I truly won't have control over anything anymore.

I know it’s a bit silly to think this way. Xiaoling wants to help, and my grandson means well too. But sometimes, I just can’t help it. Times have changed, and at my age, I can’t keep up—no matter how hard I try.

Tomorrow is another day.

Sometimes I try to offer him advice, but honestly, I don't know how much help I can really be.

The hardest part is feeling like there’s nothing I can change.

I’ve always believed that as long as you are diligent, careful, and experienced, you can do a good job. That’s how I’ve lived these thirty-seven years. But now... experience doesn't seem to be worth much anymore. Machines are more precise than I am, and the young people learn new things faster. When I emphasize the advantages of traditional inspection in the office, it sounds like I’m just defending myself. Even I can hear it.

Xiao Ling and Jian Hao are in Shanghai. I wish I could spend more time with the kid. He watches cartoons on a smartphone, while I’m still using a notebook to record the dates for watering the flowers. It feels like we live in two different worlds.

I’m not one to grumble, but tonight... I just can’t sleep.

I have to keep using that new system tomorrow. I have to learn it. I know it’s necessary. But I really don’t want to admit that I might truly be falling behind.

—

Maybe I should get some sleep. I have work tomorrow.

Xiao Ming, there’s something I wanted to talk to you about today.

The new Quality Director over at the factory gave me a call. He said they’ve been having issues with their newly installed inspection system—the defect detection mechanism isn't precise enough, and some subtle quality issues are slipping through. He wants to bring me in as a consultant to help them straighten things out.

I was a bit surprised at first, considering I’m so close to retirement. But after thinking it over, I realized it’s a good opportunity. I discussed it with your mother, and she agrees it’s worth a try. The consulting fees are quite decent, and I’m thinking of using that money to supplement our retirement fund. As you know, your mom needs regular check-ups for her high blood pressure, and we want to spend more time with our grandson in Shanghai—all of that requires a bit of a financial cushion.

I told him that I can’t go back full-time, but I can head down to the site two or three times a month or provide guidance remotely. This new system is like a young employee—it’s very capable but lacks experience; it needs someone to show it the ropes. It seems my old inspection standards and experience can still be put to good use.

How is business on your end? I imagine the competition in building materials is still pretty fierce. If you ever run into problems and want to talk things through, feel free to call me anytime. Between the two of us, we can always find a way.

If you’re free this weekend, why not bring the kids over for dinner? Your mother misses you all.

Xiuying, I wanted to say a few words to you.

I was just chatting with Xiao Ming, and some of the things he said really got me thinking. I’ve realized... I might have been overbearing. All these years, I’ve been recording your blood pressure, reminding you to take your meds, and restricting your diet. I thought I was helping, but looking back, I see that I might have just been adding to your stress. You told me before that my monitoring made you feel tense, but I didn't truly listen then. Now, I understand.

In my work, I’m used to controlling everything, and I brought that same mindset home. But you’re right—life isn’t a system that can be perfectly managed. There are things I simply have to learn to let go of.

Starting today, I want to relax a bit. I won’t be watching over your blood pressure management as closely as I did before. I trust you to remember your medication on your own. We’ll still go for our regular check-ups and follow the doctor’s advice, but I’m going to stop obsessing over every single number.

I just want you to know—this isn't me giving up on you. It’s me trying to love you in a different way. A gentler, more trusting way.

I hope you can understand.

This isn't about caring less for her health; it’s about finding a better way. It feels like we’ve shifted from "me managing her" to "us managing this together." It feels different.

July 17, 2024, Evening

I spoke with Xiaoling on the phone today. She’s in Shanghai, and her voice sounded a bit rushed. She mentioned that Jianhao has been pestering her again, wanting to video chat with me. I could tell she wanted me to spend more time with the boy, but she felt awkward saying it directly—she’s just too busy with work over there.

Without overthinking it, I just said, "Alright, teach me how to use that software."

Xiuying laughed beside me. She said I’ve spent my whole life being afraid of new things—that I used a Nokia for twenty years before I was willing to switch—and yet here I am, volunteering to learn video calling. I ignored her. I was just thinking about little Jianhao; he’s ten now, and we’ve met so few times I could count them on one hand. If it’s for a chance to talk to him, what does it matter if I have to learn something new?

I could feel Xiaoling breathe a sigh of relief over the phone. She said, "Dad, you don't have to force yourself," and I just replied, "I’m taking good care of your mother. You just focus on your work in Shanghai and don't worry about us." As I said those words, I suddenly felt as if I had found something—it’s not a job, but it is a kind of responsibility.

After hanging up, I watched Xiuying busy in the kitchen. The blood pressure monitor was still sitting on the coffee table, waiting for tomorrow morning’s reading. Throughout my thirty-seven years at the factory, I got used to setting standards, keeping records, and making improvements step by step. Now, I’m applying that same system to taking care of my wife and being there for my grandson. Xiuying says I’m "stricter than a doctor." I know she’s joking, but I also know that this is just who I am now.

Tomorrow, I’ll have Xiaoling teach me how to video chat on the phone. It might be a hassle, but it doesn't matter.

Life must go on. And it must go on in an orderly fashion.

January 19, 2023, 7:00 PM

Xiuying said my dark circles are getting bad. I didn’t deny it, because it’s the truth. I haven't been sleeping well lately.

This morning’s meeting keeps playing on a loop in my head. Digital transformation, organizational optimization, efficiency improvements... these words sound normal enough, but in that conference room, I heard something else between the lines. I’ve been at this factory for 37 years; I know how to read the subtext.

What will happen to the Quality Inspection Department? I don’t know. Can that new system replace manual inspection? Yes. So, what about me?

This is the first time I’ve ever thought this way.

I’ve spent my whole life doing one thing—ensuring quality. It’s not just a job; it’s... I don’t know how to put it. It’s just the feeling that if a product passes through my hands, it has to be right. That is my pride. In that group photo on the office wall from ten years ago, those employees—they all trusted the standards I set. I have never let a customer down.

But now a machine can do this. Faster, more accurately, without getting tired, without making mistakes.

I’m thinking, if the department really gets restructured, I’m only two years away from retirement. Early retirement? But will the pension be enough? Xiuying needs long-term medication for her high blood pressure. Xiao Li’s building materials business hasn't been stable lately; I want to help him sometimes, but... I don't even know how I could. Then there’s Xiaoling and Jianhao in Shanghai—I want to spend more time with my grandson, but now even my job isn't secure.

I made some lists in my notebook. If the department is cut, what can I do? Consulting? But who wants a 63-year-old veteran worker for consulting? Keep doing quality inspection? What company would hire me? Am I overthinking this?

Xiuying didn’t say anything else, but I can tell she’s worried. She’s moving faster in the kitchen than usual, which means she’s nervous too. I didn’t want to worry her more, so I said, "It probably won't affect anything, it's all just rumors." But I don't even believe myself.

I’m not an anxious person. I’ve always believed that as long as you do your work conscientiously, things will be fine. But this time, being conscientious doesn't seem to be enough.

 the insomnia is new. I lie in bed, and my mind is full of that meeting. Xiuying is fast asleep, and I don’t want to wake her. I just lie there, staring at the ceiling, thinking about those automated systems, thinking about these 37 years.

I still have to go to work tomorrow. I have to be like I always am—checking reports, handling complaints, holding meetings with my staff. As if nothing has happened.

But everything has happened.

I don’t know what to do.

Good morning, Xiaoling.

I tried using that app you installed for me again yesterday. I took some photos of the flowers and wanted to mark the watering dates, but I searched for ages and couldn't figure out how to add text. In the end, I just took out my notebook and wrote it down by hand. I also tried that "cloud backup" you mentioned, but I’m still a bit uneasy about it—what if the data gets lost? So for now, I’m doing both: taking a photo on my phone and writing it down in my notebook. Your mom says I’m making things too complicated, but I feel more secure this way.

There’s something I wanted to ask you: how do I zoom in on the photos? The leaves on the pink roses I photographed have been turning a bit yellow lately, and I wanted to get a closer look to see exactly what’s wrong, but the phone screen is too small to see clearly. Could you teach me when you have some time? Sorry to keep bothering you with these things.

How are you doing in Shanghai? Is work busy? How is Jianhao lately? If you have time this weekend, maybe we could do a video call; I haven’t had a good look at him in a while.

I’m still fumbling around with this phone and don't quite understand some of the features yet, but I’ll take it one step at a time. Don’t worry, I’ll keep practicing.

— Dad

Xiuying, I’ve been thinking about it all night. When you asked me yesterday, "Are you talking about us?" I didn't give you a proper answer.

The truth is, yes. It’s not just about you; it’s about me too.

All these years at the factory, I got used to planning everything down to the last detail—inspection standards, procedures, making sure every step was exactly right. I think I ended up bringing that same approach home—your blood pressure, mealtimes, when to go to the hospital. I thought that was the way to take good care of you.

But after hearing what you said yesterday, I finally understand. You aren't a product that needs managing. You are my wife, and we are supposed to be in this together.

I’m still learning how to do that. I might not be very good at it yet. But I want to try—to listen more to what you think, like you said, instead of always insisting on my plan.

Thank you for being so patient with me all these years. And thank you for being willing to tell me all that yesterday.

How about we decide together what to have for breakfast tomorrow morning?

November 15, 2022, 7:00 PM

I spent half an hour watering the flowers on the balcony. While Xiuying watched TV in the living room, I listened to the sound of water trickling through the soil and suddenly felt a wave of exhaustion. It wasn’t physical; it was more like a tightly wound string in my heart had finally gone slack.

Three weeks ago, I was still struggling with insomnia. Every night, I’d find myself wide awake at two or three in the morning, my head spinning with Xiuying’s blood pressure readings. 135, 140, 138... I kept a log for a month, trying to find a pattern, trying to "manage" those numbers by tweaking her diet, her routine, and her mood. It was just like managing product pass rates back at the factory—I thought if I could control the process, I could control the outcome.

But Xiuying’s blood pressure simply wouldn't cooperate.

Last week, she suddenly asked me, "Are you taking care of me, or are you torturing me?" I didn't say anything. She continued, "Every day you’re watching me, recording me, as if I’m a product that needs to be inspected." I wanted to defend myself, but then she said, "I know you mean well. But... I’m a little tired."

In that moment, I realized that everything I had been doing—the meticulous records, the precise plans, the control over every tiny detail—might have been entirely misguided.

Starting yesterday, I stopped recording her blood pressure. I told Xiuying that from now on, she can eat whatever she wants and rest whenever she feels like it. My only job is to accompany her to the hospital for her check-ups on time. Everything else... I’ll leave it to the doctors, and to her.

Today she told me I "look much more relaxed." Only then did I realize I’ve been sleeping quite well these past few nights. No dreams, no waking up in the middle of the night in a panic.

While watering the plants, I looked at the leaves of the Chinese roses and thought about what I’d been doing. I had made a watering schedule for every single pot and logged every fertilization date, as if that could guarantee they would grow perfectly. But the truth is... flowers know when to take in water and when to rest. I only need to provide the conditions; the rest should be left to their own natural rhythm.

Some things really don't need my control. They will grow according to their own laws.

Xiuying, my son, my grandson... perhaps they are the same. I’ve always tried to care for them using "quality management" methods, but people aren't products. People have their own pace, their own choices.

I need to remember this moment—the moment I realized that letting go is also a form of care.

Tomorrow, I’ll get back to playing chess. It’s been a long time since I truly enjoyed a game.

July 16, 2023, Night

Xiuying asked me what was wrong again today. I said it was nothing, but she could tell. We sat for a long time during dinner; the food in our bowls had gone cold.

I spoke with Li Ming on the phone this afternoon. He mentioned that the building materials store is looking to pivot to online sales and asked for my thoughts. I gave him some advice—safe advice, the kind I’ve always given. I talked about quality, reputation, and not being too eager for quick success. As soon as I finished, I regretted it.

I wonder, is the advice I gave him actually useful? Those are ideas from 1998. Business today doesn't seem to be something I can understand anymore. What young people need might be something I simply can't provide. All I know is how to follow the rules and stick to the old ways.

Xiuying said I’ve always been a capable man. I shook my head and said that maybe that’s a thing of the past.

She didn't say anything else; she just squeezed my hand.

The factory has been installing a new inspection system lately—it’s all automated. I looked it over, but there are still functions I don’t quite grasp. When the young technician was explaining it to me, I could sense him slowing down his speech. I’ve been in that position for over twenty years, and now I’m being replaced by a machine. Perhaps that’s just the way the times are going.

I wonder, what value do I have left? The advice I give my son might not help him; it might even mislead him. The factory’s new system has no use for me. I’m due to retire in two years. What happens then?

I flipped through the group photo from ten years ago hanging on the office wall. Everyone from the department was there back then, and everyone looked so full of energy. Now, some have resigned, and others are nearing retirement age. We are all getting old, and our methods are getting old with us.

Maybe I need to learn something new. But I don’t know where to start.

Back to work tomorrow. I’ll just keep doing the job well, the old-fashioned way. That’s still something I can do.

July 1, 2024. Morning.

6:30 AM. The alarm didn’t go off, but I woke up anyway.

A biological clock doesn’t just change because of a retirement certificate. For thirty-seven years, opening my eyes at 6:30 has been a part of who I am. I lay in bed, staring at the ceiling, my mind fully aware—I don’t have to go to the office anymore.

But those words feel hollow.

I sat up and spent ten minutes reading the news on my phone. A habit. Then it hit me: after I finish the news, what do I do next? There are no inspection reports to review, no production departments to coordinate, no quality complaints to handle. I could go back to sleep, I could sit on the sofa, or I could go water the flowers on the balcony. But none of those things feel the same as that ten-minute news break before work.

It wasn’t until I got up to get dressed that it truly sank in. I reached for my loungewear—that old, soft gray T-shirt—instead of my uniform. The uniform is still hanging in the closet, pressed and crisp. I don’t know when I’ll wear it again. Maybe never.

The moment I pulled on that T-shirt, I felt a strange sense of weightlessness. It was as if I had suddenly transformed from a body with mass into something light and drifting.

Wang Xiuying was still asleep. I tiptoed into the kitchen and poured myself a glass of water. The Chinese roses on the balcony are blooming beautifully; I logged the watering date last week. Do they need water today? I checked the soil; it was still damp. I just stood there, looking at the flowers, not knowing what to do with myself.

Before, every day of mine was clearly defined. And now?

I know this is normal. Everyone has to retire eventually. Rationally, I understand that. But rational understanding and emotional acceptance seem to be two different things. For thirty-seven years, I was the Director of the Quality Inspection Department. I was the reliable one, the one people trusted. On the office wall, there’s still a department photo from ten years ago and charts of quality achievements over the years. Those belong to me.

Now, I am nothing.

I sat on the sofa, looking around the living room. The house is the same house, bought in 1998; we never thought about moving. But now it suddenly feels vast and empty. As if it’s waiting for me to fill it.

I need to do something. Starting tomorrow, I have to adjust my routine; I can’t just drift along like this. Maybe I can take better care of the flowers, or play a few more rounds of chess with my friends. Li Ming’s business has been under a lot of pressure lately; I could listen to his ideas more. And then there’s Li Xiaoling and my grandson in Shanghai…

But for now, I’m just sitting here. 6:45 AM. The sun isn't even fully up yet.

What else is there for me to do?

March 16, 2023, 10:30 PM

By the time I pulled out that yellowing photo album, Xiuying was already asleep. I sat on the small balcony; the Chinese roses were swaying gently in the night breeze, but I couldn't move.

Photos from over a decade ago. Me back in my days as a section chief, standing in front of the production line in my work uniform, smiling with such confidence. There’s also the department group photo taken in 2013—I remember the weather was beautiful that day. It was two in the afternoon, and everyone had put on their newly issued uniforms. That photo is pinned to the office wall now; I see it every day.

Time really flies.

I said those words in a raspy voice, but Xiuying didn't hear me.

From operator to group leader, section chief, director... I climbed up step by step. Every step felt solid, and I remember every stage. Back then, I felt useful. The department needed me; the company needed me. And now? Now there’s that new system, the young people, and technical documents I can barely understand. I’m gathering twenty years of quality data—what am I trying to prove? That I still have value?

But does it even matter?

There are even older photos in the album. The year we bought the house in 1998—Xiuying and I standing downstairs with the two kids in front of us. We bought that place with our savings and a loan. I remember thinking back then that this was the best I could give them. The house is still there, the mortgage is long paid off, and the children are all grown up, busy with their own lives. Xiaoling is in Shanghai, and Li Ming is running his business. They don't need me anymore. The grandkids are growing up too, watching cartoons on phones I barely know how to operate.

I turned a page, and a tear fell.

I didn't expect to cry. I’ve always prided myself on my self-control; no matter how much pressure I was under, I could always calmly list solutions and push through them one by one. But I can't make a list for this. Because this isn't a problem on the production line or a figure in an inspection report. This is time. I can't inspect it, I can't correct it, and I can't stop it.

Everything is changing. I am changing.

I closed the album, my fingers lingering on the cover for a long time. I still have to go to work tomorrow, look over the materials for the new system, and take Xiuying to the hospital for her blood pressure check-up. The day after is Wednesday—chess with my friends. Then it’s the weekend, and maybe Li Ming will call to say he’s run into some problem with his business again.

I need to rethink what to do after retirement. But right now, I can't figure it out.

Right now, I’m just tired. So tired.

The roses on the balcony are still swaying. I remember the last time I watered them was three days ago.

September 26, 2022, 7:45 PM, Overcast

Another afternoon of tossing and turning with this thing.

My daughter called this afternoon. She said using my phone to record the flower data would be more convenient than my notebook, and she even walked me through how to set up reminders. As I listened, I started getting a bit irritable, but I didn’t want her to think her old man was stuck in his ways, so I reluctantly agreed to give it a try. I pressed whichever buttons she told me to. I meant to set it for "water every three days," but I must have hit something wrong because the phone started blaring every day at 3 PM.

Beep, beep, beep, beep... that sound is enough to drive a man crazy.

I spent ages trying to turn it off to no avail, and finally, I just put the whole thing on silent. Xiuying was laughing at me from the sidelines, saying, "See? That’s what happens when you won't learn properly." I couldn't even argue back. This phone is like a black box; I have no idea why it starts ringing on its own, and I wonder if the data will just vanish into thin air like that, too.

I ended up pulling out my notebook again. I wrote "Notebooks are reliable" in red ink and re-recorded today’s watering for the Chinese roses. For over twenty years, this notebook has never let me down. The data is right there on the paper, clear as day—something I can see and touch.

This old brain of mine... it really can’t keep up anymore.

I know my daughter means well. I get it. But this stuff is just too complicated. I’ll have to keep tinkering with it tomorrow, otherwise I’ll look too stubborn. But I’m keeping the notebook regardless; it’s the only way I feel at ease. If the phone acts up again, at least I’ll have a backup.

I’m playing chess with the guys on Wednesday, and I bet they’ll have a good laugh at my expense again. Everyone says times have changed, and I know that. But some things just can’t be changed overnight.

Whatever, time for bed. I'll try again tomorrow.

I might be late for the chess game next Wednesday; need to let my chess buddies know in advance.

November 15, 2022 Afternoon

Sitting in the office, the sky outside the window is a bit overcast. I just made a change to that table in my notebook, switching it from twice a day to twice a week. My fingers hovered over the pen for a long time before I finally crossed out those numbers.

Do I regret it? I don’t know. It’s more like... a sense of relief?

Xiuying replied to my text at noon with a smiley face. Just a single smiley face. But I stared at it for a long time.

I’ve been asking myself a question these past few days—what exactly have I been doing? For thirty-seven years, I’ve been in this position setting standards, checking details, and ensuring everything is under control. This method works at work. Product pass rates are high, and customers are satisfied. I’m proud of that.

But what about Xiuying’s blood pressure? I made such a detailed plan for her: daily logs, weekly reviews, doctor’s orders written all over the calendar... and the result? Her blood pressure still fluctuates. I did everything I could, yet I couldn’t change a thing.

It was in that moment I realized—I might have been doing the wrong thing all along.

Not that caring for her is wrong. But... I’ve been trying to manage a person’s body the same way I manage a product. As if as long as I’m meticulous and dedicated enough, I can eliminate all uncertainty. But life doesn't work that way. The human body isn't a machine, and Xiuying isn't a production line.

I wrote a sentence in my notebook: "The essence of management is caring, not control. Trust the doctor; trust Xiuying."

My hand was shaking as I wrote it.

I always thought I was protecting her. Looking back now, maybe I was just using my anxiety to suppress her autonomy. She told me the other day that my "care" made her feel a bit suffocated... I took those words to heart, even though I didn't respond well at the time.

Li Ming has said similar things. I’m always trying to give him advice, as if my experience can solve everything. But he’s thirty-five now; he has his own judgment. When did I become this kind of person?

Maybe it’s because I’m so afraid of losing control. In the factory, control equals safety. But at home... at home, what I need is to learn how to let go.

Work continues tomorrow. Only two years left until retirement. In these two years, I need to figure out—what kind of person should I be after I retire?

I can’t stay like this. I can’t bring the office mindset back home.

Xiuying’s smiley face makes me feel like maybe she’s already forgiving me. I need to forgive myself, too—forgive myself for not being perfect, and for not being able to control everything.

Thinking this way, my breathing feels a little easier.

Monday, June 24, 2024, 6:30 AM

I didn’t sleep well last night; I was wide awake by five. It was still dark outside, but I got up anyway. Wang Xiuying was still asleep, so I didn’t turn on the lights—I just sat there in the living room.

Actually, it wasn’t that I couldn’t sleep. It’s just... my mind kept drifting back to yesterday.

Yesterday morning, I pulled out that old notebook—the one I use to track when to water the plants. I flipped to the last blank page and suddenly thought: why not record my life after retirement too? Not in a dazed, passive way, but... a planned life, a life of my own design.

I spent over an hour writing down a weekly schedule. Monday, Tuesday, and Thursday: video calls with my grandson. Wednesday afternoon: chess with my friends. Friday: take Xiuying to the hospital for her check-up. Weekends: tending to the plants, watching the news, and organizing the family photo albums. And—two afternoons a week, if the new director has any questions, I can drop by the office for a bit to offer some advice.

When I got to that part, I stopped writing and stared at the page for a long time. My eyes felt a bit stinging.

In that moment, I finally understood. I’m not losing anything. I’m... redesigning. I’m no longer the "Director of Quality Inspection," but... a grandfather, a husband, a friend, a mentor, a gardener. All these identities added together feel somehow more complete than before.

When Xiuying woke up, I showed her the notebook. She put on her reading glasses and read it line by line, a smile slowly spreading across her face. She said, "This is good. This is very good." Then she patted me on the shoulder.

We had morning tea together. She said she wants to join in on the weekend photo album project; she wants to reorganize all the photos from over the years. We can look through them and reminisce together.

The sun is up now. Outside the window is the same old neighborhood, with people doing their morning exercises downstairs. Everything looks the same as yesterday, but I feel different.

It’s not anxiety, nor is it forced acceptance. It’s... certainty.

I know how to live the days ahead now.

It was just after 8:00 PM. Li Jianguo sat on the sofa as the closing credits of the evening news rolled on the TV. Wang Xiuying came out of the kitchen, sat down beside him, and handed him a glass of warm water.

He took it but didn't drink right away, just holding the glass in his hands.

"How was your day?" Wang Xiuying asked, same as always.

Li Jianguo was silent for a moment. "It was alright... just..." He paused, his gaze fixed on the TV screen, though he clearly wasn't watching it. "Today, Wang Hao—that young man who’s taking over my old position—was telling me about that new testing system. Said the accuracy rate hits 99.8%, higher than manual inspection."

Wang Xiuying didn't respond immediately; she just watched him.

"I was just thinking..." Li Jianguo paused again, his voice dropping softer. "I’ve been doing this for over thirty years. I’ve always believed that quality is the lifeblood of a company. But now..." He rubbed his eyes. "It seems like a machine can do it better."

He turned to look at his wife, a trace of exhaustion in his eyes. "A couple of days ago, Ming called. He said the competition at his building materials store is getting tough and asked me what to do. I told him to stay steady, build a solid foundation, and not to go chasing reckless risks. But after I said it, I started wondering... is my advice outdated? Maybe young people today don't run businesses that way at all."

Wang Xiuying reached out and patted him on the shoulder.

Li Jianguo took a sip of water. "I’m just... not so sure anymore. I feel like everything I’ve built up over the years might..." He didn't finish the sentence, only shook his head. "Forget it, maybe I’m overthinking. I’ve just been a bit tired lately."

He leaned back against the sofa. Wang Xiuying didn't say anything; she just sat there beside him, same as always. The only sounds in the room were the TV and the occasional honk of a car from the street below.

Li Jianguo’s phone screen lit up—it was a WeChat message from Li Jianhao, a photo of a handicraft he’d made in a class in Shanghai. Li Jianguo looked at it, the corner of his mouth twitching into a faint smile, but that sense of weariness didn't quite fade.

He put the phone down, gripped the warm glass again, and sat there in silence.

October 16, 2022  Sunny

Another morning spent fussing over things.

Xiuying asked why I bother writing everything down in my notebook after taking photos. I told her I have to do both just to be safe. She sighed and didn’t say anything else. I know she means well, but I just can’t bring myself to trust those photos on the phone. They look clear enough when I take them, but as soon as I see those tiny thumbnails on the screen, I start doubting—is it clear enough? Can you really see the condition of the flowers? What if the phone breaks one day and all the photos are gone?

So, I spent another twenty minutes toggling between my phone and my notebook, re-recording the status of yesterday’s pink roses. The leaves are still a bit yellow; I might be overwatering. I wrote down in the notebook: "October 15, yellowing leaves, consider watering less frequently." Only after writing it down did I finally feel at ease.

But honestly, I’m starting to annoy myself. It’s just a phone—why is it so difficult? My daughter insisted I switch to it, saying it would make things easier, but look at me now. It’s actually more of a hassle. I have to double-check everything for fear of making a mistake. This isn't like me.

Back when I was on the shop floor, I could inspect a part and know if there was a problem with just one glance. Back then, I trusted my own judgment. Now? I can’t even figure out a phone.

Sometimes I wonder, if I can’t even learn something new like this, what will I do when I retire in two years? If I can’t work, if the flowers die because of my negligence, and if I can’t even use a phone... then what use am I?

Xiuying has been worrying about me again lately. I can tell. She’s being more meticulous in the kitchen than usual, as if she’s trying to compensate for something. I want to tell her I’m fine, but I don’t even believe it myself.

I’ll try that photo annotation feature again tomorrow. My daughter taught me last time, but I’ve forgotten again. I’ll ask her one more time.

I need to take a good look at that pot of roses today. At least with that, I still know what I’m doing.

Xiuying, I have to tell you about what happened today.

I went to that training for the new system this morning, and to be honest, I was incredibly nervous at first. Sitting there, watching those young people clicking away on their computers, I felt like a total outsider. All those interfaces and workflows—I had to learn everything from scratch. While the trainer was speaking, I kept thinking to myself that I might never actually get the hang of this.

But… guess what?

During the break, I started wondering if there was a way to integrate all the experience we’ve gathered from our old inspection methods into this new system. I brought this up to the trainer—the idea of combining traditional expertise with the new technology.

His eyes lit up the moment he heard it. He said it was a very interesting perspective and even asked me to share my thoughts with the rest of the group. In that moment… I don’t know how to describe it, but I felt like I was actually useful for once.

I know it might not seem like a big deal, but I’ve been under so much pressure lately and haven't been sleeping well. This tiny bit of recognition today made me feel like… maybe I’m not completely obsolete? Maybe my experience still counts for something?

Do you think I’m overthinking it?

I’m still a bit tired, but my heart doesn't feel as heavy anymore.

Come home early tonight; let’s have dinner together.

November 22, 2024, Evening

I had a phone call with Xiaoling. She’s been swamped with work lately, but she still carved out some time to chat with me.

I took the initiative to tell her about the recordings—the stories I’ve been recording recently about my younger days in the factory and some family matters. To be honest, I was a bit self-conscious about bringing it up; I was afraid she’d think her old man was just messing around with something trivial. But after she listened, she said something that I’m still thinking about now:

"Dad, something like this will be a lifelong treasure for Jianhao."

I didn't say much at the time, just gave a quiet "mm-hmm." But my eyes welled up a bit.

Then she added, "Dad, you’ve changed lately. It’s like you’ve found something."

I replied, "Yeah, I think I have," my voice sounding a little raspy.

After hanging up, I sat on the balcony looking at that pot of Chinese roses. It’s sprouted new buds again recently. It occurred to me that I always thought retirement was the end of life—either just killing time or waiting for the end. But it seems that’s not the case.

Over the past month, I’ve learned how to take photos, record audio, and make video calls on my phone. At first, I just wanted to spend more time with Jianhao, fearing that as he grew up, he wouldn't need his grandfather anymore. But now I understand: I’m not just learning a technology; I’m doing something more important than any job. I’m leaving my stories—the years I’ve lived—to him.

Just like back at the factory, I’ve always believed that details matter and quality matters. Now I finally realize that for a family, these things are just as important.

Xiaoling said I’ve changed. Maybe so. I’m no longer just that father who only knows how to say "work hard, study hard." I’ve started to have something else to pass on to them.

I’ll continue recording tomorrow. Jianhao said he wants to hear the story of the first time I entered the factory.

I need to think carefully about how to tell it so he finds it interesting.

August 17, 2024  Sunny

I woke up at 6:30 this morning. After watching the morning news broadcast, I had the plain congee and pickled vegetables Wang Xiuying prepared. It is now 6:45, and I am sitting on the small balcony, holding this smartphone in my hand.

Yesterday, my daughter taught me how to make video calls over the phone, and I wrote down the steps in my notebook. I practiced again this morning. Open the app—step one. Find the contacts—step two. Click my daughter’s name—step three. Then, I’ll see her face.

I tried it repeatedly for fifteen minutes. The first time was a success; I saw my daughter in her office in Shanghai, and she asked with a smile, "Dad, can you see me clearly?" I told her I could. Then, I accidentally pressed a button at the bottom of the screen, and the whole thing went black.

I felt a bit nervous at that moment, but then I thought—isn't this just like a quality inspection at work? If one step goes wrong, you just start over. It’s no big deal. I took a deep breath, reopened the app, and tried again. This time, I was more proficient.

I practiced a few more times until I remembered every step. My finger movements on the screen are gradually becoming more confident.

Wang Xiuying was clearing the dishes in the kitchen. She walked over, saw me practicing, and gave my shoulder a gentle pat. She didn't say anything, just that one pat. I turned to look at her; she was smiling. I smiled back. After forty-one years, sometimes words aren't necessary.

I’m thinking about how the weekend is only two days away. My daughter said she would video call me on Saturday night, and my grandson will be there too. I want to see his face and hear his voice. I must master this phone.

Not for any other reason, just for this.

I’m going to practice again in a little while. I need to etch these steps into my mind. Just like every inspection procedure I performed at the factory—repeat, confirm, and repeat again. Until it becomes a part of me.

I feel pretty good right now. A bit nervous, but mostly, I’m looking forward to it.
