# Quality Issue Record - Call with New Director

**Date:** September 18, 2024, Afternoon
**Participants:** New Director of Quality Inspection Department
**Recorded by:** Li Jianguo

---

## Issue Overview
The automated inspection system is failing to detect **minute cracks at weld seams**. The current parameter settings of the new system may not be properly calibrated to the characteristics of these specific defects.

## Specific Details
- **Defect Type:** Subtle longitudinal cracks at welding sites (primarily at corners), approximately 2-5mm in length and no more than 0.3mm in depth.
- **Missed Detection Rate:** Approximately 8-12% of this batch.
- **System Issue:** The recognition rate of the new automated scanning system for these defects is significantly lower than that of manual visual inspection.
- **Customer Feedback:** Issues have already been identified by customers during use, and quality complaints have been formally filed.

## Details to Confirm
1. Scanning precision parameter settings of the new system (specifically sensitivity to minute cracks).
2. Whether there have been any changes to the welding process for this batch.
3. Sample comparison—physical inspection required to confirm the exact morphology of the defects.
4. Magnification levels and light sources used during manual inspection.

## Preliminary Thoughts
- **Parameter Adjustment:** It may be necessary to lower the scanning threshold to increase sensitivity to subtle defects.
- **Process Check:** Determine if the welding department needs to adjust temperature or speed parameters.
- **Hybrid Inspection Plan:** Retain manual spot checks for critical areas until the system is fully calibrated.

## Follow-up Plan
- Visit the factory this weekend to inspect the physical samples and observe the system in operation.
- Request the new Director to prepare 3-5 defective samples alongside qualified products for comparison.
- Obtain the specific model number and technical specification documents for the new system.
- May need to consult with the Welding Section Chief.

---

**Note:** This issue is not overly complex, but system calibration will take time. No rush—let’s get the facts straight first. I will provide the new Director with a comprehensive adjustment plan after the site visit this weekend.

# Wednesday, July 19, 2023

**Chess Game - Mental Recovery**

Played some chess at the community center tonight and won a few rounds. My fellow players all remarked that I seem "much more spirited."

Lately, my head has been far too crowded—the new system, the company restructuring, my wife’s follow-up exams, my son’s business... It’s only when I’m sitting at the chessboard that these things finally start to settle. My focus has returned, and my hands have stopped shaking.

Lao Wang and the others asked how I’ve been lately. I told them, "Not bad, not bad." In truth, it’s just during these two hours that I feel like a man who can still make his own decisions.

Note this down as a reminder to myself: not everything is in decline. I can still win a game of chess.

**Keyword: Patience**

Xiaoling, I thought about what you said all night. You’re right—it’s better to take initiative than to just sit here worrying. I’ve decided to go ahead with that system training organized by the company.

I just spoke with Xiao Wang from my department, and he said the training will last about two weeks, an hour each day. To be honest, I’m still a bit nervous; learning new things at my age is definitely an uphill battle. But like you said, if I don't learn it now, I’ll be even more behind later. I’ll just look at it as a way to recharge my batteries.

I wanted to ask you, though—how long does it usually take to truly get the hang of these kinds of systems? I don’t just mean knowing how to operate it, but reaching a point where it’s second nature, like how I am with the traditional methods. Also, are there any tricks to it? For instance, is there anything specific I should memorize, or is it mostly just a matter of practice?

How is work going on your end? And how is Jianhao doing lately? While I was playing chess this Wednesday, I was thinking that once I retire, I really need to find some time to head down to Shanghai and spend more time with you all.

Thanks for being so patient and explaining everything to me yesterday. Your mom also told me I didn't need to be so tense. I just feel that if I don't keep up with certain things now, it’ll only be more of a headache down the road.

August 20, 2024, 8:15 PM

I just put my phone down, and my heart is still racing.

When my daughter was teaching me how to video call this afternoon, I was actually a bit nervous. All those buttons and icons made my head spin. But at the thought of being able to see Jianhao, I braced myself and followed her steps, memorizing them over and over. She was very patient, and I was very serious—treating it like a work inspection, step by step.

After dinner, I tried it on my own. My fingers poked and prodded at the screen; the camera pointed at the ceiling, then my chin, then my nose. Xiuying was laughing at me from the side, and I laughed too. I thought to myself, *This is it—it’s going to be a total mess when I actually make the call.*

But Jianhao picked up.

When I saw his face, I truly couldn't believe it. There he was on the screen, looking every bit his ten-year-old self, wearing that blue T-shirt. As soon as he saw me, he laughed and asked, "Grandpa, where’s your face?" Only then did I realize the camera was tilted again. We both burst out laughing.

He showed me the new Lego model he’d built, some kind of aircraft. I couldn't see it very clearly, but I listened intently as he explained every detail. He sounded so excited, and I just kept nodding along with "Mm-hmm" and "Right." It felt... just like we were sitting together.

As the call was ending, he asked me when I was coming to Shanghai. I said, "Grandpa is learning how to use this phone so I can see you more often." He was so happy.

After I hung up, I said to Xiuying, "I saw Jianhao. I heard his voice."

She patted me on the shoulder.

I sat for a while, watching the phone screen go dark. I remembered my daughter saying, "Dad, you're a fast learner," while she was teaching me. In that moment, I realized I wasn't just learning how to use a phone; I was learning how to be a better grandfather.

This phone means something different to me now.

I’ll try again tomorrow. I want to make sure I remember that shortcut perfectly.

Xiuying, Xiaoling just called. Jianhao is on a flight this afternoon and will be here by tonight. He’s staying for two whole weeks!

I just heard Jianhao on the phone saying he wants to play chess with Grandpa, and suddenly... well, look at me getting all worked up. I haven't even made your breakfast yet; I was too busy asking him when he’d arrive and how things have been in Shanghai lately.

I was thinking, I need to tidy up the balcony and move those flowerpots so he doesn't bump into them. I also need to find the chessboard—I remember putting it in the cabinet. And... oh, right, he loves your braised pork, so we need to go buy the meat ahead of time.

Is there anything else you think we need to prepare? Should I wash and change the bedding in his room? I’ll get on that in a bit.

These next two weeks are finally something to look forward to.

I didn't say another word.

But that sentence has been looping in my head ever since. Am I helping her, or am I just adding to her stress? I’ve never looked at it that way before. For over thirty years, this is just how I’ve done things—documenting every detail, streamlining every process, minimizing every risk. It was how I worked at the factory, how I handled quality control, and how I’ve approached life. I always thought this was what it meant to be responsible.

But now... has my care turned into a form of control?

I flipped through the notebook. Two full months of blood pressure readings, color-coded with different pens, complete with trend charts. I’ve even been researching which times of day the readings are most stable and which foods affect the numbers. This doesn't look like caring for a person; it looks like managing a production line.

Xiuying is in the kitchen; I can hear her washing the dishes. She’s right—her health has actually been fine, and her hypertension is well under control. Yet, it’s as if I’m waiting for something bad to happen, pre-emptively writing every "what if" into that notebook.

I don't know how to change. Relax the monitoring? Then I’ll start worrying—what if something goes wrong one day and I don't catch it? But if I keep going like this, Xiuying is right: it isn't fair to her.

She isn't my subordinate. She isn't a project for me to manage. She is my wife.

I understand the logic, but actually putting it into practice... I’m not there yet.

Maybe I should stop and ask myself: Am I helping her live better, or am I just helping myself feel more secure?

The answers to those two questions might not be the same.

July 24, 2023, Night

Master Wang came by.

He knocked on the door out of the blue around three in the afternoon. I was sitting on the balcony at the time, listlessly staring at the flowers. The leaves on the roses had turned yellow again, but I couldn't even be bothered to write it down. He said they’d all noticed I hadn’t been around for chess lately and felt something was off, so he decided to check in on me.

To be honest, I didn’t want anyone to see me like this. But he came in, sat on the sofa, and skipped the pleasantries, simply asking me what was wrong.

I didn't expect myself to cry. I just sat there, and the tears suddenly came pouring out. It’s been over thirty years, and I don’t think I’ve ever cried in front of anyone. But at that moment, I couldn't keep it all suppressed anymore—the restructuring at the company, Xiuying’s blood pressure, Li Ming ignoring my advice, and... myself.

I told Master Wang a lot. I told him I felt useless. I said no one listens to my suggestions anymore, and it feels like my experience has no value. I told him I don’t know what I’m capable of doing anymore. I told him I’m afraid of losing my job, afraid for my wife’s health, and afraid my son’s business won't hold up. I told him I feel like I’m being phased out by the times.

Master Wang listened for a long time without interrupting. Finally, he said something that I still remember clearly:

"Value isn't determined by your position; it’s determined by your attitude toward life. You’ve contributed to the factory for over thirty years—that is an unchangeable fact. The changes you’re facing now aren't your failure; they are just the progress of the era."

I didn't know how to respond. But hearing him say that, it felt like something inside me finally loosened.

Before he left, he asked me to play chess next Wednesday, saying, "Don't miss it again. We all need you."

I’m sitting here now, watching the sky grow dark. Xiuying is in the kitchen cooking, and the news is playing on the TV. Life goes on. Maybe I’m not without value; maybe... I just need to re-evaluate what truly matters.

Tomorrow morning at 6:30, I’ll still get up on time. I’ll watch the news for ten minutes and have breakfast. Then I’ll water the flowers and record the date.

Maybe that’s enough.

Xiuying, the new director came to my office today. He said he wanted to pick my brain about some of the testing procedures. I walked him through everything, and he listened very intently, even taking notes in his notebook.

Later, he mentioned that once he’s fully settled in, he’d like to keep coming back to me for more details. I told him that’s no problem—I’m still here, after all.

As we were talking, I caught a glimpse of that department photo on the wall from ten years ago. I felt a sense of quiet pride. Over the years, I’ve set such rigorous testing standards that everyone called me harsh, but looking at it now, those things actually matter. Otherwise, the new director wouldn't be coming to me for advice.

I’ve started a list in my notebook, organizing all the experience I’ve gathered over the years. I’m thinking of passing all this down before I retire—it feels like the right way to ensure a complete handover.

Tell me, does this make me still feel a bit useful?

What happened in the office this afternoon is still spinning in my head. That sentence I said to Xiao Wang—"Are you testing my patience?"—doesn't even sound like me now that I think about it. In thirty-seven years, when have I ever spoken to an employee like that? There were indeed issues with the report format, but it didn't warrant that kind of reaction. I saw the look on Xiao Wang's face. He felt wronged.

Afterward, I felt a bit of regret, and a bit of exhaustion. Deeply exhausted.

Xiuying asked me if I had a bad day at the office. I said no, but she saw right through me. She always sees through me. She said, "You've been so wound up lately." I didn't deny it.

I'm wondering what is actually going on with me.

Maybe it’s Xiuying’s blood pressure. Lately, I get incredibly nervous before every check-up. Even though the numbers are okay, I always feel like something is going to go wrong. I made such a detailed plan for her—the low-sodium diet, the medication schedule, the daily logs—I thought that by doing this, I could control everything. But... you can't. Some things simply cannot be controlled.

Maybe it’s the retirement. Two years to go, but I’ve started thinking: what comes after? I’ve been in this position my whole life; now that I’m leaving, who am I?

Maybe it’s all of these things combined. My wife’s health, my son’s business, my daughter being in Shanghai, my grandson growing up... and the way this company is changing. The young people use some system for inspections, while I’m still using the old methods. I want to maintain the standards, but perhaps I’m just trying to find peace of mind through "perfection."

My tone with Xiao Wang was uncalled for. That wasn't about quality control; that was me venting.

I should have a word with him tomorrow.

For now, I’ll water the flowers. The leaves on the roses are looking a bit wilted.

# Wednesday, December 11, 2024, Evening

While we were playing chess today, Old Wang suddenly asked me if I felt it was a pity to retire so early. To be honest, I used to wonder about that myself, especially right after I retired. But being asked again today, I realized my answer has become very clear.

I laid it all out for him—accompanying Xiuying to her hospital check-ups, where her blood pressure is now better controlled than ever because I can monitor every little change in real-time; having more video calls with Jianhao, like last week when I taught him how to play chess (he still doesn't quite get it, but it felt good just doing it); tending to my flowers with more care, like that Chinese rose that produced eighteen blooms this year, all of which I’ve recorded in my notebook; and then there’s the consulting work for the company—I don’t have to go in every day anymore, which actually allows me to think through problems more thoroughly.

Old Wang laughed after hearing this and said I was "retired but not resting." I told him it’s not exactly that; it’s more that... every stage of life has its own meaning.

Looking back, it’s been thirty-seven years since I first entered the factory in 1984. I went from operator to squad leader, then section chief, and finally director. Back then, I just wanted to do my job well and take it one step at a time; I never looked for shortcuts or "leaps" in my career. Now that I’m retired, I have the same mindset—it’s not that my career is over, but rather that it’s continuing in a different way.

Xiuying mentioned again today that I’ve become a bit more talkative lately. I’ve noticed it too. It’s not because I’m idle, but because once you’ve figured certain things out, you naturally want to express them. I used to think staying silent was the "safe" way to be, but now I understand that speaking your mind is a form of connection—for yourself and for those around you.

I’m taking Xiuying to the hospital again tomorrow. Although her hypertension is stable, I want to record it more systematically. The doctor mentioned last time that we should watch for patterns in her blood pressure fluctuations, and I want to approach this more scientifically.

Life like this feels quite full.

# Tuesday, June 25

**The Breakfast Shop — Twenty Years of Change**

6:50 AM, the same old spot. In Auntie Wang’s shop, the hum of the soy milk machine sounds just as it always has. But today, I lingered a little longer.

I chatted with Auntie Wang, and she told me the stove has been replaced three times, the prices on the menu have climbed from 1.5 to 5 yuan, and the wonton shop across the street closed down ages ago. I’ve been eating here for twenty years, yet I never really gave it much thought. Auntie Wang shook her head with a smile. "You," she said, "you’re the same every time—youtiao, soy milk, and pickles. Hasn't changed in twenty years."

I realized then that it wasn't that the shop hadn't changed; it was that I hadn't been looking closely enough.

A young man was in the back kneading dough, his movements much faster than the apprentices of the past. Auntie Wang said it’s her son; he came back to help out after graduating from university this year. We talked for a bit, and she asked about my job. I told her I’m close to retirement. She said, "That’s perfect. You’ll have more time to sit and stay a while. This shop of ours—it looks the same, but it’s actually changing all the time."

On the way home, I remembered that photo on the office wall from ten years ago. Everyone in the department was still there back then. Now, some have transferred, and others are nearing retirement too.

Remember to buy some fresh peas for Xiuying this afternoon; she’s been craving them lately.

While watering the plants tonight, two more roses bloomed. Note it down—June 25, sunny, watered once, buds have opened.

September 19, 2024, 7:30 PM

I received a call from Director Wang this afternoon. It was a bit unexpected.

He said the new system has been struggling to identify those micro-crack defects. No matter how they adjust the parameters, it just won't work, and it’s affecting the delivery of the current batch of orders. He asked if I could find some time to take a look or perhaps offer some remote guidance. As I listened, my mind instinctively started cycling through the old methods—optical angles, surface roughness, stress distribution... I haven't touched that stuff in three months, yet it’s still my first reaction.

I told him I could take a look, but it would have to wait until the weekend; I can’t let it interfere with taking Xiuying to the hospital. He said that was fine and suggested I come by Monday morning.

After hanging up, I sat there for quite a while.

While playing chess with my friends this evening, I mentioned it in passing. Old Wang said, "A person like you shouldn't really be idle." I didn't argue; I just focused on moving my rook. But his words did strike a chord.

It’s not that I want to go back to work. That chapter is over, and I know that clearly. I spent over twenty years as the Director of the Quality Inspection Department—that was enough. All those daily reports and endless coordination; just thinking about it now makes me feel tired.

But this feeling of being needed... it’s different.

Over the past three months, I’ve rearranged my life. I take Xiuying to the hospital every month and mark the doctor’s orders on the calendar. I play chess with my old colleagues on Wednesdays. The roses on the balcony are in full bloom now, and I’ve kept a log for every pot. I’ve been calling my grandson more often, too. Life has order; it feels full.

Yet, sometimes I wonder if everything I’ve accumulated over thirty-seven years is just meant to be set aside.

Director Wang’s call today made me realize... it’s not. Those things are still there. I can see problems others can’t, and I can think of solutions others wouldn't. That doesn't just disappear because I retired.

If I could act as a consultant—using that experience occasionally to supplement my pension—it would help me take better care of Xiuying, and maybe I could even offer some advice for Ming’s business... it seems like a good balance.

It’s not about going back to the past, but about letting the things from the past continue to be useful in a new way.

I’ll go take a look on Monday. I’ll get to the bottom of the problem first, then decide on the next steps.

Xiuying is watching TV now; I should go join her.

Xiao Ming, I had a video call with your brother and his family today. Little Hao has learned a new chess opening; I told him some stories about product inspections back at the factory, and he actually listened quite intently. As for this smartphone, I’m still a bit clumsy with it, but being able to see their faces and hear their voices makes things so convenient.

Your mom’s blood pressure was pretty good today. I accompanied her to the hospital for her follow-up, and the doctor said it’s well under control. Don’t worry too much; I make sure to take her there every month.

By the way, how is your business going lately? Competition in the building materials sector is indeed fierce, but you need to think about how to truly understand your customers' needs. I believe that if you nail both quality and service, repeat customers will naturally follow. Feel free to share any thoughts you have with me.

I played another game of chess with my old colleagues on Wednesday. It’s still the same group of us; we’ve kept this up for eight years now. When you get old, you rely on little hobbies like this to pass the time.

Take good care of your wife and the kids. Give me a call if anything comes up.

Xiuying, I just got off the phone with Xiaoling.

She’s in Shanghai, and she said it’s still quite hot over there. We talked for a good while. She mentioned work has been pretty busy lately, but Xiaohao is learning chess now, and she asked if I could teach him. I said of course, whenever they come back to visit.

Toward the end of the call, she suddenly started crying. I panicked a bit and asked her what was wrong. She said… she said she noticed my tone has changed lately. She said I don’t sound as… as heavy as I used to. She told me she had been worried I’d feel lost after retiring, but hearing me talk now, she feels like I’ve found something new.

So I told her that I’ve realized a few things recently. I said that while working for thirty-seven years was a part of who I am, it isn’t everything. Now I have you, I have her, I have Xiaohao, and I have my flowers… these are all wonderful things. I told her that I actually feel like having all this time to be with you all, and to watch the roses slowly bloom—it’s something I never had before.

After she heard that, she cried even harder, but it was that kind of… you know, a cry of relief. Later she said, "Dad, I’m so happy for you."

I’m sitting on the balcony now, looking at that pot of red roses, thinking about what she said.

Xiuying, don’t you think this is enough? I mean, not worrying about things that haven’t happened yet, and just living our lives well right now. What do you think?

August 8, 2023 | Evening

Sitting on the balcony, the roses haven't been watered yet. My phone is set aside, and I’m thinking about that conversation from today.

This afternoon, I was discussing inspection parameters with Xiao Wang—the new guy taking over my position. The system had flagged another batch of products as defective, but with over thirty years of experience, I could tell they were actually fine. In the past, I might have dismissed the system outright or gotten angry, thinking the whole thing was unreliable. But today, I didn't.

Xiao Wang and I looked at the system's logic together. I told him it wasn't that the system was bad; it’s just that we need to use our experience to help it become smarter. We adjusted the parameters, incorporating my understanding of material properties. In the end, the system got it right.

The way Xiao Wang looked at me changed. It was as if he realized for the first time that I wasn’t competing with him over who was more professional—I was teaching him how to make the new tools work for us.

Sitting here alone tonight, it suddenly hit me—maybe I’ve had it wrong all along. I thought retirement was the end, that I’d be rendered obsolete by new systems and younger people. But today I realized that my value isn't in how many products I can inspect, but in my ability to teach others how to do it better.

Thirty-seven years. I’ve learned a lot. Those things don't just disappear because a new system comes along. On the contrary, they have a new purpose now.

What else can I contribute? I’ve been asking myself that for a long time. Maybe the answer isn't "what can I still do," but "what can I still teach."

The roses are still waiting. I’ll water them tomorrow. My thoughts are a bit of a mess tonight, but it’s not a bad kind of mess. It’s that feeling of being moved by something.

Maybe I’m not that old after all.

Xiuying, you’re still awake, right?

I was just standing out on the balcony for a bit. The flowers have all withered from the frost; it’s been really cold these past few days.

Actually... I wanted to talk to you. It’s nothing major, just something that’s been weighing on my mind lately.

Have you heard anything recently about the company doing some kind of "digital transformation"? My manager mentioned it briefly during a meeting last week. They talked about bringing in automated inspection systems and moving everything to the cloud. I didn’t quite follow all of it, but I got the feeling... it sounds like our department might be facing some staffing adjustments.

I know these things are often just talk and might not even happen. But I can't help but wonder—what if they actually go through with it? We’re doing okay for ourselves now, and the house has been paid off for a long time. But if I were to retire early, wouldn't my pension be a lot lower? It’s just something I’ve been thinking about.

And another thing—all I’ve known all these years is this inspection work. If the department is gone, what else could I do? I’m 63 already; learning new things at this age...

It’s not that I’m obsessed with making more money. It’s just that we still have your health to look after, Xiaoling and her family are in Shanghai, and Xiaoming’s business isn't exactly stable. I just want to keep contributing for as long as I can.

Don’t worry about me, though. I’ve just been having some trouble sleeping, and I couldn't get myself out of bed the last couple of mornings. That’s not like me.

What do you think?

November 23, 2022, Evening

Sitting on the balcony, the roses are still in bloom. I took a few photos with my phone while watering them today. I can see every single vein on the petals—even clearer than with my own eyes. This discovery caught me a little by surprise.

While playing chess with Old Wang and the others this afternoon, I told them about my current method: using phone photos paired with my notebook entries. Old Wang said, "Old Li, it sounds like you’ve found your balance." I didn't think much of it at the time, but looking back now, those words seem to have struck a chord.

Before this, I always felt like I was running away. Using two systems felt like admitting defeat. I thought I should either master the phone or stick strictly to my notebook—how could I have it both ways? I even felt a bit ashamed of this workaround, as if I were cheating myself.

But after what Old Wang said, I finally realized... maybe this isn't failure; it's just finding my own way. The phone photos offer clarity, while the notebook provides a sense of security and reliability. Together, my flower records are actually more complete than ever before. It’s not a compromise; it’s... adaptation, I suppose.

I’ve been wondering what I can still do now that I’m retired. My biggest fear is being unable to learn anything new, being unable to do anything at all, and becoming useless. But now I seem to understand that I don't necessarily have to master new things completely to find a way forward. This thought makes me feel a bit lighter.

Xiuying asked me how I was feeling today, and I told her I was doing alright. She smiled and said I’ve been frowning a lot lately. It’s true, it has been a tiring stretch. But while playing chess today, I didn't feel as restless.

New rosebuds have appeared. I’ll continue my records tomorrow.

# Thursday, September 12, 2024

**[Notes]**

Xiuying took the initiative to go for a walk today, and we spent half an hour strolling around the neighborhood. I didn't make a "walking schedule" like I usually do; I just accompanied her and listened to her talk about Auntie Wang from downstairs. When she got a bit tired, we sat under a tree to rest. She leaned on my shoulder and said it felt quite nice.

Back home, her blood pressure was 130/85—not bad at all. Instead of rushing to log it in the calendar, I asked her how she felt. She said she felt much more relaxed after the walk.

In the evening, Xiuying tidied up the kitchen herself and told me I didn't need to help. As I sat in the living room watching the news, she hummed a little tune while walking past the kitchen. I haven't heard that sound in a long time.

—

I’ll be playing chess with Big Brother Wang and the others again next Wednesday. He called today to say there’s a newcomer and wanted me to go "give him some pointers." I laughed and said I’m no grandmaster. Actually, I was thinking that after retirement, maybe I could spend more time on this. Not to win, but just... to sit together, play some chess, and chat. That’s enough.

Our grandson is supposed to call this weekend. Xiuying said she wants to tell him stories from when she was young. I said, "That sounds great—you tell the stories, and I’ll be right there listening."

But it’s fine. I’ll just keep learning tomorrow. I’ve still got two years left anyway; I’ll figure it out eventually.

I marked all the key data in red—the 99.7% passing rate, and how our customer complaint rate has been the lowest in the industry for years. I just sat there at my desk, staring at those numbers, and I had this feeling I can’t quite put into words.

We went through all of this together. Do you remember those years? I used to talk to you about work all the time at home. Back then, we had just finished paying off the mortgage, and I started obsessing over how to make the inspection standards even stricter. But things are different now. The higher-ups brought up that automated inspection system again the day before yesterday; they’re saying we need to bring in new machinery. I looked over some of the materials, and to be honest, I still don’t really understand how that system works. Those technical documents gave me a headache—they're full of terms I don't know. I asked Xiao Li, that young inspector, and he spent ages explaining it to me, but I’m still a bit lost.

Tell me, am I really getting old? (A short laugh, but the voice sounds a bit tired)

I’ve been thinking... I’m retiring in a couple of years anyway. Does all this data, all these achievements—do they still matter? Once the machines arrive, will they even need someone like me anymore? Everything I’ve spent my whole life doing...

But don't worry, I’m just overthinking things. While I was sorting through the files today, I pulled out that department group photo from ten years ago. Do you remember that one? Everyone from the department was in it. Everyone was still around back then.

What do you think? Am I just worrying too much?

(A brief pause)

Oh, by the way, have we set the date for your follow-up appointment at the hospital this month? I’ve noted down all the doctor’s orders on the calendar.

# Pre-retirement Ledger

**March 8, 2023 | 1:45 AM**

Can't sleep. Might as well get up and go over these numbers one more time.

---

## Basic Situation (Current Knowledge)

**Estimated Pension** (Based on current policy estimates)
- Basic Pension: approx. 3,200 RMB/month
- Corporate Annuity: approx. 1,800 RMB/month  
- Total: approx. 5,000 RMB/month
- ~~Calculated 4,800 before; checked the documents, should be around 5,000~~

**Monthly Essential Household Expenses** (Xiuying and me)
- Mortgage: 0 (Paid off back in 1998)
- Living Expenses: 1,500 RMB (Rice, oil, salt, wet market prices)
- Xiuying’s Medical: approx. 300-400 RMB (Hypertension meds, monthly check-ups)
- Utilities (Water, Electricity, Gas): 200 RMB
- Phone bill, daily sundries: 100 RMB
- **Subtotal: 2,100-2,200 RMB**

Remaining: approx. 2,800-2,900 RMB/month

---

## What if...

**Scenario 1: Xiuying’s health fails**
- What if her hypertension worsens and she needs hospitalization?
  - Medical insurance covers most, but what about out-of-pocket costs?
  - Emergency reserve: 5,000 RMB (Not enough, change to 8,000)
  - ~~Change again to 10,000 RMB~~
  - Current household savings: approx. 47,000 RMB

**Scenario 2: I land the consulting work**
- The new Director might have questions for me.
- If I can work as a consultant, what’s the fee per session? (Don't know the market rate; ask colleagues?)
- Conservative estimate: 500-800 RMB/session, 2-3 times a month?
- This could add 1,000-2,400 RMB/month.

**Scenario 3: My son’s business**
- Li Ming mentioned lately that competition at the building materials store is fierce.
- If he runs into cash flow issues, will he ask to borrow money?
- How much can I help? (Can't touch the retirement fund)
- Maximum support limit: 10,000 RMB.

**Scenario 4: Worst-case scenario**
- No consulting work.
- Xiuying needs long-term medication or hospitalization.
- Li Ming’s business actually goes under.
- Relying solely on the 5,000 RMB pension, minus 2,200 RMB living costs, leaves 2,800 RMB.
- How long can we hold out?

---

## Recalculation

**Plan A: Everything goes smoothly**
- Pension: 5,000 RMB
- Consulting: 1,500 RMB (Conservative)
- Monthly Surplus: 5,000 + 1,500 - 2,200 = **4,300 RMB**
- Annual Surplus: approx. 51,600 RMB

**Plan B: No consulting work**
- Pension: 5,000 RMB
- Monthly Surplus: 5,000 - 2,200 = **2,800 RMB**
- Annual Surplus: approx. 33,600 RMB
- In this case, how long would the 47,000 RMB savings last in an emergency?
- ~~47,000 ÷ (Emergency spending - Monthly surplus)~~
- Can't figure it out; too complicated.

**Plan C: Xiuying needs extra medical care**
- Assume an extra 5,000 RMB in medical costs per year.
- Monthly expenses become: 2,200 + 400 = 2,600 RMB.
- Monthly Surplus: 5,000 - 2,600 = **2,400 RMB**.
- Still manageable.

---

## Current Concerns

1. **Market rate for consulting is unclear**—Ask old colleagues next week, or ask the new Director directly? But that might seem too desperate/calculating.

2. **Xiuying’s health**—This month’s check-up results aren't out yet. Wait for the doctor’s advice. She’s been saying she feels tired lately; is her blood pressure up again?

3. **Li Ming’s business**—On the last call, he said orders this year aren't as good as last year. I want to help him, but I have to prepare for my own retirement. How do I find that balance?

4. **Are savings enough?**—47,000 RMB sounds like a lot, but if something really happens, what can that small amount actually do?

---

## One More Thing

What’s actually going to happen with the company reforms? I heard there might be a structural reorganization. If my department is merged or restructured, will my position be affected? I’m supposed to retire in two years, but if I’m sidelined now, the chances of getting that consulting work become even slimmer.

Besides, if the new system really goes live across the board, will they even need a consultant like me?

---

## Final Thoughts

Every time I go over these accounts, I feel more exhausted.

The numbers are correct, but it’s what lies behind them—the uncertainty, the changes, the risks—that’s what truly keeps me awake.

Still have to be up at 6:30 AM. Put the notebook down for now and try to get some sleep.

If I still can't sleep, I'll look at the quality performance charts. At least those are solid and real.

It turns out I was right. The flower is now completely open, with a vibrant red color and layer upon layer of petals—I counted about thirty of them. September 28, 2022 | Sunny | Morning

It was only yesterday that I noticed the leaves on my pink rose bush had turned yellow.

I stared at the pot for a long time. The leaves were already wilting, and the soil felt bone-dry to the touch. I kept trying to calculate in my head when I last watered it... I flipped through the memos on my phone, then through my notebook, but for the life of me, I couldn't be sure. There’s an entry in my notebook for September 24th, but as for my phone... I don’t even know if I recorded it or not.

This has never happened before.

I’ve had this plant for nearly three years. I know its watering cycle by heart and have never let it go thirsty. I even remembered to switch to a three-day schedule this autumn. But these past two weeks, I’ve been constantly fumbling with that phone—taking photos, making logs—because my daughter insisted I use the thing. And the result? I ended up forgetting the most important task of all.

I watered the plant last night and noted the time in my notebook. Then I just sat there on the balcony, looking at those yellowing leaves, feeling a heavy knot in my chest. If I hadn't been so distracted and frustrated by this phone, I would have caught the problem much sooner. I kept turning the phone over in my hands, still unsure how to tell if those photos and notes were actually saved properly. A notebook is different; once I write it down, it’s right there, clear as day.

Wang Xiuying asked me what was wrong. I told her it was nothing, just that the flowers were wilting a bit. She didn't say much else.

I can't help but wonder: if I’m starting to fail at something as simple as tending to a plant, what else is left? I’m still confident in my work; I know the inspection standards and procedures inside out. But I retire in two years. Two years. Once work is gone, will I truly be unable to keep up with anything?

My daughter wants me to learn how to use the phone; she says it’s for my own good. I know that. But I’m still... I’m still a little afraid. Afraid of losing data, afraid of pressing the wrong button, afraid that I’ll never actually learn.

When I woke up this morning, I decided I’m going back to using my notebook to track the plants. I can use the phone, but I can’t abandon the notebook. At least this way, I feel a bit more at peace.

Xiao Ming, you there?

Xiao Ling called today and said I’ve seemed more energetic lately, haha. I hadn't really noticed it myself, but she’s right—I have been feeling different these days.

Believe it or not, I just learned how to use voice input on my phone a couple of days ago. I used to think all these new features were too complicated, but once I tried it, I realized it’s actually not that hard. Now, when I’m too lazy to type, I just speak into it. Your mom even poked fun at me. I told her, just because we’re getting older doesn't mean we can't learn anything new; the key is just giving it a shot.

I was thinking about how you’re dealing with all the changes and competition in the building materials industry right now. Sometimes when you talk about it, it sounds like you’re under a lot of pressure. But look at me—your dad is almost at retirement age and I’m still learning new things. Some things look intimidating, but you don't realize they aren't that scary until you actually dive in.

How’s business lately? Do you need me to help brainstorm anything? Let’s grab dinner this weekend and have a good talk.

Oh, and your mom told me to ask how you guys are eating lately and how the kids are doing.

September 18, 2022 | Sunny | 22:00

I tried using the Notes app on my phone again today.

While watering the flowers on the balcony this afternoon, I remembered the steps my daughter taught me during the National Day holiday. I took out my phone and opened "Notes" just as she showed me. I wanted to record the status of the roses—the buds have grown another size, and the leaves have turned a deeper green. I typed very carefully, tapping out each character one by one... but when I got to the phrase "department photo from ten years ago," I suddenly stopped.

My finger just hovered over the screen, motionless.

What was I thinking? That photo... my subordinates gave it to me. They were all so young back then. Now, some have been promoted, and others transferred to different departments long ago. I’m still right here. Lost in those thoughts, I ended up deleting the entire line.

In the end, I didn't record anything.

Xiuying called me for dinner from the kitchen, so I put the phone down.

After dinner, I took out my notebook and recorded everything the old-fashioned way. "September 18: Rose buds approximately 1.2cm in diameter, leaves dark green, watered once with dechlorinated tap water." When the pen is on the paper and the words are written out, I feel at peace. I can see it, I can flip back to it; the data is right here, and it won't get lost.

As for the phone... I’m still not quite sure about it. My daughter is right; it is convenient, but what if the data just vanishes one day? I’ve used pen and paper my whole life and never had a problem. To be honest, I feel uneasy handing everything over to a "black box" I don't really understand.

I know my daughter means well. She says that once I retire, using my phone will be more convenient and can even set automatic reminders. I nod and listen, and I’ve tried. But... let’s just leave it at this: I’ll use both. I’ll take a photo with the phone to save it, and then write it down in my notebook. That’s the only way I feel secure.

Anyway, at my age, I’m slow to learn new things. I can try, but... I just can't fully rely on it.

I’ll be retiring in two years. I’ve been thinking about it a lot lately; sometimes I wake up in the middle of the night and just wonder. I can still do the work—I’m confident in quality inspection. But what about after retirement? The flowers at home, chess, spending time with family... will that be enough?

Even recording data about flowers requires learning something new now. Times have changed, and I’m falling behind.

Forget it, I’ll stop overthinking. Tomorrow, I’ll just keep watering the flowers.

Xiuying, something came to mind at the office today.

Xiao Wang asked me about the new system, so I started telling him about how meticulous traditional inspections are and the things a machine might miss. As I went on, even I felt I was being a bit long-winded. He just smiled and said, "Director, don't you worry, we’ll still be following your lead."

But I’ve been thinking—those words sound nice, but once the system is fully implemented and staff are downsized, "following my lead" will become a thing of the past. I didn't say anything more to Xiao Wang; I just turned around and went back to my office.

At lunch, I looked at that photo on the wall from ten years ago. Everyone in the department was there back then, and everyone was so driven. Now, some have retired, and others have been transferred. I was thinking that for these thirty-seven years, I’ve been just like that photo—fixed right here in this spot.

Xiuying, I wanted to ask you something. Do you still remember our retirement plan? I mean, if I didn't retire in two years, but... a year early, or even sooner, would our pension be enough? I’m not seriously considering it yet; I just want to think it through ahead of time.

I heard recently that the company is bringing in some new inspection system, an automated one. Management says it’s an upgrade, not a layoff. But you know how it is—you take those words with a grain of salt. All these years, what I’ve feared most isn't the hard work; it’s the day people realize they don't need me anymore.

Tell me, if a person is no longer useful, what value do they have left?

I haven't been sleeping well lately, as you’ve probably noticed. It’s not physical pain; my mind just won't shut off. I’m thinking about the house, your medical bills, Li Ming’s business, our grandson’s education... all of it needs to be secure. I don't want to be caught off guard when the time comes.

What do you think? Or maybe we could find some time to go over the books again?

December 11, 2024, Evening

Old Wang said something during our chess game today that has stayed with me.

I won two games in a row and was in high spirits. Xiuying mentioned that I’ve seemed much more energetic lately, and I feel it too. It’s probably because of the recordings—I’ve been telling stories for Jianhao these past few days and recording them on my phone. My daughter said Jianhao listens to them so intently and even asked when Grandpa is coming over again.

When my chess buddies asked why I’ve been so happy lately, I told them about what’s been going on with Jianhao. I talked about teaching him chess, telling him stories about my days at the factory, and using my phone to record them... As Old Wang listened, his eyes seemed to glisten a little. He said, "Old Li, you’re doing something big here."

I didn’t quite catch his meaning at first. Something big? I’m just spending time with my grandson and sharing some of my experiences.

But then he added, "In this life, the only things we can truly leave behind for the kids are these stories. By doing this, Jianhao will always remember his grandfather."

Sitting there, it suddenly clicked.

All those years at the factory, I was obsessed with product quality and inspection standards; I thought that was where my value lay. After I retired, I felt a real sense of loss for a while—like I was no longer useful. But now... I’m bringing that same dedication to spending time with my grandson, recording these stories, and teaching them how to be good people. Isn't this just another kind of "quality control"?

I spent thirty-seven years ensuring product pass rates; now, I’m going to use the rest of my time to ensure these memories and stories are passed down intact.

Old Wang said he wanted to tell stories to his grandson too and asked me how to record on a phone. I showed him how. Seeing how earnest he was reminded me of when I first started learning how to use a smartphone.

On the way home, the air was freezing, but I didn't feel the cold at all.

Xiuying asked if I won my games. I told her I did. She smiled and said, "You really are different now."

I knew exactly what she meant.

I’m no longer that old man who is afraid of change and new things. I’ve learned how to use a phone, and I’ve learned how to find new meaning in this new stage of life. And... I’m starting to believe that this meaning was always there; I’m only just now seeing it clearly.

I’ll keep recording for Jianhao tomorrow. There are still so many stories left to tell.

Jianhao laughed and asked, "Grandpa, where’s your face?" That’s when I realized he could see me, too. He was telling me all about the new Lego set he built; he was talking so fast I couldn't catch everything, but I just kept watching him. I watched his mouth move, watched his eyes.

You know, I was thinking—it felt just like back at the factory when I’d go over inspection reports. You have to look closely; you have to hear every single detail. I told myself to listen to him properly, not to get distracted.

Later he said, "Grandpa, why aren't you saying anything?" and I realized I’d just been staring at him without speaking. So I asked him about the colors of the Legos, and off he went again.

When you hugged me from behind just now, I was still holding the phone. At that moment, I really… I could hardly believe it. I always thought this kind of thing was beyond me, but our daughter said I could do it, so I gave it a try. I didn't expect it to actually work.

I’m going to practice a few more times later so I don't get confused next time. I’m a little tired now, but… I’m very happy.

Do you think I’m just getting old? That something so simple takes me this long to learn?

(Pause)

But it’s okay. I’ll keep learning. To be able to see Jianhao often, I’m willing to learn anything.

Go get some rest. I’m going to look at the phone a bit longer and go over the steps our daughter taught me one more time, just so I don't forget.

July 23, 2023, Night

...

Another exhausting day. Ming called me this afternoon. He said he wants to launch an online store and give up on the physical shop. My immediate instinct was to talk him out of it—the building materials business is all about inspecting the goods and comparing quality; how could it possibly work online? I spent ages analyzing it for him, telling him not to rush and that he needs to play it safe.

He listened to what I had to say, and then simply told me his mind was made up: he’s going through with the transition.

I didn't say anything more. I just said, "It's your call," and hung up.

Xiuying asked me what was wrong. I told her that when it comes to the building materials industry, I just don't understand it anymore.

As soon as the words left my mouth, I stunned even myself. The truth is, I do understand it—how could I not? It’s just that... I don't have the energy to think about these things anymore. If Ming won't listen to me, then my advice is useless anyway. Maybe he’s right. Maybe this is just how the times are meant to change.

I’ve worked in the Quality Department for so many years, always sticking to my standards, and the clients have always been satisfied. But now? The young people under me are using all these smart systems while I’m still using the old methods. The higher-ups are talking about "restructuring," and I don't even know how much longer I’ll be sitting in this chair. Tomorrow I have to take Xiuying to the hospital for her follow-up; her blood pressure has been high again lately.

I’m a little tired.

It’s not physical exhaustion; it’s a different kind of tired. It’s the feeling that I’m becoming useless in this world. My son doesn't listen to my advice, and I don't know how much time I have left at the company. I’m supposed to retire in two years, but right now, even imagining life after retirement feels empty.

The roses on the balcony still need watering. I’ll get up at 6:30 tomorrow morning, watch the news for ten minutes, and then water the flowers. It’s time to log the watering dates again.

These things still need to be done.

It’s just... sometimes I wonder if I really have grown old.

# Work Log

**Date:** Monday, January 16, 2023  
**Weather:** Sunny  
**Recorded by:** Li Jianguo

---

## Morning Key Tasks

**8:30-9:15 Communication with QC Inspector Xiao Wang**

Xiao Wang reported the inspection data from Production Line D for last Friday. The newly introduced automated inspection system had issues identifying dimensional deviations in some products—three items were flagged as "pass" by the system, but manual re-inspection revealed minor surface defects.

Key points of discussion:
- The automated system relies primarily on data parameters and has limited capability in identifying certain latent defects (such as microscopic surface cracks, material inconsistency, etc.).
- Experienced inspectors can make comprehensive judgments through multi-dimensional information such as visual observation, touch, and sound.
- It is recommended to maintain necessary manual spot checks after the system inspection, especially for critical components.

Xiao Wang agreed with this suggestion. He mentioned that operational training for the new system is still ongoing; while younger employees are learning quickly, they lack a sufficient understanding of the system's limitations.

**9:20-10:00 Compiling Inspection Reports**

Summarized the quality data for the first 15 days of this month. The pass rate remains at 98.7%, basically flat compared to the same period last year. The customer complaint rate has decreased slightly.

---

## Reflections

This conversation with Xiao Wang highlights a key issue: relying solely on automated systems is not enough. Quality management requires a combination of systems and experience. The inspection standards and processes our department has established over the years cannot be simply replaced by machines.

I plan to call a full department meeting this afternoon to emphasize the importance of manual inspection in quality control, while also requiring everyone to familiarize themselves with the new system's operation as soon as possible—this is an inevitable trend.

---

# MEMO

**Date: March 16, 2023, 10:30 PM**

**Subject: A few things before retirement**

---

I was flipping through that ten-year-old group photo in the office recently and felt a sudden wave of nostalgia. I’ve been thinking about these things again today, so I figured I’d just write them down to stop them from circling in my head all day.

## 1. Regarding the Work Handover

I need to compile a checklist of the Quality Department's data from the past thirty-plus years:
- Annual qualification rate statistics (1998–2023)
- Records of major quality incident handling (Zero major incidents—this needs to be stated clearly)
- Customer complaint resolution rates and satisfaction data
- The evolution of inspection standard development

This isn't about proving anything; I just feel a record should be left behind. It’ll be useful for the new Director when they take over. I plan to digitize all of this and save it on the computer before I retire. I’m not great with those new systems, but I can manage basic spreadsheets.

## 2. Post-Retirement Arrangements

**Consulting Work:**
I could consider continuing to offer advice to the new Director. No need to go in every day—maybe two or three times a month. This way, I can supplement my pension and keep myself busy. Xiuying says being idle is no good, and I think she’s right.

**Time Allocation:**
- Keep the Wednesday chess games; that’s a non-negotiable.
- Spend more time gardening at home; the roses are doing well this year.
- Try to spend as much time as possible with Xiuying. Continue the monthly hospital check-ups; I need to keep an eye on her blood pressure.
- Try to visit Jianhao in Shanghai more often. He’s ten now, and I want to be there for him more.

**Regarding my younger son, Li Ming:**
I need to keep thinking about his building materials business. Maybe I’ll actually have more time to reflect on this after I retire. I’ll have a proper chat with him about his recent operations the next time we speak on the phone.

## 3. On the Matter of "Value"

I’ve been thinking a lot lately. I used to think value was just work, achievements, and those data points. Now I’m slowly realizing it’s not just that.

Managing a department well for over thirty years without any major product quality issues—what does that say? It speaks to attentiveness, a sense of responsibility, and perseverance. These things don’t disappear just because I’m retired.

Only the form changes. It shifts from quality management in the office to looking after Xiuying’s health, spending time with my grandsons, and giving my son advice. I suppose these are also forms of "quality"—the quality of life.

I’m not very good at putting these things into words, but that’s the gist of it.

## 4. Specific Reminders

- [ ] Specific arrangements for consulting work—find a time to talk to the supervisor

- [ ] Wednesday chess game as usual
- [ ] Call Jianhao this month to see how he’s doing
- [ ] Put together some thoughts on the building materials industry for a proper chat with Li Ming

Time really flies. Sometimes I look in the mirror and hardly recognize myself. But there’s no need to be too pessimistic. The things that need doing still need doing, and the people who need my company still need me there.

That’s all for now.

Monday, July 10, 2023. Sunny.

I skipped lunch today. I just sat in the office, staring out the window at the factory buildings for a long time.

This morning, Master Wang asked me what I was writing in my notebook. I told him I was helping Li Ming with some business matters. He casually mentioned that his grandson works at an internet company, talking about things like "big data" and "cloud computing"—terms I don't quite understand. He sounded quite proud, so I just smiled and didn't say much. But in that moment, I felt a sudden pang of sadness—I’m starting to lose touch with the world of the younger generation, too.

I spoke with Li Ming on the phone the day before yesterday. He said he wants to start an online store and overhaul the supply chain model. I gave him some advice, all very steady and practical ideas—consider the costs, watch the market, ensure the quality is rock-solid. I sounded confident when I said it, just like a supervisor should. But after hanging up, I started wondering: does he really need advice like mine? I’ve spent over thirty years in quality inspection; I know how to make a product good. But he’s facing a market that is shifting entirely, an internet-driven world, things I’ve never experienced. Is my advice just an old way of trying to solve new problems?

The factory has been pushing automation systems lately, too. At last week’s meeting, the leadership talked about improving inspection efficiency and introducing "intelligent testing equipment." I didn't say anything at the time, but I knew in my heart—those things will eventually replace what we do now. I’ve been in this position for thirty-seven years, relying on experience and an eye for detail. But machines don't need those things; they just need a program.

I have always believed that quality is the lifeblood of an enterprise. I’ve never wavered in that belief, and I don't want to. But now I’m wondering: is that enough anymore? In this day and age, is quality enough? Or is it just that I can't keep up?

Skipping lunch isn't like me. I’ve always been someone who eats on schedule. But today, I just couldn't sit still; my mind is a mess.

I’m retiring in two years. I used to think about it occasionally as something far off in the distance. Now, it feels like it’s closing in. What should I do? Keep doing consulting? Or just stay home, tend to flowers, and play chess? But if I do that, what use am I anymore?

There’s another meeting this afternoon. I’ll have some water first and try to clear my head.

# Friday, August 16, 2024 – Sunny

Woke up at 6:30 AM again, right on time. Xiuying was still asleep, but I didn’t get up immediately like I usually do. I just lay there for a while, thinking.

The little things that happened yesterday are still spinning in my head.

She sat on the edge of the bed of her own accord, waiting for me to take her blood pressure. I had my opening line all ready—“Xiuying, time for your blood pressure”—but she spoke first. She said, "You don't have to remember to remind me every time anymore; I know this is good for me now." I was stunned for a second; I almost fumbled the monitor.

It sounds like a simple sentence, but in that moment, something clicked. All these years of keeping track of doctor’s orders, setting alarms, and reminding her to take her pills—I thought that was what "taking care" meant. But the truth is... she doesn't need me to "manage" her anymore. She understands now; she’s accepted it. This is her own choice, not a plan I’m forcing her to follow.

I didn’t say anything; I just took her blood pressure. The numbers were good—everything normal.

Later, she suggested a walk. It wasn’t the first time, but this time felt different. She wasn't waiting for my arrangement; she just said, "Let’s go for a walk." I was about to say, "Okay, we have a walk scheduled for today"—but the words reached my lips and I changed them to, "Okay, let’s go together."

Just that one small difference in phrasing.

As we walked downstairs, I wondered if I’ve spent all these years bringing my work habits home with me. At the factory, I had to set standards, execute plans, and ensure every step was perfect. I took pride in that. But Xiuying isn't a production line. My value shouldn't just be about "getting things right."

I feel a bit lost, to be honest. That feeling of "everything is under my control" has faded a little. But at the same time... there’s a sense of relief I can’t quite put into words.

Maybe this is what I need to learn. Not to give up being meticulous, but to add some flexibility. Not to stop caring, but to change the way I care.

Xiuying is still sleeping. I should get up and watch the news for ten minutes.

But this time, I’ll wait for her to wake up and ask her what she wants to do today.

# Work Log

**Date: Tuesday, August 8, 2023**
**Weather: Sunny, 32°C**

---

I noticed something worth documenting in the inspection workshop this morning.

While checking the third batch of products, the new smart inspection system’s assessment of a specific weld seam didn't align with my visual inspection. The system flagged it as "Marginal Pass," but based on my years of experience, the depth of that seam should be held to a stricter standard. In the past, I might have just dismissed the machine’s conclusion outright. But this time was different.

This afternoon, I sat down with Xiao Wang, the technician, and we went over the issue in detail. I explained my criteria clearly—it’s not that the system is wrong, but rather that its parameters might need fine-tuning based on the actual characteristics of our products. Xiao Wang listened intently and took notes. We looked at the magnified images of the weld together, and he showed me how to adjust the tolerance values in the system.

The conclusion we reached is this: it’s not that the system isn't good; it’s that we need to use our experience to help it become smarter.

To be honest, this realization came as a bit of a surprise—and it was my own thought. I used to think it was either human experience or the machine that was right. Now I understand that the 37 years of knowledge I’ve built up in this role isn't meant to be replaced by the new system; instead, it can be used to guide the system to work better. Thinking about it this way makes me feel much more at ease.

The thought was still on my mind as I headed home this evening. I feel like I’ve found a new role—not just as an inspector, but as someone helping new technology successfully take root. Being able to do something like this before I retire makes it all feel meaningful.

I’ll follow up on the results of the parameter adjustments tomorrow.

Sunday, July 21, 2024, 6:45 AM

Yesterday, I took Xiuying’s blood pressure again. It came out to 145/92. Looking at those numbers, my heart skipped a beat.

Last week it was 138/88; I can’t believe it jumped this much in just a week. I didn’t say anything at the time; I just gave a non-committal "mm-hmm" and measured it again. The result was the same. She didn't seem to mind much, saying it was probably just the heat and all the walking she did yesterday. But I know something is off.

Last night, I dug out the pamphlets the doctor gave us and looked some things up on my phone—I’m still not very good with this smartphone, but this is something I need to understand. It’s true that blood pressure fluctuations can be affected by activity, emotions, and the weather, but a jump like this... it makes me uneasy.

I went back through the last three months of records in my notebook. Starting in mid-June, there’s been a clear upward trend. I marked it in red. We used to go for walks twice a week, but it seems that’s not enough anymore. The doctor mentioned increasing aerobic exercise; my previous plan was probably too conservative.

So, I’ve made a decision—starting this week, we’re switching to three walks a week. Monday, Wednesday, and Friday, thirty minutes each time. I wrote the new plan in red ink in the notebook and noted the date next to it. Xiuying will see it tomorrow. I’ll have to explain it clearly so she understands this isn't just a sudden whim, but a calculated decision.

I know what I’m doing. This is exactly how I handled problems during my thirty-plus years at the factory—detect an anomaly in the data, identify the cause, develop a plan for improvement, and then execute. Applying this to Xiuying’s health, I can feel that familiar sense of "control" returning. It’s not a bad thing. It means I can still do something; I can still protect her.

But I have to admit, when I saw that 145, I was genuinely nervous. This isn't like a defective part at the factory that can be reworked or scrapped. This is Xiuying’s body.

The sun is up. I need to head downstairs to buy breakfast. Getting these thoughts down has made me feel much more settled.

November 28, 2022, 8:00 PM

When Xiuying held my hand and said, "You don't need to completely change yourself," I suddenly felt the tightness that had been weighing on my chest these past few days loosen a bit.

We were sitting on the sofa, her right beside me. The news was playing on the TV, but neither of us was really watching. I was thinking about how, after all these years, she is still the one who understands me best.

I’ve been agonizing over things these last two days—the smartphone, the notebook, retirement, and whether I’m truly getting old. My mind has been a bit of a mess from overthinking. I even caught myself wondering: if I can’t even master a phone, what will I be able to do after I retire? That thought was particularly painful. It felt like I still had some worth at work, but once I left the factory, I’d be nothing.

But Xiuying said that retirement doesn't necessarily mean everything is over.

We talked for a long time. I told her I wanted to keep gardening after I retired and that I couldn't stop my monthly logbooks. She said, "That sounds good." I said I also wanted to keep playing chess with my friends and perhaps do some consulting work for the new director—after all, thirty-seven years of experience shouldn't just be thrown away. She said "good" again, adding that it would also bring in some extra income to supplement the pension.

Most importantly, I want to spend more time with my grandson. This thought made me realize that perhaps my value isn't limited to quality inspection.

Thinking about it now, I don't need to completely change who I am. I’m still the same Li Jianguo—organized, detail-oriented, fond of keeping records, and trusting of my notebooks. Those things won't change. But perhaps I can define myself in a new way. It’s not about forcing myself to learn every single new thing; it’s about staying true to myself while slowly accepting the changes that are necessary.

I still need to use the phone, but I can keep using my notebooks too. There’s nothing wrong with both ways coexisting.

Holding Xiuying’s hand, I suddenly wasn't so afraid anymore. Maybe retirement isn't an end, but another beginning.

Tomorrow I have to take her to the hospital for her follow-up appointment. Life goes on, one day at a time.

Xiuying, I’m home.

Everyone showed up for the farewell party today. I gave a little speech and talked about everything that’s happened over the years. Towards the end, I... my voice cracked. I didn’t expect myself to get like that.

They gave me a photo album filled with pictures from all these years. My hands were shaking a bit when I took it.

I’m sitting on the sofa now, feeling a bit tired. Not physically, just... my head is a bit of a blur.

How was your day? Did you have breakfast on time? What did you have for lunch?

I’ve put the group photo and the achievement awards in a cardboard box for now; I’ll hang them up once I’ve figured it out. Which wall in the living room do you think would be best?

What do you want for dinner? I’ll go out and get the groceries.

Xiuying, are you still up? I wanted to talk to you about something.

I went over your blood pressure logs from the past two weeks again today and organized them into that spreadsheet. Look, for the week of November 1st to the 7th, your morning readings were mostly between 140 and 145, and they didn't really drop much in the afternoon. I’ve followed everything the doctor said—low-sodium meals, making sure you take your medicine on time, and going for daily walks. But the numbers are still stuck there.

I’m wondering if the medication isn't effective enough anymore, or if maybe the dosage needs to be adjusted. We can't just wait around for the next follow-up; I think we should go to the hospital and talk to the doctor sooner. Maybe tomorrow or the day after, rather than waiting for the original appointment.

I know you’ve been exhausted lately, but I really don't think we should put this off. Your health is the most important thing. I’ve got all the records organized and ready to show the doctor so they can get a clearer picture of what’s going on.

What do you think? Do you have time tomorrow?

March 2, 2023, 10:30 PM

Insomnia again. I was tossing and turning in bed while Wang Xiuying had long been asleep, her breathing steady and even. Not wanting to wake her, I got up and sat in the study.

I went to the Technical Department this afternoon to ask about that new system. I thought getting a better understanding might clear up some of my doubts. Instead, I was handed a thick stack of technical documents. I flipped through them for half an hour until my head felt like it was spinning. "Database architecture," "cloud storage," "real-time synchronization"... I recognize all these words, but when they’re strung together, they might as well be written in a foreign language. The young technician was patient enough to explain things to me, but I could tell he was dumbing it down. In that moment, I thought: *That’s it. I might really never learn this.*

I called Li Xiaoling. She was busy with work in Shanghai; I could hear it in her voice. I told her, "I might really be too old to learn these new things." She paused for a moment on the other end, then said, "Dad, don't talk like that. You’ve been at the factory for so many years; what haven't you seen? It’s just a system. You just have to learn it." Her voice was firm, as if she were trying to bolster my courage. I didn't say much else and hung up.

But her words have been spinning in my head. She’s right—how could I say something like that? It’s been 37 years. From a line worker to a manager, what difficulty haven't I overcome? It’s just a system...

At dinner tonight, I told Wang Xiuying that the company is holding a training session and that I’ve decided to go. She glanced at me and didn't say anything, just nodded. I could feel her support.

I took out my notebook and wrote "New System Training – March 15," then added "Must Learn" in parentheses afterward. The tip of my pen rested on the paper for a long time.

It’s not that I want to run away. I’m just... a little tired. It feels like everything I’ve spent half my life building is suddenly being replaced by something invisible. The inspection records I can touch with my hands, the details I can judge by experience, the methods I can teach my subordinates—is all of that really becoming useless?

But I also know this isn't up to me. Time moves forward, and I can't just stand still. Just like when the planned economy transitioned to the joint-stock system, I don't like change, but I always manage to adapt. This time will be no different.

Back to work tomorrow. I will attend that training. I will learn that system.

I have no other choice.

Perhaps this is just what must be done.
