# February 22, 2023, 9:00 PM

Xiuying was right; I’m probably overthinking things. But then again, overthinking is better than being caught off guard.

During dinner, she asked if I’ve been under a lot of pressure at work lately, saying I seemed distracted while eating. I didn't want to hide anything, so I told her about the rumors regarding the restructuring. After listening, she tried to comfort me, saying that I’ve been at this factory for thirty-seven years and that the leadership trusts me—they wouldn't just get rid of me that easily. She also mentioned that the house is paid off and we have our pensions, so there’s no need to worry too much.

Hearing her say that did make me feel a bit more relaxed at the time. But after she went to watch TV, I sat in the study for a long while, still thinking.

The problem is... everything she said is right, yet none of it is enough.

There’s a question I’ve been thinking about, one I haven't dared to ask out loud: Is it more accurate for the younger staff to use that new system than for me to do manual inspections? I’m not trying to put myself down; I’m genuinely wondering. For thirty-seven years, I’ve built a system based on attention to detail and experience. But if a machine can do it just as well, or even better... then what do these thirty-seven years even mean?

No, I shouldn't think like that. My inspection standards are industry-recognized; the product pass rates and customer satisfaction levels are right there. Those aren't just empty numbers. But times are changing, and I can see it. The younger generation is much more proficient with smartphones than I am, and they’ll certainly be faster with the system. It’s not that I can’t learn, it’s just... it feels a bit late.

I listed a few things in my notebook:

**If the department is cut:**
- How much is the compensation for early retirement?
- Will my pension be affected?
- How much can I make up through consulting fees?

**If I stay but the department is downsized:**
- How will the staffing be adjusted?
- Will the scope of my responsibilities change?
- How long will it take me to learn the new system?

**Family situation:**
- Xiuying’s high blood pressure medication—how much per month?
- If she needs more check-ups, how much will medical insurance cover?
- Ming’s building materials business—competition has been tough lately; has he told me the whole truth?

**After retirement:**
- Can I spend more time with my two grandsons?
- Can I continue doing consulting work? How much could I earn?

I know making a list like this might seem a bit... anxious. But this is my way of doing things. Once it’s written down, at least my mind is clear. It’s not just aimless worrying; it’s preparation with a plan.

I still have to go to work tomorrow. What needs to be inspected will be inspected, and the standards that need to be strict will remain strict. That won't change. Regardless of whether the new system comes or not, my professional integrity will not be compromised.

It’s just... sometimes I think, there are only two years left until retirement. In two years, what can really change?

Or rather, is two years enough time for me to prepare for anything?

September 17, 2024  Sunny

While watering the plants on the balcony this afternoon, I suddenly remembered the photography technique my daughter taught me last time. The roses are blooming beautifully—the pink pot already has five blossoms—so I thought, why not try to capture them with my phone?

I felt a bit nervous when I took out my phone. The first few shots were all blurry, or the flowers were cut off halfway. I didn't rush, though. Just like inspecting products at the factory, I first looked for what went wrong. The light was too harsh, so I moved to a different spot. The angle wasn't right, so I adjusted it. After about seven or eight tries, I finally got a clear shot. You can see the texture of the petals clearly, and the background is blurred just right.

After taking the photo, I sat there and stared at it for quite a while.

When I called my daughter tonight, I brought it up myself. she laughed on the other end and said, "Dad, you're making progress so fast." It made me think that maybe I’m not as "hopeless" as I thought. All these years, I’ve felt like I couldn't learn new things, but the truth is I just never really tried. Now that I have a motivation—to video call Jianhao and show him what the flowers look like—it doesn't seem that difficult anymore.

Later, I jotted down the steps for taking a photo in my notebook:
1. Lighting—don't shoot against the light.
2. Angle—keep the flower in the center.
3. Distance—don't get too close.
4. Pause for a moment before pressing the shutter to let the lens focus.

As I was writing, I realized that this is exactly the same method I use to set inspection standards in the Quality Department. You break something complex down into steps; every step has a reason, and every step requires repeated verification. Only this time, I wasn't inspecting a product—I was learning something new. It feels different.

I have a video call with Jianhao on Tuesday. I want to show him this photo of the roses. Maybe he’ll ask me how I took it. When that happens, I can teach him.

The thought makes me quite happy.

Xiuying, I won three games of chess today.

A winning streak, actually. Xiao Wang and the others said I’ve been in top form lately and even asked if I’ve been taking some kind of tonic (laughs). I just told them it’s probably because I’ve been using my brain more lately. I gave some advice regarding the new director’s project, and they said the results were quite good.

Actually, I wanted to tell you—when you supported me in taking on this consulting work, I was still a bit hesitant. Now I finally understand what you meant. It’s not that I *have* to do something, it’s just—how should I put it?—it feels good to know I’m still useful.

I also looked into the consulting fees. It’s not a huge amount, but it adds up over a month. I was thinking we could use the money to get you a better blood pressure monitor and those vitamins the doctor said you need to take. Also, for Chinese New Year this year, could we spend more time with Jianhao? I want to buy him some things he likes and teach him how to play chess.

But I’ve made up my mind: this shouldn't interfere with our current life. I’ll still play chess on Wednesdays as usual, and the flowers still need watering. I’ll just use some of my spare time to answer questions for the new director. It feels like a good balance.

What do you think?

# Diary

**Friday, October 25, 2024, 8:30 PM**

I got home from playing chess to find my wife already asleep. I’m sitting on the balcony now, looking at the silhouettes of the Chinese roses in the dark, feeling a bit restless.

This afternoon at the community center, I played three rounds with a few old chess buddies. Around five o'clock, I took the initiative to tell them, "Gentlemen, I have to call my grandson now; this is our scheduled time." They all smiled and said it was no problem, even asking why I’ve been so punctual lately.

I didn't offer much of an explanation. I took out my phone, started the video call, and my grandson’s face appeared on the screen. He was at his home in Shanghai, wearing his school uniform with his backpack behind him. We talked for about ten minutes—he told me his school is having an autumn field trip this week and asked if I had any advice. I told him about an outing organized by the factory back when I was young, and he listened very intently.

After hanging up, I didn't put my phone away immediately. My chess friends gathered around and asked if I had been video chatting with my grandson. I nodded, and then, for some reason, I pulled up my photos to show them a picture of us together—it was taken the last time they came home for Chinese New Year.

They all said, "Wow, Director Li, you’re really getting the hang of that smartphone." Someone else asked, "When did you learn how to take photos?"

I just smiled. To be honest, I didn't expect myself to be so proactive about showing it off. The old me certainly wouldn't have done that. But in that moment, I just wanted them to know—about my grandson, our connection, and the fact that I can do these things now.

On the way back, I was thinking about something. For thirty-seven years at the factory, I was the Director of the Quality Inspection Department. Back then, my job was to be the gatekeeper; I had to be strict, and there was no room for even the slightest oversight. My superiors trusted me, and my subordinates listened to me. That was my identity, and I was proud of it.

But things are different now. I’m still that same meticulous person, still someone who cares deeply about details. It’s just that now, I’m using those qualities to be a grandfather. I remember the time for my calls with my grandson with the same precision I used to record inspection data. I learned to take photos on my phone with the same diligence I used to master inspection tools. I tell my grandson stories with the same patience I used to explain inspection standards to my staff.

This isn't a step down, nor is it a sense of loss. It’s just... a transition.

My life has a new rhythm now. The 5:00 PM video call every Wednesday has become a fixed fixture, just like Wednesday night chess. Watering the roses every morning and noting the date. Accompanying Xiuying to the hospital every month. These things might seem ordinary, but they are the entirety of my life now.

I’m no longer just "adjusting" to retirement; I am enjoying it.

My friends' surprise caught me off guard, but more than that, it gave me a sense of... fulfillment. Not pride, exactly, but a confirmation—that I’m doing the right thing. I haven't been left behind by the times; I’m embracing change in my own way.

The roses are swaying gently in the night breeze. I’ll need to water them tomorrow morning.

September 29, 2024  Sunny

When the new director called yesterday afternoon, I was sitting in my office, still holding a test report. He said there was an issue with the system's logic for identifying defects and wanted my take on it. I didn't answer right away. Instead, I glanced at the department photo from ten years ago hanging on the wall, and then at the inspection standards I had drafted back then—they’re still posted on his desk.

It was a strange moment. It wasn't exactly the feeling of being needed; it was more like... a sense of continuity.

I asked him a few questions, just as I have for thirty-seven years—first about the background, then the symptoms, and finally what he thought himself. But this time was different. I didn't give him the answer; I guided him to think it through. To be honest, it felt fresh. It wasn't the "this is how you should do it" of a director, but more like discussing a move with a friend over a game of chess—equal and gentle.

When he thanked me at the end, I said, "It’s the least I could do." As the words left my mouth, I realized my tone had completely changed. It was no longer that professional, detached cadence, but something... relaxed. It was as if I had truly accepted my retirement, and now I was simply continuing what I’ve always done, just in a different way.

This morning, as I sat at the dining table biting into a fried dough stick, Xiuying asked how yesterday went. I didn't go into detail; I just smiled. She seemed to sense something and smiled back.

I’ve been wondering, what exactly is this? Is it a reluctance to leave work behind, or have I found a better way to define myself? I’m not sure. But what I do know is that I don’t want to go back to sitting in that office every day, nor do I want to be the one setting those harsh standards for others. I also don’t want to let being "needed" disrupt my life as it is now—playing chess with friends every Wednesday, watering the flowers on weekends, and taking Xiuying to the hospital every month. These things matter.

Maybe I could give this consulting thing a try. But there must be boundaries. I can’t let it become a new source of pressure, nor can I let myself slip back into that "it has to be me" mindset.

I am no longer the person who has to prove himself. But it seems I don't need to completely disown that person, either.

I need to give Jianhao a call tonight to see how he’s doing. Also, I must check the leaves on the China roses; they’ve been looking a bit wilted lately.

Xiuying, I was teaching Jianhao how to play xiangqi in the activity room today. That kid is a quick learner. I spent ages explaining "the horse moves in a day-shape and the elephant moves in a field-shape" to him, and he finally got the hang of it.

Old Wang, one of my chess buddies, saw how meticulously I was teaching and remarked, "The way you're teaching that kid is just as earnest as when you used to train new employees back at the factory." I didn't say anything at the time, but thinking back, he’s right—it really is the same.

I felt so energized while teaching him, guiding him through it step by step. Seeing him slowly start to understand gave me such a sense of joy. It makes me feel like I can still do something meaningful, rather than just idling away my time. My grandson is coming over again next month, so I’ll have to think about what to teach him next.

Don't you agree? Having someone who needs your guidance makes you feel so much more grounded.

Xiuying, look at my phone. Xiaoling taught me how to do video calls today, and I’ve memorized it all.

She demonstrated it for me twice, but I was afraid I wouldn’t remember clearly, so I drew a flowchart in my notebook. Look—first you tap this green icon, then find Jianhao’s name, and then press that camera button. I even numbered the steps, just like our inspection procedures back at the factory; step by step, no room for error.

Xiaoling said my method is actually quite good and that I’m learning faster than she expected. Ha! I told her all those years of work didn't go to waste—no matter how complex something is, there’s always a logic to it.

I want to try calling Jianhao tomorrow. I’m a bit nervous, worried I’ll mess up the steps and end up with a black screen—how embarrassing would that be? But then I think about being able to see his face and hear his voice, and suddenly these little difficulties seem like nothing. He’s ten now, so big already, and we only get to see him a few times a year.

Do you think I’m learning this the right way? Should I practice a few more times just to be absolutely sure?

Oh, Xiaoling also taught me how to adjust the camera angle so he can see my face clearly. I tried it out on the balcony; the light is quite nice there. Our pot of Chinese roses is right in the background, too—it looks lovely.

Let’s have dinner early. After we eat, I’m going to go over the flowchart one more time to make sure I have the position of every button memorized. I really can’t let things fall through the cracks this time.

July 28, 2024. Night.

Xiuying said I looked upset yesterday.

I didn’t explicitly mention that she ate things she wasn’t supposed to, but she noticed anyway. After forty-one years, she knows me too well. As I sat at the dining table watching the braised pork in her bowl, I didn’t say a word, but inside... it felt like discovering a defective product at the factory. All the standards had been breached.

I was a bit angry. Not at her, but at myself.

Sitting on the balcony tonight, I took out my notebook to write down the sentence: "She is not a product." Halfway through, I stopped. Then, I crossed it out forcefully and wrote again: "Caring for a person is not the same as managing a product."

I sat there for a long time, staring at those two lines.

For thirty-seven years, the habits I built at the factory—standards, records, inspections, improvements—those things were useful for products. But Xiuying isn't a product. She is a human being. A person with cravings, a temper, and her own thoughts.

That menu I drafted, the blood pressure logs, all those plans I thought were so meticulous... I believed that was how I was taking care of her. But in reality? I was managing her life like a job. I was holding her to that standard of "perfect execution."

She ate a piece of braised pork. It’s a small thing. But my reaction was as if she had violated some major disciplinary code.

This is wrong.

I remember her smiling at me yesterday and saying, "You, Director, still trying to manage people even at home." I didn't truly listen then. Now I understand—she was telling me, in her own gentle way, to stop.

I don’t know how to adjust. This is just how my brain works. When I see something that doesn't meet the standard, I want to correct it. But what Xiuying needs probably isn't a perfect plan, but a... husband. A husband who can be there for her, understand her, and occasionally allow her to be a little headstrong.

The roses are still in bloom. I forgot to water them today.

Tomorrow, I need to have a real talk with her. Not an explanation, but an apology.

I’ve been wondering what’s left for me to do now that I’m retired. My old company calls sometimes to ask for advice for the new manager, and I help out. But that always feels a bit empty, like I’m just repeating the past.

But teaching Jianhao chess... that’s different. This is something I’m passing down to him, something he’ll use for the rest of his life. It’s not just the skill, it’s... how should I put it? A way of doing things.

Tonight, Xiuying asked how my day was, and I told her it was pretty good. She said, "You’ve been in much better spirits lately." I hadn't really noticed, but it seems she's right.

Maybe what I’ve been looking for isn't a way to keep doing factory work, but a new way to be "needed." I just haven't fully figured out what that looks like yet.

Tomorrow, I have to take Xiuying to the hospital for her follow-up. Wednesday, back to chess with the guys. Maybe next time Jianhao comes over, I can teach him some more difficult openings.

One step at a time.

Wednesday, July 3, 2024
Sunny

Another day for chess. I woke up at 6:30 again, spent ten minutes reading the news, and had breakfast. Xiuying mentioned that I haven't been eating much lately. I didn't say anything, but I had half a bowl of porridge more than usual.

When I arrived at the community activity room at 3:00 PM, Old Wang and the others were already there. We set up the board as usual. As I sat down, my mind was still on the office—the new director asked me about the details of the inspection process last week. I gave him a few suggestions, but I'm not sure if he actually took them to heart. My mind kept wandering, and I couldn't focus on the game.

I made an obvious blunder in the middle of the match and didn't even realize it. Old Wang asked me, "Why are you so out of it today?" I paused for a long time before saying, "I guess I just haven't adjusted yet." The moment I said it, I felt strange—I’ve been retired for a month now; why am I still saying I "haven't adjusted"?

I lost in the end. Losing itself is nothing new; I used to lose all the time. But today felt different. I sat there, staring at the board, and suddenly I didn't want to be there anymore. Usually, I’d stay and chat for a while after a game, but today I said I had things to do at home and left in a hurry. Truth is, there was nothing to do at home. Xiuying was watching TV, and the plants had already been watered.

On the walk back, I wondered: what exactly am I doing? Before I retired, I knew exactly what I had to do every day—check production flows, review reports, handle complaints. And now? Now I have all the time in the world, yet I can’t seem to do anything right. I can’t even play a decent game of chess.

I can’t go on like this. But then again, I don’t know what else to do.

Tomorrow is another day. Xiuying’s follow-up appointment for her high blood pressure is coming up soon; I need to make sure I mark it on the calendar.

October 26, 2022, 8:00 PM

Xiuying checked her blood pressure again today. I didn’t ask her to; she wanted to do it herself. 148/92.

I sat beside her, watching the numbers pop up, and my heart sank. At her last check-up, it was 146/88, and the doctor said it was under control. But this time... I didn't say anything immediately. I just took out my notebook and wrote the numbers down. Xiuying asked me, "Is it high again?" I said, "It’s fine, just keeping a record." But in truth, I was thinking that this trend isn't right.

I always thought that as long as she took her medicine on time, watched her salt intake, and went for regular check-ups, her blood pressure would stay stable. It’s like back at the factory—set the process, set the standards, do the inspections, and problems won't arise. But now I’m realizing that this logic might not apply here. There are some things you just can't control, no matter how careful you are.

While playing chess this afternoon, I made a terrible move. Old Zhang didn't say anything; he just gave me a look. I knew he could tell—my head wasn't in the game. I was thinking about Xiuying, wondering what happens if her blood pressure keeps fluctuating like this. The doctor said prevention is key, so I’ve been constantly thinking about how to prevent it. Should I check her pressure every day? Should I be even stricter with her diet? But if I do that, will she feel like I’m over-worrying?

I’m 63 this year. I’ll be fully retired in two years. I used to spend all my time thinking about how to arrange my life after retirement, thinking I might do some consulting work to supplement my pension. But now I suddenly realize that maybe that isn't the most important thing. Maybe what I should be thinking about is how to take good care of Xiuying. How to be by her side and give her peace of mind.

I’m not the type of person who likes to admit powerlessness. But sitting here, looking at the notebook filled with blood pressure logs, medication schedules, and dietary restrictions, I feel a bit tired. I want to control everything, but I’m finding that I can't.

Xiuying is in the living room watching TV right now. I can hear her voice. She’s fine, right there in the next room. But I’m still a little afraid.

Maybe I should talk to her tomorrow. Not about the blood pressure, but about... our days ahead.

# Monday, October 21, 2024 Sunny

I noticed I was walking a bit faster than usual when I stepped out of the new director’s office this morning.

I got a call yesterday saying there were still issues with the parameters of the new system, and they wanted me to come take a look. I was a bit hesitant at first; after all, I’m not the director anymore, and these are things they should be handling themselves. But Xiuying said, "The fact that they’re reaching out means you’re still useful." Once she said that, I decided to go.

I spent two hours in the office this morning. Looking at those data sets, those workflows—the things I used to handle every single day—it all felt quite familiar. But there was a difference: this time, instead of saying "change it to this," I asked them, "Have you considered trying this approach?" Their eyes lit up, and they started discussing it. I just stood by, offering a suggestion here and there.

It felt different. Before, as the director, my word was the standard; my subordinates just had to execute. Now... how should I put it? It felt like I was discussing things with them rather than giving orders. I even worried a little about whether my suggestions were sound, but they were listening and thinking very seriously.

When I walked out of the office, I found my step was a bit lighter. Not the lightness of pride, but... a feeling of being needed. But this "being needed" is different from before. Back then, I was needed because I was the director—I had to be. Now, it’s because I have experience. I can choose to help, or I can choose not to. This freedom of choice feels very comfortable.

When I got home this afternoon, Xiuying asked me how it went. I said, "Not bad." She smiled and said, "I knew you’d be useful."

I think I’m starting to understand now. Retirement isn’t the end; it’s just a change of pace. I don’t have to sit in that office every day, but I can go back once in a while. I don’t have to be responsible for every decision, but I can share what I know. It feels more like freedom this way.

I’ll check back tomorrow to see if their testing yielded any results.

**Li Jianguo**
The factory is going through an "intelligent transformation." I see the young people using those systems; they do inspections much faster than I do. And the quality is fine. I just thought...

He stops again, staring at the screen.

I did. Don't overthink things. Come and eat.

Li Jianguo puts down his phone but doesn't go inside immediately. He watches the roses for a while longer, his fingernail gently brushing a dewdrop off a leaf.

At 6:20 AM, he finally walks into the kitchen.

# February 18, 2023 Work Log

**Date**: February 18, 2023  
**Weather**: Overcast, 8°C  
**Working Hours**: 8:30 AM – 5:30 PM

---

## Morning Session

8:30–11:00 AM: Conducted routine production line inspections. The yield rate for precision parts on Line 3 in Workshop A reached 98.5%, meeting standards. Discussed acceptance criteria for the new batch of raw materials with Foreman Li; emphasized strict control over dimensional tolerances—there is absolutely no room for slack in this stage.

11:15 AM – 12:00 PM: Reviewed the weekly inspection report summary. A total of 2,847 products were inspected with a 99.2% pass rate. The 11 non-conforming units have all been traced back to specific processes. These figures are industry-leading. Thirty-seven years on the job—these numbers speak for themselves.

---

## Afternoon Session

2:30 PM: Discussed the upcoming automated inspection system with Inspector Xiao Wang.

Xiao Wang raised several points:
- Whether the system can seamlessly integrate with the existing database.
- Whether advance employee training is required.
- How legacy inspection records will be archived.

My response:

I understand that new systems are the way forward. However, what I want to emphasize is that the reason our current inspection process maintains such a high pass rate isn't just about the tools—it’s about **experience and judgment**. A machine can read data, but it cannot read subtle changes on a product’s surface, the "feel" of the material, or abnormal signals on the production floor. These are things I’ve accumulated over thirty-seven years, and they are what everyone in this department is learning.

Automation can handle standardized inspection items, which is fine. But for complex situations requiring human judgment, we must still rely on experience. Therefore, my recommendation is: the new system should be a **supplement**, not a **replacement**.

Regarding data integration, I suggested:
1. Thorough compatibility testing must be conducted before the system goes live.
2. Legacy data must be preserved in full; I have already organized the inspection archives from over the years into hard-copy backups, stored in the office cabinet.
3. Employee training must be scheduled in advance; we cannot rush into this.

Xiao Wang listened attentively. He is a promising young man; though he’s only been with the department for two years, he’s a quick learner. I told him that no matter how the system changes, the core principle of quality inspection remains the same—be responsible for every single product, and be responsible for the company’s reputation.

Wednesday, January 18, 2023, 10:45 PM

Can’t sleep again.

This is the third night this week. Yesterday, I woke up at 2:30 in the morning, my head spinning with the words "digital transformation" and "organizational optimization" that the leadership kept throwing around during the meeting. They said it wasn't about layoffs, but I’ve been at this factory for thirty-seven years; I know how to read between the lines. What they really meant was—the automated systems are coming online, and the Quality Department is going to be "streamlined."

I lay in bed while Wang Xiuying slept soundly. I didn't want to wake her. I just lay there in the dark, thinking and thinking, my mind refusing to stop.

Machines can do the inspections. But what about me? What use is a director like me anymore?

Once that thought took hold, it was like it grew roots. I tried to distract myself—counting sheep, thinking about when to fertilize the roses on the balcony—but nothing worked. Finally, I got up, turned on the desk lamp, and pulled out my notebook.

I made a list in the book:
1. If the department is cut, what else can I do?
2. Retirement consulting—how much could I earn?
3. A small business? Helping Li Ming run his building materials shop?
4. Is the pension enough? Combined with Xiuying’s, how much would we have a month?

As I was writing, I noticed my handwriting was a bit messy. That’s not like me. My notebooks have always been neat and orderly.

Later, I spent some time on my phone looking up "starting a business after retirement" and "quality consulting." The screen made my eyes ache. I’m still not great with these gadgets. My daughter tried to teach me before, but I can never remember. I still trust pen and paper more.

But pen and paper can’t change reality.

I thought about that group photo on the office wall from ten years ago. There were so many people in the department back then, everyone full of energy. And now? The system is coming, people are being "streamlined." Is experience worth anything? A machine doesn't need experience; it just needs a program.

I know what I’m really thinking. I’m wondering—am I truly old? Have I really fallen behind?

When I got up at 6:30 this morning, I looked in the mirror. Dark circles under my eyes. Xiuying asked if I was feeling unwell. I said no, just that work has been busy lately. She didn't ask again.

It’s almost eleven now. I’m still not tired.

Tomorrow I have to take Xiuying to the hospital for her hypertension follow-up. The doctor said she needs regular check-ups. I’ve noted all the doctor’s orders on the calendar. At least that is something I can control. At least I can still take care of her.

But then a thought hit me—what if my pension gets cut? What if I’m forced into early retirement? Will I still be able to take care of her?

Forget it. Stop thinking. If I keep going, the sun will be up.

I’ll put the notebook away. I'll deal with it tomorrow.

Or the day after.

This was supposed to be a time for a slow handover, a buffer for me, but instead, I’m being asked to learn this completely foreign system. What if I can’t learn it? What if the company thinks I, as a supervisor, can’t keep up anymore? It’s been thirty-seven years. I worked my way up from an operator to where I am now. What did I rely on? My understanding of quality, my meticulousness, and my experience. But this system seems to be saying—none of that matters anymore; the machines are enough.

I wonder, is this just a prelude to layoffs?

When I got up at 6:30 this morning, it was still dark. I sat on the balcony looking at the roses. A few of the pots haven't been growing well lately. I pulled out my logbook; the watering dates and fertilization times were all clearly recorded. I can remember these little things so precisely, so why can’t I learn that system?

It’s not that I’m against it. I know times are changing, and I’m not the type to stubbornly cling to old ways. But why does it feel like this change is coming so fast—so fast that I haven't even had time to react?

I have to take Xiuying to the hospital for her follow-up this afternoon. Her blood pressure has been a bit high again lately, and the doctor said we need to be careful. I need to note the new medical instructions on the calendar. Life at home goes on; the kids are busy with their own lives, my grandson is in Shanghai, my son’s business is struggling... all these things are still there, and now I have to squeeze out the energy to learn something I don't really understand.

I’m tired.

But I also know that tomorrow I still have to go to the office, still have to sit in that chair, and still have to act like everything is under control.

But it’s just after 6:00 AM, and the sun isn't up yet. Let me just sit here a little longer.

Good morning, Xiuying. I just finished watering the flowers; the roses are coming along nicely.

But... there’s something I need to tell you. Today is my last day at the office. I didn't say much to you at the farewell party yesterday, and it’s only just starting to sink in now.

I still woke up at 6:30 this morning—force of habit, I guess. When I got up, I blanked for a second and started wondering what reports I needed to check today. Then it hit me—there aren't any.

I saw that group photo our colleagues gave me, the one from ten years ago, our department’s "family portrait." It’s been on the office wall for so long. When I packed it into the cardboard box today, I stood at the doorway for a long time. I didn't let anyone see; I just turned around and left.

I’m feeling a bit... how should I put it? A bit out of sorts. It’s like I suddenly don’t know what I’m supposed to do with myself. My days used to be packed, but now, after watering the flowers and reading the news, there’s nothing left.

Did you have breakfast today? Did you eat on time?

I was thinking, maybe tonight we could look at where to hang these things on the walls at home. Or you could help me decide. Making the call on my own feels a bit...

Anyway, it’s nothing, I just wanted to let you know. Are you there?

May 16, 2023, Night

I sat there for a long time after closing the office door.

The morning meeting is still spinning in my head. The Director said we must fully switch to the new system within three months. I took a red pen and marked the date on the calendar—August 16th. I pressed the tip so hard the paper almost tore. Looking at that red mark felt like staring at a countdown.

When he asked if I had any objections, I paused for a long time before saying, "Understood." I heard my own voice; it sounded calm, but my heart was actually pounding. My subordinates were all looking at me—I could feel it. In the past, they listened to whatever I said. Now, I don't even know what to say myself.

When I got back to the office, I didn’t chat with Old Li like I usually do. I just shut the door. It’s not like me, but I just didn’t want to talk.

I pulled out a department photo from ten years ago. We were all so young then; my team looked so happy. I remember that year our pass rate hit a record high, and customer complaints were at an all-time low. That photo is still pinned to the wall. Looking at it, I couldn't help but wonder—does any of that still count for anything?

Three months. I keep calculating that number. Three months to learn a system that is completely foreign to me. I’m 63; I retire in two years. Why do I have to learn this now?

Xiuying asked me what I wanted for dinner. I said, "Whatever." She gave me a look but didn't ask further.

I keep thinking: what if I can't learn it? What if, three months from now, I still can't figure it out? How will they look at me? Will they think I’m truly old and can't keep up? I’ve worked for 37 years, rising from an operator to a director. No one understands quality management better than I do. But now, it feels like none of that matters. As long as there are machines, experience seems useless.

I started a list in my notebook—functions to learn, processes to master, logic to understand. I stopped halfway through. Just looking at the list made me feel exhausted.

The roses on the balcony haven't been watered yet. I forgot. That’s not like me; I never forget.

Tomorrow goes on. The technicians said they’ll be training us on Thursday. I need to get my notebook ready.

What else can I do?

Playing chess at the community center today, a young man came up and asked me how to avoid getting checkmated. I walked him through a few basic strategies, and to my surprise, he was listening so intently that he actually managed to win a game later on. As he said goodbye and thanked me, I realized I’d been talking for quite a while, haha.

I’ve been here every Wednesday for the past eight years. My chess buddies say my game hasn't slipped at all—if anything, I’ve become more steady. Sometimes it feels good to know that these skills are still needed.

🎯

# Quality Inspection Consulting - Technical Memo

**Date**: September 15, 2024  
**Subject**: Defect Identification Issues in the New Automated System  
**Status**: Preliminary Diagnosis Phase

---

## I. Core Issue

The new system is failing to identify **surface cracks at specific angles** (micro-level), leading to the outflow of some non-conforming products. This issue occurred twice during my tenure; at that time, it was resolved through manual inspection combined with parameter adjustments.

---

## II. Preliminary Diagnostic Approach

1. **System Parameter Issues**
   - Do the optical sensor angle settings cover the typical presentation angles of such defects?
   - The threshold settings might be too high, causing micro-cracks to be filtered out.

2. **Incomplete Sample Library**
   - Does the new system’s training set include sufficient data for these specific defects?
   - Need to confirm whether our department’s historical defect archives were used during system training.

3. **Inspection Workflow Gaps**
   - Is there a disconnect between the automated system and the manual re-inspection phase?
   - Should these types of defects be supplemented by manual spot checks at a specific stage of the process?

---

## III. Information Required for Further Investigation

- [ ] Which specific production line and product model are affected?
- [ ] At what point were the non-conforming products discovered (how long after production)?
- [ ] How long has the new system been operational, and when did this issue first emerge?
- [ ] What is the current handling plan (total rework or partial processing)?
- [ ] What adjustments has the new Director already attempted?

---

## IV. Potential Solution Directions

**Short-term (Immediately Actionable)**
- Reinstate manual spot checks, specifically 100% re-inspection for this product category.
- Adjust the optical angle range and sensitivity thresholds in the system parameters.
- Retrieve our department’s historical defect archives to supplement the system’s sample library.

**Medium-term (Requires System Optimization)**
- Coordinate with the system vendor for specialized algorithm optimization targeting these defects.
- Establish a hybrid inspection mode combining the new system with traditional methods.
- Develop a specialized Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for this type of defect.

**Long-term**
- Refine the system’s continuous learning mechanism and regularly update the sample library.

---

## V. Work Plan

**This Week**
- Chat with old colleagues during Wednesday’s chess game to see if they recall the specifics of how we handled this.
- Organize relevant inspection records and defect classification archives from my tenure.
- List the specific parameters that need to be reviewed on-site.

**Next Week**
- Schedule a factory visit to observe the new system in operation firsthand.
- Conduct a detailed technical exchange with the new Director.
- Define the specific scope and timeline for the consulting engagement.

---

## VI. Personal Reminders

- This work must not disrupt my current pace of life; primarily utilize the time before Wednesday chess or during weekends.
- Confirm the consulting fees; this income can supplement the pension and support my wife’s regular check-ups and plans with our grandson.
- Don’t over-invest—this is consulting, not going back to a full-time job.
- Xiuying is right: skills don't vanish just because you retire. Doing this well is a way to honor my professional career.

---

*Note: Discuss this plan with Xiuying during breakfast tomorrow morning. She will be supportive.*

# Steps for Making a WeChat Video Call

**Date: September 10, 2024 – Evening**

Recording the steps for today’s video call with Jianhao so I don’t have to keep asking Xiaoling next time. Today’s call was a success—25 minutes! Much longer than expected.

## Operating Procedure

**Step 1: Open WeChat**
- Tap the WeChat icon on the phone’s home screen (the green one).
- Log in (using the account and password my daughter taught me).

**Step 2: Find the chat window with Li Xiaoling**
- Find "Li Xiaoling" in the "Contacts" list.
- Tap her name to open the chat history.

**Step 3: Tap the Video Call button**
- At the top of the chat window, there is a button that looks like a "telephone."
- Press and hold it, and two options will pop up: "Voice Call" and "Video Call."
- Select "Video Call."
- Wait for them to answer (usually it’s very quick).

**Step 4: Adjust the screen angle**
- Place the phone on the coffee table, propped up against a cup or a small object for support.
- Adjust the angle so the kids can see my face and the things behind me.
- Avoid backlighting; make sure to face the window.

**Step 5: Prepare the old photos**
- Find the old photos in the album before the call and keep them within reach.
- This way, I can tell Jianhao stories, which is much more interesting than just chatting.
- Today I talked about our family photo from the year he was born; he listened very intently.

## Key Reminders

- Don’t rush to hang up; sometimes the kids suddenly remember something they want to say.
- If the picture freezes, don’t panic—just wait a few seconds.
- Speak clearly and don’t stay too far from the phone.
- It’s best to set a fixed time every week so Jianhao remembers.
- **Next time, remember to ask Xiaoling if we can call at the same time every week to keep a routine.**

---

*This method is just like the inspections I used to do at the factory—Step 1, Step 2, one step at a time, and you won't make mistakes. I’ll try it again next week, and I should be able to memorize it by then.*

July 24, 2023, 8:00 PM

Sitting on the balcony, I looked at the potted rose I’ve ignored for over a week. The leaves are starting to wither. It was only then that I realized what I’ve actually been doing these past few days—nothing at all.

Ever since those rumors started at the office, it’s felt like something has been weighing me down. The restructuring, the new system, my son rejecting my advice… these things hit me one after another, and I just completely lost my spark. When Xiuying asked if I wanted to eat, I just said, "Whatever." When she said she had a headache, I just mechanically dug out her prescription without truly considering how she was feeling. I didn’t even go to play chess; I told my friends I wasn't feeling well. All lies.

I just sat there, not wanting to do a single thing.

But for some reason today, I suddenly couldn't stand it anymore. Looking at this plant, I remembered how I used to keep records—the watering dates, the amount of fertilizer, when to prune. Those notebooks are now gathering dust in a drawer. It’s as if I had given up on myself, just as I had given up on these flowers.

I watered the plant. I did it carefully, just like I used to. Then I pulled out my notebook and recorded today’s date and the condition of the flower. As my hand moved to write, I finally felt like I was alive again.

Xiuying was in the room watching TV. I walked in and suddenly said to her, "I should go to the IT department and learn that new system. I can't keep running away like this."

She looked up at me, a hint of surprise in her eyes. I was surprised myself.

"I should go and understand it instead of just hiding," I said again. "Hiding doesn't change anything."

Xiuying didn't say a word, but she rested her hand on my shoulder.

I know there are still a lot of things I haven't figured out. Who knows what will happen at the company. But at least… at least I can’t just give up like this. Not for some grand principle, but simply because… I don’t want to live this way.

The flowers are still here, the notebook is still here, and Xiuying is still here.

I can still do something.

Xiuying, are you asleep yet? I’ve been sitting downstairs for a while.

I didn't sleep well last night, and my head feels a bit cluttered. It’s not that I’m feeling unwell physically, it’s just... I’m a little tired. Work has been piling up lately, and I might be overthinking things.

Don't worry, I just need some quiet time. I’ll be up as usual tomorrow morning, and everything will go on as planned.

By the way, are we supposed to go check on the shop with Li Ming this weekend? I’d like to hear what’s on his mind lately. Also, do you have enough blood pressure medication left? I’ll go with you to the hospital for your follow-up next week; I’ll make a note of the time.

Sometimes I wonder... if everything I’ve done all these years still matters. But that’s just me overthinking again. Go get some rest, don't let me keep you up. I’ll sit here for a bit longer and then head up.

# MEMO

**Date: September 29, 2022, 8:15 PM**

---

Two years left until retirement.

Today, my daughter suggested again that I use my phone to track my flower data, so I tried out the memo feature. Taking photos, typing, saving… I followed every step she showed me. She says it’s more convenient than a notebook and that the data won’t get lost. I told her, "Alright, I’ll give it a try."

But while I was on the bus, a thought suddenly hit me—

**If I can’t even master a phone, what will I be able to do after I retire?**

I’ve used notebooks for thirty-seven years and never had a single problem. Flower records, doctor’s orders, work notes—everything is laid out clearly. I’m in control of it. But this phone… I’m still not so sure. What if the data just vanishes one day? At my age, I’m slow to learn new things, and my memory isn’t what it used to be.

My daughter says I need to keep up with the times. But why does it feel like the world is sprinting ahead while I’m just standing still?

I’m still confident in my quality inspection work; I can do a good job for these last two years. But what about after that? No more job, no more department, no more of the processes and standards I know so well. Just gardening, playing chess, and spending time with family. Will that be enough?

Then there’s my eldest grandson in Shanghai; I spend so little time with him. The business at the building materials store has been unstable lately, and there’s nothing I can do to help my younger son. Wang Xiuying’s high blood pressure is under control for now, but I’m always worried…

My phone’s memo app now has the watering schedule for the Chinese roses. I need to make sure—is this data really safe?

I’ll ask my daughter again tomorrow, or… maybe just go over the steps she taught me one more time.

---

**To-Do:**
- Verify phone data security
- Adjust autumn watering cycle for the roses
- Talk to my chess buddies about this on Wednesday
- Call my daughter next week to check on my grandson

Xiuying, I’m in.

I sat for a while and did some thinking. Xiaoling got annoyed with all my questions; I could tell from her tone. *Sigh.*

I tried following her instructions and took a few photos of the flowers. But I’m still not quite at ease—I always feel like things stored in this phone aren't reliable. I’ve used my notebook for so many years; I just flip it open and everything is right there, clear as day. But this phone? If everything just vanished one day, I wouldn't even know what happened.

Do you think I’m really losing it? To have my own daughter teach me how to use a phone over and over again... at my age...

That pot of pink roses still hasn't recovered. It’s starting to frustrate me just looking at it. Maybe my old methods were wrong, but now I don't even trust my own judgment anymore.

How about we just stick to the old way? I’ll give the phone a try, but I’m still going to keep notes in my notebook. I’ll feel more secure that way.

Have you eaten yet?

# June 14, 2024, 8:00 PM

Spent the whole afternoon flipping through photo albums.

It started with that one from 1998 when we bought the house. Xiuying and I were standing at the entrance of that old residential compound; we looked impossibly young. Back then, the pressure of the mortgage was suffocating, but looking at it now, the smiles in that photo were real. Then came the ones from 2000—the kids were still small. Xiaoling was in her red dress, and Mingming was sitting on my lap. Flipping further, there were various department group photos. In the one from ten years ago, my hair wasn't nearly this grey.

I realized I’m looking at these photos in a strange way. Not flipping through them quickly like I used to, but lingering on each one, trying to find something. What am I looking for? I can’t quite say. Maybe I’m looking for the "me" from back then—the one who still had things to do, who woke up at 6:30 every morning with a clear purpose.

My flower logbook was sitting next to me. I added another page today. I didn't just record the watering dates and amounts; I also noted the number of leaves on the roses, the size of the buds, and that it was a cloudy day. When Xiuying came in and saw me writing all this, she didn't say anything; she just sat beside me for a while. I knew what she wanted to say. She says, "You still have me, and you still have this home."

I patted her hand. I didn't say much.

Actually, I can feel what I’m doing—using these details to fill a void. Every number, every entry, feels like an attempt to prove I’m still in control of something. At work, I controlled inspection standards, quality, and every link in the department. And now? Now, the only things I can control are when these roses get watered and when their leaves will grow.

Rationally, I know this is normal. People need to adapt; it takes time. But emotionally... emotionally, it feels like something has been suddenly pulled away. I’m still going through the motions, only to find my hands are empty.

Tomorrow is Wednesday; I’m going to play Chinese chess again. I can do that. A chess game has rules, an opponent, and a win or loss. For those two hours, at least, I know what I’m doing.

That’s all for now. I’ll think more tomorrow.

June 28, 2024, Evening

Had a video call with Xiaoling.

I originally just wanted to ask how Jianhao was doing, but she suddenly burst into tears. She said working in Shanghai is exhausting, and sometimes she wonders if she’s missing out on something important. I didn't say anything; I just listened. She said, "Dad, sometimes when I look at your Moments, seeing you watering flowers on the balcony, having dinner with Mom, playing chess with your friends... it feels like you have a clearer sense of how to live than any of us."

I said, "Xiaoling, what I have now is very simple—your mother, you and Jianhao, your brother’s family, and my Chinese roses. That’s enough. It’s not that work isn't important, it’s just that... I finally don't have to rely solely on work to prove who I am anymore."

Xiuying is in the kitchen; I can hear her humming a tune.

Life is just like that. Bit by bit, growing slowly.

# Quality Management Notes: November 15, 2024

**Question from the New Director:**
System inspection data shows a 98.5% pass rate, yet customer complaints are actually rising. How do we make sense of this contradiction?

**My Reflections:**
It’s a good question. It shows he’s starting to think about the relationship between "data" and "reality."

The core of quality management isn't about chasing pretty numbers; it’s about meeting the customer’s actual needs. Data is merely a reference point. In all my years in this department, the most important thing I’ve learned is that **the best decisions come from combining human experience with system data.**

Specifically:
- Systems can detect "hard" metrics like dimensions and appearance.
- But how a product is used in a customer’s hands, how long it lasts, or whether latent issues will emerge—that requires experience.
- Some problems don't show up in the data, but an old-timer can spot them just by touch.

**My Advice to Him:**
Don’t just look at the pass rate. Visit customers regularly and listen to their honest feedback. Build a "customer feedback to product improvement" reverse loop. That way, system data and real-world experience can finally align.

He listened intently. It reminded me of my time as a shift leader, when my mentor taught me the same thing—never trust things only at face value.

**Why I’m Recording This:**
If similar inquiries come up later, this line of thinking can be reused. Perhaps this is what I can continue to do—not necessarily returning to the office, but sharing this experience whenever it’s needed.

Feels pretty good.

# Diary

Monday, January 16, 2023  Sunny

Another restless night. At 2:00 AM, I was still playing back what Xiao Wang said.

In the office this morning, he asked me when the new system would completely replace manual inspection. I spoke a bit too fast, perhaps sounding a little defensive—I told him that machines can’t replicate human experience, and that certain subtle defects can only be spotted by a seasoned inspector. He nodded as he listened, but I could see the doubt in his eyes, as if he were thinking, "Chief, you’ve said this a dozen times already."

I know I’m defending myself. But why?

This afternoon, I dug out a department photo from ten years ago. Everyone looked so young then, smiling so brightly. I’ve been in quality inspection for twenty-five years now, rising from team leader to section head, and finally to department chief. Thirty-seven years in total. I spent thirty-seven years building this department into the gold standard of the industry; our pass rates and customer satisfaction are second to none. That’s not a boast; it’s a fact.

But what about now? Management talks about introducing automated systems, calling it "technological upgrading." I can hear the subtext. Headcounts will be adjusted. What use will a chief like me be then?

I drew up a table in my notebook: Traditional Inspection vs. Automated Systems.

Traditional Inspection:
- Can judge subtle product variations based on experience
- Can adapt to unexpected situations on the fly
- Can respond quickly to special customer requirements
- Thirty-seven years of accumulated expertise

Automated Systems:
- Fast
- Never gets tired
- Low cost
- ...

I stopped writing there. Because I know that no matter how many reasons I list, the wheels of time will keep turning. I can’t stand in their way.

Xiuying asked me what was wrong today, saying I was staring into space during dinner. I told her it was nothing, just busy at work. She didn't push. We’ve been married for forty-one years; she can sense my moods, but she also knows I like to figure things out on my own first.

I have to take her back to the hospital for a follow-up tomorrow. She needs to keep taking her blood pressure medication. I need to write the doctor’s orders on the calendar. Life goes on.

But I’m still wondering—what if I really do retire early? Will the pension be enough? Li Ming’s business has been under a lot of pressure lately; my younger daughter is in Shanghai, and my grandson is ten now—I’ve spent so little time with them. These are all things I need to consider.

I’m not a man who panics easily. I lived through the wave of layoffs years ago and came out the other side. But this time feels different. This time, it’s my professional worth itself that’s being questioned.

Forget it, enough thinking. Back to work tomorrow. The job still needs to be done right. As long as I’m still in this position, I can still prove my value.

June 28, 2023, 7:00 PM

I smiled today while I was on the phone with Xiao Ming.

I need to write this down because, over the past few months, it feels like I haven't smiled much at all.

Xiao Ming told me that sales at the building materials store dropped again this month. The competition is fierce, and he’s starting to panic. Listening to his anxiety, I suddenly thought back to my thirty-plus years in quality management. I told him, "Sticking to your own standards is more important than blindly following trends. Look at me all these years—I’d rather be overly strict with inspections than relax requirements just to speed up shipments. In the long run, your reputation is worth more than anything."

After hearing that, Xiao Ming’s tone seemed to relax a bit. He said, "Dad, you’re right. I’ve just been letting the competition rattle me lately."

When I hung up, I suddenly realized something—what I said was true. My thirty-seven years of experience haven't gone to waste.

For the past two months, I’ve been struggling to learn that new automation system. It’s been exhausting, and I often feel like I can’t keep up with the times. Sometimes I wonder if this old brain of mine is just useless now. But after talking to Xiao Ming, I realized... the principles of quality management don't become obsolete just because there’s a new system. The system is just a tool. How to use that tool, how to set standards, and how to view quality—those things still depend on experience and judgment.

I smiled. It was that kind of feeling where something inside you suddenly loosens up.

Xiuying heard me smile and asked if Xiao Ming had some good news. I said no, just... I just remembered a few things.

I’m sitting on the balcony now. The Chinese roses have sprouted new buds. I watered them and noted the date in my notebook. Tomorrow, I’ll keep at it. Some things are just like that—you have to take them bit by bit; you can’t rush it.

Maybe my learning this system is the same way.

2023.6.28

Sooyoung, I just took some photos of the roses on the balcony. I used the phone and even logged the watering time in that notes app.

Here, take a look— (hands over the phone) I’m still not quite used to it and pressed a few wrong buttons, but at least I didn't break anything. This way, I won't have to carry my notebook around every time.

Actually, the notes feature on this phone is pretty good. It’s more convenient than I thought. The photos are clear, the timestamp is automatic, and I just have to type a few words.

Is breakfast ready? I’m going to wash my hands first.

# October 26, 2022, Evening

Another terrible game of chess.

When I was playing with Old Wang and the others today, I couldn’t even manage a single win. My mind was completely preoccupied with that phone. Old Wang asked me what was wrong, so I told him about how I’m using both the phone and my notebook now just to feel secure. He just smiled and said, "Then keep using them both. After all, we’ve used pen and paper our whole lives."

Hearing him say that made my chest feel even tighter.

He’s right, but for some reason, those words really stung. It’s as if people of our generation are just destined for this—to be phased out by the times. I tried using that photo feature on the phone again, but I still can’t figure out how to label the dates. My daughter explained it to me once, but I can’t remember. She didn’t say anything, but I could tell she was a little disappointed.

What I fear most is losing data. I checked everything over and over, terrified that I might accidentally delete something. It’s a horrible feeling. In all the years I’ve used a notebook, I’ve never had this kind of anxiety.

When I got home, I glanced at the pot of pink roses on the balcony. The leaves have turned a bit yellower. I really haven't looked after it much lately; I’ve been too busy obsessing over that phone. I can’t even do the one thing I’m good at anymore. I wonder, is this a sign?

My brain just can’t seem to learn new things.

Or perhaps, I’m just old, and I should just accept that reality. I’m retiring in two years—what will I even be able to do then? If I can’t even master a phone, how can I say I’m still useful? I still have my footing at work, but even that will be gone soon. I’m starting to think that maybe my only value lies in my job. Once I leave that...

I don't want to think about it anymore.

I’ll water the roses tomorrow. Maybe they can still be saved. I’ll keep using the notebook, and as for the phone... I’ll take it slow. Old Wang is right anyway; that’s just how it is at our age.

— Li Jianguo

# Diary

**December 7, 2024 | Sunny | 20:30**

Xiuying asked me again today, saying that I’m doing all this to prepare for her old age. I corrected her right then and there—it’s not for your old age, it’s for *our* old age.

She smiled. It was a smile I know well—the kind that comes from feeling truly understood.

I accompanied her to the hospital for a follow-up this afternoon. Her blood pressure is still a bit high; the doctor said she needs to keep taking her medication and watch her diet. On the way back, it suddenly occurred to me that I’ve been tracking these numbers for years, but they’ve always been scattered—scribbled on calendars or sticky notes. Today, I thought, why not organize them properly?

I got to work right after dinner. I took out a notebook and compiled the blood pressure readings, doctor’s orders, and medication history from the past three months into a table. Xiuying watched from the side and asked what I was doing. I told her that starting next year, this is how we’ll keep track. Every month after her check-up, we’ll update it. That way, if any issues arise later, the doctor can see everything clearly at a glance.

That’s when she said, "You’re preparing for my old age."

And I said those words—it’s not for your old age, it’s for our old age.

I actually stunned myself a little when I said it. But that is how I truly feel. We have walked together for forty-one years, and we will keep walking. Her health is a part of my life. It’s not that I am "taking care" of her; it’s that we are experiencing this together.

As I write this, she is already asleep. I’m sitting in the study, looking at this table. Every month next year, I will record new data here. It’s nothing extraordinary, but to me, it’s like tending to my flowers—it requires records, a plan, and constant attention.

I suppose this is what life is. It doesn't need grand promises; it just needs a notebook, a pen, and the persistence to show up every month.

I have to go to the company for a consultation tomorrow. But these things don't conflict. I can be a Quality Director, and I can be a husband. I can help the new director solve problems, and I can build a health archive for Xiuying. This is all my life.

Thinking this way makes me feel very much at peace.

August 16, 2023  Sunny

It was another ordinary day, yet somehow it felt a little different.

I spent the morning in the office. The interface of the new system is as complicated as ever, but I realized I wasn't as frustrated as I was two weeks ago. I ran through the inspection process several times; even though I still hit the wrong buttons occasionally, I’m starting to anticipate where the issues might pop up. It’s a strange feeling—like I’ve finally picked up a new rhythm.

In the evening, I took Xiuying to the hospital for her follow-up. Registration, queuing, blood pressure checks—I’ve done these things a thousand times. But today, while sitting in the waiting room, it suddenly occurred to me that I could use the "Notes" app on my phone to record her readings. I used to use scraps of paper, which were easy to lose. I tried opening the app and even set a reminder for the 15th of every month to prompt us for the follow-up.

Watching me tap away at my phone, Xiuying smiled and said, "You’re finally learning how to use these new gadgets."

"I still press the wrong things sometimes," I replied. But deep down, I knew this time was different. It wasn't about being forced to learn; it was about actually seeing the use in it. When I showed her the reminder, there was a look of relief in her eyes. I felt it, too.

On the way home, I thought to myself: maybe this is what change looks like. It’s not about mastering everything all at once, but gradually realizing that these "new things" aren't some kind of monster. It’s the same at the factory—no matter how complex the new system is, it’s just a different way of doing what I’ve been doing for over thirty years.

The key is, I can still do it.

I still have value.

That thought brought me a sense of peace I can’t quite describe. It’s not pride, exactly... just a feeling of being grounded.

Tomorrow, we keep going.

July 19, 2023, Night

Xiuying asked me what was wrong. I said nothing, just a bit tired. She’d been busy in the kitchen all day, yet she was still looking out for me; all I could give her in return was this half-hearted brush-off. During dinner, she asked again. I just gave a muffled "mm-hmm" and kept my head down, shoveling rice into my mouth. I put the bowl down after barely finishing a third of it. I could feel her worried gaze on me, but I pretended not to notice.

I don’t know when it started, but I just don’t feel like talking much anymore. Or rather, I don’t know what to say.

Li Ming called again this afternoon, asking for my take on his supply chain transformation project. I stammered out a few sentences, but even I could hear how hollow my words sounded. He probably sensed it too, because he ended the call pretty quickly. I sat in my office, staring at that department photo from ten years ago on my desk. I remembered how certain I used to be about every decision regarding inspection standards back then. And now? Now I can’t even give my own son a decent piece of advice.

What he needs is someone who understands the internet and new business models, not an old man who only knows how to follow the book.

As for Li Jianhao, he’s ten years old now, yet we still see each other so rarely. The last time we spoke on the phone, he asked me how to watch cartoons on his phone, and I fumbled through it without being able to teach him. What can I offer him? A grandfather who doesn’t understand new things and can’t keep up with the times.

Thirty-seven years. From the moment I entered the factory in 1984 until now, I gave my best years to that inspection department. I used to be proud of our product quality, proud of our customer satisfaction. But now, when I flip through those quality reports, I wonder—does any of it still matter? Or am I just using past achievements to comfort my current, powerless self?

Xiuying’s high pressure has been acting up again, and I haven’t even accompanied her to the hospital for a follow-up this month. I’m supposed to be the one taking care of her, the one giving her a sense of security, but right now I can’t even take care of myself. I look so drained in front of her; she must be worried. But I don’t know how to comfort her, because I can’t find any reason to feel comforted myself.

Maybe I really am just old. Not in years, but in spirit—outdated and left behind.

I have to go to work again tomorrow. I have to look at those inspection reports, hold meetings with subordinates, and pretend that everything is fine. I retire in two years. And then what? Grow flowers? Play chess? Or just keep sitting here, thinking about how useless I’ve become?

Forget it, stop thinking. Time to wash up and go to bed.

November 13, 2022, 6:30 AM

I didn’t take Xiuying’s blood pressure on schedule this morning.

This is a first. For over thirty years, I have almost never broken a plan I set for myself. But this morning, I stood in the kitchen for a long time with the monitor in my hand, and in the end, I put it down.

Xiuying is still asleep. I’m sitting in the living room, looking over the advice sheet the doctor gave us last time. I’ve read it many times now.

"Measure 3-4 times a week; daily measurements are not necessary," the doctor wrote. How did I manage to interpret that as needing to do it every single day? I asked Xiuying, "The doctor didn't tell us to measure it every day, did he?" She gave me a strange look and nodded.

I don’t know what I’m doing.

Lately, I’ve been doing what I thought was "right." Taking her to the hospital every month, transcribing the doctor’s orders onto the calendar, measuring her blood pressure every morning, noon, and night, and recording it all in a notebook. I even started researching which monitors are the most accurate and which time of day yields the most authentic data. I thought this was how I could help her keep it under control.

But Xiuying’s blood pressure still fluctuates.

Last week she said, "When you hover over me like this, it actually makes me more nervous."

I didn't respond at the time. I thought, *That’s impossible. I’m helping you.* But sitting here now, I’m starting to wonder... am I really helping her, or am I... hurting her?

On my office wall, there’s a group photo of the department from ten years ago. Back then, I knew exactly what I was doing—setting standards, executing them, and getting results. That’s how quality management works. Details are everything. I’ve always believed that.

But people aren't products. Xiuying isn't something I can "improve" through monitoring and management.

I wrote the word "Why" over and over in my diary, then crossed them all out.

Maybe... maybe I should trust the doctor. Trust Xiuying herself. Instead of always trying to control everything through my plans. But if I let go, and if something goes wrong, what will I do?

My fingers are trembling. I admit that.

I have to go to the office tomorrow. Two years left until retirement. I’ve been thinking about how to arrange my life after I retire, but now I realize that I might need to learn how to let go first.

I just don’t know how to let go.

Xiuying is up. I can hear her in the bedroom. I should go pour her a glass of water.

But this time, I won’t ask her how her blood pressure is.

Maybe... I’ll start with that.

# Notebook Entry | Tuesday, November 15, 2022

**A Reminder to Myself**

---

The essence of care is concern, not control.

I’ve been turning this sentence over in my mind for days. Perhaps I had it wrong from the very beginning.

**Signs of over-controlling:**
- Checking Xiuying’s blood pressure logs every single day.
- Writing out the doctor’s orders in exhaustive detail, as if recording them one more time offers extra insurance.
- Trying to eliminate all uncertainty through "perfect execution."
- Blaming myself for what I did wrong whenever her blood pressure still fluctuates.

**What real care should look like:**
- Accompanying her to the hospital and listening to what the doctor has to say.
- Reminding her to take her medicine on time, but without monitoring her every move.
- Trusting the doctor’s expertise and trusting Xiuying’s own sense of responsibility.
- Accepting that some things will not go exactly according to plan.

---

**Work and family are different.**

At the factory, I can guarantee quality through strict standards. But people aren't machine parts. Neither is Xiuying.

Ming and Xiaoling both said I’m "too wound up." I didn't want to hear it at the time, but I’m slowly starting to understand. Maybe they’re right.

---

**Mindset shifts to make before retirement:**

1. Trust > Monitoring
2. Companionship > Management
3. Acceptance > Perfection
4. Letting go > Control

This might be harder than learning anything new. But it’s a lesson I must learn.

---

I haven't been sleeping well lately. I’ll try to go to bed early tonight and stop overthinking.

Tomorrow is another day.

The main focus will be adjusting the defect identification mechanism—their new system still has some loopholes when it comes to surface defects on certain precision parts. I have a few ideas that might help them iron things out.

**Aug 25 Mark**: ✓ Showed off the video call in front of my chess buddies today; they all said it was great. Old Man Wang even asked me how I learned to do it. Felt good.

Xiuying, while I was playing chess with the guys today, Old Wang asked me if I regretted retiring. It got me thinking, and I ended up giving them quite an earful (laughs).

Actually, looking back at the past two years of retirement, I’ve gained quite a lot. Being able to accompany you to the hospital for your check-ups every month and seeing your blood pressure get steadier each time—that’s something I never had the time to do properly when I was working. I can also video call Jianhao often to teach him some chess moves and hear about what’s going on at school. And when Mingzi ran into trouble with his business, I was actually able to sit down and help him analyze things.

Then there are the flowers on the balcony. Now that I have the time, I can finally figure out how to grow them properly. When that Chinese rose bloomed last time, I had everything recorded in my notebook—the watering dates, the fertilization schedule, and exactly when the buds first appeared. That feeling of watching them grow slowly is something I never experienced back at the factory.

Things haven't been completely idle with the company, either. The new director still comes to me with questions. Being able to keep helping out gives me a real sense of purpose.

I told them that life is just like that—every stage has its own meaning. Our task now is to live this stage well. This kind of life feels very fulfilling.

What about you? Do you have any special feelings about these past two years? I feel like our life now is so much more relaxed compared to back when we were dealing with the mortgage.

# July 19, 2023, Morning

I lay there for forty-five minutes.

I woke up at 6:30, as usual. But I didn’t get up. I just lay there, staring at that old crack in the ceiling, thinking about the word "integration" from yesterday’s notice.

Xiuying is still asleep. She had another coughing fit last night; I heard it clearly. Between her high blood pressure and this cough, I feel a heavy weight in my chest. I’ve already marked next month’s follow-up appointment on the calendar, but I have a nagging feeling that the results might be different this time.

"Integration."

The word keeps spinning in my head. Integrating the Quality Inspection Department with the Automated Systems Department. I know what that means. The things those young people do with machines and software are ten times faster than I am. Everything I’ve built up over thirty years—those standards, that experience, the principles I thought were non-negotiable—might just become redundant in the face of a system.

I didn’t watch the news. For the first time in all these years, it’s 6:45 and the TV isn't on. Xiuying asked if I was feeling unwell. I said no, just woke up late today. She didn’t push it and lay back down.

I’m wondering, if the integration really happens, I still have two years until retirement. What do I do for those two years? Learn those systems? I tried using the smartphone my daughter gave me; I pressed the wrong buttons so many times. Those codes, those interfaces—to me, they’re like a language from another world.

I have a meeting with the higher-ups tomorrow. They said it’s to "hear my thoughts." I know what that means.

Xiao Li called to say a new competitor moved in near the building materials store this month and asked if I had any advice. I stammered for a while and ended up saying nothing useful. He probably sensed it. How can I help him? I can’t even figure out my own situation.

Xiuying started coughing again. I didn’t dare move, afraid of waking her.

It’s 6:45 now. I should get up. I should make breakfast, watch the news, and live the way I’ve lived for the past thirty years. But I sat on the edge of the bed for a long time before finally standing up slowly.

The man in the mirror looks like a stranger.

July 8, 2024, 8:15 PM, Overcast

Another day where nothing happened.

Li Ming called this afternoon. He said the building materials store just landed a big order, something that might turn things around for the first half of the year. He sounded exhilarated; I could hear the expectation in his voice—he was waiting for me to analyze the deal for him, just like I used to.

But as I listened, all I could manage was "Mm-hmm" and "Is that so?"

When he asked for my opinion, I said, "You decide. You know the business better than I do."

There was a brief silence on the other end after I said that. I could feel his disappointment, even though he didn't say it outright. He went on about a few more details, but my responses remained the same. Finally, he asked, "Dad, are you feeling alright?"

I quickly said, "I'm fine, just a bit tired," and hurried off the phone.

It’s been several hours now, and I’m still thinking about it.

Why did I treat him like that? In the past, when he talked about business, I would listen intently, help him break things down, and tell him what to watch out for. Back then, I felt useful. He needed me. And now? Now, I have nothing to give him. I can’t even find the energy to listen.

It’s not physical exhaustion. It’s a different kind of tired.

Sitting here in this house, looking at the photos on the wall, the roses on the balcony—they’re all still here, but I don’t know what I’m doing here anymore. For thirty-seven years, I knew exactly who I was: I was the head of Quality Control; I was a reliable man; I was needed. And now? Now, I’m just an old man pacing around the house.

Li Ming doesn’t need a weary father. He needs the dad who listens and gives him advice. But I can’t even do that right anymore.

My wife is in the kitchen clearing the dishes. She didn’t ask what Li Ming and I talked about. She’s long since grown used to my silence.

I want to call him back and tell him I didn’t mean it that way. But I don’t know what to say. I’m afraid he’ll realize that I just feel empty inside, that I have nothing left to think with.

I’ll still get up at 6:30 tomorrow morning. Watch the news. Water the flowers. Life goes on like this.

But I really don’t know how much longer I can keep going like this.

Up at 6:30 AM. After catching up on the news, I headed to my usual spot for breakfast.

The owner handed me my soy milk and fried dough sticks as she always does. "Looking sharp today, Lao Li!" she chirped. I just smiled and didn't say much. But she was right—I have been feeling a lot lighter these past couple of days.

Yesterday, the new director called. He said there was a glitch in the quality system and wanted to pick my brain. I was hesitant at first, but after talking it over with Xiuying, she said, "You’ve never been one to sit idle. Instead of overthinking things at home, why not just help out? It’s not like it’ll get in the way of your gardening or chess games." She had a point.

I offered a few suggestions, and that was that. Simple enough. But as I hung up the phone, it hit me—that feeling of being needed is truly something special. It’s not about doing anything grand; it’s just that sense that "your experience still matters." It makes you feel grounded.

Twenty years of the same breakfast, yet today it tasted exceptionally good.

I suppose that’s just how life is. You let go when it’s time to let go, but if you can still contribute in the right way, that’s a pretty good place to be.

#PeacefulDays #TheFeelingOfBeingTrusted
