# Monday, April 3, 2023  Sunny

Sitting in the office, the sunlight outside the window is blinding. I closed my eyes for a moment, but I’ve decided I still need to write down what happened yesterday.

That meeting yesterday—I probably won’t forget it for the rest of my life.

10:00 AM, the new system demonstration. I spent over an hour preparing, filling my notebook with dense notes on the operating procedures. I told myself it wasn't that hard; it was just learning a new piece of software. But when I sat in front of that big screen with everyone’s eyes on me, my hands started to shake.

I messed up the data export step. A flurry of red error messages popped up on the screen, and I couldn't make sense of any of them. I could hear people whispering below. My face flushed instantly—I could feel it burning.

Xiao Wang walked up. "Director, let me help you." Just like that, he took the mouse from my hand in front of everyone. I sat there, unable to say a word, only managing a vague, "The system seems a bit unstable." But I knew. It wasn't the system. It was me.

In the afternoon, I sat alone in my office, staring at that error message over and over. I recorded every single step in my notebook, my pen pressing so hard it nearly tore through the paper. I wondered: Am I really getting old?

Thirty-seven years. I started as a front-line operator and worked my way up to where I am today. No one understands quality management better than I do. The inspection standards I established are strict, but our pass rates and customer satisfaction are the best in the industry. Those aren't things just anyone can do.

But what about now? Now, a young person can do in two keystrokes what used to take me an hour.

It’s not that I’m against the system. I know times are changing, and machines are certainly more accurate than people. But the interface design is so convoluted; it doesn't follow human logic at all. I’m struggling to learn it, and it’s not because I’m not smart enough, it’s because... I can’t quite put my finger on it. Maybe it really is just because I’m old.

Only two years left until retirement. Two years ago, I thought these final years would be about a smooth transition, letting the younger generation gradually take over. But now, I have to master an entirely foreign system in these two years while trying to maintain my authority in front of my subordinates. How is that even possible?

I didn't sleep well last night. Xiuying was fast asleep beside me, her breathing steady. I was thinking about her high blood pressure—she needs another check-up this month. I have to write down the doctor’s orders... Then I thought about Xiao Ming’s business; competition in the building materials industry is so fierce now, how long can he hold on? And then there’s Xiao Ling and Jian Hao in Shanghai—how long has it been since I’ve spent any real time with them?

If I can’t even learn a simple system now, what else can I still do for this family?

I picked up my pen and continued recording those operating steps. Maybe if I try again tomorrow, I’ll do better. Maybe.

But right now, I’m not so sure.

Saturday, September 28, 2024 – Sunny

2:30 PM. Just got back from the factory.

The new director’s call came through while I was on the balcony watering the roses. Hearing his voice was a bit of a surprise at first—they hadn't reached out much over the last two months, so I assumed that was that. But his first words were, "Your input is truly vital to us," and I stopped what I was doing.

He explained the issues with the new system; the defect recognition mechanism in the smart inspection equipment has some loopholes. As he described the symptoms, several possible causes immediately sprang to mind. Professional habit, I suppose—after thirty-odd years, you don't just switch it off.

I didn't agree right away, just told him I’d think about it. After hanging up, I stood on the balcony for a while. My wife, Xiuying, came out and asked if something was wrong. I told her the situation—that the new director wanted me to drop by the factory and offer some advice.

She listened and then said calmly, "Well, give it some thought. You can tell me what you decide over dinner." That one sentence was enough to put my mind at ease.

Sitting here in the study now, I’m reflecting on what this means. It’s not about them having a crisis and needing me to "put out the fire"—I’m long past wanting that feeling. It’s that they need my perspective. Those two concepts feel entirely different.

When I retired two months ago, I felt a bit of a void, though I didn't mention it to anyone. Watering flowers, playing chess, watching the news—the days are orderly, but sometimes it feels like I’ve done it all before and I’m just repeating myself now. My wife noticed, but she didn't say anything; she just started asking me to watch TV with her more often.

But this call made me realize that maybe I don't have to leave that world behind entirely. I’ve accepted retirement; I don't want to be the director anymore—I don't need the stress and responsibility of that position. But if I can help them in a different way, using what I’ve built up over the years, that seems like a valid way to live.

I could go take a look, offer some suggestions. Not as a return to the past, but in a different capacity. "Consultant"—that’s a good word for it. It has boundaries, it offers choice, and it won't disrupt my current rhythm.

And then there’s the consulting fee—I haven't asked how much yet, but if there is one, it might help with my wife’s healthcare, and I could buy my grandson something he wants when he visits. These are practical considerations, too.

I’ll call the new director tomorrow and tell him what I’m thinking.

It’s still early. The rosebuds have grown a bit larger; I should record today’s progress in my notebook.

# June 28, 2024, 7:30 PM

**Video Call with Xiaoling — A New Perspective on Life**

*Tag: Important*

---

During my call with Xiaoling today, something finally clicked for me.

When she asked how retirement was going, I didn't just brush it off with the usual "Oh, it's fine" like I used to. I told her how I spend every day with Jianhao now, teaching him how to play chess and telling him stories from my youth. I told her about the dozen or so pots of Chinese roses I’ve planted on the balcony, and how I keep a detailed log for every single one. I said that these things make me feel fulfilled—it’s a different kind of fulfillment than being in the office, but it’s meaningful in its own way.

Xiaoling was quiet for a long time after listening. Then she said, "Dad, hearing you talk like this makes me so happy."

In that moment, it hit me. For over thirty years, I was always working, always charging forward, terrified that if I ever stopped, I’d lose my value. Now that I’ve let go, I can finally see clearly: I’m not just the Director of Quality Inspection. I’m also a grandfather, a husband, and someone who knows how to grow flowers. These identities are just as important.

When I mentioned this conversation to my wife, she laughed from the kitchen and said, "I knew you’d figure it out sooner or later."

I need to mark this date down. Not because it’s particularly special, but because on this day, I finally stopped looking back with regret.

# Analysis of the New System's Defect Identification Mechanism

**Date:** September 17, 2024, AM  
**Subject:** Consultation on Automation System Issues in the Quality Inspection Department  

---

## I. Issue Overview

Received a call from the new Director regarding loopholes in the new automated inspection system's ability to identify certain special defects. While the system handles routine parameter deviations well, it has a low recognition rate for composite defects—specifically those involving **microscopic surface cracks combined with internal stress imbalance**. This issue is familiar to me; we encountered it before and managed it using a combination of traditional methods and manual spot checks.

---

## II. Potential Solutions

1. **System Parameter Adjustment**
   - Check if defect recognition thresholds are set too high.
   - Confirm if image acquisition resolution is sufficient to capture microscopic features.
   - Verify whether the algorithmic model includes a sample library for these composite defects.

2. **Inspection Process Optimization**
   - Add a targeted manual re-inspection stage following the automated system.
   - Implement stratified inspection for high-risk product batches.
   - Establish a defect sample library to train the system with real-world cases.

3. **Technical Integration Plan**
   - Avoid total reliance on the new system; retain traditional inspection methods for critical stages.
   - Use the new system for high-volume routine inspections while leveraging manual expertise for complex defects.

---

## III. Information Needed for Further Clarification

- [ ] Which specific product types are experiencing this issue?
- [ ] What is the approximate miss rate? Is there statistical data available?
- [ ] What are the current parameter configurations of the new system?
- [ ] What was the pass rate for these products when using traditional inspection methods?
- [ ] Has there been any related quality feedback from customers?

---

## IV. Key Communication Points

- Emphasize that this is not a flaw in the system itself, but rather an issue of adaptation to specific application scenarios.
- The solution will likely require a debugging cycle; it won't be an overnight fix.
- Suggest conducting small-batch trials to verify the effectiveness of the plan before a full rollout.
- Costs and timelines need to be clearly communicated upfront.

---

## V. Personal Notes

Technically, this isn't a difficult problem. The key is to understand the logic of the new system and find the "sweet spot" where it aligns with actual production needs. I'll give it more thought this afternoon to see if I've missed anything.

I'll keep these notes for now. If I decide to take on this project, I'll move forward using this framework.

Xiuying, are you awake?

I can’t sleep. I’ve been awake since a little after one in the morning. I lay there for nearly an hour with my head spinning, so I finally decided to just get up.

About the department, about retirement, and... about our life. I was wondering what I’ll even be able to do for these next two years if the department really gets restructured. Whether the pension will be enough. Your blood pressure has been a bit high lately; I was looking at the records on the calendar and got a little worried. And then there’s Ming’s business—he mentioned on the phone last time that the competition is getting too intense...

July 25, 2023   Evening

I’m a bit tired today, but it’s not the bad kind of tired.

Xiao Wang from the IT department came to see me this morning. He said the new testing system is about to start its trial run and asked if I wanted to learn it ahead of time. I agreed without even thinking. To be honest, I’ve been avoiding this for the past two months—just watching the younger staff navigate those interfaces I don’t understand makes me feel frustrated. But I don’t know what happened today; I just felt like giving it a try.

Xiao Wang gave me a demonstration. Sitting next to him, I was completely lost at first. The menus, the data tables, the charts… it’s nothing like the way I’m used to working. But instead of giving up like I usually do, I started asking him a lot of questions. Why is it categorized this way? What does this parameter mean? Could we possibly…?

Xiao Wang was very patient. He said, "Director Li, the logic of this system is actually quite clear. It just digitalizes the manual testing steps we’ve always done." As I listened, it suddenly clicked—isn't this exactly what we’ve been doing all along? It’s just a different format. I even suggested an idea: what if we integrated the data from the new system with our traditional quality control records? That way, we could use the new technology without losing the experience we’ve built up over the years.

Xiao Wang’s eyes lit up. "Director Li, that’s a great idea. I’ll go talk to the higher-ups about it."

I told him, "It might take me a while to fully master this system, but I believe I can learn it." I was a little surprised at myself as I said it.

For the past two months, I’ve been thinking that maybe I really can’t keep up anymore. Maybe you just have to accept your fate when you get older. Watching my son insist on the online transition and seeing the company go through reforms made me feel like something obsolete. But today… things felt different.

It’s not that all the problems are solved. But at least I haven't been completely defeated. I can still learn. I can still find solutions.

It was sweltering on the way home. I kept thinking about it on the bus. When Xiuying asked me how my day was, I said, "Not bad." She glanced at me, seemingly sensing something, but she didn't press for details.

I’ll go find Xiao Wang again tomorrow. I want to learn this system inside and out. Maybe it’s not a big deal in the grand scheme of things, but to me… it feels quite important.

Xiao Ming, chess was quite interesting today. I was halfway through a game with Old Wang and the others when I remembered it was time for my call with Jianhao, so I hopped on a video call with him first. You know, we have this set for the same time every Wednesday.

Jianhao picked up right away. He told me all about what happened at school today and showed me the new book he bought. I showed the phone to Old Wang and the group—let them see what Jianhao looks like now, and I showed them that photo I took of him last time. Haha, I didn't do a great job with that one; the angle is a bit crooked, but I didn't really mind. Old Wang and the others all said Jianhao looks just like you, especially those bright eyes.

Later, Old Wang asked me how I suddenly became so tech-savvy. I told them that over the past month or so, I’ve been figuring things out on my own just so I could have proper chats with my grandson. Now, video calls are no problem, and I’ve learned how to take photos. Even if they still don't look that great, at least I can capture his face.

To be honest, it feels quite different. I used to think these new gadgets were far removed from my life, but now I feel—this is just part of my life now. Chess on Wednesdays, video calls with Jianhao on Wednesdays, taking care of your mom, and tending to my roses. These things might seem small, but added together, they make my days feel very full.

How is business going on your end lately? If you’re free this weekend, let’s have a good talk.

Xiuying, I want to have a real talk with you.

I took what you said this morning to heart, but I didn't respond properly, and I’m sorry for that. When you told me to get some rest, I just gave you a brief "mm-hmm." It wasn't because I didn't appreciate it; it’s just that... I’m having a hard time putting it into words right now.

I lay in bed for a long while thinking. Waking up at 6:30 has been a habit for thirty-seven years; my body just does it on its own. But after I woke up, it hit me that I no longer have a reason to be up that early. I stayed in bed for over an hour, eyes wide open, but I just couldn't get back to sleep.

You know, I went through all my usual routines this morning—watering the plants, checking the news, having breakfast. But it felt like I was playing a part, like I was just imitating myself. Before, these were things I did before heading to work; they had a purpose. Now... now that they're done, there’s nothing next.

Logically, I know retirement is a good thing, but emotionally, I’m feeling this strange sense of emptiness. It’s not exhaustion, and I don’t feel unwell. It’s just a feeling that something is missing, like I’m drifting and have nowhere to land. I keep looking at that department group photo, wondering where to hang it in the house, but what I’m really doing is... I’m wondering what I have left to do.

Xiuying, tell me the truth—what do you think I should do now? I’m not asking how to kill time; I truly want to know how I’m supposed to fill this void.

I know you’ll have some ideas.

It was just after 9:00 PM. Li Jianguo had finished washing up and sat on the edge of the bed, picking up his phone. Wang Xiuying was already asleep, so he didn't turn on the light; the glow from the screen illuminated his face.

He opened WeChat and found his wife’s chat window, pausing for a moment. His finger hovered over the screen. Finally, he typed a few words, then deleted them. He typed again, and deleted them again.

Eventually, he sent this message:

---

**Thank you for what you said today. I’ve been thinking about it, and you’re right. I should be able to learn this system. It’s not like it’s anything particularly difficult; it’s just a bit unfamiliar at first. I’ll keep asking that young man tomorrow.**

---

After a few seconds, he added another line:

**Get some sleep. I still have to take you to the hospital for your follow-up tomorrow.**

---

He put down his phone and lay back in bed, not looking at the screen again.

Outside the window, the night in the old residential compound was very quiet. He closed his eyes, his mind still tracing the interface of that system—but this time, it wasn't anxiety he felt. It was a calm, methodical way of thinking, just like the quality inspection work he had done for thirty-seven years.

One step at a time. He’d learn it eventually.

July 19, 2023, Evening

Spent the afternoon playing chess with some old colleagues and won three games.

I haven't been playing well this past month. My mind has been elsewhere, and I’ve been making some glaringly bad moves. Old Wang and the others could tell; they didn't say it outright, but I could feel it. I don’t know what it was about today, but the moment I sat down, things felt different. My head was clear, I could read the board, and I struck when the time was right. After I won, Old Wang patted me on the shoulder and said, "Hey, you seem much sharper today." I just smiled and didn't say much.

To be honest, I’ve had too much on my mind lately. That new system—at first, I really thought I’d never learn it. Watching the younger staff operate it so fast while I sat there, trying to follow step-by-step and still making mistakes... there were a few times I thought, *forget it, this stuff just isn't meant for people like me.*

But... slowly, it started to feel less daunting. The key is patience. I mentioned this to the technician, and he nodded, saying that’s how it is for most people at the start—you can’t rush it. It reminded me of my thirty-odd years in quality inspection; it was the same way. Nothing is done perfectly all at once; it all happens one step at a time.

I was thinking about this while playing chess today. Chess is the same—you can't be impatient. If you see the board clearly before you move and stay patient, you can win.

The sun was still out when I headed home. Cycling through the old street, I remembered how often I used to walk this way when I was young; the factory shuttle used to stop right at the corner. It’s still the same street, but I’m not that young man anymore. Still... it doesn't seem as scary as I imagined.

Xiuying made braised fish for dinner. She asked how my day was, and I told her it was pretty good. She said I’ve looked exhausted lately, but seemed a bit more spirited today. I didn't explain much and just kept eating.

I have to go back to learn that system tomorrow. But thinking about it now, it seems alright.

*9:00 PM. Looked through last year’s quality achievement reports again. So many people from the department photo ten years ago are gone now. It’s still a bit painful to think about. But I can't just give up like this.*

July 23, 2023, Morning

I woke up later than usual. I heard the alarm when it went off, but I didn't get up right away. I stayed in bed for another ten minutes or so, staring at the cracks in the ceiling, my mind a complete blank. Xiuying was already up; the scent of congee drifted in from the kitchen.

I stood in front of the mirror for a long time while washing up. The person in the reflection felt like a stranger. The eyes were empty, as if something had hollowed them out. I tried to force a smile at myself, but I couldn't. I just stood there, watching, for a long time.

At breakfast, Xiuying served me a bowl of congee. I sat down, picked up the spoon, and took half a sip. It tasted of nothing. When I set the bowl down, Xiuying asked if I was feeling unwell. I said no, just that I had no appetite. She didn't say anything else, but I saw her frown.

After eating, I walked out onto the balcony. The Chinese roses were still there. They haven't been watered in a week. The leaves are starting to wither, and the buds have stopped growing. I stood there and looked at them for a while. I knew I should water them. My notebook was right there; I could even record it—the date, the amount of water, the state of the flowers—just as I’ve done for thirty years.

But I didn't move. I just stood there, looking at them.

I can’t even manage a small thing like this. It’s just watering a flower—what else am I capable of doing?

Things at the company are still up in the air. I haven't heard my son mention his business lately. My daughter sounded exhausted over the phone last time. My grandson watches cartoons on a phone, and I still don't know how to operate it.

Xiuying was clearing the dishes in the kitchen. I heard the sound of the faucet. Her blood pressure was a bit high again this month; the doctor said we need to be more careful. I wrote the medical advice on the calendar, but I don't know what use there is in writing these things down anymore.

The leaves of the roses swayed in the wind.

I still didn't go to water them.

**August 30, 2023, 8:15 PM**

## Thoughts on the New System

**Strengths of the system:**
- Fast defect identification; fewer misses compared to manual inspection.
- Clear data logging, making it easy to trace back.
- Reduces bias from human judgment.

**Points to keep in mind:**
- The system might miss some subtle quality issues; we still need to rely on experience.
- It can't completely replace people; it should be a collaborative effort.

**Questions for the technician next time:**
- Can the parameters be adjusted to better fit our specific product characteristics?
- How can we import inspection standards from previous years so that new employees can follow the criteria?

**On training subordinates:**
- Xiao Wang is the fastest learner. I can have him master it first, then help me teach the others.
- Need to make it clear to them: the system is a tool, not a replacement. The key is still understanding the product.
- Develop a simple learning plan and take it step-by-step.

**Personal thoughts:**
I was just talking to my chess buddies about the new system. They asked me how it was going, and I said, "It’s actually pretty good; it helps us catch things we might otherwise overlook." After saying that, I realized I’ve truly accepted it. I’m not as resistant to it as I was a while ago.

I still have the chance to get this right before I retire—to get the system running smoothly and get the younger staff up to speed. That way, I'll feel at peace when it's time to hand over the reins.

Continue practicing the system operations tomorrow.

Xiuying, are you asleep yet?

I’ve been sitting in the living room for a while, thinking back on my phone call with Li Ming today. The more I think about it, the more uneasy I feel.

Do you remember what I told him? All that talk about playing it safe, building a solid foundation first, and not rushing into expansion. I sounded so certain, as if I really knew what I was talking about. But looking back now, what do I actually know? Words like "online transition" and "supply chain"—I was a bit lost just hearing them. Those things didn't even exist in my day.

I’m wondering if the advice I gave him might actually end up hurting him. He trusts me so much; he thinks I have experience. But my experience... it seems it’s already outdated.

Take the new systems at the factory, for example—even the inspections are all computerized now. I’m still stuck using the old methods. Sometimes the supervisors ask if I can use the new system, and I tell them... I tell them I’m just more comfortable with the old way. The truth is, I don't know how to use it. I don't want to admit it, but that's the reality.

The advice I gave Li Ming probably won't help him; it might even mislead him. I’m starting to wonder, what value do I still have?

I’m retiring in two years. I used to think that was a good thing—that I could finally rest. But now... I’m a little scared. Scared that after I retire, I’ll be even more useless. Scared that I won’t even have the chance to offer the kids advice anymore.

Tell me, what is there left for me to do?

—Li Jianguo

Wednesday, October 19, 2022, 2:20 PM

I’ve been sitting in the office for a while, staring at the quality performance charts on the wall and that department group photo from ten years ago. Suddenly, a question hit me—how much longer can I keep doing this?

I spent the morning at home fumbling with that cell phone again; another failure. I still haven't figured out that "cloud drive" thing my daughter installed for me. I wanted to take photos of the flowers to track their dates, but I kept hesitating over the buttons, terrified I’d accidentally delete something. In the end, I gave up, took out my notebook, and wrote it down by hand like I always do. That pink rose bush has yellowing leaves because I’ve been preoccupied the last couple of days. Looking at it made my chest feel tight.

But it’s different once I’m at the office. When I’m handling a quality report, I know exactly what to do. When I coordinate with the production department, they listen to me. I can feel that I carry weight here. But that’s exactly why I’m becoming more afraid.

I’m starting to think that my skills, my entire sense of value, seem to be tied solely to this job. For thirty-seven years, this is what I’ve lived for. But what about retirement? I’m retiring in two years. Will I still be "me" then?

I can’t even learn how to use a phone, and I’m always forgetting things. My daughter is patient when she teaches me, but I still don’t get it. Is my brain really starting to fail? If I can’t even learn new things, what will I do after I retire? Grow flowers? Play chess? Can those things really fill my days?

I wrote that sentence down in my notebook. My hand shook a little as I did.

It’s not that I’m retiring right this second, but the question has started to loom over me in a very real way. I’m not the kind of person who can just idle away the time. I need to be useful. I need to be needed.

Xiuying’s blood pressure has been a bit high lately. I need to take her for a follow-up appointment next week. My grandson is in Shanghai, so I don’t get to see him much. I can’t be of much help with my son’s business, either. So what happens then? Will I really be reduced to just watering flowers, playing chess, and flipping through photo albums at home?

I’m not dismissing those things. I like gardening. But that’s because I still have a job; I still have an identity. I am the Director of the Quality Inspection Department. I am reliable. I am useful.

What if all of that is gone?

The office is very quiet. I haven't told anyone what I’m thinking. I never do.

How much longer can I do this?

Xiuying, I had a video call with Jianhao today.

He asked me why the roses on the balcony were blooming again, so I showed them to him and told him that this year’s flowers are even redder than last year’s. He listened so intently and even asked why some of the buds hadn't opened yet.

It got me thinking—maybe I should just teach him how to garden. When they come over for summer break, we can plant a pot together on the balcony, and he can handle the watering and keep a log himself. I showed him my notebook, and he seemed really interested.

We talked for quite a while before hanging up.

Don't you think that sounds like a lovely idea?

The IT department came by to conduct operational training for the new system. Attendees: myself, Li Ming (my subordinate, not my son), and two inspectors.

The system is version EQC-2023. Key features include:

- Real-time report generation

The advantage of the new system is speed and accuracy, but I noticed an issue—it primarily focuses on data recording and statistics. For quality issues requiring human judgment (such as grading the severity of surface defects), the experience of our inspectors is still necessary. I proposed an idea: could a "Notes Module" be added to allow inspectors to record observational details used in traditional methods? This would bridge the gap between the new system and our old practices.

The instructor said it was a good suggestion and would provide feedback to the development team.

- Start a trial run of the new system in the Quality Control Department next Monday, beginning with Production Line A.
- Conduct a secondary training session for my subordinates to ensure they can operate it independently.
- Compare inspection results between the old and new systems for one week to check for discrepancies.

**Notes:**
I feel this system is quite usable. Although it felt unfamiliar at first, the logic is clear. The key is how we utilize it. We can't rely entirely on the system; we still need to depend on human eyes and experience.

February 8, 2023  Sunny

I woke up late today. I didn't open my eyes until 6:45, fifteen minutes later than usual. I lay in bed for a while longer, listening to the sound of sweeping downstairs before slowly getting up. Xiuying was still asleep, so I didn't turn on the light.

The roses on the balcony need watering. I didn't have time to look closely yesterday, so today I checked the leaves with a magnifying glass—the leaves on the third pot on the left are turning a bit yellow. I dipped a cotton swab in fresh water and wiped them clean, leaf by leaf. The soil felt a bit dry, but not excessively so. I recorded the details: 6:47 AM, approximately 150ml of water, 60% soil moisture.

My hands were shaking a little. I didn't tell myself why; I just noticed it while I was recording the data. The strokes were a bit crooked when I wrote the numbers, so I erased them and wrote them again.

That phrase from yesterday’s meeting keeps spinning in my head. "Digital transformation," "institutional optimization"—I’ve heard too many words like these. During the wave of layoffs in the nineties, so many people left while listening to words just like those. I survived back then because I was still useful. What about now?

Machines can perform inspections. I know that. Systems can judge whether something is qualified or not, generate reports, and trace data. So, what have these thirty-seven years been for?

No, I shouldn't think like that. I still have two years. Two years is enough time to prove a lot of things. Experience cannot be replaced by numbers. Details, judgment, the "feel" for the product—these are things machines don't have.

I looked again at that ten-year-old group photo in the office. My subordinates were laughing happily. They were so young back then.

The flowers still need to be properly cared for. Not a single detail can be overlooked. I wrote down the growth record for the roses in my notebook again—leaf color, number of buds, the date of the last fertilization. Lately, I’ve found that I need to observe even more closely; there can’t be the slightest bit of sloppiness. Once you let your guard down, problems arise.

Xiuying is up. I can hear her in the kitchen. She has a follow-up appointment at the hospital today, and I need to go with her. I’ve put the medical instruction booklet in my bag.

There is still so much to do. So many details to confirm.

I need to take another look at those three plants that aren't growing well.

October 24, 2022, 6:15 AM

Since coming back from the hospital yesterday, Xiuying’s blood pressure has been fluctuating again. The doctor said her medication needs adjusting, and I didn’t sleep well all night.

I got up just after four in the morning and sat in the living room for a while. Looking out the window before dawn, I thought of the way Xiuying asked me, "My blood pressure isn't that serious, is it?" I didn't give her a direct answer. I knew I should have comforted her, but what I said was, "Prevention is important. It’s better to keep it under control now than to regret it when things get serious."

She didn't say anything else. I could sense she wanted to hear me say everything would be fine, but I couldn't do it. Because I don't know if it will be fine myself.

All these years, I’ve felt that as long as I’m meticulous enough, I can manage everything. Inspection standards must be strict, household accounts must be kept clear, Xiuying’s medical orders must be written on the calendar, and follow-ups must be done on time every month. I thought that was enough. But the doctor’s words yesterday—"The blood pressure control isn't ideal"—felt like being told there was a flaw in my plan.

I’ve started wondering: what if her health continues like this? What if one day I can no longer keep it under control?

This thought makes me very uneasy. I’m not used to this.

I checked her blood pressure this morning. Still half-asleep, she asked me, "Tense again?" I didn't deny it. Perhaps she saw through me long ago. I wonder if I’m being too high-strung about her. But then, I can't think of any other way to be. Relax? What if something goes wrong?

The notes in my journal are growing longer. Medication times, dietary restrictions, the date of the next check-up. Sometimes I stop and look at what I’ve written, and it feels a bit foreign. It’s like I’m performing a quality inspection, but the subject is my wife’s body.

I’m retiring soon. Perhaps this is the first lesson I need to learn—that some things are truly beyond my complete control.

Xiuying is still sleeping. I should go make her breakfast.

December 3, 2024, Evening

Ran into the new director at the office again today. He said he had a question about a testing procedure and wanted to pick my brain, so I sat down to walk him through it. Halfway through, he said, "Director Li, it’s so good to have you here."

I froze for a second, then naturally replied, "I’m not the director anymore, but I’m happy to help."

I surprised even myself when I said that. There wasn't a hint of bitterness.

If this had happened six months ago, I probably would have felt a bit awkward. Back then, I was still dwelling on how I’d suddenly gone from being the "Director" to just "retired." I’d look at that ten-year-old group photo still hanging on the office wall and feel... something. But now? It feels like I’ve truly accepted it. Not a forced acceptance, but a genuine one.

As I continued the explanation, I noticed myself saying, "In my experience," rather than "This is how you should do it." The difference seems subtle, but the feeling is entirely different. Before, I was giving orders; now, I’m sharing. I’m passing on what I know rather than managing him.

He listened intently and even took notes. Watching him write, I suddenly felt... this is actually quite nice. I don’t have to come here every day anymore, dealing with those annoying complaints and endless coordination, yet I can still put years of accumulated knowledge to use. I’m still useful.

Xiuying asked me today how the consulting fees were. I told her they’re not bad. She smiled and said, "Then keep at it, just don't overwork yourself." We both know the money isn't for supporting the household; it’s just to make me feel like I still have value.

I think I’ve finally figured it out—I can be all these identities at once. I’m the same old chess buddy on Wednesdays, Xiuying’s husband at home, a grandfather when I’m with my grandson on weekends, and a consultant when I drop by the office. These roles are all real, and they don’t conflict with one another.

I used to think retirement was an "end." Now, it feels more like a "pivot."

Back to watering the plants tomorrow. The Chinese roses have new buds.

Monday, September 5, 2022. Sunny.

The thought crossed my mind again today.

I spent the morning at the office handling a few inspection reports. In the afternoon, while sitting at my desk, my eyes suddenly drifted to that ten-year-old department photo on the wall, and I spaced out for a moment. I didn't snap out of it until a colleague asked when I’d have the new quality standard documents organized. These last two years have truly flown by.

At dinner tonight, Xiuying mentioned that our daughter might come home for the National Day holiday. My first thought was how nice it would be to see Jianhao again. But then I remembered how, during her last visit, my daughter kept nagging me about using my phone to track the watering dates for my plants instead of always reaching for my notebook. She installed some "Notes" app on my phone that day and showed me how to use it. I asked her then—why change? I’ve used this notebook for so many years, and it’s never let me down.

My daughter said the phone is more convenient, and Jianhao chimed in, saying, "Grandpa, this way you don't have to carry your notebook around." As I listened, I couldn't help but wonder—what if the data gets lost? Is a phone really reliable? All my records are in there: watering dates, fertilization schedules, monthly progress... these things matter to me. If they suddenly vanished, what would I do?

In the end, I agreed to give it a try. But I made it very clear to my daughter—I’m keeping the notebook just in case. She laughed and said I was being too cautious. Maybe so. I just don't feel at ease.

The app is on my phone now, but I still find myself reaching for the notebook to write things down. Sometimes I record the same thing twice, which feels a bit silly. But it’s the only way I feel secure.

I sat on the balcony for a while looking at the roses; the autumn buds are already coming in. I jotted down today’s watering amount in my notebook—still using a pen. A twenty-year habit isn't something you change overnight.

Sometimes I think about how I’ll be retiring in a couple of years. What will I do then? The job will be gone, the professional identity gone. The flowers at home, chess, spending time with family... is that enough? Now, even a small thing like tracking my plants requires learning something new. My daughter means well, I know that. But in my heart, there’s this... how should I put it? A feeling that things are slowly slipping through my fingers, and I can't quite hold on.

Anyway, I’ll try that phone app again tomorrow. But I’m not throwing away the notebook.

# MEMO

**Date:** Wednesday Afternoon, September 18, 2024

**Subject:** Redefining the "Project"

---

I’ve been thinking about something lately.

In the past, back at the department, I always used the word "project"—quality inspection projects, process improvement projects, customer complaint resolution projects. Every project had a goal, a timeline, and a performance evaluation. That was the way we worked, and it was the way I got things done.

But recently, I’ve realized I’m using that same mindset to look at spending time with Xiuying, teaching my grandson, and playing chess with my friends. I didn’t notice it at first, until yesterday while watering the flowers on the balcony. I was recording the watering dates in my notebook when it suddenly hit me—I am treating my life the same way.

The difference is, this time, the meaning of "project" has completely changed.

**The "Accompanying Xiuying" Project**
- Monthly hospital check-ups (fixed schedule)
- Recording doctor’s orders on the calendar (ensuring execution)
- This isn't a task; it is life itself.

**The "Teaching My Grandson" Project**
- Teaching the rules of Chinese chess
- Telling stories from when I was young
- Spending a few extra days with him during his school breaks
- This isn't about completion; it is about companionship.

**The "Community Chess" Project**
- Arriving at the activity room on time every Wednesday
- Eight years of consistency
- This isn't entertainment; it is friendship.

**The "Gardening" Project**
- Recording the growth cycle of the roses
- Observing every single bud
- This isn't a pastime; it is the observation of life.

The "projects" of the past were about results—pass rates, efficiency, and a sense of achievement. The "projects" of now are about the process—every detail, every moment of company, and every ordinary day.

It’s not that I’ve given up on anything; it’s that I’ve finally understood something.

That obsession with detail hasn't disappeared; it’s just pointed in a different direction.

# Memo

**Date:** June 28, 2023, 7:00 PM

**Subject:** Business Talk with Li Ming — A Few Reflections

---

I had a phone call with Xiao Li today, and he was talking about the building materials store again. Competition has been fierce lately; some shops have started price wars, and he’s weighing whether to follow suit. I listened for a while and then told him one thing: **Maintaining your own standards is more important than blindly following the crowd.**

The moment I said it, I gave myself pause.

For thirty-seven years, from my days as an operator to where I am now, this is the principle I’ve lived by. No matter how the world changed, my inspection standards never wavered. Harsh? Yes, they are. But it is precisely because of this "harshness" that our pass rates are high and our customers trust us. This isn't about short-term gains; it’s about long-term credibility.

The problem Xiao Li is facing is actually quite similar to mine: **Competition isn't a price war; it’s a battle of differentiation.** If his store only competes on price, he’ll never beat the big chains. But if he can differentiate himself through service, quality, and reputation, that’s a different story.

I suggested that instead of following the trend of cutting prices, he should focus his energy on improving service quality and product selection. Make the customers feel that buying materials from him gives them peace of mind. That’s the only way to survive in the long run.

Writing this down, it suddenly occurred to me—shouldn't this same principle apply to the new system I’m currently learning?

I’ve been worried that this new system might invalidate my experience. But perhaps I’ve got it wrong. Maybe the system and my experience aren't at odds; maybe they can be integrated. The system improves efficiency, but **standards and principles must still be upheld.** Those never go out of style.

When I talk to the new director tomorrow, perhaps I can approach the conversation from this angle.

---

**Note:** Keep this thought in mind; it might be useful for future consulting work.

# Diary

Saturday, October 5, 2024  Sunny

Yesterday, I dug out that quality inspection notebook from 1998. I’m not sure what triggered the memory—maybe it’s because that phone call from the new director last week is still rattling around in my head.

The pages have yellowed a bit. Opening to the first page, my handwriting looks just as it did then—neat to the point of being stiff. I remember when I was first promoted to the Quality Department; I would pore over every single inspection standard, terrified of missing something. Back then, I wrote a sentence in the margin: "Quality is the lifeblood of the enterprise, and it is my responsibility." Looking at it now, it seems a bit brash and youthful, but that earnestness was real.

Xiuying saw me sitting on the balcony for a long time and asked what was on my mind. I showed her the notes, pointing to one specific entry, and told her how many people complained that this standard was too strict at the time—but it later proved to be the reason our department maintained such high pass rates. She smiled and said, "Look at you, still taking pride in these things after all these years."

I said, "It’s not pride. I’m just thinking... these things, this experience and knowledge, they ought to be passed down."

Xiuying set down her watering can and looked at me. "Well, you’re already doing that, aren't you?"

Her words gave me pause. It’s true. Over the past two months, I’ve tried to explain things in as much detail as possible whenever the new director asks me a question. When Li Ming called the day before yesterday saying there was an issue with the procurement standards at the building materials store, I analyzed it for him and explained how to establish a more scientific inspection process. I even explained the logic of "quality control" to my grandson when he asked why I was checking his homework—though he probably didn't quite grasp it.

I have no desire to go back to the office. That stage of my life is over, and I’ve made my peace with that. But this consulting work seems like a perfect balance. I don’t have to rush to the factory by 6:30 every morning or argue over standards repeatedly in meetings, yet my expertise can still be put to use. The fees will also supplement my pension, so I won't have to worry so much about Xiuying’s medical expenses, and I can spend more time with Jianhao during the holidays.

Flipping through the notebook, I thought that maybe this is what I’m meant to do next. Not "Li Jianguo, Director of Quality Inspection," but "Li Jianguo, a man who understands quality." It sounds almost the same, but it feels entirely different.

When Xiuying came back in, I had the notebook resting on my lap, my finger still pressed against that line: "Quality is the lifeblood of the enterprise." She didn't say anything more; she just sat down beside me, and we watched the China roses together for a while.

I feel like this is the right decision.

July 20, 2023, Night

I was in the office sorting through inspection reports when Ming called. He sounded ecstatic, telling me his building materials store is going digital. He’s already been in touch with several e-commerce platforms and plans to launch officially next month. As I listened to him ramble on about livestreaming and social commerce, my mind struggled to keep up.

I wanted to give him some advice. After all, I’ve kept a close eye on his business all these years; the competition is fierce, and moving online is a viable path. I thought back to my thirty-plus years of experience in quality management—any change requires a plan, a risk assessment, a steady hand...

But the words reached my lips and stayed there.

In a weary voice, I simply said, "You decide. I trust you."

After hanging up, I sat in my office, staring blankly at a department group photo from ten years ago.

What was I thinking? "I trust you"? That wasn't what I really felt. I was giving up. What I was actually saying was: *I don't know how to help you anymore, so please stop asking me.*

Ming didn't need empty words like "I trust you." He needed someone with experience to break things down for him—when is the best time to launch? How do you control costs? What if it fails? I used to be able to anticipate these questions. But now... I don't even trust my own judgment.

For over thirty years, I worked in quality inspection. The stricter the standards I set, the higher the pass rate. The logic was simple and effective. But now? Everything is changing. E-commerce, livestreaming, social platforms... I can barely keep the names straight. It took being forced to give up my twenty-year-old Nokia for a smartphone, and even now, I’m constantly pressing the wrong buttons.

How can I offer advice to a son who is moving his business online?

I flipped through the calendar on my desk, which was cluttered with notes: Xiuying’s follow-up appointments, various inspection data, dates for chess matches. These records make me feel like I’m still in control of something. But in reality? I’m only in control of things that are becoming obsolete.

Before hanging up, Ming said, "Thanks for your trust, Dad."

I didn't answer. I just gave a noncommittal "mm-hmm."

I realize now that I wasn't trusting him. I was admitting my own powerlessness.

# March 29, 2023, 9:00 PM

Sitting on the balcony, I noticed the rosebuds have grown a bit more. I picked up my notebook; it’s been a long time since I’ve recorded anything like this. My hand felt a little stiff, but I still wrote down today’s data—watering time, the color of the leaves, the number of buds.

This is a ritual I haven't performed in quite a while.

A few days ago, I didn't have the heart for any of this. That meeting notice about "institutional restructuring" felt like a stone pressing down on my chest. I started questioning everything—whether my thirty-seven years of work still held any value, if those rigorous inspection standards had already been rendered obsolete by the new system, and if I had truly grown old and useless. I didn't even go to play chess, which was a first in eight years. When Xiuying looked at me that day, I knew she was worried.

But this afternoon, while sitting here watering the flowers, a thought suddenly struck me.

I took my pen and wrote a sentence on a blank page of the notebook: "Times are changing, but I can still learn. I am not passively accepting change; I am actively adapting to it." After writing it, I underlined the sentence with a pencil.

It was a strange gesture. It felt like I was signing my name, or rather, confirming something to myself.

I don't know what that meeting will decide. Maybe there will be layoffs, maybe reassignments, or maybe nothing will happen at all. But I suddenly realized—I can't just surrender here. I can learn the new system, I can adapt to the changes, and I can even pass my experience on to whoever succeeds me before I retire. This isn't some grand resolution; it’s just... what living is supposed to look like.

The flowers are still growing. I am still here.

Xiuying is watching TV inside; I can hear the sound. Tomorrow morning at 6:30, I will still wake up on time, watch the news for ten minutes, and have breakfast. And for next Wednesday’s chess game, I’ll be there.

Maybe I really am old, but being old doesn't mean being useless.

The rosebuds should bloom in a few days.

I spent half an hour on the phone with Xiaoling. The thing she said most was that same old line: "Dad, don't work yourself to exhaustion."

I laughed it off at the time, saying it was fine and just what needed to be done. But after hanging up, those words kept circling in my head.

I took Xiuying to the hospital for her follow-up today; her blood pressure is fluctuating again. The doctor said it’s under control for the most part but told us to be more careful. As soon as I got back, I made a new checklist for her—what time to take her meds, exactly how much salt to put in her food, and how many steps to walk each day. I even bought a new blood pressure monitor. I plan to check her twice a day, morning and night, and log it in a notebook. When Xiaoling heard me talking about all this, her tone changed.

She told me that Mom had also mentioned it to her, saying I shouldn't be so high-strung. When did Xiuying say that to our daughter? I didn't ask.

I know I’m trying to keep everything under control. After all those years at the factory, that’s just how quality inspection works—you have to manage every single step to ensure nothing goes wrong with the product. Applying that logic to Xiuying seems right, in a way. But what Xiaoling said...

I sat on the balcony for a long time, staring at the flowers. The Chinese roses haven't been doing well this year. Feeling a bit restless, I kept adjusting the position of the pots, wondering if they weren't getting enough light. Xiuying was inside watching TV; I could hear the sound, but I didn't go in.

Later, Xiaoling brought up little Jianhao. She said he’s been learning to dance lately and asked when I could come to Shanghai to see him. My heart softened instantly. I told her that once I retire, I’ll spend more time with him. She said that’s still two years away.

Two years. It suddenly hit me that I always seem to be living for some distant future—what I’ll do when I retire, what I’ll do when our health improves. But right now, Xiuying’s blood pressure is fluctuating, and I can't control it. It feels like there’s less and less I can control.

That thought makes me a bit uneasy.

I logged today’s blood pressure readings again; my handwriting was more cramped than usual. Xiuying asked what I was doing, and I just said, "Nothing, just taking notes." She didn't say anything else.

At dinner, I was extra careful with the salt. I watched her while she ate. She said, "Stop watching me eat," her voice tinged with a certain helplessness.

I know she was talking about me. But I don't know how not to watch.

I’ll continue the logs tomorrow. The next follow-up is in a month.

Never mind, I’m probably overthinking it. I just wanted to talk to you and have you listen.

I have another meeting tomorrow. I’ll see what they have to say then.

— Jianguo

December 1, 2024 – 6:45 AM

I found it.

That diary I started using back in 1984. Forty years. This morning, after finishing my tea while Xiuying was still asleep, I sat on the balcony and flipped through it page by page. It started with those yellowed sheets—"Today the master taught me how to tune the machines," "Overtime until 8 PM, got blisters on my fingers"—all the way to the entries from this October.

Consulting work, Xiuying’s check-up results, Jianhao’s math exam, that pot of Chinese roses finally blooming...

It’s all in the same book.

I stopped at the last page of October, picked up my pen, and wrote on a fresh page: "December—A Month for Summarizing a New Life." My pen paused for a moment before I continued writing.

I don’t know why, but looking at those words made my chest feel a bit tight. It wasn’t sadness, just... hard to explain. Like something is shifting, yet nothing has been lost.

Forty years. From a machine operator to a Quality Director. Now, a consultant, a retiree, a husband, a father, a grandfather. All these identities are in this book. They don’t contradict each other; instead, they’re like... how should I put it? Like the same person seen under different lights.

Xiuying’s blood pressure has been well-controlled lately. During our last video call, Jianhao said his class is going to a museum, and I promised to tell him some history. My younger son’s building materials store did decent business this month; I gave him a few suggestions last week, and he said they were helpful. The plants are all alive, and the roses bloomed longer than last year.

The new director asked me about inspection standards again last week. We talked for over an hour. He took notes very earnestly, which reminded me of my younger self.

I’m not losing anything. I am transforming.

This December, I want to properly organize everything from this year. Not for any particular reason, but just to see clearly—how, over the course of this year, I went from "someone about to retire" to "someone who has already started a new life."

Xiuying is up. I can hear her in the kitchen.

Time to go join her for breakfast.

# Factory Quality Issues - Technical Memo

**Date:** September 15, 2024, 2:30 PM
**Source:** Phone inquiry from the new supervisor

---

## Issue Overview

The new automated inspection system is failing to identify **specific types of surface micro-cracks**. These defects were detectable under the old system, but there appears to be a blind spot in the new system's parameter settings.

---

## Details (Pending Further Confirmation)

1. **Defect Characteristics**
   - Surface micro-cracks (Depth? Length?)
   - Location: After which specific process do they primarily appear?
   - How do these differ from previously encountered defect characteristics?

2. **System Issues**
   - What is the detection principle of the new system? (Optical? Ultrasonic?)
   - What are the current parameter thresholds?
   - Is the system failing to detect them entirely, or is it detecting them but flagging them as "acceptable"?

3. **Scope of Impact**
   - How many products have already bypassed detection?
   - Has there been any feedback from the customers?

---

## Potential Solutions

**Short-term (Parameter Adjustments):**
- Lower the sensitivity thresholds.
- Check the light source intensity settings.
- Adjust the scanning frequency.

**Mid-term (Process Optimization):**
- Add a manual re-inspection step after the automated inspection (specifically for this type of defect).
- Alternatively, improve the upstream production process to reduce the occurrence of such defects.

**Long-term:**
- Contact the system vendor to see if the algorithm can be optimized.

---

## Information Needed

- Which specific production line and product model are affected?
- How long has this issue been occurring?
- How is it currently being handled?

---

## Miscellaneous

I’ll wait for them to explain the full situation before making a move. That said, this is an interesting problem—issues with new systems usually aren't due to hardware failure, but rather poorly tuned parameters.

I need to think twice about whether to take on this job. I should check with Xiuying to see if she has any hospital follow-ups scheduled for this month.

September 25, 2024, Evening

I did something a bit embarrassing today while playing chess—I pulled out my phone to show Old Wang and the others my video call with Jianhao.

To be honest, I hadn't planned on doing it. Old Wang just asked how I’d been, and I casually mentioned that I’d video-chatted with my grandson again this week. I told them how Jianhao taught me how to use the camera on the phone and even took a few photos of me. As soon as Old Wang heard that, he got all excited and insisted on seeing them. I was a bit nervous at first, worried I’d fumble with the phone and make a fool of myself in front of them. But luckily, the screen lit up, the photos were all there, and I flipped through them one by one for them.

What surprised me most was their reaction. It wasn’t mockery; it was genuine amazement. Old Li said, "Goodness, Jianguo, you actually know how to use this now?" Someone else asked me how I learned. So, I walked them through the steps my daughter taught me—where to tap, how to swipe, and how to find the video call button. It was only while I was explaining it that I realized these things aren't foreign to me anymore.

During a break in the game, I gave them a little demonstration of the video call feature—I didn't actually call Jianhao, but I showed them the interface. Old Wang and the others crowded around to watch, and someone said, "You're handier with this stuff than we are." Hearing that made me feel a bit bashful, but also quite proud.

On the way home, I kept thinking about it. I used to think that at my age, learning new things was just too hard—especially something like a smartphone, which looks so complicated. But over the past two weeks or so, I’ve realized it’s not really like that. As long as you’re willing to put in the time and take it step by step, you can figure it out. It’s the same logic I used back at the factory doing inspection work—no matter how complex the process, once you break it down, it’s just a series of individual steps.

Xiuying asked me why I was in such a good mood, so I told her what happened today. She smiled and said, "You, you can learn anything." It made me think of a phrase from chess—"Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon." It seems like when you get older, people tend to underestimate you, but the potential is still there as long as you're willing to try.

Now, I’m actually looking forward to next Wednesday’s game. Not to show off, but because I feel like I have something new to do, something new to learn. The transition from "Quality Inspection Supervisor" to "Tech-savvy Grandpa" doesn't seem so hard to accept after all.

I need to video call Jianhao next week to ask him how school is going.

# Diary

Friday, August 16, 2024  Sunny

Jianhao came over yesterday. Xiuying started tidying up first thing in the morning, scrubbing the balcony until it was spotless. I didn’t really do anything special to prepare; I just went about things as usual, though I did rearrange the Chinese roses a bit, worried the boy might bump into them.

As soon as he arrived, he ran straight to the balcony, saying he wanted to see the flowers I’m growing. I was a bit surprised. Usually, when we video call, he’s always preoccupied with mobile games—he never takes the initiative to ask about the flowers. So, I started explaining them to him, beginning with the colors: the red ones are "Cinnabar," the pink ones are "Pink Lady," and then there’s that yellow pot I only planted this spring—it hasn’t bloomed yet. Jianhao listened very intently, asking why some had many buds while others had few. I told him about the watering methods—when to water more, when to water less—and the intricacies of fertilizing.

As I was talking, I realized my voice sounded different. Xiuying heard me from the kitchen and told me later, "When you talk about your flowers, you’re as serious as if you’re explaining quality standards." I laughed; it’s true.

Later, Jianhao took out his phone to take pictures. At first, I felt a bit uncomfortable; I’ve always felt it’s a bit strange to take photos next to flowers. But he was so earnest about it, even asking me what I thought of the angles. I leaned in to take a look and helped him adjust his position so the light would hit the flowers just right. He said, "Grandpa, why don't you try taking one with your phone too?" I hesitated for a moment but pulled it out anyway. It took me forever just to find the camera app, and he stayed by my side teaching me. We took several photos together; he was smiling so happily.

After we finished, I said to Xiuying, "Look how serious Jianhao is, this boy has his own ideas." As I said it, I had this feeling I can’t quite put into words. It wasn't the kind of accomplishment you get from work, but it wasn't an empty feeling either. It was a sense of... being needed, I suppose. He needed me to tell him about the flowers, needed me to help him with the angles, and needed me to take photos with him.

After Jianhao went to sleep that night, I sat on the balcony for a while. I looked at the flowers and thought back on the day. I made a note in my notebook: *August 15, explained the care of Chinese roses to Jianhao, took photos together.* Below that, I recorded the watering dates as usual.

To be honest, I’m not very good with smartphones; I still don’t quite understand all those functions. But yesterday, I didn't feel frustrated like I usually do. Instead, I found myself thinking that maybe I should learn more. That way, the next time Jianhao comes, I can help him faster.

I don’t know if this counts as progress. Regardless, things feel a little different now.

Thursday, August 1, 2024. Sunny.

The alarm went off at 6:30 AM, and I woke up as usual. I’ve kept this schedule for thirty-seven years; my body is more obedient than my brain. But today felt different—I sat on the edge of the bed for a full five minutes before getting up. Xiuying was still asleep. I just sat there, watching the morning light outside the window, my mind a total blank.

At this time in the past, I’d already be mentally mapping out the day’s inspection plan. And now? I got up and went to the balcony to water the flowers.

The Chinese roses are the same as ever, their leaves a healthy green. I picked up the watering can, but stopped halfway through. Standing there, I found myself wondering—what exactly am I supposed to do today?

Truly, I kept asking myself that over and over. What am I supposed to do today?

I looked down at my notebook, intending to log the watering date. When I picked up the pen, I realized my handwriting looked stiff and awkward, nothing like it used to be. I used to write things like, "Three new buds on the roses, leaves are lush"; now, it’s just "August 1st, watered." It’s mechanical.

I can see it myself—this isn't "recording" anymore; it’s just killing time.

Xiuying got up and asked if I wanted breakfast. I said yes. While she was tidying up the kitchen, I remained on the balcony. There wasn’t much to look at, really, just the flowers. But I stayed there anyway, not wanting to move.

I have to take her to the hospital this afternoon for her blood pressure check-up. I remember that clearly. And there’s the chess game on Wednesday. I remember all of it. But why does it all feel so distant? It’s like I’m looking at someone else’s schedule.

I thought of that group photo from my old office, the one the department gave me ten years ago. It’s still pinned up there, but I’m here.

I’ll still have to get up tomorrow. 6:30 AM.

# Memo - Retirement Planning

**Date: November 28, 2023, 9:15 PM**
**Method: Voice Input**

---

I’m recording this on my phone’s voice-to-text feature so I don’t forget again. Xiuying is watching TV, and I’m sitting on the balcony. Another Chinese rose bloomed today—it looks wonderful.

**A few directions for retirement that I need to think through clearly:**

1. **Flower Care** — This must continue. I currently keep records for all dozen or so pots, but once I retire and have more time, I can expand. I need to consider whether to keep using my paper notebook or switch to phone memos and photos. Honestly, taking photos to track the flowers' progress is much faster and easier than writing. Maybe I can combine both methods.

2. **Chess** — I have to keep up with the Wednesday sessions. My chess partners are old friends of many years; I can’t let that slip. In fact, I might even be able to play more often after I retire.

3. **Consulting Work** — I’ve been thinking about this for a long time. Having spent thirty-seven years at this company, I’ve gained some real insights into quality management. After the new director takes over, I could continue to help out as a consultant. It’s not about the money; it’s more about... well, feeling like I can still contribute. Age isn't a limitation; it's a resource. I have thirty-seven years of experience and I’ve now mastered the new system—combining those two means I still have a lot to offer.

4. **Accompanying Xiuying** — Although her high blood pressure is well-controlled, I still need to keep a close eye on it. We can’t miss her monthly check-ups, and I need to remind her not to overexert herself. We’ve lived together for forty-one years; I need to cherish our time even more now.

5. **Spending time with my grandson** — This is what’s closest to my heart. Jianhao is ten this year and lives in Shanghai. I haven't spent enough time with him during my working years. After I retire, I need to visit Shanghai more often, or have them come here. I want to teach him how to play chess and tell him stories about our generation. Also, he watches cartoons on his phone—I’ve learned how to do that now too... even if I'm still not quite used to it, at least we can watch them together.

---

**One thought:**

Recording these things on my phone is more convenient than I imagined. Voice input is fast; I don’t have to write it out character by character. The reminder feature on the memo app is also quite good. I used to be a bit resistant to these new things, but I’m starting to understand that a new tool is just a tool—as long as you use it well, it’s fine. I don’t necessarily have to give up my paper notebooks, but I can certainly use both methods together.

**A final note to record:**

I have two years left until retirement. I need to spend these two years getting ready—not necessarily to do anything "big," but to figure out how to live a meaningful life after I retire. It’s not about being idle; it’s about living with a plan. That way, even after I leave my post, I’ll still be a useful person.

---

*Voice recording ends. I’ll review this tomorrow morning to make sure nothing was missed.*

# Reflections on the Automated Inspection System

**Date: Friday morning, March 10, 2023**

**Location: Office**

---

## I. Identifying the Issues

I’ve been thinking a lot lately about the news regarding the introduction of an automated inspection system. I didn't sleep well last night, so I figured I’d better write these thoughts down to stop them from just spinning around in my head.

---

## II. The Irreplaceability of Traditional Inspection Methods

**(Insights gathered over the years)**

1.  **Identification of Micro-defects** — Machine cameras and sensors have limitations. Certain subtle surface scratches or color discrepancies that the human eye can catch might not be accurately judged by a machine.
2.  **Judging Material Texture** — Detecting anomalies in hardness or gloss just by touch comes down to experience. A machine only follows preset parameters, but in actual production, there are always deviations.
3.  **Comprehensive Judgment** — It’s not about a single metric; it’s about weighing multiple factors. For instance, a product might be slightly off in one data point but still acceptable in terms of overall quality. Machines lack that kind of flexible judgment.
4.  **Handling Emergencies** — When something goes wrong on the production line, humans can react quickly, adjust standards, and find the root cause. A machine just triggers an alarm and waits for a person to fix it.
5.  **Risk of Data Errors** — System failures, data loss, or incorrect parameter settings could lead to the missed inspection of entire batches. Our paper records and manual cross-checks are our final line of defense.
6.  **Customer Trust** — Over the years, our high pass rates and zero complaints have been built on customers trusting our professionalism and rigor—not a machine.

---

## III. Specific Cases (More to be added)

*   **The 2015 Order** — One batch of products met all the data requirements, but I spotted a potential issue in the welding. It turned out there really was a problem. If we had relied solely on machines, that batch would have passed.
*   **Last Year’s Quality Incident** — The production department suddenly changed their parameters. If it hadn't been for our manual spot checks, the consequences would have been unthinkable.
*   **Communication with the Shanghai Client** — They explicitly stated that they chose us because our inspection standards are strict and involve human judgment.

---

## IV. Action Items

*   [ ] Compile a data table of quality achievements from the past 20 years, specifically cases that "a machine might have missed."
*   [ ] Calculate our department's complaint and rework rates compared to the industry average.
*   [ ] Prepare for a discussion with leadership — the goal isn't to oppose the new system, but to explain how humans and machines must work together.
*   [ ] Research the specific functions of this automated system to identify its limitations.
*   [ ] Talk to the team and hear their thoughts.

---

## V. Personal Thoughts

To be honest, I’m not against progress. I know automation is the trend. But—

Quality inspection is a job that cannot be handed over entirely to machines. What we have built over the last thirty-plus years cannot simply be replaced.

I need to make everyone see that our value doesn't lie in opposing the new, but in knowing how to use the new effectively. The best solution is a partnership between humans and machines.

This isn't me being defensive. It’s a fact.

---

# Mobile Photography Notes

**Date: September 17, 2024**

Key Steps for Taking Photos (Taught by my daughter):

1. **Lighting is Crucial**
   - Ensure there is enough light; it shouldn't be too dark.
   - Best to take photos by a window during the day.
   - Avoid shooting against the light (backlighting).

2. **Keep a Steady Hand**
   - Hold the phone firmly; do not shake it.
   - You can lean your arms against something for support.
   - Press the shutter button gently.

3. **Composition**
   - Place the subject in the center.
   - Don't cut off the head or feet.

---

**Uses:**
- Taking photos of my grandson to send to my daughter.
- Capturing the growth of my flowers (can replace handwritten records).
- Documenting things happening at home.

**To ask my daughter next time:**
- How to adjust the brightness.
- How to delete photos that didn't turn out well.

---

*This method is just like standardized recording at work—write the steps down clearly, and it’s harder to make mistakes next time.*

It wasn't until Xiuying told me that my constant hovering felt like torture to her that I suddenly woke up. She’s my wife, not a product on my assembly line. That day, looking at the roses on the balcony, I realized that no matter how precisely I fertilize them, they have their own rhythm for blooming and fading. People are the same. Now, I’ve learned to let go. I leave the professional matters to the doctors and give her life back to her. And finally, I can get a good night's sleep myself.

It was mainly because I attended a community health lecture a while back, where the expert introduced this concept called the "family stress threshold." He mentioned that over-intervention can actually undermine a person’s ability to self-regulate—much like how constantly tilling the soil around a flower can end up damaging its roots. I gave it some serious thought and realized my old approach was indeed too rigid; I was so fixated on the numbers that I forgot Xiuying is a living, breathing human being. Now, I’ve tucked all those logbooks away in the cabinet and switched to a more subtle way of caring for her. For instance, I take her for a stroll by the river every day after dinner to chat about our younger days. This kind of "quiet nourishment" has actually done a much better job of keeping her blood pressure stable.

To be honest, it was my daughter who sat me down for a heart-to-heart talk the last time she came home. She brought me a few books on psychological boundaries and told me that with my personality, I’m prone to losing my sense of focus after retirement and over-projecting that need for control onto my family members. At first, I was defensive. But then, I happened to sneak a look at Xiuying’s diary and saw that she had written a lot about feeling stressed and wanting to go back to her hometown for a while. It really hurt to read that. I finally realized that love isn’t about standing guard twenty-four hours a day; you have to give each other some space. So now, I’ve started volunteering in the community instead.

That day, I was looking at some old photos in the office, my mind in a complete muddle, and Xiao Wang just happened to be in the wrong place at the wrong time. I took all that anxiety and helplessness about the future out on him. Looking back, I wasn't really pushing for quality—I was just venting. I feel terrible about how I treated him.

To be honest, it was a lecture I attended at the Senior University that really struck a chord with me. The professor mentioned how a family member’s anxiety can be transmitted to the patient through heart rate resonance. That’s when I realized that my constant tense expression was actually doing more harm than good. 

After that, I tried tucking away all those thick hypertension management guides in the study and replaced them with some soothing light music. Now, after dinner every day, I take her to sit by the moat and listen to old operas on the radio. Seeing her in better spirits has made me feel a whole lot more relaxed, too.

It’s mainly because I realized that my "nanny-style" care was stripping away her self-confidence. I remember one time she wanted to go grocery shopping by herself, but I insisted on tagging along. We ended up arguing for ages in the spice aisle over which low-sodium salt to pick. Seeing how hurt and frustrated she looked made me feel terrible. Later, I consulted a therapist and realized that what she truly needs is respect. Now, I’ve handed the kitchen reins back to her and just act as her sous-chef. I also take her to a community Tai Chi class on weekends. Seeing her chatting happily with the neighbors there is the only thing that has truly put my mind at ease.

I actually have Old Li to thank for this. A while back, he gave me an automated health management system, claiming it could sync data to my phone in real-time. At first, I was glued to the screen every day, watching the graphs. But I soon realized this "remote monitoring" made Xiuying so uneasy that she’d look to me for permission just to eat a piece of fruit; it made our relationship incredibly tense. Last month, I simply uninstalled the app and decided to go back to the most basic way of being there for her. Now, I take her to a countryside farmhouse for a couple of days every week to breathe the fresh air. We’ve stopped obsessing over those digital reports, and surprisingly, both of us are feeling much better.

It’s embarrassing to admit, but at first, I really treated our home like a factory workshop. I thought that as long as I bought the best blood pressure monitor and kept the most meticulous records, I could somehow "manage" the illness away. It wasn't until Xiuying asked me if I was treating her like a product on an assembly line that I suddenly snapped out of it. Later, looking at the roses on the balcony, I realized that no matter how much I hurry them, flowers bloom according to their own rhythm—and people are no different. Now, I’ve learned to let go. I stopped obsessing over the numbers and started spending more time walking with her and watering the plants. Life feels much smoother this way, and even my sleep has become a lot more peaceful.

qa_items.question: I’ve noticed you’re back at the chess club recently, and your whole energy is completely different from when you were away. Between the company restructuring and learning the new system, what kind of mental journey have you been on since the turn of the year?
qa_items.answer: Ugh, don't even get me started—I really got stuck in a rut for a while there. Back then, when I heard they were replacing our decades of hard-earned experience with some cold, lifeless automated system, I felt terrible. I kept thinking I was just old and useless, and I even quit the chess group I’d been part of for eight years. In the end, it was my wife, Xiuying, who pulled me out of it. She told me I’ve weathered all sorts of storms in my life, so I couldn't let some new machine scare me off. I finally came around—instead of worrying myself sick, I figured I might as well swallow my pride and learn from the younger folks. Now, I feel much more grounded, and I’ve even got my spark back for chess.
