qa_items.question: Lao Li, you used to be known in our circle as "Mr. Punctual"—someone who insisted on everything going exactly according to plan. But looking back at these past few months, with one thing after another popping up at home and the office, your rhythm has been thrown off quite a bit. Now that things have changed, have you started to feel a bit differently about this need to stay in control of everything?
qa_items.answer: Well… how should I put it? These past few months have really been a wake-up call. I used to think people were like machines—as long as I set the procedures and kept the data straight, life would run like clockwork. But as soon as Xiuying’s blood pressure started fluctuating, my whole "quality management" approach completely fell apart. The more I tried to control things, the more exhausted she became, and I ended up losing sleep every single night. It wasn't until I spent some time looking at those roses on the balcony that it finally clicked: flowers bloom and wither in their own time, and people are no different. I’ve tucked away that blood pressure logbook now. I’ve done what I can, and for the rest, I’ve learned to let go and let nature take its course. Honestly, I feel much more at peace now.

qa_items.answer: To be honest, I still feel a sense of unease whenever I think back on it. She told me straight out back then that I was putting too much pressure on her, and even my son said I was overthinking everything. qa_items.question: Jianguo, back then when Xiaoling warned you not to burn yourself out, if you hadn't just brushed her off and had actually stopped to reflect, do you think you would have still ended up with that sense of helplessness, staring at old photo albums and breaking into tears?
qa_items.answer: Sigh, looking back now, I really was too stubborn. When Xiaoling tried to talk sense into me back then, I just thought she was worrying over nothing. I truly believed that as long as I recorded every piece of data accurately and kept a strict handle on Xiuying’s daily routine, life would run as flawlessly as the quality of parts in the factory. But who knew that human energy and time aren't things you can simply "manage" to keep? If I had listened to her then and learned to let go a little, maybe I’d feel more at peace when flipping through the photo albums now, instead of this crushing sense of failure—the feeling that I’ve spent half my life busying myself, yet failed to grasp the things that mattered most, like watching my grandson grow up or growing old slowly with my wife. That sense of powerlessness is really just the heartache of realizing my illusion of being all-powerful has finally shattered.

If I could have softened my heart back then—if I had tried to share the family load with Xiao Ling and the others and learned to let them take care of me—maybe I would have felt more at peace while waiting for those lab results in the hospital corridor. I wouldn't have felt that crushing sense of loss, that feeling that after holding everything up my whole life, I’d ended up becoming a burden in my old age. That sense of powerlessness was, in truth, the lingering resentment of realizing I could no longer be the "pillar" of the family.

Looking back now, I realize I was far too stubborn and narrow-minded. At the time, I was obsessed with the idea that if I could just plan every cent of our household expenses and future pension down to the last detail, I’d be fulfilling my duty to the family. That’s why I pushed myself so hard to take on extra work before retiring. If only I had listened to Xiaoling’s advice—if I had shifted my focus away from the numbers in my bankbook and instead taken Xiuying to those ancient water towns she’d been talking about for so long. Maybe then, when catching up with old classmates about our later years, I would feel a sense of fulfillment rather than this deep regret, realizing that while I managed to save enough money, I missed the best years of seeing the world with my family.

I suppose it was just my nature back then. I was convinced that as long as I renovated the old house and replaced everything with the latest appliances and furniture, I could provide Xiuying with a more comfortable life and a sense of security in her later years. If only I had taken the energy I spent running around construction markets and overseeing the renovation and used it instead to video call Xiaoling, listen to Jianhao’s stories about school, or just sit a little longer with Xiuying in the small park downstairs. Perhaps then, when we finally moved in, the new house would have felt more like a home. Instead, standing there in that bright, empty living room, it suddenly hit me: all these cold, material things could never replace the laughter of a family being together.

choices.text: Sigh, it all started when I took Xiuying to the community hospital for a follow-up. The doctor pulled me aside for a private chat and told me that her physical health was actually quite solid, but my anxiety had clearly rubbed off on her. He warned that if things continued this way, her dosage would have to be doubled. That was when I realized I’d been stuck in a rut, trying to apply the same "zero-error" standards from my old quality control job to our daily life. Now, I’ve handed over the blood pressure monitoring entirely to her. I just focus on taking her fishing in the suburbs every weekend to relax. Who would have thought this "hands-off" approach would actually work so well?

I’m ashamed to admit it, but I used to think that as long as I kept the data accurate and the processes tight, I could control the outcome—I was treating her like a "product" on a factory line to be inspected. It wasn't until Xiuying asked me if I was trying to torture her that it finally hit me: this suffocating "care" was actually a form of control, and it was only stressing her out more. Now, I’m learning to treat it like growing the roses on my balcony—I water them when they need it, but otherwise, I just let nature take its course. Since I’ve let go, not only is she more relaxed, but I’ve been sleeping much better myself lately.

It was mainly because I attended a health lecture by a provincial expert a while back. He specifically mentioned that when seniors feel like they’re constantly "under surveillance," it can actually trigger the "white coat effect," causing their readings to be artificially high. Hearing that gave me a real scare; as soon as I got home, I tore up that thick stack of analysis reports. Now, I pour all my energy into studying recipes, making sure to cook something light and flavorful every day. This kind of "quiet and subtle" care doesn't provoke her resentment, and I can still gauge how she’s doing just by looking at her appetite.

To be honest, it was that last class reunion that really got to me. Old Zhang told me that he used to obsess over his wife’s health metrics too, and it led to them bickering every single day. Eventually, he just signed her up for a photography class at a senior college to get her out and about. Once her mood improved, her vitals naturally stabilized. I realized he was absolutely right, so I tucked away all those pill bottles and medical gadgets. Now, I spend every day accompanying her to the park to take photos. Since we’ve both relaxed our mindsets, she’s actually become more energetic than before.

qa_items.question: Jianguo, I see you've been doing well lately. From being so anxious you couldn't sleep when the reform rumors started at the beginning of the year, to now deciding to take the initiative to learn that new system—what do you think has been the biggest change in you during this time?
qa_items.answer: Sigh, looking back, it’s quite an emotional journey. At the beginning of the year, I was really stuck in a rut. I kept thinking, after thirty-seven years of service, I was actually going to be "optimized" out by a machine right before retirement. That sense of powerlessness was overwhelming. But eventually, I came around. Times are changing, and even an old-timer like me has to keep moving. The biggest change was probably letting go of my pride; I stopped feeling like learning new technology was embarrassing. Instead, I realized that as long as I’m still on the job, being able to blend my years of experience with the new system is the only way to truly honor this uniform. Now that I’ve found my peace, I’m finally sleeping soundly again.

evidence.statement: Late at night, Li Jianguo was browsing information on "starting a business after retirement" and "retirement consulting." He jotted down a contingency plan in his notebook for "if the department gets cut." While this might just be a "just in case" preparation, it reflects a deep-seated anxiety about his future.

Li Jianguo pulled out a dusty old photo album, and as he looked at the family pictures from over a decade ago, tears began to fall—he felt a profound sense of helplessness, unable to stop the passage of time or the changing of all things.

Li Jianguo took the initiative to express his gratitude to his wife and stated clearly, "I've decided to master this new system." This reflects his shift from passive waiting to proactive adaptation, and confirms that his emotional state has moved from despair to a sense of calm.

choices.text: I suppose I’ve found a balance between being "useful" and "useless." At the beginning of the year, I was genuinely spooked by all the talk of "downsizing"; I felt like the craft I’d spent decades honing was about to become worthless overnight. Later, I started spending my weekends digging through those thick quality standard manuals I’d compiled over the years, gradually organizing them into clearer, more concise documentation. In the process, I realized that no matter how technology iterates, the core logic of quality remains unchanged. Now, I’ve stopped obsessing over specific tools and instead focus on teaching this management logic to the younger generation. Seeing that experience being passed down has finally given me true peace of mind.

To be honest, the biggest change has been learning how to "leverage" the people around me. I used to be incredibly stubborn, trying to power through everything on my own using my old ways, but the more I carried, the more exhausted I became. Over the past few months, I’ve tried to communicate more with my old classmate Li Ming and others, listening to their fresh perspectives on running businesses and management. It made me realize that the world out there is much broader than I had imagined. Now, instead of micromanaging every little thing like I used to, I’ve learned to delegate the routine tasks and focus solely on steering the big picture. This mental "unloading" has helped me regain a sense of control that I hadn't felt in a long time.

It’s really just a change in lifestyle. At the beginning of the year, I was constantly obsessing over my pension accounts, crunching numbers over and over. The more I calculated, the more uncertain I felt, spending whole nights tossing and turning over those old records. Eventually, I just locked all those spreadsheets in a drawer and started taking community gardening classes with Xiuying, pouring my energy into fixing up the yard and studying healthy living. Now I’ve realized that as long as you keep yourself healthy and get your domestic affairs in order, those shifts in the professional world aren't nearly as daunting. Once your mind relaxes, that sense of anxiety just naturally fades away.

qa_items.question: Lao Li, seeing you finally back in the community playing chess after that rough patch you went through—what does it mean to you to have found your rhythm again?
qa_items.answer: I have to admit, with a bit of shame, that between the company restructuring and all the chaos at home lately, I really lost my center. I even broke my eight-year streak of daily chess. Back then, I kept feeling like the world was passing me by; I was even starting to feel a sense of despair. But the other day, when I finally sat back down at the chessboard—even though I lost the first game out of rustiness—by the second game, that familiar feeling of commanding the pieces came rushing back. That’s when I finally felt grounded again. I realized that as long as I can still play a game of chess and watch the flowers bloom, I’m not old yet. I can still hold my own in this era.

Listening to the sound of the water coming to a boil, I realized that even though the world around me is changing, there’s still a place for my slow-paced way of life. Even though I just stood there watching all afternoon without playing a single game, that sense of belonging made me realize that these "old bones" of mine aren't ready to be retired just yet.

To be honest, I’m a bit ashamed to admit it, but between the company restructuring and all the chaos at home lately, I really lost my footing. qa_items.question: If you hadn't listened to Xiuying and Akiko's advice back then and had instead insisted on using that "perfect monitoring system" to manage her blood pressure, what do you think your relationship would be like now?
qa_items.answer: To be honest, I’m quite ashamed of it now. If I had stubbornly stuck to my guns back then, the atmosphere at home would probably be at freezing point by now. At the time, I felt I was just doing my duty, bringing my whole quality inspection routine into the house. The result? Xiuying was so stressed she did nothing but sigh, and Mingzi kept telling me I was overthinking things. If I hadn't let go, Xiuying would have surely felt that I didn't trust her—that I was treating her like one of my "quality projects." Our forty-plus years of marriage might have truly been worn away by those cold, hard numbers. Looking back, learning to trust and simply be there for her is far more important than obsessing over data points.

qa_items.question: Lately, while watering the flowers on the balcony, you’ve often been murmuring that "there's no need to control." Was there something in particular that inspired this shift in your mindset?
qa_items.answer: To be honest, it’s a bit embarrassing, but I only realized this after a heated argument with my younger son, Li Ming. I used to try and run the household just like I managed quality control at the factory. I’d even monitor Xiuying’s blood pressure several times a day and fill up entire notebooks with logs, which ended up putting her under immense pressure. It wasn't until Li Ming told me bluntly that I was "overthinking it" that I realized my so-called "care" was actually a form of torment. Now, looking at these roses on the balcony, I see that they bloom just as beautifully even without me precisely calculating every drop of water or bit of fertilizer. People are the same way—everyone has their own rhythm. Once you learn to let go, everyone breathes a little easier.

evidence.statement: Li Ming told Li Jianguo bluntly that he was "overthinking it," pointing out that his excessive monitoring and constant worrying could make his wife anxious as well. This allowed Li Jianguo, for the first time, to see the negative impact of his behavior through his son's eyes.

Li Jianguo found understanding and support in his son's words. By stating that he "only now understands this truth," he signaled a shift in his mindset—moving from a need for total control toward a willingness to embrace acceptance.

Li Jianguo resumed his habit of watering his plants in the evening, using his observations of their growth to express his newfound understanding of "accepting the laws of nature": "Some things don't need my control; they will grow on their own, following their own natural rhythm."

choices.text: Actually, it was a trip back to my hometown a while ago that gave me this inspiration, after seeing my brother’s vegetable garden. He’s always been a carefree soul—never bothers with tilling or fertilizing, just lets the crops grow with the seasons—and yet his harvest turns out better than anyone else’s. I sat there on the ridge of the field for a long time, thinking about how I’d gotten so used to managing projects at work. I was always trying to jam every single step into a rigid process, and it just ended up exhausting me and everyone around me. I’ve finally come to terms with it now: life is like those crops. As long as the general direction is right, you might as well just let nature take its course.

It’s funny how it happened—I was watching a nature documentary the other day about the ecological balance of forests. I realized that those towering trees don't need any human intervention at all; they find their own way to survive through the wind and rain. It got me thinking about my time in the workshop before I retired. Back then, I was always so terrified my apprentices would make a mistake that I practically tried to carve every single step of the process into their brains. In the end, I failed to raise anyone who could truly hold their own. I’ve realized now that whether you’re dealing with people or plants, this kind of "hands-off" trust is actually the highest form of management.

It all started with that old school reunion of mine. I was listening to Lao Zhang lamenting how he’d planned to go all out and help his son with the grandkids after retiring. But because he was so micromanaging—even setting "standards" for how many layers of clothes the kids should wear—it ended up causing such a rift that his son’s family is now afraid to even invite him over. Hearing that gave me a real jolt. Reflecting on my own "over-involvement" in family matters lately, I realized that this need for control is actually a form of selfishness. Since coming home, I’ve tucked away all those rules and regulations. I’m learning how to be an elder who offers support without constantly poking my nose into everything.

qa_items.question: Jianguo, I’ve noticed you’ve been totally distracted during our chess games lately. You even made a huge blunder last time, and you missed our get-together for the first time this week. Is all that corporate restructuring business wearing you out?
qa_items.answer: Ugh, don't even get me started. I felt embarrassed the moment I made that move—after eight years of playing, there’s no way I should be making such a rookie mistake. To be honest, I’ve had a lot on my mind lately. With the company rolling out that new automation system, it feels like my thirty years of experience suddenly don't mean much anymore. I can’t help feeling like I’m falling behind the times. The reason I didn't show up this week was that I just couldn't get my spirits up; I was afraid I’d space out again and ruin the mood for everyone. I need to get my head straight first.

evidence.statement: Li Jianguo made a glaring blunder in the chess game—a rare occurrence in his eight-year playing career. Stunned, he tried to explain it away by saying he had been "a bit tired from work lately," but the excuse felt forced.

qa_items.question: Jianguo, I’ve noticed a pattern with you lately. Whenever you’re under a lot of pressure or facing a tough problem, those hobbies that used to help you relax—like tending to your plants on the balcony or playing chess—actually seem to make you more obsessed and even more anxious. Do you feel like you have to be in total control of everything just to have some peace of mind?
qa_items.answer: Sigh, you caught me. Honestly, I’ve realized I’ve been overthinking things lately. The other day while watering the plants, I actually found myself using a magnifying glass to inspect every single leaf, and I’ve been mindlessly doodling circles in my notebook—I even lost focus and made the wrong moves during chess. I suppose after a lifetime in quality inspection, I’ve grown used to having a standard and a sense of control for everything. Now, facing these digital transformation changes at the company that I don't quite understand, I just feel uneasy. I guess I’m trying to compensate by obsessing over the little things I can still control.

qa_items.question: Jianguo, I remember for decades you’ve always been up at 6:30 sharp like clockwork to watch the news. How come I’ve been hearing your routine is a bit of a mess lately? Between getting up early to keep an eye on Sister Xiuying’s blood pressure and not being able to sleep in the middle of the night—is something weighing on your mind?
qa_items.answer: Sigh, once you get on in years, your mind just starts filling up with worries. I used to think that as long as I followed the rules, life would stay steady. But lately, the factory’s been talking about some "digital transformation," and it’s left me feeling unsettled. I’ve been waking up at two or three in the morning, unable to get back to sleep, with nothing but pensions and departmental restructuring on my mind. On top of that, Xiuying’s blood pressure has been fluctuating lately, so I don’t even care about the news anymore. I just want to get up a bit earlier to keep an eye on her while she takes her pressure. As long as her health holds up, I can feel a little more at peace.

qa_items.answer: Ugh, don't even get me started—my mind has been a total mess lately. All we hear at the factory these days is talk about "digital transformation" in meeting after meeting. I’ve been the quality control director for nearly forty years, but looking at those cold, lifeless automated systems, I suddenly feel like a lifetime of experience has become worthless overnight. On top of that, Xiuying’s blood pressure has been acting up again, and my son’s shop is struggling, which is another headache. My head feels like it's stuffed with tangled knots. I really didn't have the heart to head over this week; I just wanted some peace and quiet. I’m honestly terrified that this old frame of mine can't keep up with the times anymore and that I've become a burden to everyone.

qa_items.question: Jianguo, back in your thirty-seven years at the factory, you were all about quality standards and following the rules to a T. How come I’ve heard you lately saying things like "some things don't need to be controlled, you just have to go with the flow"? Has taking care of Sister Xiuying lately given you some new perspective?
qa_items.answer: Sigh, I’m ashamed to admit it, but I used to think people were like machine parts—as long as the process was right and the management was strict, nothing could go wrong. A while back, I was obsessed with monitoring Xiuying’s blood pressure every single day, filling up an entire notebook with readings. In the end, I just wore her out, and I couldn't get a wink of sleep myself. Eventually, it clicked: people aren't factory products; we all have our own rhythm. It’s just like the potted roses I keep on the balcony—they’ll bloom when they’re ready. No amount of precise calculation or constant prodding will make it happen any sooner. Now, I’ve learned to let go. I leave the medical stuff to the doctors and give Xiuying her peace of mind back. Once I stopped wound up so tight, everything actually started flowing much better for everyone.

Sigh, I guess it’s an old habit from my factory days—I always feel like I need a contingency plan for everything. A while back, seeing Xiu-ying’s health declining, I went behind her back to ask old colleagues about specialist appointments and even attended a community lecture on rehabilitation. But then I realized that while I was busy planning out her diet and exercise, she felt like she was just checking off a to-do list; she didn't even smile anymore. It wasn't until I sat with her on a park bench one day, just feeling the breeze and watching her chat so happily with her friends, that it finally clicked. Instead of obsessing over her vitals and metrics, what she really needs is for me to walk with her and talk with her. When she’s in good spirits, her health will naturally find its way back.

This whole experience really gave me pause for thought. I used to think that as long as I was meticulous enough, I could keep Xiuying’s condition under control. I even taught myself a fair amount of nursing care and made sure, without fail, that she walked her five thousand steps downstairs every single day. Then one time she snapped at me, saying she wasn't my apprentice and didn't need me hovering over her 24/7. Later, when I had some downtime, I picked up a few books on wellness. One of them mentioned that "mental well-being trumps physical medicine," and it hit me that I’d been far too obsessed with external intervention. Now, I’ve learned to give the reins back to her. If she wants to move, she moves; if she wants to rest, she rests. I just handle the logistics. This "go with the flow" approach has actually brought us closer than we ever were before.

It’s mainly because I’m planning to sign up for a photography class at a university for the elderly once I retire. Between the tuition, equipment maintenance, and my usual social expenses, my current pension just doesn't quite cut it.

By my calculations, I’m basically breaking even, with maybe a hundred or two to spare. But my biggest concern is the cost of renovating the house back in my hometown later on. I actually made a specific repair list in the back of my notebook, including things like roof waterproofing and structural reinforcement for the exterior walls. Since that major expense hasn't been factored in yet, I’d say I’m just barely getting by for now.

On paper, I still have two or three thousand left over every month, but I just don't trust that number. A while back, I heard some old friends talking about how bad inflation is these days, so I tried to run the numbers again assuming a 5% annual price hike. The more I calculated, the bigger the gap became. It got me so worked up and frustrated that I just ripped the page up.

qa_items.question: Jianguo, I’ve seen how much effort you’ve put into both family matters and the company’s restructuring over the past six months. You’ve gone from that initial drive to "keep everything under control" to now being able to talk about "following the natural course of things." What do you feel has been the biggest shift in your mindset?
qa_items.answer: I’m ashamed to admit it, but after a lifetime of working in quality control, I’ve grown accustomed to the idea that as long as the processes are right and the standards are strict, the results will always be under control. These past six months have really been a wake-up call. I kept a thick logbook of Xiuying’s blood pressure readings, but the more I hovered, the more anxious she got, and I started losing sleep right along with her. It wasn't until I was out on the balcony watering the plants that it finally clicked: people are just like flowers—they have their own temperaments and rhythms. The tighter you grip, the worse they grow. I’ve learned to let go now. I give her the necessary reminders, but I let the rest take its course. Honestly, I feel much more at peace.

This statement reflects his shift from passively accepting a doctor's diagnosis to actively establishing a "risk prevention mechanism," revealing his deep-seated fear of uncertainty.

choices.text: It all started back when I was hospitalized. I used to think I was the backbone of the family—nothing could happen without my say-so, and I wouldn't even trust anyone else to change a lightbulb. But during those two weeks in the hospital, I realized that even without me hovering over everything, Xiuying kept the house running perfectly, and the young folks at the office handled things just fine. Sitting by my hospital bed and watching the shadows of the trees outside, it hit me: the world keeps spinning with or without you. Now, I’ve learned to take a back seat. I no longer feel the need to do everything myself, and by giving control back to my family and colleagues, I’ve finally reclaimed my long-lost sleep.

To be honest, I’ve spent my whole life working in quality control, so I’ve always been under the impression that as long as the processes are right and the standards are strict, the outcome is guaranteed to be under control. But these past six months have really been a wake-up call. I kept a thick logbook of Xiuying’s blood pressure readings, yet the more I hovered, the more anxious she got—and I ended up with insomnia myself. It wasn't until I was out on the balcony watering the plants that it finally clicked: people are just like flowers; they have their own temperaments and rhythms. The tighter you grip, the worse they grow. I’ve learned to let go now. I give her the necessary reminders, but I let the rest take its course. Ironically, I feel much more at peace this way.

Actually, it was that reunion with my old comrades that struck me the most. Back when I was mentoring apprentices at work, I was always a stickler for the rules—not even a millimeter of error was allowed. I brought that same rigidity home to Xiuying, even nitpicking over how she folded the dishcloths. But at the reunion, I saw my old squad leader; though his health wasn't what it used to be, he had such an open-minded outlook. He said for our generation, "taking care of your health is the greatest contribution you can make to society." It finally clicked for me. Instead of obsessing over those unchangeable little habits, I’d rather spend more time walking with her or listening to music together. Now, I no longer sweat the small stuff. As long as we’re headed in the right direction, life feels much more comfortable.

I used to believe that "diligence makes up for a lack of talent"—that as long as I ran a few more errands and kept a closer eye on things, both at home and work, everything would turn out fine. Over the past six months, I even signed up for a gardening class, hoping to revive a few half-dead azaleas I have at home. I ended up fertilizing them and moving their pots every single day, only to watch them wither even faster. Later, while hiking in the countryside with some veteran gardeners, I saw wildflowers blooming brilliantly across the hills with no one to tend them. It was then I realized that you shouldn't force things too hard. Now, I delegate the household chores to my children and enjoy some peace and quiet myself; surprisingly, the family atmosphere is more harmonious than ever.

qa_items.question: I see you’ve been back at the community center playing chess lately, and you sound much more like your old self. A while back, between the insomnia and missing all those get-togethers, I was really wondering how you were holding up. Looking back now, did those things you were worrying about ever get sorted out?
qa_items.answer: Ugh, don't even get me started—I was really stuck in a rut for a while. Back then, the factory was constantly harping on about "digital transformation," and I couldn't shake the feeling that my decades of experience were about to be rendered useless by machines. I was so worried I’d wake up at two or three in the morning, just staring at the ceiling thinking about my pension and the kids. That time I missed our chess game was simply because I didn't have the heart for it. But lately, I’ve come around. No matter how much the times change, life goes on. Honestly, after I lost that first game back at the club, I actually felt a sense of peace. I realized that as long as I can still sit down for a game of chess and look after the roses on my balcony, I’m still in control of my life. Now, I’m slowly adapting to the reforms at work. There’s no use in rushing; I’ll just focus on doing what’s right in front of me as best as I can.

evidence.statement: Li Jianguo experienced his first bout of significant sleep disturbance—waking up at two or three in the morning with his mind racing over questions like "What if the department gets cut?" and "Will my pension be enough?" For someone accustomed to early nights and early mornings, this was highly unusual.

In a strained, low voice, Li Jianguo called his chess partner and said, "I've been quite busy with work lately, so I won't be coming this week." It was the first time he had ever missed a session in a chess routine he had maintained for eight years.

Now, I’m much more at peace. Sure, I have to learn the new systems, but my decades of experience are an anchor for the team. Taking my time to mentor the apprentices is a kind of value in itself.

That’s when it suddenly hit me—life is about so much more than just what happens at the factory. Now that I’ve donated those notes to the factory museum, a huge weight has been lifted off my shoulders. qa_items.question: Jianguo, I’ve noticed you’ve eased up quite a bit lately regarding your wife’s blood pressure. You went from hovering over that monitoring chart every single day, to realizing it was actually becoming a burden for her, and now you’re both talking about ‘cheering each other on.’ Has this whole experience given you any new insights into how a couple should get along?
qa_items.answer: I have to admit, after a lifetime of working in quality control, I’ve grown accustomed to the idea that as long as you set rigid standards and micromanage every process, you’re bound to get a good product. At first, I truly treated Xiuying’s health like a production line; the charts I drew up were even more detailed than the reports at the factory. It wasn't until she told me I was suffocating her that it finally hit me—she is my wife, not my subordinate. Now that I’ve cut back on the monitoring, I actually feel more at peace. I’ve realized that for a couple, the essence of caring is trust and companionship, not rigid control. We have to learn to face the ups and downs together, rather than me just pushing blindly on my own.

This sense of shared responsibility has taught me that real harmony in life comes from this kind of clear-cut coordination, rather than one person just worrying themselves sick in the dark.

qa_items.question: Lao Li, I remember in the days following that hospital checkup, you suddenly started sticking colored notes all over the house and getting up earlier than ever—you even pushed back your news-watching routine that you'd stuck to for all those years. What was going through your head back then?
qa_items.answer: To be honest, I was feeling a bit restless back then. During that follow-up, seeing Xiuying’s systolic pressure suddenly jump to 150 really weighed on me; I felt like the old routine of "monthly check-ups" just wasn't going to cut it anymore. Having worked in quality control my whole life, I’m used to nipping problems in the bud. So, I figured I’d set up a "monitoring system" for her health using sticky notes and a more precise blood pressure monitor. Getting up fifteen minutes early that day was just to make sure I got an accurate reading before she had breakfast. After all, to me, her health is far more important than catching the evening news.

qa_items.answer: Sigh, honestly, I was really stuck in a rut back then, staring at old photo albums and crying, feeling like I was old and useless. It was my Xiuying who eventually pulled me out of it. She told me that in over thirty years, there’s no storm I haven't weathered—I’ve made it through everything from the planned economy to the joint-stock reforms, so I’d surely get through this too. I thought about it, and she’s right. Instead of sitting here waiting for retirement and worrying myself sick, I might as well use these last two years to figure this new stuff out. Even if it’s just to give my son some advice or teach my grandson how to play chess, I’ve got to keep up with the times first.

qa_items.answer: I have to admit, I’m quite ashamed of myself. It wasn't until Xiuying finally opened up and told me how much pressure she felt whenever I hovered over her to check her readings that it hit me—she’s my lifelong partner, not a spare part on an assembly line. I’ve learned to let go now. Instead of obsessing over every data point, I’ve replaced "supervision" with "companionship." Even if it’s just watering the plants or taking a stroll together on the weekend, this sense of "facing it together" has made us both feel much more at peace.

My plan is to join a RV tour with my wife after I retire. While we’re still mobile, I want to head out to the Great Northwest and see those raw, untouched landscapes. That sense of openness makes me feel like all the rules and restrictions I used to fret over just don't matter anymore.

Actually, it was returning to my hometown for a veterans' reunion last year that really moved me. I saw my old squad leader running a non-profit woodworking shop in the village after his retirement. Every day, he’d be there tinkering away with a group of kids—not to make a profit, but just for the pure joy of it. I used to think that everything needed a quantifiable goal, that I always had to strive to be the best. But now I realize that retirement shouldn't be another "quality competition." I'm planning to turn my study into a studio and sign up for a traditional Chinese painting class at the senior college. Picking up my old hobbies and just following my heart—that’s the kind of life I’m after now.

It all started when I stepped in to help my neighbor, Old Wang, settle a family dispute. After he retired, he became a bit of a busybody, which really rubbed his daughter-in-law the wrong way. While I was talking him down, it suddenly hit me that I was exactly the same—always trying to force everyone in my family into some kind of "standard mode." I’ve learned to let go now; the younger generation will find their own way. My plan for retirement is to head out to the suburbs with a few old buddies and rent a small plot of land to grow vegetables. I’m not worried about the yield, and I won’t be using any fertilizers or pesticides. I just want to experience the natural cycle of planting in the spring and harvesting in the fall. Slowing down the pace of life like that is so much better than obsessing over clocking in or hitting targets.

To be honest, I used to think life was just like the parts in a workshop—as long as I kept a sharp eye on them and set high standards, I’d get a good product. But when Xiuying’s blood pressure started acting up a while back, my "quality control" approach turned the whole house upside down; she was exhausted, and so was I. It wasn't until I started tending to the roses on the balcony that I finally figured it out: flowers bloom and wither in their own time, and people are no different. I’ve made peace with it now. I’m done trying to micromanage everything in retirement. Instead, I plan to spend more time in Shanghai with my grandson, teach him some chess, and just let things take their natural course while enjoying the family. My heart feels much more at ease this way.

I used to think as long as I stuck to my schedule, everything would be fine, but now I realize that my family’s comfort matters far more than my rigid routines. qa_items.question: Jianguo, I’ve seen how much you’ve had on your plate these past six months, juggling Xiuying’s health issues and everything with the company. Since her blood pressure started acting up last year, have you noticed any shift in your own mindset?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? It’s definitely been a long and winding road. When her blood pressure first hit 150, I was incredibly wound up. I had this idea that as long as I kept meticulous records and strictly controlled her salt intake, I could somehow "manage" the risk away. I even questioned if the doctor’s equipment was faulty. But eventually, I realized that this forced sense of control was just exhausting for both of us—the more I obsessed over the numbers, the more anxious she became. I think I’ve finally made peace with it now. My mindset has shifted from "obsessing over details" to "proactive adaptation." I’ve learned to let go; instead of staring at the blood pressure monitor every day, I’m putting my energy into things that actually matter, like going for walks with her or figuring out the new system. This feeling of "making peace with uncertainty" has actually brought me much more headspace.

When your mind is in such a mess, your game falls apart completely.

choices.text: To be honest, back then, my biggest fear wasn't losing my job—it was the fear of truly becoming "useless." That day, I skipped the chess game and spent half the afternoon wandering through the flower and bird market alone. Watching those retired old men haggling over a few cents for fish food only deepened my sense of panic; I couldn't shake the feeling that I was looking at a miniature of my own future. If I hadn't gone to that market and had instead shown up at the chessboard on time—even if just to help set up the pieces or pass around cigarettes—that sense of "being needed" would have helped me pull through those toughest days. You see, once a person drifts away from their familiar circle, it’s all too easy to fall into the illusion that the world has forgotten you.

Sigh, the truth is, I was just stuck in a dead end back then. I had this obsessive need to dig out all the bankbooks and property deeds and reorganize them just to feel some peace of mind. That day, instead of going to the gathering, I holed myself up in the study, recalculating years of ledgers one by one, trying to find a shred of security in those numbers. If only I’d listened to my wife and gone to the park to practice Tai Chi with the old gang, or even just sat by the river for a while, that suffocating feeling of being left behind by the times might not have felt so unbearable. At the end of the day, I just kept myself too tightly wound and didn't leave a window open to catch my breath.

I’ve reflected on this since then. At the time, I was even tempted to file for early retirement and went as far as spending half the night at the old factory director’s place. I skipped my usual chess game that evening, hoping to pick his brain for some advice on how to handle the "reforms." Instead, I ended up listening to him talk about the wave of layoffs back in his day all night, which only made me feel more suffocated. If I hadn't gone to see him and had just headed to the chess club as usual to play a few rounds and listen to everyone grumble about the little things in life, I might have realized that this shake-up at work was just a minor bump in the road. I wouldn't have ended up putting myself through all those sleepless nights.

Sigh, well... to be honest, my mind was such a mess back then that even if I had sat down at the chessboard, I probably would’ve just ended up staring blankly at the pieces. It’s hard to describe—that day, after turning down my old friend’s invitation and hanging up the phone, I looked at the old photos in my office and felt completely overwhelmed by this sense of being left behind by the times. I eventually forced myself to go back a week later. Even though I lost the first game miserably, the moment I touched the pieces, I felt that grounding sense of "I’m still here" finally return. Looking back now, if I hadn't hidden myself away, that deep-seated despair might have actually cleared up a lot sooner.

qa_items.question: Jianguo, I remember when you first started changing your routine to check your wife's blood pressure, you began waking up fifteen minutes early every single day. If you hadn't forced yourself to break that thirty-year habit back then, and had just stuck to your guns—getting up at six-thirty to watch the news like always—do you think your sleep quality and stress levels might have been a bit better in the years that followed?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put it? Even if I hadn’t been fifteen minutes early, I doubt I could have truly relaxed. At that time, Xiuying’s blood pressure was fluctuating wildly; for me, keeping her health under control was far more important than watching the news. That sense of "uncertainty" was the real root of my later insomnia and those midnight panics. Even if I had woken up on time, just seeing that automated system set to replace the experience of us veteran workers, or thinking about Li Ming’s business, my heart would still be racing. I suppose it’s just a common trait of our generation—we’re used to everything being "orderly and under control."

qa_items.question: Hey Li, I’ve noticed you’ve changed your wake-up time this past week and you’re putting up colored sticky notes all over the house. Is everything okay?
qa_items.answer: Sigh, it’s all because I’m worried about Xiuying. Her recent checkup showed that her blood pressure has been fluctuating wildly—her systolic reached 150—and it’s really shaken me. To make sure I measure her blood pressure on time and keep an eye on her medication, I went ahead and moved my 6:30 alarm, which I’ve kept for decades, fifteen minutes earlier. I don't even have time to check the news first anymore. As for those sticky notes, I put them up in the kitchen and bedroom because I’m afraid my memory might fail me; they’re there to remind us about low salt and medication schedules. After all, you can’t afford to be careless with things like this.

You were so against it then—it even had you losing sleep and skipping our Wednesday chess games. qa_items.answer: Sigh, I’m ashamed to admit it, but I really was stuck in a rut lately. I kept feeling like I was too old to keep up with the times, and I even thought about throwing in the towel. It was actually Xiuying who snapped me out of it. She comforted me the other day, saying that I’d made it through over thirty years of challenges—from the wave of factory layoffs to the joint-stock reforms—so why should I be afraid of just switching to a new system? I thought about it, and she’s right. Instead of sitting around worrying and crying over old photo albums, I might as well give myself one last push before retirement. Even if it’s just so I can teach my grandson chess and keep up with his pace, I’ve got to get my spark back.

qa_items.answer: I’m ashamed to admit it, but I used to think that recording every single piece of data in a notebook and plotting trend charts was the only way to be responsible. That was until Xiuying told me straight out that I was putting her under immense pressure. I went back to my study and sat there for a long time, looking at that blood pressure log—which looked more like a production line management sheet—and realized I wasn't actually taking care of her. I have to learn to let go and replace that rigid "monitoring" with genuine trust and companionship.

qa_items.question: Lao Li, ever since Sister Xiuying’s blood pressure started fluctuating last year, you’ve been doing everything you can to "manage" her health. After all that’s happened, do you have any new thoughts on this idea of needing to be in control of everything?
qa_items.answer:
To be honest, I’m a bit ashamed to admit it now. I used to think people were just like factory parts—as long as the process was right and the standards were strict, you’d get the desired result. Last year, I was obsessed with those numbers every single day, sticking up sticky notes and scribbling in my notebook, until Xiuying asked me something that left me stunned: "Are you taking care of me, or are you torturing me?" 

I’ve finally realized that people have their own rhythms, just as flowers have their own laws of nature. It’s like the roses on my balcony—you water them when they need it, but for the rest, you have to learn to let go. Although I still find myself on edge occasionally over my son’s business or the new changes at the factory, I’m trying to strip that "management standard" away from my home life. Just spending more time walking with her is worth so much more than keeping track of those cold, lifeless numbers.

qa_items.answer: Sigh, people are such strange creatures. A couple of years back, when things were piling up at work and Xiuying’s blood pressure was always acting up, I used to wake up at 6:15 sharp every single morning. It was like I had a clockwork spring wound up inside me—my head was constantly filled with checking blood pressure, logging data, and catching the news. It was exhausting, sure, but every minute felt like it had a purpose. Now that I’ve suddenly retired, that drive to "always be doing something" has just vanished. Even though I don’t have to worry about being late or dealing with quality complaints anymore, when I open my eyes in the morning and see all that empty time ahead of me, I feel adrift. I just don't know where to direct my energy for the day.

qa_items.question: Jianguo, you’ve spent your whole life in quality inspection. Looking back on all those years of experience, what would you say is the biggest difference between "controlling quality" and "looking after a life"?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put it? I’ve been thinking about this a lot lately. I suppose that’s the biggest difference between life and machine parts.

qa_items.question: Lao Li, I remember back when you first discovered Xiuying’s blood pressure was fluctuating—you were a nervous wreck. I had this idea that if I just used enough sticky notes and tracked every single data point meticulously, I could "control" the cycle of life and health just like controlling the defect rate in a factory. Over time, I slowly realized that the human body isn't like a machine part; some fluctuations won't just disappear no matter how hard I stare at them. Instead of keeping myself and Xiuying on edge every day, it’s better to turn this attention to detail into a simple lifestyle habit. Even if there are occasional ups and downs now, as long as we stay informed and follow our routine, life can still be steady and peaceful. I suppose this is the journey from "trying to control everything" to "learning to accept."

qa_items.question: Jianguo, looking back on the past two years—from your wife’s illness to your official retirement—do you feel that your insistence on "rigor and control" helped you get through the hard times, or did it actually end up trapping you?
qa_items.answer: Sigh, that question really hits home for me. At first, I was indeed "stuck." I kept trying to manage Xiuying’s blood pressure as if it were the quality control of a factory part, staring at the numbers and plotting trend charts every day. It made the whole family tense, and Xiuying even felt like I was torturing her. But eventually, it clicked: that kind of rigor, if misapplied, is just control; but if applied to respecting the natural rhythm of life, it becomes responsibility. Once I learned to let go and redirected that meticulous energy into studying how roses bloom naturally or how to play a good game of chess with my grandson, I realized this personality of mine is actually the anchor of my later years. It allows me to calmly embrace the changes I cannot control.

Once, I even messed up the settings and accidentally bricked the smart lock, leaving myself stranded outside for over an hour. During that hour, I did a lot of thinking. Our generation is used to doing everything ourselves, but now we have to learn how to "lean on others." I would tell him: don't be so hard on yourself. If you don't understand something, just ask—there’s no shame in asking the neighbor’s kid for help. Our value lies in our perspective and experience, not in how fast we can push buttons.

Old friend, I know exactly how you feel. Right after I retired, I was so desperate to prove I wasn't "washed up" that I forced myself to sign up for a programming class at a senior college. I nearly got into a shouting match with the instructor during the very first lesson because I couldn't make heads or tails of the technical jargon. Later, my little grandson pointed at his half-finished Lego set and told me, "Grandpa, you have to look at the manual first, don't just jam them in." That’s when it hit me—we have to admit that times have changed. My advice to him would be: don't rush to tackle the hard stuff right away. Find a young person in the family to be your "little teacher." We provide the experience, they provide the tech skills. This kind of "intergenerational partnership" actually helps keep our mindset more grounded.

Sigh, I can really relate to this. To be honest, I was losing sleep over that new system at the factory a while back; I was even secretly googling "how do middle-aged people learn software" in the middle of the night. But then I had an epiphany, much like the roses on my balcony—they have their own rhythm of growth, and you can't rush them no matter how anxious you are. I would tell him: we’ve lived through the planned economy and the wave of layoffs; what kind of storms haven't we weathered? The most important thing is to "let go" and "accept." Stop trying to control everything like we used to. Try to trust the younger generation and the new ways of doing things, and you'll find that some things just have a way of working themselves out.

qa_items.question: I see you've finally returned to the chess club. How did you manage to get through that period when you were missing out on the games?
qa_items.answer: Ugh, don't even get me started—my mind was a complete mess back then. The company was making a huge fuss about "digital transformation," and as an old inspector who’s been on the job for thirty-seven years, I couldn't help but feel like I was being left behind by the machines. On top of that, Xiuying’s health was acting up, and for a while, I genuinely started to wonder if I really had grown old and useless. Back then, forget about playing chess—I couldn't even sleep. I didn't even dare pick up the phone when my chess buddies called, just because I was afraid they’d ask how I was doing. Fortunately, I eventually came around and realized that no matter how the times change, life has to go on. So, last Friday, I finally sat back down at the chessboard. After winning a game, I felt like I’d finally gotten a bit of my old spirit back.

No matter how precise my records were, I couldn't force a bloom. Once I let go, everyone breathed easier, and ironically, Xiuying’s blood pressure actually stabilized.

Ugh, don't even get me started. I used to be obsessed with applying "lean management" to every little household expense. From big-ticket appliance invoices down to tiny grocery receipts, I had everything categorized and filed away—I even had a dedicated spot just for old newspapers. Then came the time a pipe burst. My first instinct wasn't to rush and shut off the water, but to dig through the maintenance manual to figure out who was liable. Seeing Xiuying scrambling around in the water made it hit me: what a home needs is a helping hand, not a "quality inspector" who’s only interested in pointing fingers. Since then, I’ve cleared out all those rigid rules and files. Things are a bit messier now, but the house finally feels lived-in.

It’s actually quite a bittersweet story. I used to bring the "safety protocols" from the factory back home with me. I insisted on checking the gas stove and door locks three times before leaving the house, and I even set up a cleaning roster for the whole family, complete with a "Household Hygiene Reward and Punishment System." When my grandson came to stay last summer, he was so intimidated by all these rules that he didn't even dare to speak above a whisper. After he went back to school, he wrote an essay describing me as a "stern exam proctor." It broke my heart when I read that, and that’s when I finally tore down all those regulations I’d plastered on the walls. Now, I play soccer with him in the living room without worrying about messing up the sofa. This kind of "rule-free" joy is what real life is all about.

qa_items.question: Jianguo, if an old colleague of yours was nearing retirement now—worrying just like you did about falling behind the times or fearing some unexpected family crisis—what insights have you gained during this period that you’d use to talk him through it?
qa_items.answer: Sigh, what can I say? I know exactly how that feels. I’d tell him not to get stuck in a rut thinking he’s useless. Honestly, the decades of experience we have is something a machine just can’t replace, and even the new director is still counting on us to show them the ropes. Another thing—when you’re feeling down, don't keep it bottled up. Talk to your family more, or head down to the community center to play a few rounds of chess with the guys. You’ll realize everyone still cares about you. As long as we don't shut ourselves off, we’ll get through this rough patch.

evidence.statement: Late at night, Li Jianguo was browsing information on "starting a business after retirement" and "retirement consulting." He jotted down a contingency plan in his notebook titled "If the Department is Downsized." While this might just be a "just in case" preparation, it reflects his deep-seated anxiety about the future.

Encouraged by his wife’s words—"You’ve adapted to every change the factory has thrown at you over the years; you can definitely handle this one too"—Li Jianguo’s mindset began to shift. He started reflecting on his thirty-seven-year career, rising from a front-line operator to the head of the quality department. Realizing he had indeed weathered numerous transformations—from the planned economy to joint-stock restructuring—he found the courage to face the new system once again.

Master Wang took the initiative to visit, saying, "We all noticed you haven't been around for chess lately, and it didn't feel right. I wanted to come check on you." This gesture broke Li Jianguo’s isolation, making him feel seen and cared for.

qa_items.answer: I honestly can't give you the exact details... I was indeed looking up all sorts of brands online a while back—checked out Omron and everything—and I kept obsessing over the accuracy. But as for which specific model I settled on or how much I ended up spending, it’s completely slipped my mind. I was so caught up in comparing specs and drowning in data at the time that I didn't really pay attention to the actual bill.

choices.text: In the end, I took the advice of my neighbor Old Wang’s son and had him get me an export-model unit directly through a pharmaceutical agent. Although it was a few hundred yuan more expensive than what’s on the market, I heard it’s the same kind doctors use themselves and is simple to operate. I figured it was better than wasting my time online trying to wrap my head around all those complicated specs.

I happened to be accompanying my sister-in-law to the community hospital for her checkup that day. I noticed a few display models set up next to the information desk, so we decided to give them a try. She found one with a voice broadcast feature particularly convenient, so I just went ahead and bought that exact model at a nearby pharmacy. It cost me about four hundred yuan; I didn't even bother looking at other brands.

Actually, I was originally planning to buy an imported brand. But when I went to the mall that day, I happened to come across a promotion for a domestic medical device brand. After carefully comparing their on-site clinical error reports, I realized the value for money was actually quite high. They even threw in six months of free consumables. In the end, I got it for less than 300 yuan, which I think was a pretty good deal.
